
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]  
> **Define the term "Thing" in the context of IoT. List any two physical identifiers used to uniquely identify a Thing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **"Thing"** in IoT is any physical object (device, person, animal, or asset) that is uniquely identifiable, capable of sensing/actuating, and connected to a network for data exchange.  
Two physical identifiers: **RFID tag** and **QR code** (or barcode, NFC). *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two identifiers.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]  
> **Differentiate between a Sensor and an Actuator with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **sensor** converts a physical quantity into a measurable electrical signal (e.g., **DHT11** converting humidity to voltage).  
An **actuator** converts an electrical signal into a physical action (e.g., a **DC motor** rotating when current is applied).  
*(1.5 marks for definitions, 1.5 marks for the examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For 14-mark questions, you MUST show *both* a diagram **and** a labelled component list. A pure text answer without a block diagram is capped at **9/14 marks** by most KTU evaluators.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model ESE]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **physical design of an IoT system**, identifying all major hardware blocks and their functions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A smart agriculture deployment requires sensors spread over 50 hectares with no grid power. Recommend suitable **sensor, microcontroller, communication technology, power source, and identifier** — and justify each choice. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The physical design of an IoT system consists of **six functional hardware blocks**:

1. **Sensors** — convert physical parameters (soil moisture, temperature, light) into electrical signals. Example: **capacitive soil-moisture sensor**.
2. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — digitises the analog sensor output for the MCU. Usually integrated in modern MCUs.
3. **Microcontroller (MCU)** — the central processor; example: **ESP32** with dual-core Tensilica CPU, integrated Wi-Fi/BLE.
4. **Actuators** — perform physical actions on command (water-pump relay, solenoid valve).
5. **Communication module** — implements the chosen wireless protocol (e.g., **LoRa SX1276** for long range).
6. **Power subsystem** — battery plus energy harvesting (solar + buck-boost regulator).
7. **Identifier** — uniquely addresses each node (e.g., **EPC Gen-2 RFID tag** for non-electronic assets, **IPv6 address** for electronic ones).

A block diagram is mandatory:

```
[Sensor] → [ADC] → [MCU] → [Comm Module] → [Gateway] → [Cloud]
              ↑          ↓
          [Identifier] [Actuator]

All blocks ← [Power Source: Battery / Solar / Mains]
```

*(7 marks: 3 for the block diagram, 2 for block-wise description, 1 for example device names, 1 for interconnections.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

For 50 hectares with no grid power, the **recommended stack** is:

| Block | Recommended Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor | **Capacitive soil-moisture + DHT22** | Low-power, accurate, robust in field |
| MCU | **ESP32 (with deep-sleep)** | 10 µA sleep current, dual-core, on-chip ADC |
| Communication | **LoRa (SX1276, 868/915 MHz)** | 2–15 km range, no SIM, no cellular cost |
| Power | **6 V 2 W solar + 18650 Li-ion (3000 mAh)** | Autonomous, no cabling |
| Identifier | **EPC Gen-2 RFID + IPv6 over LoRaWAN** | Each node has a unique 64-bit DevEUI + 128-bit IPv6 |

*Justification core point:* LoRa + solar lets a single gateway cover the entire 50-hectare plot with **zero recurring operational cost**, satisfying KTU's "energy-efficient IoT" learning outcome. *(7 marks: 1 per row + 2 for the overall justification paragraph.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model ESE]`

> **(a)** Compare **RFID, NFC, QR code, and Barcode** as physical identifiers for IoT. Mention read range, data capacity, security, and a suitable use case for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An IoT wearable for elderly health monitoring must run for **at least 6 months on a single coin cell (CR2032, 220 mAh, 3 V)**. Given the average current budget, justify the choice of **MCU, sensor, and communication protocol** with a numerical argument. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Identifier | Read Range | Data Capacity | Security | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Barcode (1D)** | 0 – 0.5 m | 1–50 chars | None (visible) | Retail product labels |
| **QR code (2D)** | 0 – 1 m | 4 000+ chars | Low (visible) | Mobile payments, tickets |
| **RFID (passive UHF)** | 1 – 10 m | 96 – 128 bits EPC | Medium (encryption optional) | Supply-chain pallets |
| **NFC (HF, ISO 14443)** | < 0.1 m | up to 4 KB | High (secure element) | Contactless cards, IoT pairing |

*Why this comparison matters in KTU:* It tests the student's ability to map **identifier type → security/privacy needs** of an IoT application (a learning outcome in PEMRT525). *(7 marks: 5 for the table, 2 for a closing application-paragraph.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Energy budget calculation:**

CR2032 capacity: $C_{\text{bat}} = 220 \text{ mAh}$ at 3 V.  
Target lifetime: 6 months = 6 × 30 × 24 = **4 320 hours**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg, max}} &= \dfrac{C_{\text{bat}}}{\text{Lifetime}} \\
&= \dfrac{220 \text{ mAh}}{4320 \text{ h}} \\
&\approx 0.0509 \text{ mA} \\
&\approx 50.9 \text{ µA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the entire node's *average* current must stay **below ~50 µA**.

**Recommended stack to meet this budget:**

| Block | Choice | Typical Current | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| MCU | **Nordic nRF52832 (ARM Cortex-M4, BLE)** | ~5.5 µA in sleep | Wake-on-event, ideal for wearables |
| Sensor | **MAX30102 (PPG heart-rate + SpO₂)** | ~600 µA active, duty-cycled | Industry standard, very low standby |
| Communication | **BLE 5.0 (advertising mode)** | ~7.5 mA TX peak, average < 20 µA | Phone as gateway, no SIM, low duty cycle |

*Numerical argument:* With a **1 % duty cycle**, the sensor's $600 \text{ µA}$ contributes only $6 \text{ µA}$ average. Adding $20 \text{ µA}$ for BLE and $5 \text{ µA}$ for MCU sleep gives **≈ 31 µA** — comfortably below the 50.9 µA budget, ensuring **> 6 months** of operation. *(7 marks: 2 for the budget derivation, 3 for the table, 2 for the duty-cycle justification.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (pin this in your notebook):**

- ☐ **Physical Design** = the *hardware* view of IoT: things, sensors, actuators, MCUs, comm modules, power, identifiers.
- ☐ **Sensor vs Actuator** — sensor *detects*, actuator *acts*. (Free 3 marks if you state this once.)
- ☐ **Microcontroller vs SBC** — MCU (Arduino, ESP32) runs bare-metal firmware; SBC (Raspberry Pi) runs an OS. The exam often tests this distinction.
- ☐ **Identifier standards** — Barcode (1D), QR (2D), RFID (UHF Gen-2), NFC (HF), IPv6 (128-bit). The EPC standard gives 96-bit unique IDs.
- ☐ **Power budget formula** — $\text{Lifetime} = C_{\text{bat}} / I_{\text{avg}}$ — must be practiced with units.
- ☐ **Communication trade-off** — Range ↑ ⇔ Data Rate ↓ (LoRa vs Wi-Fi). Use the table in §3.4 for any justification question.
- ☐ **FSPL formula** is the only range-related equation you need to memorise for this module.
- ☐ **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners cap text-only answers at 9/14.
- ☐ **Mention an identifier** even if the question doesn't — it shows you understand the *unique-addressability* requirement of every "Thing".
- ☐ **Energy harvesting + battery** is the de-facto standard for *outdoor* IoT; mention it for any field-deployment scenario.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logical Design of IoT - IoT Functional Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logical Design of IoT - IoT Functional Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the "Logical Design" of IoT?

The **Logical Design of an IoT system** refers to the *abstract, functional representation* of how data flows from the physical world, through processing layers, and finally to the end user. Unlike **Physical Design** (which specifies *which chip, sensor, or radio* is used), Logical Design answers the question: *"What functional blocks must exist, and how must they logically interact, to sense, communicate, process, store, analyse, and present IoT data?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology)**
> The logical design of an IoT system is the high-level abstraction of the system that describes:
> 1. The **IoT Functional Blocks**
> 2. The **Communication Models** between blocks
> 3. The **Communication APIs** used for interaction
> 4. The **Data Semantics** and formats exchanged
>
> It is technology-agnostic and focuses on *what* the system does, not *how* it is physically implemented.

## 1.2 The Six Canonical IoT Functional Blocks

Every IoT system — whether a smart home thermostat or a national smart-grid — can be decomposed into **six primary functional blocks**:

| # | Functional Block | Core Responsibility | Real-World Example |
|---|------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Sensing / Identification** | Acquire physical data via sensors/actuators | DHT22 temperature sensor, RFID tag |
| 2 | **Communication / Connectivity** | Transmit data across networks (wired/wireless) | Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, 6LoWPAN, MQTT broker |
| 3 | **Processing / Computation** | Transform raw data into meaningful information | Edge MCU (ESP32), Cloud server (AWS IoT) |
| 4 | **Storage** | Persist structured/unstructured data | Time-series DB (InfluxDB), Cloud S3 bucket |
| 5 | **Analytics / Intelligence** | Extract insights, predict, automate decisions | Anomaly detection ML model, rule engine |
| 6 | **User Interface / Application** | Render data and accept user commands | Mobile dashboard, Blynk app, Grafana panel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Security & Management** is treated as a *cross-cutting* functional block that overlays all six layers — it is not a standalone stage but a pervasive concern (encryption, authentication, firmware updates, access control).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "IoT as a Hospital Patient Monitoring System"**
> 
> Imagine an IoT system as a **hospital ICU**:
> - **Sensing Block** = The ECG electrodes, BP cuff, and pulse oximeter clipped to the patient.
> - **Communication Block** = The wires and Wi-Fi that carry vitals to the nurse's station.
> - **Processing Block** = The bedside monitor that computes heart rate from raw ECG waveform.
> - **Storage Block** = The patient file cabinet where the last 24 hours of vitals are logged.
> - **Analytics Block** = The AI alert system that screams "CODE BLUE!" if vitals cross thresholds.
> - **Application Block** = The doctor's tablet showing live vitals and history.
> 
> The *logical* design is the **process map** of the ICU — *who hands what to whom*. The *physical* design is the **bill of materials** — *which exact Philips monitor, which exact cable*.

This analogy makes it instantly clear: a student should never confuse *"what each block does"* (logical) with *"which specific device does it"* (physical).

## 1.4 Why Logical Design Comes First (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Order in the KTU Curriculum:**
> 1. First, we design the **logic** (functional blocks + communication models).
> 2. Then, we design the **physics** (hardware selection, circuit design).
> 3. Finally, we implement the **logic in firmware** and the **physics in hardware**.
>
> Skipping the logical design leads to the classic KTU practical error: *"the hardware works, but the system does not communicate meaningfully."*

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants Referenced in IoT Logical Design

- **Latency** (delay from sensing to decision) — typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**
- **Throughput** (data rate) — measured in **bits per second (bps)**, **kbps**, or **Mbps**
- **Data Volume** — measured in **bytes (B)**, **kilobytes (kB)**, or **megabytes (MB)**
- **Sampling Rate** — measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **samples per second (S/s)**
- **Availability** — measured as a **percentage (%)** (e.g., 99.9% uptime = "three nines")
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Functional Blocks — A Layered Analytical Breakdown

### Block 1: Sensing / Identification (The *Perception* Layer)

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Sensor** detects a physical phenomenon (temperature, light, motion, pressure).
2. **Transducer** converts the physical signal into an analog electrical signal.
3. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** digitizes the signal into discrete numerical values.
4. The digitized value is tagged with a **unique identifier** (e.g., UUID, MAC address, RFID EPC code).
5. Output: a tagged numerical data packet ready for transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this block matters:** Without *unique identification*, an IoT system cannot distinguish *"the kitchen fridge"* from *"the living-room AC"* — the entire scalability of IoT depends on identification.

**Examples of sensors:** Temperature (LM35, DHT11), Humidity (DHT22), Gas (MQ-2), Motion (PIR HC-SR501), Proximity (ultrasonic HC-SR04), Image (OV2640 camera).
**Examples of identification:** RFID tags, QR codes, NFC tokens, BLE beacons.

### Block 2: Communication / Connectivity (The *Network* Layer)

**Operational Logic:**
1. The sensing block hands a data packet to a **communication module** (e.g., ESP32's Wi-Fi radio).
2. The packet is encapsulated according to a **protocol stack** (e.g., MQTT over TCP/IP over Wi-Fi).
3. The packet travels through **one or more network hops** (gateway → router → internet → cloud server).
4. Routing decisions are made by intermediary devices based on **IP addressing, MAC addressing, or application-layer topic names** (for pub/sub).

**Communication is classified along two axes:**
- **Range:** Personal Area (PAN) → Local Area (LAN) → Wide Area (WAN) → Satellite.
- **Topology:** Star, Mesh, Tree, Peer-to-Peer.

### Block 3: Processing / Computation (The *Edge/Cloud* Layer)

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Pre-processing** at the edge: filtering, averaging, thresholding (e.g., only send data if temperature > 30 °C).
2. **Aggregation**: combining multiple sensor streams into a single composite event.
3. **Decision logic**: if-then-else rules executed locally or in the cloud.
4. **Offloading**: heavy computation (e.g., image recognition) is pushed to the cloud; lightweight tasks (e.g., LED blink) remain on-edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept: "Edge vs Cloud"**
> - **Edge computing** = processing *near* the data source (low latency, privacy-preserving, bandwidth-saving).
> - **Cloud computing** = processing *far* from the data source (high compute power, central storage, scalable).
> - The logical design must explicitly state *which block runs where*.

### Block 4: Storage (The *Persistence* Layer)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Incoming data is written to a **time-series database** (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) or **NoSQL store** (MongoDB, Cassandra).
2. Data may also be stored on the **edge** in local flash memory or SD cards for offline buffering.
3. A **retention policy** governs how long data is kept (e.g., 30 days hot storage, 5 years cold/archive).
4. Storage choice is dictated by the **CAP theorem** trade-off (Consistency vs Availability vs Partition tolerance).

### Block 5: Analytics / Intelligence (The *Value-Extraction* Layer)

**Operational Logic:**
1. **Descriptive analytics** — "What happened?" (dashboards, summaries).
2. **Diagnostic analytics** — "Why did it happen?" (root-cause analysis).
3. **Predictive analytics** — "What will happen?" (regression, time-series forecasting).
4. **Prescriptive analytics** — "What should we do?" (reinforcement learning, optimization).

> [!NOTE]
> This is where IoT transforms from *"data collection"* into *"business value"*. A temperature sensor is useless without the analytics block that decides *what to do* with the reading.

### Block 6: User Interface / Application (The *Interaction* Layer)

**Operational Logic:**
1. The application pulls processed data from storage/analytics blocks via an **API**.
2. Data is rendered using **visualization libraries** (e.g., Chart.js, D3.js) or **dashboard platforms** (Grafana, Power BI).
3. The user issues **commands** (e.g., "turn off the AC") that propagate backwards through the system.
4. **Notification services** (SMS, email, push) keep users informed of critical events.

## 2.2 Communication Models (Inter-Block Interaction Patterns)

The logical design also specifies **how blocks talk to each other**. There are four canonical communication models:

| # | Model | Flow Direction | Typical Use Case | KTU-Mentioned Examples |
|---|-------|----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Request-Response** | Client → Server → Client | Fetching sensor history on demand | HTTP/REST, CoAP |
| 2 | **Publish-Subscribe** | Publisher → Broker → Subscribers | Many devices streaming data to many consumers | MQTT, AMQP |
| 3 | **Push-Pull** | Producers push to a queue; consumers pull | Distributed log ingestion | Kafka, Kinesis |
| 4 | **Exclusive Pair** | Bidirectional, persistent full-duplex channel | Real-time bidirectional control | WebSockets, Socket.IO |

## 2.3 Communication APIs (The "Language" of Blocks)

- **REST (Representational State Transfer):** HTTP-based, stateless, request-response. The most common API style in IoT.
- **WebSocket:** Persistent TCP connection enabling full-duplex messaging. Used for real-time dashboards.
- **SOAP / XML-RPC:** Older, heavier RPC-style APIs. Rare in modern IoT but still appears in industrial systems.
- **gRPC:** High-performance, schema-based, increasingly used in microservices IoT backends.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the only reference you need for solving logical-design numericals. Memorize the formulas, units, and boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Boundary / Typical Range |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Sampling Rate** $f_s$ | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion) | Hz | Sensor-dependent (1 Hz to 100 kHz) |
| 2 | **Data Rate (Throughput)** $R$ | $R = f_s \cdot N \cdot b$ | bps | Wi-Fi: up to ~600 Mbps; LoRa: 0.3 – 50 kbps |
| 3 | **Data Volume per Day** $V$ | $V = R \cdot t$ (where $t$ is in seconds) | bytes/day | 1 MB to several TB |
| 4 | **Storage Duration** $T_{store}$ | $T_{store} = \dfrac{C_{storage}}{V}$ | days | 7 to 3650 days (varies by use case) |
| 5 | **End-to-End Latency** $L$ | $L = L_{sense} + L_{comm} + L_{proc} + L_{app}$ | ms | Critical IoT: $\lt$ 10 ms |
| 6 | **Availability** $A$ | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | 0.999 ("three nines") is common |
| 7 | **Compression Ratio** $CR$ | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | dimensionless | Lossless: 1.5 – 3×; Lossy: 10 – 100× |
| 8 | **Packet Loss Probability** $P_{loss}$ | $P_{loss} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{received}}{P_{sent}}$ | dimensionless | Acceptable: $\lt$ 1% for control traffic |
| 9 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $BDP$ | $BDP = B \cdot L_{RTT}$ | bits | Determines optimal TCP window |
| 10 | **Energy per Bit** $E_b$ | $E_b = \dfrac{E_{total}}{N_{bits}}$ | Joules/bit | Critical for battery-powered nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In every formula above:**
> - $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency (Nyquist)
> - $N$ = number of channels/sensors, $b$ = bits per sample
> - $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
> - $B$ = bandwidth (bps), $L_{RTT}$ = round-trip latency (s)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Logical Design | Why It Matters |
|-----------------|--------------------------------|----------------|
| **Smart Agriculture** | Sensors (soil, weather) → LoRa → Edge gateway → Cloud DB → ML analytics → Farmer app | Optimizes irrigation, saves water |
| **Healthcare (Remote Patient Monitoring)** | Wearable sensors → BLE → Smartphone gateway → Cloud → Doctor dashboard | Enables chronic-disease management at home |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT / Industry 4.0)** | Machine vibration sensors → OPC-UA → Edge PLC → Historian DB → Predictive maintenance | Prevents costly unplanned downtime |
| **Smart Cities** | Traffic cameras, air-quality sensors → 5G → City data lake → Urban analytics → Citizen app | Improves traffic flow, reduces pollution |
| **Smart Home** | Motion, door, temperature sensors → ZigBee/Wi-Fi → Hub → Cloud → Alexa/Google app | Convenience + energy savings |

The logical design is the **first deliverable** in every one of these engineering projects — it is the blueprint that aligns all stakeholders (hardware, firmware, cloud, app teams) before any physical device is built.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Problem Scenario (Walked Through End-to-End)

> **Scenario:** A KTU lab project requires you to design the *logical architecture* of a **Smart Classroom Monitoring System**. The system must:
> - Sense **temperature**, **humidity**, **light intensity**, and **CO₂ concentration** every 30 seconds.
> - Transmit data to a **cloud server** over Wi-Fi using the **MQTT publish-subscribe model**.
> - Store the last **90 days** of readings in a time-series database.
> - Send a **push notification** to the class teacher's phone whenever CO₂ exceeds 1000 ppm.
> - Provide a **web dashboard** showing real-time and historical graphs.

We will now derive all logical-design parameters for this system, step by step, showing every algebraic transition.

---

## 3.2 Step 1: Determine Total Data Rate ($R$)

**Given:**
- 4 sensors (Temperature, Humidity, Light, CO₂)
- Sampling interval: $T_s = 30$ seconds
- Assume each sample is encoded as a 16-bit integer (2 bytes)
- Each MQTT packet adds ~50 bytes of overhead (topic, header, JSON keys)

**Derivation:**

$$
f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s} = \dfrac{1}{30 \text{ s}} = 0.0333 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Explanation:** The sampling frequency is the reciprocal of the sampling interval. We are sampling once every 30 seconds, so the frequency is 1/30 Hz.

$$
R_{raw} = N \cdot f_s \cdot b = 4 \times 0.0333 \text{ Hz} \times 16 \text{ bits/sample}
$$

**Explanation:** Total raw bit rate = (number of sensors) × (samples per second) × (bits per sample).

$$
R_{raw} = 4 \times 0.0333 \times 16 = 2.133 \text{ bps}
$$

**Explanation:** Numerical evaluation: 4 × 0.0333 = 0.1332; 0.1332 × 16 ≈ 2.13 bps.

$$
R_{packet} = R_{raw} + \dfrac{N \cdot H_{overhead}}{T_s} = 2.133 \text{ bps} + \dfrac{4 \times 50 \text{ B} \times 8 \text{ bits/B}}{30 \text{ s}}
$$

**Explanation:** We add the protocol overhead. Each of the 4 packets is sent every 30 s, each carrying ~50 bytes of header overhead. We multiply by 8 to convert bytes to bits.

$$
R_{packet} = 2.133 + \dfrac{1600}{30} = 2.133 + 53.33 = 55.47 \text{ bps}
$$

**Final result:** $R_{packet} \approx 55.5$ bps — an *extremely* low data rate. Any IoT connectivity (Wi-Fi, 4G, even NB-IoT) can handle this easily.

---

## 3.3 Step 2: Compute Daily Data Volume ($V_{day}$)

**Given:** $R_{packet} = 55.47$ bps, $t = 1$ day = 86,400 seconds.

**Derivation:**

$$
V_{day} = R_{packet} \times t = 55.47 \text{ bps} \times 86400 \text{ s}
$$

**Explanation:** Total daily volume = bit rate × time-in-seconds.

$$
V_{day} = 4,792,608 \text{ bits} = \dfrac{4,792,608}{8} = 599,076 \text{ bytes} \approx 585 \text{ kB/day}
$$

**Final result:** $V_{day} \approx 585$ kB/day. This is tiny — a 1 GB cloud storage plan would last over **5 years** at this rate.

---

## 3.4 Step 3: Compute Storage Capacity for 90 Days ($C_{required}$)

**Derivation:**

$$
C_{required} = V_{day} \times T_{store} = 585 \text{ kB/day} \times 90 \text{ days} = 52,650 \text{ kB} \approx 51.4 \text{ MB}
$$

**Explanation:** Total storage = daily volume × retention period.

**Final result:** $C_{required} \approx 51.4$ MB. This means even the cheapest cloud time-series tier or an edge SD card is sufficient.

---

## 3.5 Step 4: Compute End-to-End Latency Budget ($L_{total}$)

The latency budget is split across the four functional stages:

| Stage | Component | Typical Latency |
|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| Sensing | Sensor acquisition + ADC conversion | 50 ms |
| Communication | Wi-Fi TX + router hop + internet | 80 ms |
| Processing | Cloud ingestion + rule engine | 150 ms |
| Application | Push notification to phone | 100 ms |

**Derivation:**

$$
L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{comm} + L_{proc} + L_{app}
$$

**Explanation:** Total latency is the sum of all per-stage latencies because the packet traverses each stage sequentially.

$$
L_{total} = 50 \text{ ms} + 80 \text{ ms} + 150 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms} = 380 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final result:** $L_{total} = 380$ ms. For a smart-classroom monitor, this is well within acceptable limits (humans cannot perceive a sub-second delay in a notification).

---

## 3.6 Step 5: Availability Computation

Assume the cloud provider guarantees 99.9% uptime, and the local gateway is up 99.5% of the time. The system is *available only if both are up* (series reliability model).

**Derivation:**

$$
A_{system} = A_{gateway} \times A_{cloud}
$$

**Explanation:** In a series system, the total availability is the product of individual availabilities (since any single failure breaks the chain).

$$
A_{system} = 0.995 \times 0.999 = 0.994005 \approx 99.4\%
$$

**Final result:** $A_{system} \approx 99.4\%$. This means the system may be unavailable for roughly **52 minutes per day**, which is acceptable for a classroom monitor but would be **unacceptable for a medical ventilator monitor**.

---

## 3.7 Step 6: Identify the Communication Model and API

For this scenario, the logical design must select:

- **Communication Model:** **Publish-Subscribe** (because many devices stream data to many consumers: dashboard, notification engine, analytics engine, historian DB).
- **Communication API:** **MQTT over WebSockets** for the dashboard and **MQTT over TCP** for the cloud ingestor.
- **Data Format:** **JSON** for human-readable messages; **CBOR** (Concise Binary Object Representation) for bandwidth-constrained edge cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not Request-Response?** Request-Response would force the cloud to *poll* every device, generating many empty requests. Pub/Sub lets devices *push* data only when they have something to report — far more efficient.

## 3.8 Complete Logical-Design Analytical Matrix

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit | Engineering Implication |
|---|-----------|--------|----------------|------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Sampling rate | $f_s$ | 0.0333 | Hz | One reading every 30 s |
| 2 | Number of channels | $N$ | 4 | sensors | Temp, humidity, light, CO₂ |
| 3 | Bits per sample | $b$ | 16 | bits | Standard ADC resolution |
| 4 | Raw data rate | $R_{raw}$ | 2.13 | bps | Trivial |
| 5 | Packet overhead | $H$ | 50 | B/packet | MQTT + JSON headers |
| 6 | Total data rate | $R_{packet}$ | 55.5 | bps | Easily handled by any radio |
| 7 | Daily volume | $V_{day}$ | 585 | kB/day | Minimal bandwidth cost |
| 8 | 90-day storage | $C_{required}$ | 51.4 | MB | Inexpensive cloud tier |
| 9 | Total latency | $L_{total}$ | 380 | ms | Acceptable for non-critical IoT |
| 10 | System availability | $A_{system}$ | 99.4 | % | Sufficient for classroom use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a numerical problem appears, *show every algebraic step* with units. Marks are awarded for *each correct transition*, not just the final answer. Skipping the units is the most common reason students lose 1–2 marks per sub-part.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The IoT Functional-Block Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PHY1: Physical World] --> B[FB1: Sensing and Identification]
    B --> C[FB2: Communication and Connectivity]
    C --> D[FB3: Processing and Computation]
    D --> E[FB4: Storage]
    D --> F[FB5: Analytics and Intelligence]
    F --> G[FB6: User Interface and Application]
    G --> H[USR: End User]
    E --> F
    SC[Cross-Cutting: Security and Management] -.-> B
    SC -.-> C
    SC -.-> D
    SC -.-> E
    SC -.-> F
    SC -.-> G
    classDef fb fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef phy fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef usr fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef sec fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    class A phy
    class B,C,D,E,F,G fb
    class H usr
    class SC sec
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **Green node** = Physical World (the *input* to the IoT system).
- **Blue nodes** = the six functional blocks traversed in sequence.
- **Red node** = the End User (the *output* consumer of IoT value).
- **Purple node** = the cross-cutting Security/Management layer (dashed lines show it touches every block).

---

## 4.2 Communication Model Flowcharts (Four Canonical Models)

### Model 1 — Request-Response

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client App
    participant S as IoT Server
    C->>S: HTTP GET /sensor/temp
    S-->>C: 200 OK {"value": 28.4}
```

**Reading:** The client *requests* data, and the server *responds*. This is the classic REST pattern.

### Model 2 — Publish-Subscribe (the most common IoT model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Sensor Node A] --> B[MQTT Broker]
    P2[Sensor Node B] --> B
    P3[Sensor Node C] --> B
    B --> S1[Subscriber: Dashboard]
    B --> S2[Subscriber: Analytics Engine]
    B --> S3[Subscriber: Alert Service]
    B --> S4[Subscriber: Historian DB]
    classDef pub fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef br fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sub fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class P1,P2,P3 pub
    class B br
    class S1,S2,S3,S4 sub
```

**Reading:** Multiple *publishers* (sensor nodes) send messages to a central *broker*. The broker then *forwards* each message to all interested *subscribers*. This is the dominant IoT model because it is **decoupled**, **scalable**, and **bandwidth-efficient**.

### Model 3 — Push-Pull

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Producer A] --> Q[Message Queue]
    P2[Producer B] --> Q
    P3[Producer C] --> Q
    Q --> C1[Consumer 1]
    Q --> C2[Consumer 2]
    classDef prod fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef queue fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef cons fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class P1,P2,P3 prod
    class Q queue
    class C1,C2 cons
```

**Reading:** Producers *push* messages into a queue; consumers *pull* messages from the queue at their own pace. Used heavily in log-aggregation and stream-processing pipelines (Kafka, Kinesis).

### Model 4 — Exclusive Pair

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    Note over C,S: Persistent bidirectional channel (WebSocket)
    C->>S: WebSocket upgrade request
    S-->>C: 101 Switching Protocols
    C->>S: {"cmd": "turn_on_ac"}
    S-->>C: {"ack": "on", "temp": 24.0}
    S->>C: {"alert": "co2_high"}
    C-->>S: {"ack": "received"}
```

**Reading:** A *single*, *persistent*, *full-duplex* connection is established. Either side can send messages at any time without re-establishing the connection. Used for real-time bidirectional control (chat, gaming, live dashboards).

---

## 4.3 The Data Lifecycle Through All Six Functional Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Physical Phenomenon] --> S1[FB1: Sensor Converts to Signal]
    S1 --> S2[FB2: Communication Module Encodes]
    S2 --> S3[FB3: Processor Decodes and Filters]
    S3 --> S4a[FB4: Storage Writes to DB]
    S3 --> S4b[FB5: Analytics Runs Algorithm]
    S4a --> S4b
    S4b --> S5[FB6: Application Renders to User]
    S5 --> U[User Issues Command or Consumes Insight]
    U --> S2
    classDef phys fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef fb fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef user fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class P phys
    class S1,S2,S3,S4a,S4b,S5 fb
    class U user
```

**Reading:** This single diagram captures the *entire logical flow* of an IoT data packet — from the physical world, through all six functional blocks, to the user, and back. It is the single most important diagram to remember for the KTU exam.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a). Define the Logical Design of an IoT system and list its six primary functional blocks. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The *logical design* of an IoT system is a high-level, technology-agnostic abstraction that describes the functional blocks of the system and how they interact to collect, transmit, process, store, analyze, and present data from the physical world.

The **six primary functional blocks** are:

1. **Sensing / Identification** — acquires physical data and uniquely identifies each node.
2. **Communication / Connectivity** — transmits data across networks using wired or wireless protocols.
3. **Processing / Computation** — transforms raw data into information (edge or cloud).
4. **Storage** — persists data for later retrieval and historical analysis.
5. **Analytics / Intelligence** — extracts insights, predictions, and decisions from data.
6. **User Interface / Application** — presents data to users and accepts user commands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation cue:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for correctly listing the six blocks (½ mark each, or proportional credit).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Paper, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b). Differentiate between the *Request-Response* and *Publish-Subscribe* communication models. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Direction** | Client initiates, server replies | Many publishers send to a broker; many subscribers receive |
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled (client must know server) | Loosely coupled (publishers/subscribers need not know each other) |
| **Scalability** | Limited (server is bottleneck) | Highly scalable (broker distributes load) |
| **Typical Use** | Web API calls, on-demand queries | Sensor streaming, fan-out event distribution |
| **Examples** | HTTP/REST, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation cue:** A clean 3-row comparison is enough for full marks; students who write only one-line differences usually lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End-Semester Model Paper, Q7]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Logical Design of a Smart Irrigation System*

**(a)** Identify and explain the **six IoT functional blocks** for a smart irrigation system that monitors soil moisture, temperature, and weather forecasts, and automatically controls water valves. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Select an appropriate **communication model and communication API** for this system, and justify your selection by comparing it with one alternative model. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Block 1 — Sensing / Identification:**
Soil-moisture sensors (e.g., capacitive probes) are placed at multiple field locations. Each sensor is assigned a **unique 128-bit UUID** for identification. A weather station provides ambient temperature and rainfall data via an API.

**Block 2 — Communication / Connectivity:**
The sensors communicate with a **LoRaWAN gateway** because the field is large (several hectares) and Wi-Fi coverage is impractical. LoRaWAN offers long range (up to 10 km) and very low power consumption (battery life of 5–10 years).

**Block 3 — Processing / Computation:**
At the **edge** (gateway), data is filtered to remove outliers and pre-aggregated into 15-minute averages. At the **cloud**, a decision engine fuses soil-moisture data with weather forecasts to compute *how much* and *when* to irrigate.

**Block 4 — Storage:**
A **time-series database** (InfluxDB) stores 5 years of moisture readings, weather data, and irrigation events. A relational database stores the list of fields, sensors, and valve configurations.

**Block 5 — Analytics / Intelligence:**
A **predictive model** (linear regression or LSTM neural network) forecasts soil moisture for the next 24 hours based on weather predictions. A **rule engine** decides: *"If predicted moisture < 30% AND no rain expected in next 12 hours, then open valve for 15 minutes."*

**Block 6 — User Interface / Application:**
A **farmer's mobile app** displays real-time soil moisture, irrigation history, and weather forecast. The farmer can override automatic decisions manually. Critical alerts (e.g., pump failure) are sent as **SMS** and **push notifications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):** 1 mark per block for naming it, 0–1 additional mark for the example/justification. Total 7 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Selected Communication Model: Publish-Subscribe (MQTT)**

**Justification:**
1. **Many-to-many topology:** Hundreds of soil sensors publish readings; multiple consumers (analytics engine, mobile app, historian, alert service) need to receive them.
2. **Low bandwidth:** MQTT packet overhead is only 2 bytes, ideal for low-power LoRaWAN.
3. **Asynchronous delivery:** Sensors do not need to wait for a server response — they can publish and go back to sleep, conserving battery.
4. **Decoupling:** New consumers (e.g., a new dashboard) can be added without modifying the sensor firmware.

**Comparison with Alternative — Request-Response (HTTP/REST):**

| Criterion | Pub/Sub (MQTT) | Req/Resp (HTTP) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Power consumption | Very low (sleep between publishes) | High (must keep connection alive) |
| Scalability | Excellent (broker fan-out) | Poor (server becomes bottleneck) |
| Latency | ~10 ms local, ~100 ms cloud | ~200–500 ms typical |
| Suitability for battery sensors | **High** | **Low** |

**Communication API: MQTT 5.0 over TCP/TLS**, with **WebSockets fallback** for browser-based dashboards.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students often write *"MQTT is faster than HTTP"* without giving a *reason* — speed is meaningless without context (bandwidth, power, latency).
> - **Common mistake 2:** Students forget to mention that the *same* logical system can use *different* APIs at *different* stages (e.g., MQTT from sensor to broker, REST from broker to dashboard).
> - **Common mistake 3:** Listing six functional blocks without *examples* — KTU expects you to map the generic block to a *specific component* in *your* system. Always include an example.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Confusing *physical design* (e.g., "ESP32 microcontroller") with *logical design* (e.g., "Processing/Computation block"). Stay at the *logical* level.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Logical Design of a Wearable Health-Monitoring System*

**(a)** Design the **logical architecture** of a wearable health-monitoring system (heart rate, SpO₂, body temperature, accelerometer) that streams data to a hospital cloud. Identify the six functional blocks with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **daily data volume** and the **end-to-end latency** for this system, given:
- 4 sensors sampled at 1 Hz
- 16-bit resolution per sample
- 100-byte packet overhead
- Latency stages: 30 ms (sense), 60 ms (comm), 200 ms (cloud processing), 150 ms (app delivery)
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Block 1 — Sensing / Identification:** Optical heart-rate sensor (PPG), SpO₂ sensor, thermistor, 3-axis accelerometer. Each device is identified by a unique **BLE MAC address** and a **patient ID** stored in firmware.

**Block 2 — Communication / Connectivity:** **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** transmits data from the wearable to the patient's smartphone gateway. The smartphone uses **4G/5G** to forward data to the hospital cloud.

**Block 3 — Processing / Computation:** The smartphone performs **edge analytics** (e.g., detects arrhythmia in real time and raises a local alarm). The cloud performs **heavy analytics** (long-term trend analysis, ML-based risk scoring).

**Block 4 — Storage:** **HIPAA-compliant cloud storage** with encryption-at-rest. Time-series database for vitals, relational DB for patient records.

**Block 5 — Analytics / Intelligence:** A **deep-learning model** classifies ECG patterns into normal, atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, etc. **Anomaly detection** triggers an alert to the on-call doctor.

**Block 6 — User Interface / Application:** A **mobile app** for the patient shows real-time vitals and historical trends. A **web dashboard** for doctors shows all admitted patients ranked by risk score.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute raw data rate:**

$$
f_s = 1 \text{ Hz}, \quad N = 4, \quad b = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
R_{raw} = 4 \times 1 \times 16 = 64 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total data rate with overhead:**

$$
R_{packet} = R_{raw} + \dfrac{N \times H \times 8}{T_s} = 64 + \dfrac{4 \times 100 \times 8}{1}
$$

$$
R_{packet} = 64 + 3200 = 3264 \text{ bps}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute daily data volume:**

$$
V_{day} = R_{packet} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 3264 \times 86400 = 282,009,600 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
V_{day} = \dfrac{282,009,600}{8} = 35,251,200 \text{ bytes} \approx 33.6 \text{ MB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute end-to-end latency:**

$$
L_{total} = 30 + 60 + 200 + 150 = 440 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final results:**
- $V_{day} \approx 33.6$ MB/day per patient
- $L_{total} = 440$ ms — acceptable for non-critical vitals, but **insufficient** for life-critical events (e.g., cardiac arrest), which need < 100 ms end-to-end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (KTU pattern):**
> - (a) 1 mark per functional block + 1 mark for the connecting narrative = 7 marks.
> - (b) 2 marks for raw rate, 2 marks for packet rate, 2 marks for daily volume, 1 mark for latency. Always show units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-page rapid revision — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Logical Design ≠ Physical Design.** Logical = *what* each block does. Physical = *which chip/cable/protocol* implements it.
- [x] The **six functional blocks** are: Sensing, Communication, Processing, Storage, Analytics, User Interface. Memorize the order (data flow direction).
- [x] **Security & Management is cross-cutting**, not a separate block — it overlays all six layers.
- [x] **Four communication models:** Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Push-Pull, Exclusive Pair. Pub/Sub dominates IoT.
- [x] **Communication APIs:** REST (request-response), WebSocket (exclusive pair), MQTT/AMQP (pub/sub). Choose based on model.
- [x] **Nyquist criterion:** Sample at $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- [x] **Data rate formula:** $R = N \cdot f_s \cdot b$ (raw) plus packet overhead for the total.
- [x] **Daily volume:** $V_{day} = R \cdot 86400$ bits/day — convert to bytes by dividing by 8.
- [x] **Latency budget:** $L_{total} = L_{sense} + L_{comm} + L_{proc} + L_{app}$ — always sum in the order data flows.
- [x] **Availability in series:** $A_{system} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \dots \times A_n$ — multiplying probabilities, not averaging.
- [x] **Logical-design exam answers** must include (i) named functional blocks, (ii) specific examples, (iii) a justified communication model, (iv) at least one quantitative calculation.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Confusing *logical* (abstract) descriptions with *physical* (hardware) choices. Stay at the *logical* level unless explicitly asked.
- [x] **CAP Theorem:** In distributed IoT storage, you can guarantee at most two of Consistency, Availability, Partition-tolerance — pick the two that matter for your use case.
- [x] **Edge vs Cloud:** Edge = low latency + privacy + bandwidth saving. Cloud = high compute + central storage + global scalability. A good logical design uses *both*.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Logical Design of IoT — IoT Functional Blocks**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Communication Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** IoT Communication Models # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *IoT and Applications (PEMRT525)*, **IoT Communication Models** (also referred to as *IoT Design Models* or *Interaction Models*) refer to the abstract, standardized patterns that govern how two or more distributed entities—such as **Things/Devices**, **Gateways**, **Cloud Services**, and **Applications**—exchange data, commands, and events over a network. These models define the **logical roles**, **directionality of data flow**, **synchronization strategy**, and **coupling level** between communicating nodes, forming the foundational layer upon which IoT protocols (like **MQTT**, **CoAP**, **AMQP**, **HTTP**, and **DDS**) are built. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus explicitly categorizes IoT Communication into **four primary reference models**: > 1. **Request-Response Communication Model** > 2. **Publish-Subscribe Communication Model** > 3. **Push-Pull Communication Model** > 4. **Exclusive Pair Communication Model** > > Additionally, the **Client-Server Model** is taught as the classical predecessor, and the **Device-to-Device (D2D)** paradigm is discussed as the modern extension for edge/5G scenarios. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "How a Restaurant Operates" Imagine an IoT system as a **smart restaurant**: - **Request-Response** is like ordering food at a counter: **You (client)** place an order, and the **cashier (server)** responds with the bill and confirmation. There is a direct, synchronous conversation. - **Publish-Subscribe** is like a **notice board in a hostel**: A student (publisher) pins a notice about a lost notebook. Every student who has subscribed to "Lost & Found" notices (subscribers) checks the board and picks it up — *the publisher does not know who is reading*. - **Push-Pull** is like a **newspaper press**: The printing press (producer) **pushes** newspapers into a delivery truck (queue), and a vendor (consumer) **pulls** bundles whenever needed. - **Exclusive Pair** is like a **private telephone call** between two friends: the line is dedicated, stateful, and bi-directional until one of them hangs up. > [!NOTE] > **Why These Models Matter in IoT:** A smart agriculture sensor in a remote field (constrained in **power, bandwidth, and memory**) cannot behave like a desktop client. The **choice of communication model** directly determines **latency, energy consumption, scalability, and reliability** — the four pillars of IoT network design. ## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary) | Term | Definition | KTU Significance | |------|------------|------------------| | **Client** | The request-initiating entity | Always initiates a transaction in Request-Response | | **Server** | The service-providing entity | Listens on a well-known **port** (e.g., 1883 for MQTT) | | **Publisher** | Source of data/event in Pub-Sub | Decoupled from subscribers | | **Subscriber** | Receiver of data/event in Pub-Sub | Expresses *interest* in a topic | | **Broker** | Middleware in Pub-Sub | Central router (e.g., **Mosquitto, RabbitMQ**) | | **Producer** | Source that *pushes* into a queue | Common in message-queue systems | | **Consumer** | Receiver that *pulls* from a queue | Decouples processing rate from arrival rate | | **Thing/Device** | Any sensor/actuator/embedded node | The "T" in IoT | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Canonical IoT Communication Models ### 2.1.1 Request-Response Model This is a **stateless**, **synchronous** (typically), **client-initiated** pattern. The client sends a *request message* containing a method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE), headers, and optionally a body. The server processes it and returns a *response* with a status code and payload. **Operational Steps:** 1. Client establishes a **TCP connection** to the server's IP/port. 2. Client transmits a **request packet** (HTTP/MQTT request/CoAP confirmable). 3. Server **parses, authenticates, and processes** the request. 4. Server returns a **response packet** with status code (e.g., HTTP 200 OK, 404 Not Found). 5. Connection is either closed (HTTP/1.0) or kept alive (HTTP/1.1 keep-alive). **Protocols Used:** **HTTP/HTTPS, CoAP (Confirmable mode), MQTT (with QoS 1/2), REST APIs** **Use Case:** Fetching sensor history from a cloud dashboard, remote control of actuators. ### 2.1.2 Publish-Subscribe Model This is a **decoupled**, **asynchronous**, **event-driven** pattern built around a central message broker. Publishers tag messages with a *topic* (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`), and subscribers register *topic filters* (e.g., `home/+/temperature`). **Operational Steps:** 1. Subscriber sends a **SUBSCRIBE** packet with topic filter to the broker. 2. Publisher sends a **PUBLISH** packet to the broker with a topic name. 3. Broker performs **topic matching** (exact, wildcard, or pattern-based). 4. Broker **forwards** the message to all matching subscribers. 5. Optional: **Last Will and Testament (LWT)** notifies other clients if a publisher disconnects unexpectedly. **Protocols Used:** **MQTT (most common), AMQP, STOMP, DDS, Apache Kafka (for cloud-scale)** **Use Case:** Real-time telemetry from 10,000 sensors to a dashboard, smart city traffic updates. ### 2.1.3 Push-Pull Model This is a **queue-based**, **asynchronous**, **rate-decoupled** pattern. Producers push data into a queue, and multiple consumers compete to pull and process it. Unlike Pub-Sub, messages are **removed** from the queue after consumption (no fan-out). **Operational Steps:** 1. Producer **writes** a message into the queue (push). 2. Queue acts as a **buffer** (in-memory, on-disk, or persistent like **Kafka log**). 3. Consumer **polls** the queue at its own pace (pull). 4. Consumer **acknowledges** successful processing. 5. If unacknowledged, message is **re-delivered** (at-least-once semantics). **Protocols Used:** **AMQP (queue mode), Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, JMS** **Use Case:** Log aggregation, video frame processing pipelines, batch analytics on sensor data. ### 2.1.4 Exclusive Pair (Bidirectional) Model This is a **stateful**, **full-duplex**, **persistent** connection between exactly **two endpoints**. Once established, both sides can send messages independently without re-requesting. Often implemented over a single long-lived TCP or WebSocket connection. **Operational Steps:** 1. Client opens a persistent connection (handshake/authentication). 2. Server **accepts** and assigns a dedicated session/handle. 3. Both peers can **freely exchange** data frames at any time. 4. Connection is **terminated** explicitly by either side (or via timeout). 5. Session state (authentication tokens, context) is **preserved** throughout. **Protocols Used:** **WebSockets, Bluetooth RFCOMM, ZigBee paired connections, TCP socket, OPC UA** **Use Case:** Smartphone-to-smartwatch control, real-time gaming, industrial PLC-to-HMI communication. ## 2.2 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates the **quantitative metrics** and **selection criteria** examiners expect for 14-mark design questions. Memorize the bolded values. | # | Metric / Concept | Formula or Rule | KTU Significance | |---|------------------|------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Latency (L)** | $L = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{transmit}$ | Lower for Exclusive Pair & Pub-Sub | | 2 | **MQTT QoS Levels** | $\text{QoS} \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ | QoS 0 = at most once, 1 = at least once, 2 = exactly once | | 3 | **Pub-Sub Topic Match** | Wildcards: `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level) | Exam-favourite for 7-mark question | | 4 | **CoAP Message Types** | CON, NON, ACK, RST | CON uses confirmable (Request-Response style) | | 5 | **Coupling Index** | $\text{Coupling} = \frac{\text{Known Partners}}{\text{Total Components}}$ | Tight: Request-Response, Loose: Pub-Sub | | 6 | **Scalability Order** | $S_{order} = \text{Req-Res} < \text{Ex-Pair} < \text{Push-Pull} < \text{Pub-Sub}$ | Pub-Sub scales to **millions** of clients | | 7 | **MQTT Retain Flag** | $R \in \{0, 1\}$ | $R=1$ stores last message for new subscribers | | 8 | **Keep-Alive Timer** | $T_{keep} \in [0, 65535] \text{ sec}$ | PINGREQ/PINGRESP at 1.5× $T_{keep}$ | | 9 | **Port Numbers (Standard)** | MQTT: **1883**, MQTTS: **8883**, CoAP: **5683**, HTTPS: **443** | Mandatory for diagrams | | 10 | **Bandwidth Efficiency** | $E_b = \frac{\text{Useful Payload}}{\text{Total Frame Size}}$ | CoAP (4-byte header) > MQTT (2-byte) > HTTP (large) | ## 2.3 Comparative Analysis — "Which Model When?" | Feature | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe | Push-Pull | Exclusive Pair | |---------|:----------------:|:-----------------:|:---------:|:--------------:| | **Synchronization** | Synchronous | Asynchronous | Asynchronous | Full-Duplex | | **Coupling** | Tight | Loose | Loose | Tight (1-to-1) | | **Statefulness** | Stateless | Stateless (broker holds topics) | Queue-based | Stateful | | **Direction** | Bidirectional (req → res) | Unidirectional (pub → sub via broker) | Unidirectional (push → pull) | Bidirectional | | **Scalability** | Low–Medium | Very High | High | Low | | **Latency** | Medium | Low | Variable | Lowest | | **Power Suitability** | Poor for battery | Excellent | Good | Poor | | **Best Protocol** | HTTP, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP | Kafka, AMQP queue | WebSocket, TCP | | **Ideal IoT Use** | Device config | Sensor telemetry | Data pipelines | Real-time control | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** A modern **smart factory** uses *all four* models simultaneously — sensors **publish** to MQTT for monitoring, maintenance server **requests** firmware updates, vision systems **push** raw frames to GPU queues, and AGV robots maintain **exclusive pair** links with the fleet manager. This is called a **hybrid IoT architecture**. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step: Selecting the Right Communication Model (KTU Design Method) When a 14-mark KTU question asks *"Choose and justify a communication model for a given IoT scenario"*, follow this **5-step framework** (this is the valuation key pattern examiners reward): ### **Step 1: Identify the System Constraints** List the **three golden constraints** explicitly: - **Power budget** (battery, energy-harvested, or mains-powered) - **Bandwidth** (cellular NB-IoT, Wi-Fi, LoRa, BLE) - **Data velocity** (bytes/sec, events/sec, batch, or real-time) ### **Step 2: Identify the Communication Pattern** Ask: *"How many senders, how many receivers, and how often?"* - **1 client ↔ 1 server, occasional** → Request-Response - **N publishers → M subscribers, continuous events** → Publish-Subscribe - **Producer writes a lot, Consumer processes slowly** → Push-Pull - **Two peers must exchange constantly** → Exclusive Pair ### **Step 3: Map to a Protocol** Use this mapping table (high-yield for exams): $$ \text{Model} \;\xrightarrow{\text{map}}\; \text{Protocol} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Request-Response} &\longrightarrow \text{HTTP} \;\vert\; \text{CoAP (CON mode)} \\ \text{Publish-Subscribe} &\longrightarrow \text{MQTT} \;\vert\; \text{AMQP} \;\vert\; \text{DDS} \\ \text{Push-Pull} &\longrightarrow \text{Kafka} \;\vert\; \text{RabbitMQ} \;\vert\; \text{AMQP queue} \\ \text{Exclusive Pair} &\longrightarrow \text{WebSocket} \;\vert\; \text{Bluetooth} \;\vert\; \text{TCP socket} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 4: Justify the Choice Using Three Justification Pillars** A complete KTU answer must justify using: 1. **Scalability**: How many devices in 5 years? 2. **Energy efficiency**: What is the duty cycle? 3. **Reliability**: Is message loss acceptable? (Yes → QoS 0, No → QoS 1/2) ### **Step 5: Draw the Architecture Diagram** Always show: **Device → Gateway → Broker/Server → Application** with the chosen protocol annotated on every arrow. (See Section 4.) ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Solving "Which Model?" Problems For a KTU 14-mark scenario, parameterize the problem: $$ \text{Score}_{model} = w_1 \cdot \text{Scalability} + w_2 \cdot \text{Energy} + w_3 \cdot \text{Latency} + w_4 \cdot \text{Simplicity} $$ Where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ and each weight is derived from the scenario's priorities. **Worked Example (Self-Check):** > *"Design communication for 50,000 streetlight sensors reporting brightness every 5 seconds over cellular."* > > - Scalability: 50,000 → **Pub-Sub (MQTT)** scores 0.9 > - Energy: battery-friendly → Pub-Sub 0.8, Req-Res 0.4 > - Latency: 5-sec tolerance → all acceptable > - Simplicity: Pub-Sub requires broker setup → 0.7 > > **Verdict:** Publish-Subscribe (MQTT) over **TCP/TLS** is optimal. ## 3.3 Quantitative Comparison: Message Overhead > [!NOTE] > This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation question** — *"Compare the header overhead of HTTP, MQTT, and CoAP for a 10-byte temperature payload."* **Given:** Payload $P = 10$ bytes. **HTTP/1.1 (Request-Response):** - Typical request line + headers ≈ **~400–500 bytes** (uncompressed) - Overhead ratio: $$ O_{HTTP} = \frac{P}{P + H_{HTTP}} = \frac{10}{10 + 470} \approx 0.0208 \;(\approx 2.1\%) $$ **MQTT 3.1.1 (Publish-Subscribe):** - Fixed header: **2 bytes** - Variable header: topic name (e.g., `s/temp` = 7 bytes + 2 length bytes) + packet ID (2 bytes) = **~11 bytes** - Overhead ratio: $$ O_{MQTT} = \frac{10}{10 + 13} \approx 0.4348 \;(\approx 43.5\%) $$ **CoAP (Request-Response):** - Header: 4 bytes (Ver, Type, TKL, Code, MsgID) + options (~5 bytes for URI-path) = **~9 bytes** - Overhead ratio: $$ O_{CoAP} = \frac{10}{10 + 9} \approx 0.5263 \;(\approx 52.6\%) $$ **Conclusion:** $\boxed{\text{CoAP} > \text{MQTT} > \text{HTTP in efficiency for tiny IoT payloads}}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Efficiency Rank:} \quad & \text{CoAP} \;\;(52.6\%) \\ & > \text{MQTT} \;\;(43.5\%) \\ & > \text{HTTP} \;\;(2.1\%) \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Four Communication Models Side-by-Side ```mermaid graph LR subgraph MODEL_A[REQUEST RESPONSE MODEL] A1[Client Device
Sensor Node] -->|HTTP GET request| A2[Cloud Server
REST API] A2 -->|HTTP 200 OK JSON| A1 end subgraph MODEL_B[PUBLISH SUBSCRIBE MODEL] B1[Publisher
Temperature Sensor] -->|PUBLISH topic home temp| B2[MQTT Broker
Mosquitto] B2 -->|Forward to matching topic| B3[Subscriber 1
Mobile App] B2 -->|Forward to matching topic| B4[Subscriber 2
Analytics Engine] end subgraph MODEL_C[PUSH PULL MODEL] C1[Producer
Camera Stream] -->|PUSH frames| C2[Message Queue
Kafka Topic] C2 -->|PULL batch| C3[Consumer
AI Inference Worker] end subgraph MODEL_D[EXCLUSIVE PAIR MODEL] D1[Peer A
Smartphone] <-->|Persistent WebSocket
full duplex JSON| D2[Peer B
Smartwatch] end style MODEL_A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style MODEL_B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style MODEL_C fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style MODEL_D fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: End-to-End IoT Architecture Using Publish-Subscribe ```mermaid flowchart TB SENS1[Soil Moisture Sensor] -->|Publish topic farm 001 moisture| GW[Edge Gateway
Raspberry Pi] SENS2[Air Quality Sensor] -->|Publish topic farm 001 air| GW SENS3[Weather Station] -->|Publish topic farm 001 weather| GW GW -->|MQTT over TLS port 8883| BRK[MQTT Broker
Mosquitto or HiveMQ] BRK -->|Subscribe topic farm 001| DASH[Farmer Dashboard
Web App] BRK -->|Subscribe topic farm 001 plus| AI[AI Crop Advisor] BRK -->|Subscribe topic farm 001 moisture| IRR[Auto Irrigation
Actuator] DASH -->|HTTPS Request Response| USR[Farmer Mobile App] IRR -->|Actuator ON OFF| SENS1 style BRK fill:#ffe082,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style GW fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Model Selection Flowchart (Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start Design IoT App] --> Q1{How many receivers
per data source?} Q1 -->|Exactly one| Q2{Need real time
bidirectional state?} Q1 -->|Many dynamic subscribers| PUB[PUBLISH SUBSCRIBE
MQTT or DDS] Q1 -->|One consumer but rate mismatch| PUSH[PUSH PULL
Kafka or AMQP] Q2 -->|Yes persistent| EXCL[EXCLUSIVE PAIR
WebSocket or TCP] Q2 -->|No stateless| Q3{Over constrained
network like CoAP?} Q3 -->|Yes| COAP[REQUEST RESPONSE
CoAP CON mode] Q3 -->|No regular web| HTTP[REQUEST RESPONSE
HTTP REST] style START fill:#90caf9 style PUB fill:#fff59d style PUSH fill:#ce93d8 style EXCL fill:#a5d6a7 style COAP fill:#ffcc80 style HTTP fill:#ef9a9a ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Hybrid IoT System) > [!NOTE] > This diagram represents a **block-level functional architecture** (per KTU 2024 evaluation guidelines) for a **Smart Building** that uses **all four models** simultaneously. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PERCEPTION[PERCEPTION LAYER Things] TH1[HVAC Sensor] TH2[Motion PIR] TH3[Smart Meter] end subgraph NETWORK[NETWORK LAYER Gateways and Brokers] GW1[Edge Gateway 1] BRK[Central MQTT Broker] end subgraph PROCESSING[PROCESSING LAYER Cloud Edge] CLOUD[Cloud Analytics] QUEUE[Kafka Stream Queue] ML[ML Inference Engine] end subgraph APPLICATION[APPLICATION LAYER] APP1[Building Manager App] APP2[Occupant Mobile App] APP3[Maintenance Dashboard] end TH1 -->|Pub Sub MQTT topic bldg 1 hvac| BRK TH2 -->|Pub Sub MQTT topic bldg 1 motion| BRK TH3 -->|Push Pull every 15 min| QUEUE BRK --> CLOUD BRK --> ML QUEUE --> CLOUD CLOUD -->|WebSocket full duplex| APP1 APP1 -->|HTTP REST request response| BRK BRK --> APP2 CLOUD --> APP3 style PERCEPTION fill:#ffecb3 style NETWORK fill:#c8e6c9 style PROCESSING fill:#bbdefb style APPLICATION fill:#f8bbd0 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define the Publish-Subscribe communication model in IoT. List any two protocols that implement it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > In the **Publish-Subscribe (Pub-Sub) model**, the message sender (called the *publisher*) does not send messages directly to a specific receiver. Instead, it categorizes each message into a *topic* and sends it to a central **message broker**. Receivers (called *subscribers*) register their interest in one or more topics, and the broker delivers matching messages to them. This decouples publishers from subscribers in **time, space, and synchronization** (1 mark). > > Key features (1 mark): > - **Asynchronous** and **event-driven** > - Supports **one-to-many** and **many-to-many** communication > - Highly **scalable** for large IoT deployments > > Two protocols that implement Pub-Sub (1 mark): **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** and **AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)**. Other valid answers: **DDS, STOMP, Apache Kafka**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Push-Pull and Publish-Subscribe models with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Push-Pull Model | Publish-Subscribe Model | |--------|-----------------|--------------------------| | **Message Handling** | Messages stored in a **queue** and removed after one consumer reads it (1 mark) | Messages **broadcast** to all subscribers; not removed from topic (1 mark) | | **Consumer Pattern** | **Competing consumers** — work distribution | **Fan-out** — every subscriber gets a copy | | **Example** | Video frames pushed to Kafka, ML worker pulls and processes (0.5 mark) | Temperature sensor publishes, dashboard + mobile app both receive (0.5 mark) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** write a generic essay covering all four models superficially. Examiners award marks for **scenario-specific justification** (scenarios carry 4 of 14 marks). > - **Always draw the architecture diagram** — it is worth 3–4 marks. A textual answer without a diagram loses 30% of the score. > - **State protocol names with port numbers** (e.g., MQTT 1883, CoAP 5683). Writing just "MQTT" without context loses 1 mark. > - **Forgetting LWT, QoS, or topic wildcards** in Pub-Sub answers is a recurring 2-mark deduction. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Request-Response** and **Publish-Subscribe** communication models in IoT. Compare them based on **synchronization, coupling, scalability, and typical protocols**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A **precision agriculture firm** in Kerala is deploying **5,000 soil-moisture sensors** across 200 hectares. Each sensor is **battery-powered**, reports **once every 30 minutes**, and the data is consumed by **three different applications**: a farmer mobile app, an AI irrigation engine, and a weather analytics cloud. **Recommend the most suitable communication model** with full justification and a labelled diagram. #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Request-Response Model (3.5 marks):** - A **client** sends a request to a **server**, which processes it and returns a response. - The interaction is **stateless** and typically **synchronous**. - Best example: **HTTP REST API** — sensor `GET /api/v1/sensor/123` returns JSON. - Tightly coupled: client must know server's address and must wait. - Fails gracefully: timeout, 4xx/5xx errors. **Publish-Subscribe Model (3.5 marks):** - A **publisher** tags each message with a **topic** and sends it to a **broker**. - A **subscriber** registers a **topic filter** (e.g., `farm/+/moisture`) and receives only matching messages. - Asynchronous, **loosely coupled**, supports **1-to-many** fan-out. - Best example: **MQTT** — sensor publishes to `farm/kochi/sensor007/moisture`, three apps subscribe. - Supports **QoS 0/1/2**, **retain flag**, and **Last Will and Testament**. **Comparison Table (included in answer):** | Criterion | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe | |-----------|------------------|-------------------| | Synchronization | Synchronous | Asynchronous | | Coupling | Tight | Loose (via broker) | | Scalability | Limited (server bottleneck) | Very high (broker scales horizontally) | | Typical Protocols | HTTP, CoAP | MQTT, AMQP, DDS | #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Identify Constraints (1.5 marks):** - **5,000 sensors** → need horizontal scalability - **Battery-powered** → must minimize radio-on time - **30-min interval** → low data rate (~20 bytes per message) - **3 heterogeneous consumers** → need fan-out delivery **Step 2 — Model Choice (1.5 marks):** $$ \boxed{\text{Publish-Subscribe (MQTT) is the recommended model}} $$ **Justification (2.5 marks):** 1. **Scalability:** A single Mosquitto broker handles 10,000+ clients; clustered brokers (e.g., HiveMQ, EMQX) scale to **millions**. 2. **Energy efficiency:** MQTT's 2-byte fixed header is **~20× smaller** than HTTP; sensor's radio is "on" only during PUBLISH, saving battery. 3. **Fan-out delivery:** One PUBLISH from sensor reaches all 3 consumers without duplication. 4. **QoS 1** ensures at-least-once delivery, critical for irrigation control. 5. **Topic design:** `farm/kerala///moisture` allows apps to subscribe hierarchically. **Diagram (1.5 marks):** See Mermaid Diagram 4.2 above — the architecture for this exact scenario. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **Push-Pull** and **Exclusive Pair** communication models. State two real-time use cases for each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A **hospital ICU** needs real-time vital-sign monitoring of **200 patients** with bidirectional nurse-station communication. The system must guarantee **< 500 ms latency** and **zero message loss**. Design the communication architecture. Which model is best and why? #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Push-Pull Model (3.5 marks):** - A **producer** *pushes* messages into a **queue** (e.g., Kafka topic, AMQP queue). - A **consumer** *pulls* messages at its own processing pace. - Decouples **production rate** from **consumption rate**. - Each message is delivered to **exactly one consumer** (competing consumers pattern). - Protocols: **Apache Kafka, RabbitMQ, AMQP**. - Use cases: (i) **Video surveillance** frames pushed to queue, AI worker pulls and analyzes; (ii) **Log aggregation** from thousands of servers to a central SIEM system. **Exclusive Pair Model (3.5 marks):** - A **stateful, full-duplex, persistent** connection between **two specific endpoints**. - Once established, both peers can send messages **freely** without re-handshaking. - Implemented via **WebSockets, Bluetooth RFCOMM, OPC UA binary**. - Use cases: (i) **Smartphone-to-smartwatch** real-time control; (ii) **Industrial PLC-to-HMI** continuous telemetry. #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Constraints Analysis (1.5 marks):** - 200 patients × multi-parametric data (ECG, SpO₂, BP) → high data velocity - < 500 ms latency → real-time constraint - Zero loss → reliability critical (life-critical) - Bidirectional → nurses must acknowledge and respond **Step 2 — Model Selection (2 marks):** - **Primary model: Exclusive Pair (WebSocket)** - **Secondary/Backbone model: Publish-Subscribe (MQTT with QoS 2)** for fan-out to nurse stations **Justification (2 marks):** - **Exclusive Pair** ensures full-duplex nurse ↔ bedside monitor session with **persistent state** (patient ID, alarm thresholds). - **MQTT QoS 2** guarantees **exactly-once** delivery, eliminating data duplication in the patient record. - WebSocket fallback to MQTT broker allows **horizontal scaling** as the hospital adds wards. **Diagram (1.5 marks):** Architecture with bedside monitor ↔ Gateway ↔ MQTT Broker ↔ Nurse Station (WebSocket + MQTT). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024:** - ✅ The **four canonical models** are: **Request-Response, Publish-Subscribe, Push-Pull, Exclusive Pair**. - ✅ **Request-Response = HTTP / CoAP CON** mode. Stateless, synchronous, 1-to-1. - ✅ **Publish-Subscribe = MQTT / AMQP / DDS**. Decoupled, asynchronous, 1-to-many, needs a **broker**. - ✅ **Push-Pull = Kafka / RabbitMQ / AMQP queues**. Rate-decoupling, work distribution, message consumed **once**. - ✅ **Exclusive Pair = WebSocket / TCP / Bluetooth**. Stateful, full-duplex, persistent, **bi-directional**. - ✅ **MQTT port**: **1883** (plain), **8883** (TLS). **CoAP port**: **5683**. - ✅ **MQTT QoS levels**: 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once). - ✅ **CoAP header**: only **4 bytes** → most efficient for tiny payloads. - ✅ **Topic wildcards**: `+` = single level, `#` = multi-level (e.g., `home/+/temp` or `home/#`). - ✅ **LWT (Last Will and Testament)**: broker auto-publishes a "death notice" if a client disappears ungracefully. - ✅ **Retain flag**: stores the last published message for any new subscriber that joins later. - ✅ **Pub-Sub scales best** for IoT; Request-Response is worst for thousands of devices. - ✅ **Hybrid IoT systems** use **all four models** simultaneously — this is the industry reality, not just theory. - ✅ **Coupling** is the **single most distinguishing feature** between the four models — always mention it in exams. - ✅ **For 14-mark scenario questions**, follow the **5-step design framework**: Constraints → Pattern → Protocol → Justify → Diagram. > **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — IoT Communication Models**

topics.title: IoT Enabling Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT Enabling Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **IoT Enabling Technologies** are the foundational hardware, software, communication, and computational pillars that collectively make the Internet of Things (IoT) functional, scalable, and intelligent. They encompass **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)**, **Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)**, **Near Field Communication (NFC)**, **short and long-range wireless protocols** (Bluetooth Low Energy, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN, Cellular IoT), **Cloud and Edge Computing platforms**, **Big Data Analytics**, **Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning (AI/ML)**, and **Embedded Systems**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is categorized under the **"Fundamentals Layer"** of the IoT Reference Architecture, sitting directly between the *Perception Layer* (sensors/actuators) and the *Network Layer* (gateways/routing).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart City Hospital 🏥

Imagine a **smart city hospital** as a living analogy for IoT enabling technologies:

| Real Hospital Object | IoT Technology Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Patient wristband with barcode | **RFID / NFC** | Identification & tracking |
| Wireless BP/Heart rate monitors | **WSN + BLE** | Sensing & short-range data |
| Hospital Wi-Fi routers | **Wi-Fi / ZigBee Gateways** | Local network bridging |
| City-wide ambulance GPS | **Cellular IoT / LoRaWAN** | Long-range communication |
| Central records database | **Cloud Computing** | Storage & processing |
| AI diagnostic engine | **AI / ML Analytics** | Intelligent decision-making |
| Local nurse-station display | **Edge / Fog Computing** | Real-time local processing |
| Sensor chips inside monitors | **MEMS + Embedded Systems** | Sensing & actuation |

> [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** No single technology enables IoT — it is the **convergence** of these layers that creates a working IoT ecosystem. Questions often ask you to **"map a given application to its enabling technology stack."**

## 1.3 The "Technology Stack" Mental Model

IoT Enabling Technologies are best understood as a **five-layer cake** 🎂:

1. **Sensing & Identification Layer** → RFID, NFC, MEMS Sensors, QR Codes
2. **Communication Layer** → BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G
3. **Computation Layer** → Embedded MCUs (ARM, ESP32, Arduino), SoCs
4. **Processing & Storage Layer** → Cloud (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub), Edge/Fog
5. **Intelligence Layer** → Big Data, AI/ML, Digital Twins

## 1.4 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU

> [!TIP] > **Examiner's Tip:** Module 1 carries **15-20% weightage** in the End Semester Exam. Questions on enabling technologies frequently appear as:
> - "Compare and contrast ZigBee and BLE" (7 marks)
> - "Explain the role of WSN in IoT" (7 or 14 marks)
> - "Discuss Cloud vs Edge Computing" (14 marks)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Technology Breakdown

### A. Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)

A **WSN** is a self-configuring network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions.

**Core Components of a Sensor Node (the "Mote"):**
- **Sensing Unit** → Physical transducer (temperature, humidity, accelerometer)
- **Processing Unit** → Microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, ESP32)
- **Transceiver Unit** → Radio (sub-GHz, 2.4 GHz)
- **Power Unit** → Battery / Energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric)
- **Optional Mobilizer / Location-finding Unit**

**Architecture Topologies in WSN:**
| Topology | Use Case | Robustness |
|---|---|---|
| **Star** | Central hub, e.g., home automation | Low |
| **Mesh** | Industrial monitoring | High |
| **Hybrid (Cluster Tree)** | Smart agriculture | Very High |
| **P2P (Peer-to-Peer)** | Ad-hoc deployments | Medium |

> [!NOTE] > **Why WSN is foundational for IoT:** Every IoT deployment at scale (smart farms, smart factories) fundamentally relies on *thousands* of WSN nodes feeding data upward.

### B. Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)

RFID uses **electromagnetic fields** to automatically identify and track tags attached to objects.

**Two Main Components:**
1. **RFID Tag** (transponder) → Contains an antenna and microchip
2. **RFID Reader** (interrogator) → Emits radio waves, receives tag data

**Three Frequency Bands (Critical for KTU):**
| Band | Frequency | Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LF (Low Frequency)** | 125–134 kHz | < 10 cm | Animal ID, Access control |
| **HF (High Frequency)** | **13.56 MHz** | 10 cm – 1 m | Smart cards, NFC, Library books |
| **UHF (Ultra-High Frequency)** | 860–960 MHz | 1–12 m | Supply chain, Toll collection |
| **Microwave** | 2.45 GHz / 5.8 GHz | < 1 m (active) | Container tracking |

**Tag Types:**
- **Passive Tags** → No battery, powered by reader's EM field. Range: short, cost: low.
- **Active Tags** → Battery-powered, broadcast signal. Range: long, cost: high.
- **Semi-Passive (Semi-Active)** → Battery for chip, reader for communication.

### C. Near Field Communication (NFC)

A short-range (≤ **10 cm**) wireless technology operating at **13.56 MHz**, derived from RFID. It enables **two-way communication** between devices, making it ideal for:
- Contactless payments (Google Pay, Apple Pay)
- Smart posters
- Pairing Bluetooth devices
- Identity documents (passports)

### D. Short-Range Wireless Protocols

**Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE / Bluetooth Smart):**
- Operates in **2.4 GHz ISM band**
- 40 channels (3 advertising + 37 data)
- Range: ~**100 m** (outdoor)
- Data rate: **1–2 Mbps**
- Power: ultra-low (~0.01–0.5 W during transmission)
- Use cases: Wearables, health monitors, beacons

**ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4):**
- Operates at **2.4 GHz** (globally) + 868 MHz (EU) + 915 MHz (US)
- Range: **10–100 m**
- Data rate: **250 kbps** (at 2.4 GHz)
- Supports **mesh networking** (up to **65,000 nodes**)
- Use cases: Smart lighting (Philips Hue), industrial control

**Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax):**
- 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands (Wi-Fi 6E adds 6 GHz)
- Range: **~50 m indoors, 200 m outdoors**
- Data rate: up to **9.6 Gbps** (Wi-Fi 6)
- Higher power consumption → not ideal for battery IoT nodes

### E. Long-Range Wide Area Network (LPWAN) Technologies

These are the **backbone of massive IoT** (smart cities, agriculture).

**LoRaWAN (Long Range Wide Area Network):**
- Physical layer: **LoRa** (Chirp Spread Spectrum by Semtech)
- Operates in sub-GHz ISM bands (868 MHz EU, 915 MHz US, 433 MHz Asia)
- Range: **2–15 km** (rural), **1–5 km** (urban)
- Data rate: **0.3–50 kbps** (low, by design)
- Battery life: **up to 10 years**
- Use cases: Smart agriculture, asset tracking, water metering

**NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT) and LTE-M (Long Term Evolution for Machines):**
- Operates in **licensed cellular spectrum** (LTE bands)
