<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Smart Object. How is it different from a generic "Thing" in IoT?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Smart Object** is a physical entity augmented with **sensing/actuation capability, embedded processing, a unique identifier, and network connectivity**, enabling it to interact intelligently within an IoT ecosystem. *(1 mark)*

The key distinction from a generic "Thing" lies in the **degree of embedded intelligence and autonomy**: *(2 marks)*
- A generic "Thing" may be merely a **passive RFID-tagged object** with no local processing or bidirectional communication.
- A Smart Object possesses **local decision-making (MCU/SoC), storage, and active communication**, allowing it to sense, interpret, store, and act upon data — even when disconnected from the cloud.
- In short: **Every Smart Object is a Thing, but not every Thing is a Smart Object.**

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between an Active Sensor and a Passive Sensor with one example each.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Criterion** | **Active Sensor** | **Passive Sensor** |
|---|---|---|
| External Power Required? | **Yes** — needs excitation signal | **No** — self-generating |
| Energy Conversion | External $\to$ Output | Input stimulus $\to$ Output |
| Example | **Ultrasonic sensor** (needs 5V excitation to emit pulses) | **Thermocouple** (generates mV from Seebeck effect) |
| *(1 mark for the distinction table)* | | |
| **KTU Note:** Active sensors often have **better accuracy and SNR** but consume more power; passive sensors are **energy-efficient** but produce weaker signals. *(2 marks for examples + engineering insight)* | | |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Before Attempting:**
> 1. **Never skip a labeled block diagram** in smart-object questions. A question worth 14 marks typically allocates **3–4 marks purely for a neat, labeled diagram**. Omitting it is the single largest mark-loss trigger.
> 2. **Always cite real component names** (e.g., "ESP32," "DHT22," "LM35") rather than vague phrases like "a sensor." Examiners reward specificity.
> 3. **Units must accompany every numerical answer.** Writing "0.049" without "°C" costs a full mark.
> 4. **Do not confuse sensor accuracy with precision** — these are commonly interchanged by students and penalized.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**A(a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the static and dynamic characteristics of sensors with relevant formulas. Why is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** critical in IoT sensor selection?

**Model Answer:**

**Part 1 — Static Characteristics (4 Marks):**
Static characteristics describe sensor performance when the input is **constant or slowly varying** (steady-state). The key metrics are:
- **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true value. Formula: $\%\text{Acc} = \dfrac{\vert x_{\text{measured}} - x_{\text{true}} \vert}{x_{\text{true}}} \times 100$
- **Precision:** Repeatability of measurements under identical conditions — independent of accuracy.
- **Sensitivity ($S$):** The ratio of output change to input change, $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$.
- **Resolution ($R$):** The smallest input increment detectable, $R = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^{n}}$.
- **Linearity:** The degree to which the output is a straight-line function of input; quantified by **% non-linearity**.
- **Hysteresis:** The difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.
- **Drift:** Slow change in output over time with constant input (aging effect).
- **Range / FSR:** Span from minimum to maximum measurable input.

**Part 2 — Dynamic Characteristics (2 Marks):**
Dynamic characteristics describe behavior when the input changes rapidly with time. Key metrics:
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** Time to reach $63.2\%$ of the final value, $y(t) = Y_{\infty}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to transition from 10% to 90% of final value, $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to settle within $\pm 2\%$ of final value, $t_s \approx 4\tau$.
- **Bandwidth:** Frequency range over which the sensor provides reliable output (typically up to $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$).
- **Dynamic Range:** Ratio of largest to smallest detectable signal, $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\max}/V_{\min})$ in dB.

**Part 3 — Importance of SNR in IoT (1 Mark):**
SNR, given by $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{signal}}/V_{\text{noise}})$, is critical because IoT sensors often operate in **noisy industrial, outdoor, or biomedical environments**. A low SNR means the digitized data will contain **corruption, false triggers, and excessive retransmissions**, draining the battery. For battery-powered IoT nodes, a **high SNR ($\geq 60$ dB)** sensor is mandatory to ensure years of maintenance-free operation.

---

**A(b) [7 Marks]** — A 16-bit ADC is connected to a voltage-output pressure sensor with a 0–10 V range and a sensitivity of $0.02 \text{ V/kPa}$. Compute (i) FSR, (ii) ADC resolution in volts, (iii) ADC resolution in kPa, and (iv) the system accuracy if the sensor's non-linearity is $\pm 0.3$ kPa.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Full-Scale Range (FSR):** *(1 Mark)*
The input range is 0 to 10 V, but converted to pressure using sensitivity:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSR}_{\text{pressure}} &= \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{S} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{0.02 \text{ V/kPa}} = 500 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) ADC Resolution in Volts:** *(2 Marks)*
$$\begin{aligned}
R_V &= \frac{V_{\text{ref}}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{16}} = \frac{10}{65536} = 1.526 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.1526 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) ADC Resolution in kPa:** *(2 Marks)*
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{kPa}} &= \frac{R_V}{S} = \frac{1.526 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}}{0.02 \text{ V/kPa}} = 7.629 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kPa} = 0.00763 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) System Accuracy:** *(2 Marks)*
Total Error = Non-linearity error + Quantization error:
$$\begin{aligned}
\epsilon_{\text{TEE}} &= 0.3 \text{ kPa} + 0.00763 \text{ kPa} = 0.30763 \text{ kPa} \\
\%\text{Accuracy} &= \left(1 - \frac{0.30763}{500}\right) \times 100 = 99.938\%
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**B(a) [7 Marks]** — With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of a Smart Object**. List the four primary functions (S-S-S-T model) and explain how each contributes to IoT operation.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for the standard.)*

A smart object's internal architecture comprises the following interconnected blocks:
- **Power Source / PMU** (battery, solar, energy harvesting) feeding a power management IC.
- **Sensor Subsystem** (e.g., temperature, humidity, motion sensors).
- **Processing Subsystem** (MCU + memory + security engine).
- **Communication Subsystem** (radio transceiver, antenna, protocol stack).
- **Actuator Subsystem** (motor driver, relay, display).

**S-S-S-T Model (4 Marks):**
- **Sense (S):** The sensor subsystem continuously samples physical phenomena and converts them to digital values via the ADC. This is the "perception" function. Example: A BME280 sensor reporting temperature, humidity, and pressure to an ESP32.
- **Store (S):** The processing subsystem retains data temporarily in RAM and persistently in flash memory. This enables **local buffering** when the network is unavailable (offline-tolerant operation).
- **Send (S):** The communication subsystem transmits stored/sensed data to a gateway or cloud using protocols like **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, or LoRaWAN**. This is the "networking" function.
- **Sometimes Act (T):** When equipped with actuators (motors, relays, valves), the smart object can **close the control loop** locally — e.g., a smart thermostat turning ON a relay based on sensed temperature exceeding a threshold.

**Engineering Significance:** The S-S-S-T model ensures the smart object is **self-aware, locally autonomous, and globally connected** — fulfilling the core IoT promise of seamless cyber-physical integration.

---

**B(b) [7 Marks]** — Compare and contrast **electrical, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators**. Provide one industrial application for each and justify why the choice of actuator energy domain matters in IoT deployments.

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **Electrical** | **Hydraulic** | **Pneumatic** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Electric current | Pressurized oil (typically 100–300 bar) | Compressed air (typically 4–8 bar) |
| Force Output | Low–Medium | **Very High** | Medium |
| Speed | High precision control | Slower, but powerful | **Fastest** |
| Precision | **Highest** (servo motors, stepper) | Moderate | Lower (compressibility of air) |
| Maintenance | Lowest | High (leakage, oil) | Moderate |
| IoT Compatibility | **Excellent** (easy to control via PWM, GPIO) | Difficult (needs solenoid valves + sensors) | Moderate (needs pressure regulators) |
| Industrial Application | **CNC machine tool feed** | **Excavator arm / hydraulic press** | **Pick-and-place robotic gripper in FMCG packaging** |
| *(3 Marks for the table + 1 mark for applications)* | | | |

**Why Actuator Energy Domain Matters in IoT (3 Marks):**
- **Power Budget:** IoT nodes are often battery- or solar-powered. Electrical actuators (especially **brushless DC and stepper motors**) are IoT-friendly. Hydraulic systems need a **dedicated pump and reservoir**, unsuitable for distributed nodes.
- **Control Interface:** Electrical actuators interface directly with MCU GPIO/PWM pins. Hydraulic/pneumatic systems need **intermediate electro-mechanical transducers** (solenoid valves), adding latency, cost, and failure points.
- **Safety & Environment:** In **explosive atmospheres (oil rigs, grain silos)**, pneumatic actuators are preferred over electrical to avoid ignition risks. In **underwater IoT**, hydraulic systems dominate.
- **Conclusion:** For most IoT edge devices, **electrical actuators dominate**. Hydraulic/pneumatic systems remain in **heavy industrial IoT** (Industry 4.0 shop floors) where force density outweighs energy efficiency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ The **"Things"** in IoT form the **Perception Layer** (Layer 1) of the 5-layer architecture.
- ✅ A **Sensor** converts a physical stimulus into an electrical signal (transduction).
- ✅ An **Actuator** converts an electrical signal into physical action (motion, heat, light).
- ✅ A **Smart Object** = Sensor + Storage + Processing + Communication + (optional) Actuator + Unique ID.
- ✅ **Passive sensors** need no external power (e.g., thermocouple); **active sensors** do (e.g., ultrasonic).
- ✅ **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$; **Resolution** $R = \text{FSR} / 2^n$ where $n$ = ADC bits.
- ✅ **Time constant** $\tau$ governs dynamic response; $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$, $t_s \approx 4\tau$.
- ✅ **SNR** in dB $= 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{signal}}/V_{\text{noise}})$; higher SNR = better IoT reliability.
- ✅ **S-S-S-T Model:** Sense, Store, Send, Sometimes Act — the four functional pillars of a smart object.
- ✅ **Differentiate Thing vs. Smart Object:** Things can be passive RFID tags; Smart Objects have local intelligence and bidirectional communication.
- ✅ **Actuator energy domains:** Electrical (IoT-friendly), Hydraulic (heavy industry), Pneumatic (fast, safe in explosive zones).
- ✅ **Static vs. Dynamic characteristics:** Static = steady-state metrics; Dynamic = time-response metrics.
- ✅ **Always** accompany numerical answers with **units**; **always** draw **labeled block diagrams** for smart-object questions.
- ✅ **IEEE 1451** is the standard for smart transducer interfacing; **IP ratings** define environmental durability.
- ✅ **Sensor selection flow:** Application requirement $\rightarrow$ Required resolution/accuracy $\rightarrow$ Sensor type $\rightarrow$ ADC bits $\rightarrow$ Power budget $\rightarrow$ Communication protocol.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Networks - Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Networks - Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a distributed, self-organizing network of spatially deployed autonomous sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions such as temperature, sound, vibration, pressure, motion, or pollutants. Each node is equipped with a **radio transceiver**, a **microcontroller**, a **power source (battery)**, and one or more **sensing elements**. The nodes communicate wirelessly over short to medium distances and forward collected data — typically via multi-hop routing — to a central **sink node** (also called a **base station** or **gateway**), which connects the WSN to the wider Internet or to a remote data-processing centre.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, WSNs are positioned as the **sensing and actuation layer** of the broader IoT stack — they generate the raw contextual data that IoT applications depend upon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT525, Module 2):**
> A WSN is *not* a traditional wireless network. The defining trait is **resource constraint**: limited energy, limited computation, limited bandwidth, and a dense, often unattended deployment. Every architectural decision (MAC, routing, security) flows from this single constraint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Ant Colony"

Imagine a **colony of fire-detecting ants** scattered across a forest. Each ant:

- Can sense heat (**sensor**)
- Can carry a tiny brain that decides whether to alert others (**microcontroller**)
- Can whisper to nearby ants using its antennae (**radio transceiver**)
- Runs on a single sugar crystal for its whole life (**battery**)
- Cannot run to the forest ranger directly, so it passes the message through many other ants until the message reaches the ranger (**multi-hop routing**)
- Sacrifices itself once its sugar runs out (**energy depletion = node death**)

That is, in essence, what a Wireless Sensor Network does. The *colony* is the network, the *ranger's cabin* is the sink, and the *forest* is the monitored environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A WSN trades off individual node capability for **collective intelligence** and **fault tolerance**. No single ant matters — the *colony* as a whole reports the fire.

## 1.3 Fundamental Distinguishing Characteristics (Board-Exam Favourites)

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Self-organization** | Nodes autonomously form a network without central administration |
| 2 | **Multi-hop communication** | Data travels through intermediate nodes to the sink |
| 3 | **Energy-constrained operation** | Battery life dictates network lifetime |
| 4 | **Dense deployment** | Hundreds to thousands of nodes over a region |
| 5 | **Data-centric networking** | Queries target *data of interest*, not specific node IDs |
| 6 | **Application-specific design** | Hardware/software optimized for a single purpose |
| 7 | **Unattended operation** | Difficult or impossible to recharge or maintain nodes |
| 8 | **Heterogeneity** | Mix of sensor types, energy levels, and capabilities |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in WSNs (Memorize These)

- **Network Lifetime**: Time until the first/last node dies — typically measured in **days, months, or years**.
- **Energy per Bit**: Energy required to transmit or receive **one bit** — measured in **Joules/bit (J/bit)** or **nanojoules/bit (nJ/bit)**.
- **Duty Cycle**: Fraction of time a node's radio is active — usually **< 1%** in energy-aware designs.
- **Node Density**: Number of nodes per unit area — expressed as **nodes/m²**.
- **Latency**: End-to-end delay from sensing event to sink reception — typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)**: Ratio of packets successfully received to packets transmitted.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners often ask: *“Why is energy the primary design constraint in WSNs?”*
> **Model Answer:** Because sensor nodes are deployed in remote, hostile, or inaccessible environments where battery replacement is infeasible. The **network lifetime is bounded by the energy budget**, making energy-aware protocols (MAC, routing, data aggregation) the central research theme.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Architecture of a Sensor Node — The Five Subsystems

Every sensor node is composed of **five tightly coupled subsystems**. KTU regularly frames questions around this block diagram.

| Subsystem | Hardware Examples | Power Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Subsystem** | MEMS accelerometer, thermistor, CMOS camera, gas sensor | Low (µA range) |
| **Processing Subsystem** | Microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, MSP430), DSP, memory (Flash + RAM) | Moderate |
| **Communication Subsystem** | Radio transceiver (e.g., CC2420, nRF24L01) | **Highest** (10–100 mA during TX) |
| **Power Subsystem** | Battery (Li-ion, alkaline), energy harvesting (solar, vibration, RF) | N/A — *source* |
| **Actuation Subsystem** *(optional)* | Stepper motor, relay, LED, buzzer | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Rule of Thumb:** Communication typically dominates energy consumption — **transmitting 1 bit over 100 m can consume as much energy as executing ~1000 CPU instructions**. Hence, *in-network processing* and *data aggregation* are central energy-saving strategies.

## 2.2 Network Topologies in WSNs

WSNs support three primary topologies, each with distinct energy and reliability trade-offs.

| Topology | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Star** | All nodes communicate directly with the sink | Simple, low latency | Sink bottleneck, poor scalability |
| **Mesh (Peer-to-Peer)** | Nodes forward data for each other | Robust, self-healing | Higher energy, more complex routing |
| **Hybrid (Cluster Tree)** | Nodes form clusters with cluster heads that aggregate data and forward to sink | Balanced energy, scalable | Cluster-head election overhead |

The **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** protocol — a KTU favourite — uses the hybrid/cluster topology with **rotated cluster-head roles** to evenly distribute the energy burden.

## 2.3 Communication Stack in WSNs

Unlike the traditional TCP/IP stack, WSN protocols are tailored for energy efficiency.

| Layer | WSN Protocol Family | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | Task assignment, query processing | TinyDB, Cougar |
| **Transport** | Reliable event delivery | PSFQ, RMST |
| **Network (Routing)** | Energy-aware multi-hop | LEACH, PEGASIS, SPIN, Directed Diffusion |
| **Data Link (MAC)** | Duty cycling, collision avoidance | S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4 |
| **Physical** | Low-power radios | IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee PHY), BLE, LoRa |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and represent standard models from energy-aware WSN literature. **KTU 2024 Scheme favours numerical problems on energy consumption.** Master the energy model below.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Model)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \dfrac{\lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$ | $P$ in Watts, $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 2 | **Two-Ray Ground Reflection** | $P_r \approx P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \dfrac{h_t^2 \cdot h_r^2}{d^4}$ | Used for $d >$ crossover distance |
| 3 | **First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman)** | $\begin{aligned} E_{TX}(k,d) &= E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \\ E_{RX}(k) &= E_{elec} \cdot k \end{aligned}$ | $k$ = bits, $d$ = distance, $n$ = path-loss exponent (2 or 4) |
| 4 | **Network Lifetime (First Node Dies)** | $L = \dfrac{E_{initial}}{E_{round}}$ | $E_{initial}$ = initial energy per node, $E_{round}$ = energy per round |
| 5 | **Cluster Head Optimal Probability (LEACH)** | $p_{opt} = \dfrac{k_{opt}}{N}$ | $N$ = total nodes, $k_{opt}$ = optimal cluster count |
| 6 | **Duty Cycle** | $DC = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}$ | Expressed as a percentage |
| 7 | **Energy per Bit per Distance** | $E_{b} = \dfrac{E_{TX}}{k \cdot d}$ | Used in routing-metric optimization |
| 8 | **Path-Loss Exponent Environments** | $n = 2$ (free space), $n = 3$ (urban), $n = 4$ (shadowed urban) | Empirical constants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When a numerical problem says *"transmit 2000 bits over 50 m in free space"*, you must use $n = 2$ in the energy model. When it says *"in a shadowed urban environment"*, use $n = 4$. **The exponent changes the answer dramatically.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

WSNs are the **sensory nervous system** of modern infrastructure. Concrete applications include:

- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture and pH sensors guiding irrigation.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Vibration sensors on bridges and skyscrapers.
- **Healthcare:** Wearable ECG/EEG sensor networks for elderly care.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Forest-fire detection, glacier-melt tracking, wildlife habitat study.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance using vibration and temperature sensors on rotating machinery.
- **Smart Grids:** Real-time monitoring of transmission lines and substations.
- **Battlefield Surveillance:** Military WSNs for intrusion detection and threat tracking.

> [!TIP]
> The same WSN stack concepts (energy model, clustering, duty cycling) directly map to the **Edge AI** and **TinyML** domains that the 2024 KTU syllabus touches upon in later modules. The energy-aware mindset you build here carries forward.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Radio Energy Model (Heinzelman Model)

The **Heinzelman energy model** is the single most important equation in KTU numerical questions on WSNs. We derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Transmit Energy Derivation

The energy consumed by the transmitter to send a $k$-bit packet over a distance $d$ has **two additive components**:

**Step 1 — Identify the two energy sinks:**
- The **electronic circuitry** of the radio (modulation, filtering, encoding) — independent of distance, proportional to bits transmitted.
- The **amplifier** stage — which must boost the signal to overcome path loss, proportional to both bits and a power of distance.

**Step 2 — Quantify the electronic cost:**
If $E_{elec}$ denotes the energy consumed by the transmitter electronics per bit, then transmitting $k$ bits consumes $E_{elec} \cdot k$ joules in the circuitry alone.

**Step 3 — Quantify the amplifier cost:**
If $\varepsilon_{amp}$ is the amplifier energy per bit per square metre (free space) and $n$ is the path-loss exponent, the amplifier cost is $\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$.

**Step 4 — Combine:**
Adding both contributions yields the standard transmit-energy equation.

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

where
- $E_{elec}$ = energy dissipated per bit by the transmitter/receiver electronics **(J/bit)**,
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ = energy dissipated by the transmit amplifier **(J/bit/m² for n=2, J/bit/m⁴ for n=4)**,
- $k$ = number of bits in the packet,
- $d$ = distance between transmitter and receiver **(m)**,
- $n$ = path-loss exponent, equal to **2 for free space** and **4 for multipath fading**.

### 3.1.2 Receive Energy Derivation

**Step 1 — Receiver electronics consume energy** to process the incoming signal, but **no amplifier** is needed.

**Step 2 — Therefore, the receive energy is purely electronic** and proportional to the number of bits received.

$$
E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

This is the *only* term; the receiver does not spend energy proportional to distance.

### 3.1.3 Total Energy in a Multi-Hop Path

For an $h$-hop path (each hop of length $d$):

$$
E_{path} = h \cdot \left( E_{TX}(k,d) + E_{RX}(k) \right) = h \cdot \left( 2 \cdot E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} \right)
$$

This result exposes a **fundamental trade-off**: shorter hops (larger $h$) reduce amplifier cost but multiply electronics cost.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Exam Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** *A sensor node transmits 2000 bits to its cluster head over 50 m. Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² (free space, $n = 2$). Compute $E_{TX}$ and $E_{RX}$.*

### Step 1 — Identify knowns
- $k = 2000$ bits
- $d = 50$ m
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $\varepsilon_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m² $= 100 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m²
- $n = 2$ (free space)

### Step 2 — Compute transmit amplifier term

$$
\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} = (100 \times 10^{-12}) \times 2000 \times (50)^{2}
$$

Let us evaluate $(50)^{2} = 2500$ first.

$$
\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{2} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 2000 \times 2500
$$

$$
= 100 \times 10^{-12} \times 5{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
= 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.5 \text{ mJ}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute transmitter electronics term

$$
E_{elec} \cdot k = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 2000 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.1 \text{ mJ}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $E_{TX}$

$$
E_{TX} = 0.1 \text{ mJ} + 0.5 \text{ mJ} = 0.6 \text{ mJ}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute $E_{RX}$

$$
E_{RX} = E_{elec} \cdot k = 0.1 \text{ mJ}
$$

### Step 6 — Total cost per packet

$$
E_{total} = E_{TX} + E_{RX} = 0.6 + 0.1 = 0.7 \text{ mJ}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The amplifier cost ($0.5$ mJ) dominates the electronics cost ($0.1$ mJ) by a factor of **5×**. This is why *shortening the transmission distance* through clustering and multi-hop routing is so effective at saving energy.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison

This table is a **favourite KTU short-note question**. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Protocol | Type | Energy Strategy | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LEACH** | Clustering | Randomized CH rotation | Balanced energy dissipation | Random CH selection can be sub-optimal |
| **PEGASIS** | Chain-based | Greedy chain formation | Minimum transmission distance | Excessive delay due to chain traversal |
| **SPIN** | Data-centric | Meta-data negotiation | Eliminates redundant data | Not guaranteed delivery |
| **Directed Diffusion** | Data-centric | Interest-based gradient setup | Energy-aware paths | Setup overhead |
| **S-MAC** | MAC | Synchronized sleep/wake | Low duty cycle | Sleep latency |

## 3.4 Threshold Distance Derivation (LEACH Variant)

A common derivation question: *Find the threshold distance $d_0$ at which the free-space and multipath models consume equal energy.*

**Step 1 — Equate the amplifier costs at $d_0$:**

$$
\varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{0}^{2} = \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{0}^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d_0$:**

$$
d_{0}^{2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}
$$

$$
d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** If $d \le d_0$, use the free-space model ($n = 2$); if $d > d_0$, use the multipath model ($n = 4$).

For typical values $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² and $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴:

$$
d_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.3} \approx 87.7 \text{ m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Workbook Practice:** KTU numerical questions on the energy model often supply a threshold distance and ask you to switch between $n = 2$ and $n = 4$. Always confirm *which model applies* before computing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sensor Node

This diagram maps the **five internal subsystems** of a sensor node and shows how they interact. It substitutes for a physical cross-section drawing and is fully KTU-board-examinable.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PWR["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM"]

        c2["ANTENNA"]

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> m1
    m1 --> m2
    m1 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    m1 --> a1
    batt1 --> m1
    batt1 --> s1
    batt1 --> c1
    harv1 --> batt1

```
