<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a *sensor node* and a *sensor network*. List the five subsystems of a sensor node.

**Model Answer:**
A **sensor node** is a single physical device that senses, processes, and communicates data. A **sensor network** is a coordinated collection of such nodes working together to monitor a region and report to a central sink. The five subsystems of a sensor node are:

1. **Sensing Subsystem** — physical transducers and ADC.
2. **Processing Subsystem** — microcontroller and memory.
3. **Communication Subsystem** — radio transceiver and antenna.
4. **Power Subsystem** — battery and energy harvester.
5. **Actuation Subsystem** *(optional)* — motors, relays, LEDs.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** Why is *energy efficiency* the primary design constraint in a WSN? Name two energy-aware MAC protocols.

**Model Answer:**
Sensor nodes are often deployed in remote, hostile, or inaccessible environments where battery replacement is infeasible. Since the **network lifetime is bounded by node battery life**, all design decisions (MAC scheduling, routing paths, transmission power, data aggregation) are subordinated to energy conservation. Communication dominates the energy budget — transmitting 1 bit can cost ~1000× more energy than processing it — so the radio must be duty-cycled and transmissions minimized.

Two energy-aware MAC protocols:

1. **S-MAC (Sensor-MAC)** — synchronizes sleep/wake cycles among neighbours.
2. **B-MAC (Berkeley-MAC)** — uses clear-channel assessment and low-power listening.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

### OPTION A

**Q.A.** *(a)* Explain the **architecture of a Wireless Sensor Network** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **sink node** and **multi-hop communication** in WSNs. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **LEACH (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy)** protocol. Explain how cluster-head rotation contributes to **balanced energy dissipation** across the network. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The WSN architecture consists of:

- **Field sensor nodes** — deployed densely across the region to be monitored.
- **Cluster heads (CHs)** — aggregating nodes that consolidate intra-cluster data.
- **Sink / Base station (BS)** — the gateway connecting the WSN to external networks.
- **Communication links** — wireless, short-range radio (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4).

**Role of the sink:** The sink is the **data collection endpoint**. It may also act as a **gateway** to the Internet, an **edge processor** for analytics, or a **control point** that issues queries back into the network. In tree-based topologies, the sink is the root.

**Role of multi-hop communication:** Since direct long-range transmission from every node to the sink is energy-prohibitive (recall $d^2$ or $d^4$ scaling), nodes **relay** their data through intermediate neighbours. This trades **latency** and **complexity** for **drastic energy savings**, while also extending the coverage area far beyond the radio range of a single node.

**Block diagram:** (Refer to the WSN Network Architecture figure in SECTION 4.2.)

**Marks distribution (typical):**
- 2 marks — correct identification of all four components.
- 2 marks — clear explanation of sink role.
- 2 marks — clear explanation of multi-hop.
- 1 mark — neat labelled diagram.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**LEACH** is a **clustering-based, self-organizing, adaptive protocol** proposed by Heinzelman, Chandrakasan, and Balakrishnan.

**Key ideas:**

1. **Cluster formation phase** — Each node independently decides to become a **cluster head (CH)** for the current *round* by choosing a random number $r$ between 0 and 1 and comparing it to a threshold $T(n)$.

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{p}{1 - p \cdot \left( r \bmod \dfrac{1}{p} \right)} & \text{if } n \in G \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $p$ is the desired fraction of CHs and $G$ is the set of nodes that have not been CHs in the last $1/p$ rounds.

2. **Steady-state phase** — Non-CH nodes join the nearest CH (based on RSSI). Each CH aggregates received data, applies **signal-processing functions** (e.g., averaging, suppression), and forwards the compact result to the sink.

**Cluster-head rotation → balanced energy dissipation:**

Because radio transmission is energy-dominant, the CH role would otherwise drain certain nodes prematurely. LEACH solves this by:

- **Randomized rotation** of the CH role every round.
- Ensuring that every node serves as CH **approximately $p \times$ (total rounds)** times during its lifetime.
- Spreading the high-energy burden (CH-to-sink long-distance transmission) **evenly** across all nodes.

The result is a **1/p-fold increase** in effective network lifetime compared to static clustering.

**Marks distribution (typical):**
- 2 marks — protocol phases explained.
- 2 marks — threshold equation stated correctly.
- 2 marks — rotation justification.
- 1 mark — lifetime improvement quantified.

---

### OPTION B

**Q.B.** *(a)* With the help of the **first-order radio energy model**, derive the expressions for **transmit energy** and **receive energy** of a sensor node. Define all symbols. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A sensor node transmits **4000 bits** to a cluster head located **80 m away** in a **multipath environment** ($n = 4$). Given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\varepsilon_{amp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, compute the total energy consumed per packet. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The first-order radio energy model decomposes energy consumption into **transmitter electronics**, **amplifier**, and **receiver electronics** contributions.

**Transmit Energy:**

The transmitter electronics consume $E_{elec} \cdot k$ joules to encode and modulate $k$ bits. The transmit amplifier must boost the signal to overcome path loss proportional to $d^{n}$. Therefore:

$$
E_{TX}(k, d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}
$$

**Receive Energy:**

The receiver dissipates only the electronics cost, since there is no amplifier stage:

$$
E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k
$$

**Symbol definitions:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Number of bits | bits |
| $d$ | Distance between transmitter and receiver | m |
| $n$ | Path-loss exponent | dimensionless (2 or 4) |
| $E_{elec}$ | Energy per bit by electronics | J/bit |
| $\varepsilon_{amp}$ | Amplifier energy per bit per $d^{n}$ | J/bit/m$^n$ |

**Marks distribution:**
- 2 marks — correct $E_{TX}$ equation with derivation.
- 2 marks — correct $E_{RX}$ equation with derivation.
- 2 marks — symbol table.
- 1 mark — clear linkage to WSN design.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $k = 4000$ bits
- $d = 80$ m
- $n = 4$ (multipath)
- $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit $= 50 \times 10^{-9}$ J/bit
- $\varepsilon_{amp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴ $= 0.0013 \times 10^{-12}$ J/bit/m⁴

**Step 1 — Compute the electronics term.**

$$
E_{elec} \cdot k = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 4000 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.2 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the amplifier term.**

First, $d^{n} = 80^{4}$.

$$
80^{2} = 6400
$$

$$
80^{4} = 6400^{2} = 40{,}960{,}000 = 4.096 \times 10^{7}
$$

Now,

$$
\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n} = 0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 4000 \times 4.096 \times 10^{7}
$$

Intermediate calculation:

$$
4000 \times 4.096 \times 10^{7} = 1.6384 \times 10^{11}
$$

Multiplying by $\varepsilon_{amp}$:

$$
0.0013 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.6384 \times 10^{11} = 0.0013 \times 1.6384 \times 10^{-1}
$$

$$
= 2.130 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 0.213 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_{TX}$.**

$$
E_{TX} = 0.2 + 0.213 = 0.413 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_{RX}$.**

$$
E_{RX} = E_{elec} \cdot k = 0.2 \text{ mJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Total energy per packet.**

$$
E_{total} = E_{TX} + E_{RX} = 0.413 + 0.2 = 0.613 \text{ mJ}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer:** $E_{total} \approx \mathbf{0.613 \text{ mJ}}$ per packet.

**Marks distribution:**
- 1 mark — correct unit conversions.
- 2 marks — correct $d^{n}$ evaluation.
- 2 marks — correct $E_{TX}$ and $E_{RX}$ values.
- 1 mark — final sum.
- 1 mark — units in the answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Forgetting to convert nJ → J or pJ → J is the #1 source of mark loss. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong path-loss exponent** — Using $n = 2$ in a multipath problem (or vice versa) makes the answer wrong by a factor of $(d_{0}/d)^{2}$ — typically ~1000×.
> 3. **Forgetting $E_{RX}$** — Many students compute only $E_{TX}$ and miss the receive cost, losing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the symbol table** in part (a) — Examiners explicitly award marks for "Define all symbols." Do not omit it.
> 5. **Missing the threshold distance check** — In some problems you must first decide between $n = 2$ and $n = 4$ using $d_0$. Jumping straight to a formula loses a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A WSN is a self-organizing, multi-hop, energy-constrained network of cooperating sensor nodes reporting to a sink.
- **Five Subsystems** of a node: *Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power, Actuation.*
- **Primary Design Constraint:** *Energy.* All protocols are subordinated to it.
- **Energy Model (Heinzelman):**
  - $E_{TX}(k,d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$
  - $E_{RX}(k) = E_{elec} \cdot k$
  - $n = 2$ (free space) or $n = 4$ (multipath)
- **Path-loss Exponent:** Choose $n = 2$ if $d \le d_0$; choose $n = 4$ if $d > d_0$.
- **Threshold Distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs} / \varepsilon_{mp}}$ (typically ~87.7 m).
- **Topologies:** *Star, Mesh, Cluster Tree (Hybrid).*
- **Clustering Protocols:** *LEACH* (rotated CH), *PEGASIS* (chain).
- **Data-Centric Protocols:** *SPIN, Directed Diffusion.*
- **MAC Protocols:** *S-MAC, B-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4.*
- **Radio Energy Dominance:** Transmit 1 bit ~ 1000× cost of one CPU instruction.
- **Duty Cycle:** Fraction of time the radio is active; key lever for energy savings.
- **Network Lifetime Metric:** Time until the *first node dies* (FND) or *last node dies* (LND).
- **Cluster Head Probability (LEACH):** $p_{opt} = k_{opt} / N$, where $k_{opt}$ is the optimal cluster count.
- **Key Applications:** Agriculture, healthcare, structural monitoring, military, smart grid, industrial IoT.
- **Communication Stack:** Application → Transport → Network → Data Link → Physical.
- **WSN's Place in IoT:** WSN is the *sensing and actuation layer*; IoT cloud and analytics sit above.
- **Cross-Layer Insight:** Energy-aware design spans *every* layer — physical (low-power radios), MAC (sleep scheduling), network (clustering/routing), application (data aggregation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks - Connecting Smart Objects - Communication Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Communication Protocols for Wireless Sensor Networks - Connecting Smart Objects - Communication Criteria # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is a spatially distributed autonomous network of low-power, low-cost sensor nodes that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions (such as temperature, sound, pressure, humidity, motion, or pollutants) and transmit their collected data through wireless links to a central sink or Base Station (BS) for further processing and analysis. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, a WSN is treated as the foundational data-acquisition layer of any large-scale IoT deployment. The sensor node typically consists of four sub-systems: | Sub-system | Function | KTU Exam Keyword | |---|---|---| | **Sensing Unit** | Acquires analog/digital data from the environment | ADC + Sensor | | **Processing Unit** | Microcontroller/MCU for local computation | MCU + Memory | | **Transceiver Unit** | Wireless radio for communication | RF Transceiver | | **Power Unit** | Battery / Energy harvesting module | Energy Budget | ## 1.2 Smart Objects — The KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Smart Object (KTU PEMRT525 terminology):** > A *Smart Object* is a physical or virtual entity that possesses **three mandatory capabilities** — **sensing**, **processing**, and **communicating** — augmented with **identification** (e.g., RFID, IPv6 address) and **actuation** capabilities, enabling it to interact intelligently with its environment and other smart objects. ### 1.2.1 The 5A Capability Stack of a Smart Object $$ \text{Smart Object} = \{\text{Identifier},\ \text{Sense},\ \text{Process},\ \text{Actuate},\ \text{Communicate}\} $$ > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Lightbulb" Example:** > Think of a smart object as a **smart lightbulb** in your room. It *senses* darkness (via LDR), *processes* whether to switch ON (via a tiny MCU), *communicates* the status to your phone (via Wi-Fi/BLE), and *actuates* by turning ON the LED. It also has a unique *identifier* (its MAC/IP address) so the cloud knows which bulb it is. Now scale this idea to thousands of bulbs forming a WSN — that's IoT. ## 1.3 Communication Criteria — The Seven Pillars For any smart object to "join" a WSN, the communication subsystem must satisfy **seven fundamental criteria** prescribed by the KTU syllabus. These criteria act as the **design constraints** that drive protocol selection. | # | Criterion | Unit / Metric | Typical Range for WSN | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Range** | meters (m) | 1 m – 100 m | | 2 | **Power Consumption** | milliwatts (mW) | < 100 mW | | 3 | **Data Rate / Throughput** | bits per second (bps) | 20 kbps – 250 kbps | | 4 | **Latency** | milliseconds (ms) | < 1 s (often < 100 ms) | | 5 | **Security** | bit-strength (AES-128) | 128-bit minimum | | 6 | **Mobility Support** | m/s | 0 – 30 m/s | | 7 | **Network Topology** | Star / Mesh / Tree | Multi-hop supported | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Trick — "R-P-D-L-S-M-T"** > Remember the seven criteria as: **R**ange, **P**ower, **D**ata rate, **L**atency, **S**ecurity, **M**obility, **T**opology. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The WSN Protocol Stack — Layered View A WSN protocol stack closely mirrors the **TCP/IP model** but is *slimmed down* to suit resource-constrained nodes. The KTU syllabus typically asks for a 5-layer interpretation: ### 2.1.1 Layer-by-Layer Function (High-Yield) 1. **Application Layer** - Houses user-defined software (e.g., temperature monitoring). - Protocols: **CoAP, MQTT, HTTP, DDS**. 2. **Transport Layer** - Ensures reliable / unreliable delivery. - Protocols: **UDP (preferred)**, TCP (rare in WSN). 3. **Network Layer** - Performs addressing and routing. - Protocols: **RPL (IPv6 Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)**. 4. **Adaptation / Link Layer** - Bridges IPv6 (large) ↔ IEEE 802.15.4 (small frames). - Protocols: **6LoWPAN**, **IEEE 802.15.4**. 5. **Physical Layer** - Modulation, encoding, RF transmission. - Frequencies: **2.4 GHz ISM band** (global, license-free). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Gold Line:** > The unique protocol in the WSN stack is **6LoWPAN** — it performs **header compression + fragmentation** so that an IPv6 packet (which is huge) can be squeezed into an IEEE 802.15.4 frame (max **127 bytes**). ## 2.2 Detailed Communication Criteria — "The Why" Behind Each ### 2.2.1 Range ($R$) The **maximum line-of-sight (LoS)** distance over which two nodes can maintain a bit error rate (BER) below $10^{-3}$. $$ R = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \cdot P_{r,\min}}} $$ Where: - $P_t$ = transmitted power - $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains - $\lambda$ = wavelength - $P_{r,\min}$ = receiver sensitivity threshold ### 2.2.2 Power Consumption ($P_{total}$) For duty-cycled sensor nodes, the **average power** is: $$ P_{total} = \alpha \cdot P_{active} + (1-\alpha) \cdot P_{sleep} $$ Where $\alpha$ is the **duty cycle** ($\alpha = T_{on}/T_{total}$). > [!TIP] > **Why this matters:** A node sleeping 99% of the time and transmitting only 1% can extend battery life from days to **years** (Energy Harvesting domain). ### 2.2.3 Data Rate ($R_b$) Shannon-Hartley limit (theoretical maximum) is: $$ C = B \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bps]} $$ For practical WSN radios: $R_b$ ranges from **20 kbps (802.15.4 at 868 MHz)** to **250 kbps (2.4 GHz)**. ### 2.2.4 Latency ($\tau$) End-to-end delay across **N hops**: $$ \tau = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( t_{proc,i} + t_{queue,i} + t_{tx,i} + t_{prop,i} \right) $$ ### 2.2.5 Security The de-facto cipher is **AES-128** (Advanced Encryption Standard, 128-bit key). Energy cost per encryption is approximately: - **AES-128 in hardware:** ~ **1.62 µJ / 128-bit block** (TinySec benchmark). ### 2.2.6 Mobility Support Measured by the maximum node speed $v_{max}$ before the link breaks. For 802.15.4 at pedestrian speed (1.4 m/s), Doppler shift is negligible. For vehicular WSN (60 km/h ≈ 16.7 m/s), protocol must support **fast handoff**. ### 2.2.7 Network Topology | Topology | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---| | **Star** | Simple, low-latency | Single point of failure, short range | | **Mesh** | Self-healing, scalable | Higher power, routing complexity | | **Tree (Cluster)** | Energy-efficient (LEACH) | Cluster-head bottleneck | | **Hybrid** | Balanced trade-off | Complex management | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Free-space range | $R = \sqrt{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 / (16\pi^2 P_{r,\min})}$ | m | Max LoS distance | | Duty-cycle power | $P_{avg} = \alpha P_{on} + (1-\alpha) P_{off}$ | mW | Average energy use | | Shannon capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Max theoretical throughput | | Hop delay | $\tau_{hop} = t_{prop} + t_{tx} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$ | s | Per-hop latency | | Frame budget | $L_{802.15.4} = 127$ bytes | B | Max MAC payload | | IPv6 header | $L_{IPv6} = 40$ bytes | B | Compressed by 6LoWPAN | | Energy per bit | $E_b = P_{tx} / R_b$ | J/bit | Radio efficiency | | Battery life | $T_{life} = C_{bat} / I_{avg}$ | hours | Node lifetime | | Path loss (FSPL) | $PL_{dB} = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | dB | Free-space path loss | | Link budget | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB}$ | dBm | Received power | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** This formula sheet is **sufficient** to solve every Part B (14-mark) numerical/analytical question the KTU board has asked on WSN communication criteria in the last five years. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy-Per-Bit Trade-off (Classic KTU Numericals) > **Problem:** A WSN node operates at $P_{tx} = 50$ mW with a data rate of 250 kbps. Calculate the **energy consumed per bit** transmitted. ### Step 1 — Identify the given parameters We are given: - Transmitted power: $P_{tx} = 50$ mW $= 50 \times 10^{-3}$ W - Data rate: $R_b = 250$ kbps $= 250 \times 10^{3}$ bits/second ### Step 2 — Recall the energy-per-bit formula Energy per bit is defined as the power required to transmit a single bit of information: $$ E_b = \dfrac{P_{tx}}{R_b} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute the values $$ E_b = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W}}{250 \times 10^{3}\ \text{bits/s}} $$ ### Step 4 — Perform the division $$ E_b = \dfrac{50}{250} \times 10^{-3-3} $$ $$ E_b = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{J/bit} $$ ### Step 5 — Final result $$ E_b = 2 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{J/bit} = 0.2\ \mu\text{J/bit} $$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** At this energy cost, transmitting a 1-MB image from a node would consume $0.2\ \mu\text{J} \times 8.4 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bits} \approx 1.68\ \text{J}$ — equivalent to powering an LED bulb for 16 seconds. **This is why WSNs avoid bulk data and prefer local aggregation.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Battery Lifetime Calculation > **Problem:** A sensor node has a duty cycle of $\alpha = 0.01$ (1% active), with $P_{active} = 60$ mW and $P_{sleep} = 0.003$ mW. It uses two AA batteries in series ($V = 3$ V, total $C_{bat} = 2500$ mAh). Estimate node lifetime in **days**. ### Step 1 — Compute the average current draw Battery voltage: $V = 3$ V. Active current: $$ I_{active} = \dfrac{P_{active}}{V} = \dfrac{60\ \text{mW}}{3\ \text{V}} = 20\ \text{mA} $$ Sleep current: $$ I_{sleep} = \dfrac{P_{sleep}}{V} = \dfrac{0.003\ \text{mW}}{3\ \text{V}} = 0.001\ \text{mA} = 1\ \mu\text{A} $$ ### Step 2 — Weighted average current $$ I_{avg} = \alpha \cdot I_{active} + (1-\alpha) \cdot I_{sleep} $$ Substituting $\alpha = 0.01$: $$ I_{avg} = 0.01 \times 20 + 0.99 \times 0.001 $$ $$ I_{avg} = 0.2 + 0.00099 \approx 0.20099\ \text{mA} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute battery lifetime in hours $$ T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2500\ \text{mAh}}{0.20099\ \text{mA}} $$ $$ T_{life} \approx 12{,}438\ \text{hours} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to days $$ T_{life} = \dfrac{12{,}438}{24} \approx 518.3\ \text{days} \approx 1.42\ \text{years} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the canonical "two-AA battery" lifetime** often quoted in IEEE WSN literature (e.g., Crossbow MICAz motes). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Link-Budget Analysis for Range Estimation > **Problem:** Calculate the maximum range of a 2.4 GHz IEEE 802.15.4 node given $P_{tx} = 0$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, and $P_{r,\min} = -95$ dBm. Assume free-space propagation. ### Step 1 — Convert all quantities to dB - $P_{tx} = 0$ dBm - $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi - $P_{r,\min} = -95$ dBm - Frequency: $f = 2400$ MHz ### Step 2 — Write the free-space path loss equation $$ PL_{dB} = 32.4 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) $$ ### Step 3 — Use the link-budget identity $$ P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - PL_{dB} $$ For successful reception: $P_{rx} \geq P_{r,\min}$. ### Step 4 — Solve for the maximum allowable path loss $$ PL_{\max} = P_{tx} + G_t + G_r - P_{r,\min} $$ $$ PL_{\max} = 0 + 2 + 2 - (-95) = 99\ \text{dB} $$ ### Step 5 — Equate and solve for $d$ $$ 99 = 32.4 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) $$ Compute $20 \log_{10}(2400)$: $$ 20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.60\ \text{dB} $$ ### Step 6 — Isolate $d_{km}$ $$ 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) = 99 - 32.4 - 67.60 $$ $$ 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) = -1.0 $$ $$ \log_{10}(d_{km}) = -0.05 $$ ### Step 7 — Final range $$ d_{km} = 10^{-0.05} \approx 0.891\ \text{km} = 891\ \text{meters} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Outdoor LoS range ≈ 891 m. In indoor multipath environments, this shrinks to **30–100 m** — a key reason for mesh topologies. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major WSN Protocols | Protocol | IEEE Std | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Topology | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **ZigBee** | 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | Mesh | Home automation | | **Bluetooth LE** | 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | 1–2 Mbps | 10–50 m | Star | Wearables | | **Wi-Fi (HaLow)** | 802.11ah | 900 MHz | 100 kbps | 1 km | Star/Tree | Smart agriculture | | **LoRaWAN** | proprietary | 868/915 MHz | 0.3–50 kbps | 2–15 km | Star-of-stars | Long-range IoT | | **6LoWPAN** | 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | Mesh | IPv6 over WSN | | **Thread** | 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 30 m | Mesh | Nest smart home | | **Z-Wave** | proprietary | 908 MHz | 100 kbps | 30 m | Mesh | Lighting, locks | | **NB-IoT** | 3GPP | LTE bands | 200 kbps | 15 km | Star (cellular) | Smart metering | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Smart Object Internal Architecture (KTU Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Temperature, Humidity, Motion] --> B[Sensing Unit
Sensor + ADC] B --> C[Processing Unit
MCU + Memory] C --> D[Transceiver Unit
RF Radio 2.4 GHz] D --> E[WSN Sink
Gateway] E --> F[Cloud Server
IoT Platform] F --> G[End User
Dashboard Mobile App] C --> H[Power Unit
Battery 3V / Energy Harvesting] H --> B H --> C H --> D A --> I[Actuator
LED, Motor, Relay] C --> I I --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-level mapping:** The flowchart captures the canonical *sense → process → communicate → actuate* loop. The **Power Unit** is the *shared lifeline* drawn as an auxiliary rail — a common KTU diagram motif. --- ## 4.2 WSN Protocol Stack (5-Layer View) ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Application Layer
CoAP MQTT HTTP] L2[Transport Layer
UDP TCP] L3[Network Layer
RPL IPv6] L4[Adaptation Layer
6LoWPAN] L5[Data Link Layer
IEEE 802.15.4 MAC] L6[Physical Layer
2.4 GHz DSSS Radio] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 L4 -.Header compression.-> L5 L3 -.Routing.-> L4 ``` --- ## 4.3 Communication Criteria Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is range > 1 km?} Q1 -- Yes --> P1[Use LoRaWAN or NB-IoT] Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is data rate > 1 Mbps?} Q2 -- Yes --> P2[Use Wi-Fi or BLE 5] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need IPv6 connectivity?} Q3 -- Yes --> P3[Use 6LoWPAN over 802.15.4] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need mesh self-healing?} Q4 -- Yes --> P4[Use ZigBee or Thread] Q4 -- No --> P5[Use Bluetooth LE] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Hack:** This decision tree is a 7-mark "design question" goldmine. Draw it during your ESE to score full marks on protocol-selection problems. --- ## 4.4 Star vs Mesh vs Cluster Topology (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STAR["Star Topology"] S1[Node A] --- H1[Base Station] S2[Node B] --- H1 S3[Node C] --- H1 S4[Node D] --- H1 end subgraph MESH["Mesh Topology"] M1[Node A] --- M2[Node B] M2 --- M3[Node C] M3 --- M4[Node D] M4 --- M1 M1 --- M3 M2 --- M4 end subgraph CLUSTER["Cluster Tree"] CH1[Cluster Head 1] --- C1[Node A] CH1 --- C2[Node B] CH2[Cluster Head 2] --- C3[Node C] CH2 --- C4[Node D] CH1 --- CH2 end ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper] **Define a Smart Object. List the five key capabilities a smart object must possess.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Smart Object** is a physical or virtual entity embedded with **sensing, processing, communication, identification, and actuation** capabilities, enabling it to perceive its environment, make local decisions, and interact with other smart objects over a network. The **five key capabilities** are: 1. **Identification** — unique ID (RFID, IPv6 address) 2. **Sensing** — gather data from environment 3. **Processing** — local computation via MCU 4. **Actuation** — control actuators (motors, relays) 5. **Communication** — transmit data via wireless protocols > (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing the five capabilities with brief explanation.) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List and briefly explain any three communication criteria for Wireless Sensor Networks.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Range:** Maximum distance over which two sensor nodes can communicate reliably; typically 1–100 m for WSN (1 mark). 2. **Power Consumption:** Energy budget per node, usually < 100 mW, often duty-cycled to extend battery life to years (1 mark). 3. **Data Rate:** Throughput supported by the radio, typically 20–250 kbps for IEEE 802.15.4-based WSNs (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **layered protocol stack of a Wireless Sensor Network**, with emphasis on the role of **6LoWPAN** in bridging IPv6 with IEEE 802.15.4. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A WSN node transmits at $P_{tx} = 30$ mW with a data rate of 100 kbps. If the node is active for 5% of the time and sleeps at 0.05 mW, calculate **(i)** the energy per bit transmitted, **(ii)** the average power consumption, and **(iii)** the estimated battery life in days for a 2000 mAh, 3 V battery. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Layered Protocol Stack of WSN:** The WSN protocol stack is a **5-layer** simplified version of the TCP/IP model, optimized for resource-constrained nodes. **Layer 1 — Application Layer:** Provides the interface for user-defined sensing applications. Common protocols include **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** and **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**. (1 mark) **Layer 2 — Transport Layer:** Provides end-to-end delivery. **UDP** is preferred over TCP because TCP's handshake overhead is too expensive for low-power nodes. (1 mark) **Layer 3 — Network Layer:** Performs addressing and routing using **IPv6** addressing (128-bit) and **RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks)**. RPL builds a **DODAG** (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph). (1 mark) **Layer 4 — Adaptation Layer (6LoWPAN):** This is the *unique layer* of the WSN stack. 6LoWPAN performs three functions: - **Header compression** — compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header to as little as 2 bytes. - **Fragmentation** — splits large IPv6 packets into multiple IEEE 802.15.4 frames (max 127 bytes each). - **Mesh addressing** — enables multi-hop forwarding at the link layer. (2 marks) **Layer 5 — Data Link + Physical Layer (IEEE 802.15.4):** Operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** worldwide, with 16 channels, 250 kbps data rate, and DSSS modulation. (1 mark) **Conclusion (1 mark):** 6LoWPAN acts as a **"translator"** between the IP world (large packets) and the WSN world (small frames), making the WSN an *IP-addressable* extension of the Internet — the foundation of IoT. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Given:** - $P_{tx} = 30$ mW - $R_b = 100$ kbps $= 100 \times 10^3$ bps - Duty cycle $\alpha = 0.05$ - $P_{sleep} = 0.05$ mW - Battery: $C_{bat} = 2000$ mAh, $V = 3$ V **(i) Energy per bit (2 marks):** $$ E_b = \dfrac{P_{tx}}{R_b} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{3}} = 3 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{J/bit} = 0.3\ \mu\text{J/bit} $$ **(ii) Average power consumption (2 marks):** $$ P_{avg} = \alpha P_{active} + (1-\alpha) P_{sleep} $$ $$ P_{avg} = 0.05 \times 30 + 0.95 \times 0.05 $$ $$ P_{avg} = 1.5 + 0.0475 = 1.5475\ \text{mW} $$ **(iii) Battery life (3 marks):** Average current: $$ I_{avg} = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{V} = \dfrac{1.5475\ \text{mW}}{3\ \text{V}} = 0.5158\ \text{mA} $$ Battery life in hours: $$ T_{life} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2000}{0.5158} \approx 3877\ \text{hours} $$ Battery life in days: $$ T_{life} = \dfrac{3877}{24} \approx 161.5\ \text{days} \approx 5.4\ \text{months} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU Model Paper 2024] **Q. (a)** Compare and contrast the **three major WSN communication protocols** — ZigBee, Bluetooth LE, and LoRaWAN — across the seven communication criteria (range, power, data rate, latency, security, mobility, topology). **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the **architecture of a typical smart sensor node** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each unit and justify why **energy harvesting** is becoming the preferred power source for next-generation WSNs. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks **Comparison Table (5 marks):** | Criterion | ZigBee | Bluetooth LE | LoRaWAN | |---|---|---|---| | IEEE Standard | 802.15.4 | 802.15.1 | Proprietary | | Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 868/915 MHz | | Range | 10–100 m | 10–50 m | 2–15 km | | Data Rate | 250 kbps | 1–2 Mbps | 0.3–50 kbps | | Power | Low (~30 mW) | Low (~10 mW) | Very Low | | Latency | 15–30 ms | 7.5 ms | 1–10 s | | Security | AES-128 | AES-CCM | AES-128 + App Key | | Mobility | Low | Medium | Low | | Topology | Mesh | Star | Star-of-stars | **Analysis (2 marks):** - **ZigBee** is best for **indoor, dense, mesh networks** (smart homes). - **Bluetooth LE** suits **personal-area, short-burst, mobile apps** (wearables, beacons). - **LoRaWAN** dominates **long-range, low-data, outdoor** deployments (agriculture, metering). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks **Smart Sensor Node Architecture (4 marks):** A typical WSN node consists of four sub-units: 1. **Sensing Unit** — sensor (analog) → signal conditioning → ADC → digital data. 2. **Processing Unit** — microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0) + flash memory for data processing and storage. 3. **Transceiver Unit** — short-range RF radio (e.g., CC2420) operating in the 2.4 GHz band. 4. **Power Unit** — battery (2×AA) supplemented by energy harvesting. **Role of Each Unit (2 marks):** - Sensing = data acquisition - Processing = local decision making & aggregation - Transceiver = wireless communication - Power = lifeline of the node **Energy Harvesting Justification (1 mark):** Next-generation WSNs use **solar, vibrational (piezo), thermal, or RF harvesting** because: - Battery replacement is **infeasible** in remote deployments (e.g., forest, ocean, structural health monitoring). - Harvesting offers **virtually infinite lifetime**. - Combined with ultra-low-power MCUs, it enables **zero-maintenance IoT**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing WSN with MANET** — WSNs are data-centric and resource-constrained; MANETs are mobile and IP-centric. (Lose 1–2 marks.) > 2. **Wrong units for data rate** — Always write **kbps or Mbps**, not "kbps/sec". > 3. **Forgetting duty cycle in power calculations** — KTU board specifically tests $\alpha$-weighted average power. A direct use of $P_{active}$ alone is a **common 2-mark deduction**. > 4. **Missing the 6LoWPAN role** — In any 14-mark stack question, omitting header compression and fragmentation loses **2 marks minimum**. > 5. **Mixing ZigBee and IEEE 802.15.4** — ZigBee is the *upper-layer protocol*; IEEE 802.15.4 is the *physical + MAC layer*. They are NOT the same. (Lose 1 mark.) > 6. **Range in dB instead of meters** — Always convert dB path loss to distance in the final step. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] **WSN** = distributed network of autonomous sensor nodes → sink → base station. - [x] **Smart Object** = Identifier + Sense + Process + Actuate + Communicate. - [x] **7 Communication Criteria:** Range, Power, Data Rate, Latency, Security, Mobility, Topology. - [x] **5-Layer WSN Stack:** Application (CoAP/MQTT) → Transport (UDP) → Network (RPL/IPv6) → Adaptation (6LoWPAN) → MAC/PHY (802.15.4). - [x] **6LoWPAN** = IPv6 header compression + fragmentation for 802.15.4 frames (max 127 bytes). - [x] **IEEE 802.15.4** = 2.4 GHz, 250 kbps, 16 channels, DSSS modulation. - [x] **Energy per bit** $E_b = P_{tx}/R_b$ — fundamental radio efficiency metric. - [x] **Battery life** $T = C_{bat}/I_{avg}$ where $I_{avg} = \alpha I_{on} + (1-\alpha) I_{off}$. - [x] **Free-space path loss** $PL_{dB} = 32.4 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$. - [x] **ZigBee** = mesh, indoor; **BLE** = star, wearables; **LoRaWAN** = long-range, outdoor. - [x] **AES-128** is the standard WSN security cipher (~1.62 µJ per block). - [x] **Star** = simple; **Mesh** = self-healing; **Cluster Tree** = energy-efficient (LEACH).

topics.title: Unified Data Standards - Protocols - IEEE 802.15.4 - The Physical Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Unified Data Standards - Protocols - IEEE 802.15.4 - The Physical Layer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Unified Data Standards in IoT? **Unified Data Standards** refer to a set of globally accepted communication protocols that ensure interoperability, scalability, and seamless data exchange between heterogeneous IoT devices manufactured by different vendors. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context for *PEMRT525 - IoT and Applications*, this concept sits at the heart of **Module 3**, focusing on protocols that allow constrained devices to "speak a common language." > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Unified Data Standards, Protocols, IEEE 802.15.4, The Physical Layer (PHY), MAC Layer, ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, RFID, and the role of these protocols in the IoT protocol stack. ## 1.2 IEEE 802.15.4 — The Foundation Standard **IEEE 802.15.4** is a technical standard that defines the operation of **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)**. It specifies the **Physical (PHY) Layer** and the **Media Access Control (MAC) Layer** for short-range, low-power, low-data-rate wireless communication. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** *IEEE 802.15.4 is a standard maintained by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) for wireless network operations operating in the personal operating space (POS) of 10 meters. It focuses on low-data-rate, ultra-low complexity, and ultra-low power consumption, making it ideal for IoT sensor networks and home/building automation.* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🏠 Imagine you walk into a **busy international airport**. Hundreds of people speak different languages — English, Hindi, Malayalam, Tamil, Japanese. Without a **common language (English)**, chaos would reign. Similarly, in IoT: - A temperature sensor made by **Texas Instruments** needs to talk to a gateway made by **Qualcomm**. - A ZigBee lightbulb from **Philips** must respond to a **Samsung SmartThings** hub. - **IEEE 802.15.4** acts as that **"common wireless language"** that ensures tiny IoT devices can communicate regardless of the manufacturer. Think of IEEE 802.15.4 as the **"postal service rules"** — it defines: - **How** a letter (data packet) is structured (PHY layer) - **When** you are allowed to drop it in the mailbox (MAC layer) ## 1.3 The Physical Layer (PHY) — First Contact with the Wireless World The **Physical Layer** is **Layer 1** of the **OSI 7-Layer Model**. It is the lowest layer and is responsible for the **actual transmission and reception of raw bit streams over a physical medium** (radio waves, in the case of IEEE 802.15.4). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Functions of the IEEE 802.15.4 PHY Layer:** > 1. **RF Transceiver Activation/Deactivation** — turning the radio ON/OFF to save power > 2. **Channel Frequency Selection** — choosing the right frequency band > 3. **Data Modulation & Demodulation** — converting digital bits to radio signals > 4. **Transmitting/Receiving PPDUs** (PHY Protocol Data Units) > 5. **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** — sensing if the medium is free > 6. **Energy Detection (ED)** — measuring signal strength in the channel > 7. **Link Quality Indication (LQI)** — reporting the quality of the received signal ### Conceptual Analogy for the PHY Layer 🎙️ Think of the PHY layer as a **postman on a bicycle**: - The postman has a **bag (antenna)** to carry letters (data packets). - He rides on a specific **route (frequency channel)**. - Before starting his delivery, he **listens** to know if the road is clear (CCA). - He **encodes** his letters in a specific format (modulation) so the receiver can understand. ## 1.4 The OSI Layer Mapping | OSI Layer | IEEE 802.15.4 Equivalent | Responsibility | |---|---|---| | Layer 7 — Application | Application Layer (User-defined) | Data semantics | | Layer 3 — Network | Network Layer (e.g., 6LoWPAN) | Routing | | Layer 2 — Data Link | **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC** | Addressing, Channel Access | | Layer 1 — Physical | **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY** ⭐ | RF transmission, Modulation | ## 1.5 The Three Operating Frequency Bands > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory KTU Memory Point:** IEEE 802.15.4 operates in **three unlicensed industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) radio bands**. | Frequency Band | Region | Number of Channels | Data Rate (Original 2003) | Data Rate (2006 Revision) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **868 MHz** | Europe (ETSI) | **1 channel** | **20 kbps** | 100 kbps (O-QPSK) | | **915 MHz** | USA, Australia (FCC) | **10 channels** (1–10) | **40 kbps** | 250 kbps (O-QPSK) | | **2.4 GHz** | Worldwide (Global) | **16 channels** (11–26) | **250 kbps** | **250 kbps** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Memory):** > - Global channel spacing in 2.4 GHz band: **5 MHz** > - Channel 0 (868 MHz) center frequency: **868.3 MHz** > - Channel 1–10 (915 MHz) center frequency: **906 + 2(k-1) MHz**, where k = channel number > - Channel 11–26 (2.4 GHz) center frequency: **2405 + 5(k-11) MHz** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of the IEEE 802.15.4 PHY Layer The PHY layer in IEEE 802.15.4 consists of two main service access points (SAPs): 1. **PHY Data Service** — accessed via the **PD-SAP** (PHY Data Service Access Point) 2. **PHY Management Service** — accessed via the **PLME-SAP** (PHY Layer Management Entity SAP) The PHY layer communicates with the MAC sublayer through these SAPs, exchanging **PHY Protocol Data Units (PPDUs)**. ## 2.2 Frequency Bands and Channel Allocation — Mathematical Logic The 802.15.4 standard (2003 release) supports the following bands: ### A. The 868 MHz Band (Europe) - **Single channel** (channel 0) - **Center frequency:** $f_c = 868.3 \text{ MHz}$ - **Chip rate:** 300 kchips/s - **Modulation:** BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying) - **Bit rate:** 20 kbps ### B. The 915 MHz Band (North America) - **10 channels** (channels 1 through 10) - **Center frequency formula:** $$ f_c = 906 + 2(k-1) \text{ MHz}, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 10 $$ - **Channel spacing:** 2 MHz - **Chip rate:** 600 kchips/s - **Modulation:** BPSK - **Bit rate:** 40 kbps ### C. The 2.4 GHz Band (Global) - **16 channels** (channels 11 through 26) - **Center frequency formula:** $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11) \text{ MHz}, \quad k = 11, 12, \ldots, 26 $$ - **Channel spacing:** 5 MHz - **Chip rate:** 2000 kchips/s (2 Mcps) - **Modulation:** O-QPSK (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) - **Bit rate:** 250 kbps > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The 2.4 GHz band is widely used because it is **globally available** and supports the **highest data rate of 250 kbps**, making it the de-facto choice for most modern IoT deployments like ZigBee. ## 2.3 Modulation Techniques Explained ### 2.3.1 BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying) — Used in 868/915 MHz In BPSK, the **phase** of the carrier signal is shifted between two states (0° and 180°) to represent binary 0 and binary 1. $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for bit } 0 \\ A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) & \text{for bit } 1 \end{cases} $$ The amplitude remains constant, only the phase changes. **BPSK has the best noise immunity** among the 802.15.4 modulation schemes. ### 2.3.2 O-QPSK (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying) — Used in 2.4 GHz In O-QPSK, the carrier is split into two orthogonal components (I and Q). One component is **delayed by half a symbol period** to prevent simultaneous phase transitions, reducing signal envelope variations. - Each symbol represents **4 bits** (16-ary orthogonal modulation) - Uses **Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)** with chip sequence length 16 - Higher spectral efficiency and **higher data throughput** ## 2.4 Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) in IEEE 802.15.4 > [!NOTE] > **DSSS** is a spread-spectrum technique where each data bit is multiplied by a **PN (Pseudo-Noise) sequence** called a **chip sequence**. For every 4 data bits in 2.4 GHz mode, **16 chips** are transmitted. $$ \text{Chips per data bit (chip rate)} = \frac{2000 \text{ kchips/s}}{250 \text{ kbps}} = 8 \text{ chips/bit} $$ But since 4 bits are grouped into one symbol: $$ \text{Chips per symbol} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \text{ chips/symbol} $$ Wait — let me correct the KTU standard. For 2.4 GHz O-QPSK: - Bit rate: **250 kbps** - Chip rate: **2 Mcps** (2,000 kchips/s) - Chips per bit: **8 chips/bit** (each bit is spread using an 8-chip PN sequence) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Chip-to-Bit Ratio:** $\frac{2 \text{ Mcps}}{250 \text{ kbps}} = 8$ chips per bit ## 2.5 PHY Protocol Data Unit (PPDU) Structure The PPDU is the frame structure transmitted by the PHY layer. It has the following components: | Field | Length | Description | |---|---|---| | **Preamble** | 4 bytes (32 bits) | Used for symbol synchronization by the receiver | | **Start of Frame Delimiter (SFD)** | 1 byte | Marks the end of preamble and start of frame | | **Frame Length (7 bits)** | 1 byte (7 LSBs) | Specifies the length of the PHY payload | | **Reserved (1 bit)** | 1 bit | Reserved for future use | | **PHY Payload (PSDU)** | Variable | The MAC frame (PSDU = PHY Service Data Unit), max 127 bytes | | **Total PPDU Maximum** | — | 4 + 1 + 1 + 127 = **133 bytes** | $$ \text{Max PPDU size} = 32 + 8 + 8 + (7 \times 8) = 127 \text{ bytes max for PSDU} $$ ## 2.6 Clear Channel Assessment (CCA) Modes The PHY layer provides **3 modes of CCA** to the MAC layer: | Mode | Method | Description | |---|---|---| | **Mode 1** | Energy above threshold | Reports busy if any energy above ED threshold is detected | | **Mode 2** | Carrier sense only | Reports busy ONLY if it detects an IEEE 802.15.4 signal | | **Mode 3** | Carrier sense AND energy | Reports busy if EITHER energy > threshold OR an 802.15.4 signal is detected (most robust) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Default CCA Mode used in most 802.15.4 networks:** **Mode 3** (combination check), as it provides the best channel availability assessment. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 | # | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 868 MHz center frequency | $f_c = 868.3$ | MHz | Single channel, Europe | | 2 | 915 MHz channel frequency | $f_c = 906 + 2(k-1)$ | MHz | k = 1 to 10 | | 3 | 2.4 GHz channel frequency | $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ | MHz | k = 11 to 26 | | 4 | 2.4 GHz bit rate | 250 | kbps | Global default | | 5 | 915 MHz bit rate | 40 | kbps | USA | | 6 | 868 MHz bit rate | 20 | kbps | Europe | | 7 | 2.4 GHz chip rate | 2,000 | kcps | kchips per second | | 8 | Chip-to-bit ratio | 8 | chips/bit | 2.4 GHz band | | 9 | Max PSDU size | 127 | bytes | MAC payload max | | 10 | Max PPDU overhead | 6 | bytes | Preamble + SFD + Length | | 11 | Max total PPDU | 133 | bytes | Including PSDU | | 12 | Number of 2.4 GHz channels | 16 | channels | Globally available | | 13 | Number of 915 MHz channels | 10 | channels | North America | | 14 | Number of 868 MHz channels | 1 | channel | Europe | | 15 | Max transmission range (LOS) | 100 | meters | Outdoor, ideal | | 16 | Typical indoor range | 10–30 | meters | Home/office | | 17 | Transmit power (typical) | 0 to 10 | dBm | Configurable | | 18 | Receiver sensitivity (2.4 GHz) | −85 | dBm | Typical | | 19 | SIFS (Short Interframe Spacing) | 12 | symbols | Minimum turnaround | | 20 | LIFS (Long Interframe Spacing) | 40 | symbols | MAC layer parameter | ## 2.8 Why is IEEE 802.15.4 PHY So Critical for IoT? 1. **Ultra-Low Power:** The PHY is designed to support **sleep modes** where the radio is completely off — enabling battery lifetimes of **months to years**. 2. **Low Complexity:** Modest chip rate and simple modulation keep the hardware **cheap** (a typical 802.15.4 radio costs < $1). 3. **Robustness:** DSSS spreading and O-QPSK modulation provide **resistance to interference** from WiFi, Bluetooth, and microwave ovens. 4. **Interoperability:** Any certified 802.15.4 device from any vendor can communicate with any other — a **mandatory requirement** for IoT ecosystems. 5. **Foundation for Higher Layers:** ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, Matter (formerly Project CHIP), and WirelessHART all build on top of IEEE 802.15.4. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The maximum **data throughput** of IEEE 802.15.4 is **250 kbps**, which is **2,000 times slower than a typical WiFi network (54–600 Mbps)**. This is **intentional** — IoT sensors don't need to stream video; they need to send tiny packets of telemetry while consuming micro-watts of power. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum PPDU Transmission Time The **time to transmit a maximum-sized PPDU** is a critical parameter in 802.15.4 networks. Let us derive it for the 2.4 GHz band. ### Step 1: Identify the maximum PPDU size The maximum PPDU consists of: - Preamble: 4 bytes = 32 bits - SFD: 1 byte = 8 bits - Length field: 1 byte = 8 bits - PSDU (PHY payload): 127 bytes = 127 × 8 = 1,016 bits $$ \text{Total PPDU bits} = 32 + 8 + 8 + 1016 = 1064 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 2: Calculate the time per bit In the 2.4 GHz band, the data rate is **250 kbps**. $$ T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{250{,}000} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ µs/bit} $$ ### Step 3: Calculate the total transmission time $$ T_{PPDU} = N_{bits} \times T_{bit} $$ $$ T_{PPDU} = 1064 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} $$ $$ T_{PPDU} = 4256 \times 10^{-6} = 4.256 \text{ ms} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** The maximum time to transmit a PPDU in the 2.4 GHz band of IEEE 802.15.4 is **$T_{PPDU} = 4.256 \text{ ms}$**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Channel Frequency for a Given Channel Number ### Problem: Find the center frequency for Channel 18 in IEEE 802.15.4. **Step 1: Identify the band** Channel 18 falls in the range 11–26, so it is in the **2.4 GHz band**. **Step 2: Apply the formula** For the 2.4 GHz band, the center frequency is given by: $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(k - 11) \text{ MHz} $$ where $k$ is the channel number. **Step 3: Substitute $k = 18$** $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(18 - 11) $$ $$ f_c = 2405 + 5 \times 7 $$ $$ f_c = 2405 + 35 $$ $$ \boxed{f_c = 2440 \text{ MHz}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check:** The 2.4 GHz WiFi channel 1 is centered at 2412 MHz, and ZigBee channel 18 is centered at 2440 MHz. This is precisely why WiFi interferes with ZigBee on overlapping channels — they share the same band! --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Chip Rate vs. Data Rate Relationship For the 2.4 GHz O-QPSK modulation: ### Step 1: Data rate The nominal bit rate is: $$ R_b = 250 \text{ kbps} $$ ### Step 2: Spreading factor Each data bit is encoded using a **PN sequence of length 8**: $$ \text{SF} = 8 \text{ chips/bit} $$ ### Step 3: Compute chip rate $$ R_c = R_b \times \text{SF} $$ $$ R_c = 250{,}000 \times 8 = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ chips/s} = 2 \text{ Mcps} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** $R_c = 2 \text{ Mcps}$ — this matches the IEEE 802.15.4 specification exactly. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Detection Threshold and Receiver Sensitivity The minimum detectable signal level is typically expressed in dBm. The relationship between power in milliwatts and dBm is: $$ P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{mW}}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) $$ ### Problem: Convert a receiver sensitivity of $0.0001$ mW to dBm. **Step 1: Identify the power** $$ P = 0.0001 \text{ mW} = 10^{-4} \text{ mW} $$ **Step 2: Apply the formula** $$ P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{-4}) $$ $$ P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \times (-4) $$ $$ \boxed{P_{\text{dBm}} = -40 \text{ dBm}} $$ ### Problem: A transmitter outputs +10 dBm. The path loss is 70 dB. What is the received power? **Step 1: Free-space path loss formula** $$ P_{RX} = P_{TX} - L_{path} $$ **Step 2: Substitute** $$ P_{RX} = 10 - 70 = -60 \text{ dBm} $$ **Step 3: Compare with receiver sensitivity** If the receiver sensitivity is $-85$ dBm, then $-60$ dBm > $-85$ dBm, so the signal is **successfully received** (received power is stronger than sensitivity). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — PHY Layer Performance Parameters | Parameter | 868 MHz | 915 MHz | 2.4 GHz | Significance for KTU | |---|---|---|---|---| | Modulation | BPSK | BPSK | O-QPSK | Affects spectral efficiency | | Data Rate | 20 kbps | 40 kbps | 250 kbps | Higher rate = faster transmission | | Chip Rate | 300 kcps | 600 kcps | 2000 kcps | Higher chip rate = more bandwidth | | Symbol Rate | 20 ksymbols/s | 40 ksymbols/s | 62.5 ksymbols/s | Related to bit rate | | Bits per Symbol | 1 | 1 | 4 | O-QPSK packs 4 bits/symbol | | Rx Sensitivity (typ) | −92 dBm | −92 dBm | −85 dBm | Better sensitivity = longer range | | Tx Power (max) | +10 dBm | +10 dBm | +10 dBm | Regulatory limit | | Range (outdoor) | 1000 m | 1000 m | 100 m | Lower freq = longer range | | WiFi Interference | None | None | **High** | KTU essay-favorite! | | Number of Channels | 1 | 10 | 16 | More channels = more capacity | --- ## 3.6 Detailed Derivation: Maximum Network Capacity ### Step 1: Time to transmit one max-size frame We calculated $T_{PPDU} = 4.256$ ms. ### Step 2: Maximum number of frames per second $$ N_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{PPDU}} = \frac{1}{4.256 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 235 \text{ frames/second} $$ ### Step 3: Maximum data throughput $$ \text{Throughput} = N_{max} \times \text{PSDU size} \times 8 $$ $$ \text{Throughput} = 235 \times 127 \times 8 \approx 238{,}760 \text{ bits/s} \approx 238.76 \text{ kbps} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The **maximum theoretical throughput** is approximately **238.76 kbps**, which is **just below** the nominal 250 kbps rate. The difference accounts for protocol overhead (preamble, SFD, length field, interframe spacing, ACK wait time, etc.). --- ## 3.7 Power Budget Analysis (Link Budget) A typical 802.15.4 link budget calculation: $$ P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{path} - L_{misc} $$ where: - $P_{TX}$ = Transmit power (dBm) - $G_{TX}$ = Transmitter antenna gain (dBi) - $G_{RX}$ = Receiver antenna gain (dBi) - $L_{path}$ = Path loss (dB) - $L_{misc}$ = Miscellaneous losses (fading, cabling, etc.) (dB) ### Example Calculation Given: - $P_{TX} = +10$ dBm - $G_{TX} = G_{RX} = 2$ dBi - $L_{path} = 75$ dB - $L_{misc} = 5$ dB $$ P_{RX} = 10 + 2 + 2 - 75 - 5 = -66 \text{ dBm} $$ Since $-66 \text{ dBm} > -85 \text{ dBm}$ (sensitivity), the link is **viable**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Link Margin = $P_{RX} - \text{Sensitivity} = -66 - (-85) = 19$ dB.** A positive link margin of ~19 dB indicates a reliable communication link with significant safety margin against fading. --- ## 3.8 Comparison with WiFi (802.11) PHY | Feature | IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY) | IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Low-rate IoT | High-rate networking | | Max Data Rate | 250 kbps | 54 Mbps–9.6 Gbps | | Frequency | 868/915/2400 MHz | 2.4/5/6 GHz | | Range | 10–100 m | 30–300 m | | Power Consumption | < 50 mW (active) | 500–1000 mW | | Battery Life | Months to years | Hours | | Tx Power | < 10 dBm | 15–30 dBm | | Cost | < $1 (radio chip) | $3–$15 | | Typical Use | Sensor networks | Internet, video | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 IEEE 802.15.4 Protocol Stack in the OSI Reference Model ```mermaid graph TB App[Application Layer
