The PHY layer is the **first and last** point of contact with the wireless medium. --- ## 4.7 Complete Mermaid Block — All-in-One Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PROTO[Protocol Stack] APP[Application Layer] NET[Network Layer] MAC[MAC Layer] PHY[PHY Layer] end subgraph PHYFUN[PHY Functions] ENC1[Encoding DSSS] MOD1[Modulation BPSK O-QPSK] FREQ1[Frequency Selection 868 915 2400] CCA1[CCA Energy Detection] TXRX1[Tx Rx Activation] end subgraph SPECS[802.15.4 Specifications] S1[Data Rate 20 40 250 kbps] S2[Channels 1 10 16] S3[Modulation BPSK O-QPSK] S4[Sensitivity -85 dBm] end APP --> NET NET --> MAC MAC --> PHY PHY --> ENC1 PHY --> MOD1 PHY --> FREQ1 PHY --> CCA1 PHY --> TXRX1 PHY -.Uses.-> S1 PHY -.Uses.-> S2 PHY -.Uses.-> S3 PHY -.Uses.-> S4 style APP fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style NET fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style MAC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style PHY fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF style ENC1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style MOD1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style FREQ1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style CCA1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style TXRX1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style S1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style S2 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style S3 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style S4 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]** **Q: Define the IEEE 802.15.4 standard and mention its primary applications in IoT.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > IEEE 802.15.4 is a standard for **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)** that defines the **Physical (PHY) and MAC layer** specifications for short-range, low-power, low-data-rate wireless communication. It operates in the **ISM bands** (868 MHz in Europe, 915 MHz in USA, 2.4 GHz globally) with data rates of **20 kbps, 40 kbps, and 250 kbps** respectively. > > **Primary IoT Applications:** > 1. **Home and building automation** — smart lighting, HVAC control > 2. **Industrial monitoring** — wireless sensor networks for machine health > 3. **Healthcare** — wireless patient monitoring, wearable health devices > 4. **Smart agriculture** — soil moisture and environmental sensing > 5. **Asset tracking and RFID-based location services** > > The standard is the **foundation for higher-layer protocols** like ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, and Matter. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Q: List the three ISM frequency bands supported by the IEEE 802.15.4 PHY layer and their respective data rates.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Band | Region | Modulation | Data Rate | Channels | |---|---|---|---|---| | 868 MHz | Europe | BPSK | 20 kbps | 1 (Ch 0) | | 915 MHz | North America | BPSK | 40 kbps | 10 (Ch 1–10) | | 2.4 GHz | Worldwide | O-QPSK | 250 kbps | 16 (Ch 11–26) | > The 2.4 GHz band is the **most popular** choice globally because it offers the **highest data rate** and is **regulatory-approved worldwide**, including in India. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern, each module question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.** --- ### 🅰️ Question A: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model QP]** **Q: (a)** Explain the architecture and key functions of the IEEE 802.15.4 Physical Layer in detail. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Calculate the center frequency for channels 15, 20, and 25 in the 2.4 GHz band. Also calculate the time required to transmit a maximum-size PPDU. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — IEEE 802.15.4 PHY Architecture (7 Marks):** The IEEE 802.15.4 Physical Layer is the **Layer 1** of the OSI reference model, responsible for the **actual over-the-air transmission and reception of data**. It has the following architecture and key functions: **1. RF Transceiver Control:** The PHY layer manages the activation and deactivation of the radio transmitter/receiver to enable **ultra-low power sleep modes**. A typical 802.15.4 device spends most of its life in sleep mode, waking up briefly to transmit. **2. Channel Frequency Selection:** The PHY supports **27 channels** spread across **3 ISM bands**: - 868 MHz (1 channel) - 915 MHz (10 channels) - 2.4 GHz (16 channels) **3. Data Modulation and Demodulation:** - **BPSK** is used in the 868/915 MHz bands for robustness. - **O-QPSK with DSSS spreading** is used in the 2.4 GHz band for higher data rate. **4. PPDU Transmission:** The PHY encapsulates the MAC frame (PSDU) into a **PHY Protocol Data Unit (PPDU)** with: - **4-byte Preamble** for synchronization - **1-byte SFD** for frame start marker - **1-byte Length field** (7-bit length + 1-bit reserved) - **PSDU** (max 127 bytes) **5. Clear Channel Assessment (CCA):** Three modes of CCA are provided: - **Mode 1:** Energy above threshold - **Mode 2:** Carrier sense only - **Mode 3:** Both (most robust) **6. Energy Detection (ED):** Estimates the received signal power in the channel to support CCA and channel selection algorithms. **7. Link Quality Indication (LQI):** Reports the quality of the received signal back to the MAC for upper-layer adaptation. --- **Part (b) — Frequency and Transmission Time Calculation (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Center frequency for Channel 15** For the 2.4 GHz band: $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(k - 11) \text{ MHz} $$ $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(15 - 11) = 2405 + 20 = 2425 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 2: Center frequency for Channel 20** $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(20 - 11) = 2405 + 45 = 2450 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 3: Center frequency for Channel 25** $$ f_c = 2405 + 5(25 - 11) = 2405 + 70 = 2475 \text{ MHz} $$ **Step 4: Time to transmit maximum-size PPDU** Maximum PPDU bits: - Preamble: 32 bits - SFD: 8 bits - Length: 8 bits - PSDU: 127 × 8 = 1016 bits - **Total: 32 + 8 + 8 + 1016 = 1064 bits** Bit duration in 2.4 GHz band: $$ T_{bit} = \frac{1}{250{,}000} = 4 \text{ µs/bit} $$ Total transmission time: $$ T_{PPDU} = 1064 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 4256 \text{ µs} = 4.256 \text{ ms} $$ **Final Answer:** $T_{PPDU} = 4.256 \text{ ms}$ --- ### 🅱️ Question B: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model QP]** **Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of the IEEE 802.15.4 PPDU. Mention the maximum size of each field. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Differentiate between BPSK and O-QPSK modulation schemes used in IEEE 802.15.4. Also explain DSSS spreading with an example. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — PPDU Structure (7 Marks):** The **PHY Protocol Data Unit (PPDU)** is the frame structure that the PHY layer transmits over the air. It consists of four fields: | Field | Size | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Preamble** | 4 bytes (32 bits) | Used by the receiver for **symbol synchronization** and AGC setting | | **Start of Frame Delimiter (SFD)** | 1 byte (8 bits) | Marks the end of the preamble and start of the actual frame | | **Frame Length** | 1 byte (7 bits length + 1 bit reserved) | Indicates the number of bytes in the PSDU | | **PSDU (PHY Service Data Unit)** | Variable (max 127 bytes) | Contains the MAC Protocol Data Unit (MPDU) | **Maximum total PPDU size:** $$ \text{Max PPDU} = 4 + 1 + 1 + 127 = 133 \text{ bytes} $$ **Diagram (Description):** ``` [Preamble 4B] [SFD 1B] [Length 1B] [PSDU ≤ 127B] 32 bits 8 bits 8 bits ≤ 1016 bits ``` The **preamble** is a sequence of alternating 0s and 1s (specifically 0x00) used by the receiver to lock onto the incoming signal. The **SFD** is a fixed byte pattern (0xA7) that signals the end of the preamble. The **length field** tells the receiver exactly how many bytes to expect in the PSDU. Finally, the **PSDU** carries the actual MAC frame with addressing and payload data. --- **Part (b) — BPSK vs O-QPSK and DSSS Spreading (7 Marks):** **BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying):** - Used in **868 MHz and 915 MHz** bands - **2 phase states** (0° and 180°) representing binary 0 and 1 - **1 bit per symbol** - Highest noise immunity among 802.15.4 schemes - Lower data rate (20/40 kbps) - Simpler hardware design **O-QPSK (Offset Quadrature Phase Shift Keying):** - Used in **2.4 GHz** band - **4 phase states** with **offset** between I and Q channels - **4 bits per symbol** (16-ary modulation) - Higher spectral efficiency - **Higher data rate (250 kbps)** - More complex transmitter/receiver **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | BPSK | O-QPSK | |---|---|---| | Frequency Band | 868/915 MHz | 2.4 GHz | | Bits per Symbol | 1 | 4 | | Data Rate | 20/40 kbps | 250 kbps | | Symbol Rate | 20/40 ksymbols/s | 62.5 ksymbols/s | | Resistance to Noise | High | Moderate | | Complexity | Low | High | **DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum) Spreading Example:** In 2.4 GHz O-QPSK mode, each data bit is spread using an **8-chip PN sequence**. Example: To transmit a data bit "1": - Original bit: `1` - PN sequence (one of 16 possible): `00111100` (8 chips) - Transmitted: `00111100` For data bit "0": - Same PN sequence is transmitted but **logically inverted**: `11000011` At the receiver, the chip sequence is **correlated** with the known PN code to recover the original bit. This spreading provides: 1. **Resistance to narrowband interference** (only ~1/8 of the chip stream is corrupted) 2. **Low probability of detection** 3. **Multiple users can share the same band** (CDMA-like behavior) For a 4-bit data symbol `1010`, the transmitter maps it to a 32-chip sequence using one of 16 orthogonal PN codes, and this 32-chip sequence is transmitted using O-QPSK modulation. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deduction in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting the unit of data rate** — Always write "kbps" not "kbs" or "kBps" (this can cost 0.5 marks). > 2. **Confusing 868 MHz and 915 MHz formulas** — Remember: 868 MHz has a single hardcoded channel; 915 MHz uses the formula $f_c = 906 + 2(k-1)$ MHz. > 3. **Mixing up "channels 11–26" with "16 channels"** — Yes, 11 to 26 inclusive is exactly 16 channels. Both expressions are correct. > 4. **Not mentioning the modulation** — For 2.4 GHz, ALWAYS mention **O-QPSK**; for 868/915 MHz, mention **BPSK**. Half-marks are often lost here. > 5. **Forgetting the chip rate** — When asked about O-QPSK spreading, you MUST mention **2 Mcps chip rate** with **8 chips per bit**. > 6. **Drawing the PPDU without the Length field's reserved bit** — The standard says 7-bit length + 1-bit reserved. If you write "1 byte length" without the reserved bit, you may lose 0.5 marks. > 7. **Confusing CCA Mode 2 with Mode 3** — Mode 2 is carrier sense ONLY; Mode 3 is BOTH. Examiners check this carefully. > 8. **Using incorrect PPDU total** — The **maximum PSDU is 127 bytes** (not 128, not 256, not 1024). The total PPDU with overhead is **133 bytes maximum**. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 25 Critical Points:** 1. ✅ IEEE 802.15.4 defines **PHY + MAC** layers only (not network or application). 2. ✅ The standard is for **Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs)**. 3. ✅ Three ISM bands: **868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz**. 4. ✅ **Total 27 channels** (1 + 10 + 16) supported across all bands. 5. ✅ **2.4 GHz is the global default** with **16 channels (11–26)** and **5 MHz spacing**. 6. ✅ **2.4 GHz center frequency formula:** $f_c = 2405 + 5(k-11)$ MHz. 7. ✅ **915 MHz center frequency formula:** $f_c = 906 + 2(k-1)$ MHz. 8. ✅ **Data rates:** 20 kbps (868), 40 kbps (915), 250 kbps (2.4 GHz). 9. ✅ **Modulation:** BPSK in 868/915; O-QPSK in 2.4 GHz. 10. ✅ **Chip rate in 2.4 GHz:** 2 Mcps (2,000 kchips per second). 11. ✅ **Spreading factor:** 8 chips per bit in O-QPSK mode. 12. ✅ **PPDU structure:** Preamble (4B) + SFD (1B) + Length (1B) + PSDU (≤127B). 13. ✅ **Maximum PSDU size:** 127 bytes. 14. ✅ **Maximum total PPDU:** 133 bytes. 15. ✅ **Preamble purpose:** Receiver symbol synchronization. 16. ✅ **SFD value:** 0xA7 (7-bit length + 1-bit reserved). 17. ✅ **Three CCA modes:** Energy only, Carrier only, Combined. 18. ✅ **Default CCA mode in most networks:** Mode 3 (combined). 19. ✅ **Receiver sensitivity (2.4 GHz):** typically −85 dBm. 20. ✅ **Maximum transmit power:** typically 0 to +10 dBm. 21. ✅ **PHY provides two SAPs:** PD-SAP and PLME-SAP. 22. ✅ **DSSS spreading** provides resistance to narrowband interference. 23. ✅ **Higher-layer protocols built on 802.15.4:** ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, Matter, WirelessHART. 24. ✅ **IEEE 802.15.4 vs WiFi (802.11):** Slower (250 kbps vs 54+ Mbps) but lower power, longer battery life. 25. ✅ **Maximum PPDU transmission time (2.4 GHz, max size):** 4.256 ms. --- ## 📚 Quick Reference Card ``` ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ IEEE 802.15.4 PHY — INSTANT REVISION CARD ║ ╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣ ║ Frequency Bands : 868/915 MHz (BPSK) + 2.4 GHz (O-QPSK)║ ║ Channels : 1 + 10 + 16 = 27 total ║ ║ Data Rate : 20 / 40 / 250 kbps ║ ║ Chip Rate (2.4G) : 2 Mcps, 8 chips per bit ║ ║ Modulation : BPSK (sub-GHz), O-QPSK (2.4 GHz) ║ ║ PPDU Max : 133 bytes (4+1+1+127) ║ ║ PSDU Max : 127 bytes ║ ║ CCA Modes : 3 (energy/carrier/combined) ║ ║ Tx Power : 0 to +10 dBm ║ ║ Rx Sensitivity : -85 dBm (2.4 GHz) ║ ║ Range : 10–100 m typical ║ ║ Built Upon By : ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, Matter ║ ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ ``` --- > **End of Module 3 Notes — IEEE 802.15.4 Physical Layer** > *All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme ESE examinations!* 🎓

topics.title: The Media Access Control Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Media Access Control Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Media Access Control (MAC) Layer** is the lower sublayer of the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** in the OSI reference model. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** and **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs)**, the MAC layer is the most critical protocol layer governing how multiple constrained, battery-powered, and resource-limited devices share a common wireless communication medium without destructive interference.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the MAC sublayer is responsible for:

1. **Channel Access Control** — Determining *when* a node is permitted to transmit on the shared medium.
2. **Frame Addressing & Identification** — Embedding and parsing source, destination, and PAN identifiers.
3. **Error Detection Triggering** — Initiating acknowledgement (ACK) and retransmission procedures.
4. **Network Formation & Maintenance** — Beacon generation, synchronization, association, and disassociation.
5. **Energy Management** — Duty cycling, sleep/wake scheduling, and power-state transitions.

The de facto standard for low-rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (LR-WPANs) used in IoT is **IEEE 802.15.4**, which defines both the **Physical (PHY)** and **MAC** sublayer specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The MAC layer in IoT is *not* a generic Layer-2 topic. KTU examiners specifically test the **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC protocol** along with its suitability for **Low-Power Lossy Networks (LLNs)** and how it differs from classical CSMA/CD Ethernet MAC.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Air Traffic Controller at a Busy Airport"

Imagine a single, narrow **runway** shared by **hundreds of small aircraft** (your IoT sensor nodes) that are all trying to take off and land, but each aircraft has very **little fuel** (battery capacity) and can carry only **small parcels** (tiny data frames).

- The **MAC layer** acts as the **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** standing at the tower.
- The ATC ensures that **no two aircraft land or take off at the same moment** (collision avoidance).
- It assigns specific **time slots** to specific aircraft (TDMA-like behaviour in GTS).
- It tells idle aircraft to **power down their engines and rest** (sleep mode — energy conservation).
- It also handles **emergency priority landings** (CSMA/CA random backoff for critical data).

Without the MAC layer's discipline, all aircraft would simply attempt to use the runway simultaneously, leading to **collisions**, **data loss**, **retries**, and ultimately **drained batteries** — which is precisely what happens in a chaotic IoT deployment without proper MAC design.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology Table

| Term | Definition | Typical Value in IoT |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Duty Cycle** | Fraction of time a node's radio is active | < 1% (target) |
| **Superframe Duration (SD)** | Active period within one beacon interval | 15.36 ms – 245.76 ms |
| **Beacon Interval (BI)** | Time between two consecutive beacons | 15.36 ms – 251.65824 s |
| **Backoff Period** | Unit of random waiting time | 20 symbols (≈ 320 µs @ 2.4 GHz) |
| **CAP** | Contention Access Period | Variable (must accommodate ≥ 3 backoff periods) |
| **CFP** | Contention-Free Period | Protected GTS slots |
| **PAN ID** | Personal Area Network Identifier | 16-bit (0x0000 – 0xFFFE) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - 1 symbol duration at 2.4 GHz O-QPSK PHY = **16 µs**
> - 1 symbol duration at 868/915 MHz BPSK PHY = **20 µs**
> - A **Unit Backoff Period (UBP)** = **20 symbols** ≈ **320 µs**

---

## 1.4 Position of the MAC Layer in the IoT Protocol Stack

> [!NOTE]
> The MAC layer in IoT sits sandwiched between the **PHY layer** (below) and the **upper Data Link / Logical Link Control / Network** layer (above). It directly interfaces with the radio hardware.

```
+-----------------------------------+
|   Application Layer (CoAP, MQTT)  |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Transport Layer (UDP)           |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Network Layer (6LoWPAN, RPL)    |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Adaptation / LLC Layer          |
+-----------------------------------+
| >>>> MAC LAYER (IEEE 802.15.4) <<<<|  <-- THIS LAYER
+-----------------------------------+
|   PHY Layer (IEEE 802.15.4)       |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Radio Hardware (Transceiver)    |
+-----------------------------------+
```

---

## 1.5 Why the MAC Layer is the *Heart* of IoT Energy Efficiency

Battery replacement in deployed IoT nodes (e.g., in agricultural fields, structural health monitoring of bridges, or inside industrial machinery) is either **prohibitively expensive** or **physically impossible**. Therefore, the MAC layer's energy-management strategy often determines the **operational lifetime** of the entire IoT system — typically 1 to 10 years on a single coin-cell battery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three dominant sources of energy waste in an unoptimized MAC protocol are:
> 1. **Idle Listening** — Radio ON while no data is present (the *biggest* waste).
> 2. **Collision** — Retransmission overhead.
> 3. **Overhearing** — Receiving frames destined for other nodes.
> 4. **Control Packet Overhead** — Excessive beacons/ACKs.
> 5. **Protocol Complexity** — Complex computations waking the MCU unnecessarily.

A well-designed IoT MAC protocol (like IEEE 802.15.4 in beacon-enabled mode) tackles all five systematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of MAC Protocols for IoT

MAC protocols for WSN/IoT are broadly classified into three families. Understanding the taxonomy is essential for KTU long-answer questions.

### A. Contention-Based MAC Protocols
Nodes compete for the channel using random access. Simple, scalable, but prone to collisions.

- **CSMA / CSMA-CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance) — Used in **IEEE 802.15.4** and Wi-Fi (802.11).
- **Pure ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA** — Foundational, used in early RFID/RF systems.
- **S-MAC, B-MAC, X-MAC, ContikiMAC** — WSN-specific contention protocols with duty cycling.

### B. Schedule-Based (Contention-Free) MAC Protocols
Nodes transmit in pre-assigned time slots. No collisions, but requires strict synchronization.

- **TDMA-based protocols** — LEACH, PEGASIS, TRAMA, LMAC.
- **GTS in IEEE 802.15.4** — Localized TDMA within a superframe.
- **GSA (Global Schedule Algorithm)** — Centralized scheduling.

### C. Hybrid MAC Protocols
Combine contention and scheduling for best of both worlds.

- **IEEE 802.15.4 (beacon-enabled mode)** — Slotted CSMA/CA in CAP + TDMA-like GTS in CFP.
- **Z-MAC, IEEE 802.15.4e TSCH** — Time-Synchronized Channel Hopping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is pure CSMA/CD not used in IoT wireless MAC?"*
> **Answer:** CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) requires the node to *transmit and simultaneously listen* for collisions, which is **impossible in a wireless radio** (the transmitted signal swamps the receiver). Hence wireless IoT MACs use **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance) — they *avoid* collisions before they happen through random backoffs and acknowledgements.

---

## 2.2 IEEE 802.15.4 MAC — Operational Modes

IEEE 802.15.4 supports **two operational modes** of channel access:

| Mode | Synchronization | Access Mechanism | Energy Profile |
|------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Beacon-Enabled Mode** | Yes (coordinator broadcasts beacons) | **Slotted** CSMA/CA in CAP + GTS in CFP | Highly energy-efficient (predictable sleep) |
| **Non-Beacon-Enabled Mode** | No (asynchronous) | **Unslotted** CSMA/CA | Simple, but requires always-on receiver or polling |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU answers, always explicitly state which mode you are discussing. The default for IoT deployments is the **beacon-enabled mode** because of its superior energy profile.

---

## 2.3 The Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled Mode)

The superframe is the *heart* of the beacon-enabled IEEE 802.15.4 MAC. It defines the active communication window and is bounded by two consecutive beacon transmissions from the **PAN coordinator**.

The superframe structure is controlled by two parameters:

- **macBeaconOrder (BO)** — Determines the **Beacon Interval (BI)**.
- **macSuperframeOrder (SO)** — Determines the **Superframe Duration (SD)**.

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Formulas

The standard requires $0 \le \text{SO} \le \text{BO} \le 14$.

$$
\text{BI} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{BO}}
$$

$$
\text{SD} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{SO}}
$$

where the **aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960 symbols** (equivalent to 15.36 ms at the 2.4 GHz O-QPSK PHY).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> One symbol period at 2.4 GHz = 16 µs, therefore:
> $$\text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} = 960 \times 16\,\mu s = 15.36\,ms$$
> The **active portion** of the superframe consists of **16 equally-sized time slots**, each of duration:
> $$\text{Slot Duration} = \frac{\text{SD}}{16} = 2^{\text{SO}} \times 60\,\text{symbols}$$

### 2.3.2 The Three Regions of the Superframe

1. **Beacon (Slot 0)** — Coordinator broadcasts beacon frame containing PAN ID, superframe spec, GTS list, and pending addresses.
2. **Contention Access Period (CAP)** — Slotted CSMA/CA used by regular nodes.
3. **Contention-Free Period (CFP)** — Up to **7 GTS slots** allocated by the coordinator to specific nodes for guaranteed bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constraint:** The CAP must be long enough to accommodate **at least 3 backoff periods** and one full inter-frame space. KTU students often forget this rule when solving superframe design problems.

The **inactive portion** of the superframe is the time the entire network may sleep:

$$
\text{Inactive Duration} = \text{BI} - \text{SD} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times (2^{\text{BO}} - 2^{\text{SO}})
$$

### 2.3.3 Duty Cycle Calculation

The duty cycle is the fraction of time the radio is *active*:

$$
\text{Duty Cycle (\%)} = \frac{\text{SD}}{\text{BI}} \times 100 = \frac{2^{\text{SO}}}{2^{\text{BO}}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2^{\text{BO} - \text{SO}}} \times 100
$$

This simple expression is **extremely high-yield** for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.4 CSMA/CA Algorithm — The Slotted Variant

The IEEE 802.15.4 slotted CSMA/CA algorithm in the CAP follows this precise sequence:

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. Initialize: **NB = 0** (Number of Backoff attempts), **CW = 2** (Contention Window size, in backoff periods), **BE = macMinBE** (min Backoff Exponent, default = 3).
2. Wait for the **next backoff slot boundary** (slotted behaviour).
3. Perform random backoff for $0$ to $(2^{\text{BE}} - 1)$ backoff periods.
4. Perform **CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)** twice (CW decrements from 2 to 0).
   - If channel is **busy** at both CCAs → increment NB, BE = min(BE+1, macMaxBE = 5), and if NB ≤ macMaxCSMABackoffs (= 4), go back to Step 2.
   - If NB > 4 → declare **channel access failure**, drop the frame, and notify the upper layer.
5. If channel is **idle** at both CCAs → transmit the data frame.
6. Wait for ACK from receiver. If ACK not received within macAckWaitDuration, increment retry counter; if retries > macMaxFrameRetries (= 3), drop the frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two CCAs (CW = 2)?** A single CCA may be misleading due to **hidden node** issues and channel fading. Two consecutive idle samples give a much higher probability that the channel is genuinely free. This is a *favourite* KTU conceptual question.

---

## 2.5 IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Frame Format

Every MAC Protocol Data Unit (MPDU) follows this structure:

| Field | Octets | Purpose |
|-------|--------|---------|
| Frame Control | 2 | Specifies frame type, addressing mode, security, etc. |
| Sequence Number | 1 | Used for ACK matching and de-duplication. |
| Destination PAN ID | 0 / 2 | PAN identifier of receiver (omitted if intra-PAN). |
| Destination Address | 0 / 2 / 8 | Short (16-bit) or extended (64-bit) address. |
| Source PAN ID | 0 / 2 | PAN identifier of sender. |
| Source Address | 0 / 2 / 8 | Source node address. |
| Auxiliary Security | 0 / 5 / 6 / 10 | Optional security header (AES-CCM-128). |
| Frame Payload | Variable | Upper-layer data (up to 127 bytes after encryption). |
| FCS | 2 | Frame Check Sequence (ITU-T CRC-16). |

> [!NOTE]
> The **maximum MAC payload** is 127 bytes (aMaxMACPayloadSize). After security overhead, the effective upper-layer payload drops to 81 bytes, which is why 6LoWPAN fragmentation is so important — addressed in the next module.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the consolidated cheat sheet. Memorize it for the ESE.**

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| Beacon Interval (BI) | $\text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{BO}}$ | symbols | $0 \le \text{BO} \le 14$ |
| Superframe Duration (SD) | $\text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{SO}}$ | symbols | $0 \le \text{SO} \le \text{BO} \le 14$ |
| Base Superframe Duration | **960** | symbols | = 15.36 ms at 2.4 GHz |
| Inactive Period | $\text{BI} - \text{SD}$ | symbols | Sleep duration |
| Duty Cycle | $\dfrac{2^{\text{SO}}}{2^{\text{BO}}} \times 100$ | percent | Equals $100 / 2^{\text{BO-SO}}$ |
| Time-Slot Duration | $2^{\text{SO}} \times 60$ | symbols | Total 16 slots in active period |
| One Backoff Period | 20 | symbols | = 320 µs @ 2.4 GHz |
| Min Backoff Exponent (BE) | 3 | unitless | Default macMinBE |
| Max BE | 5 | unitless | macMaxBE cap |
| Max CSMA Backoffs | 4 | attempts | macMaxCSMABackoffs |
| Max Frame Retries | 3 | attempts | macMaxFrameRetries |
| Contention Window (CW) | 2 | backoff periods | Two CCAs required |
| Max GTS | 7 | slots | Reserved within CFP |
| Max MAC Payload | 127 | octets | Pre-encryption |
| ACK wait duration | 54 | symbols | macAckWaitDuration |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MAC layer underpins every modern IoT technology stack:

- **Zigbee (802.15.4)** — Uses 802.15.4 MAC with mesh networking on top.
- **Thread (802.15.4)** — Uses 6LoWPAN + RPL over 802.15.4 MAC.
- **WirelessHART / ISA100.11a** — Industrial process automation, uses TDMA + channel hopping.
- **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** — Has its own MAC, uses frequency hopping.
- **LoRaWAN (Long Range WAN)** — Pure ALOHA-derived MAC (class A devices use ALOHA-style uplink).
- **NB-IoT / LTE-M (3GPP)** — Use scheduled cellular MAC.

A solid grasp of 802.15.4 MAC therefore gives a student fluency across **all** of the above technologies.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Computing Beacon Interval and Duty Cycle

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A Zigbee network operates in the **2.4 GHz O-QPSK PHY band**. The PAN coordinator is configured with $\text{BO} = 6$ and $\text{SO} = 4$. Compute:
(a) The **Beacon Interval (BI)** in milliseconds.
(b) The **Superframe Duration (SD)** in milliseconds.
(c) The **Duty Cycle**.
(d) The **Inactive Period** in milliseconds.
(e) The **Duration of one time slot** in microseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Data:**
> - One symbol period at 2.4 GHz = 16 µs
> - $\text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} = 960$ symbols

### Step 1 — Compute BI in symbols

Using the formula from Section 2.3.1:

$$
\text{BI} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{BO}}
$$

Substituting $\text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} = 960$ and $\text{BO} = 6$:

$$
\text{BI} = 960 \times 2^{6} = 960 \times 64 = 61{,}440\;\text{symbols}
$$

**Logic:** Each increment of BO doubles the beacon interval. Starting from 960 (BO=0), 6 doublings give $960 \times 2^6$.

### Step 2 — Convert BI to milliseconds

$$
\text{BI (ms)} = 61{,}440\;\text{symbols} \times 16\,\mu s/\text{symbol}
$$

$$
\text{BI} = 61{,}440 \times 16 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{ms} = 983{,}040 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BI} = 983.04\;\text{ms} \approx 0.983\;\text{s}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute SD in symbols

$$
\text{SD} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{SO}} = 960 \times 2^{4} = 960 \times 16
$$

$$
\text{SD} = 15{,}360\;\text{symbols}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert SD to milliseconds

$$
\text{SD (ms)} = 15{,}360 \times 16 \times 10^{-3} = 245{,}760 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SD} = 245.76\;\text{ms}}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Duty Cycle

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{\text{SD}}{\text{BI}} \times 100 = \frac{2^{\text{SO}}}{2^{\text{BO}}} \times 100 = \frac{2^{4}}{2^{6}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{16}{64} \times 100 = \frac{1}{4} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Duty Cycle} = 25\%}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Inactive Period

$$
\text{Inactive Period} = \text{BI} - \text{SD} = 983.04 - 245.76 = 737.28\;\text{ms}
$$

Alternatively, in symbols:

$$
\text{Inactive} = 61{,}440 - 15{,}360 = 46{,}080\;\text{symbols}
$$

Converting to ms: $46{,}080 \times 16\,\mu s = 737{,}280\,\mu s = 737.28\,ms$. ✓ Consistent.

### Step 7 — Compute One Time Slot Duration

The active period contains 16 equal slots:

$$
\text{Slot Duration} = \frac{\text{SD}}{16} = \frac{15{,}360}{16} = 960\;\text{symbols}
$$

Converting to microseconds:

$$
\text{Slot Duration} = 960 \times 16\,\mu s = 15{,}360\,\mu s = 15.36\;\text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{One Slot} = 15.36\;\text{ms}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** A 25% duty cycle means the radio is OFF for 75% of the time. If a node draws 20 mA when active and 1 µA when sleeping, the average current consumption is roughly:
$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.25 \times 20 + 0.75 \times 0.001 \approx 5.001\;\text{mA}
$$
This is a 4× reduction compared to an always-on radio, directly extending battery life by 4×.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — CSMA/CA Backoff Computation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A sensor node attempts to transmit a data frame using slotted CSMA/CA. The initial parameters are:
$\text{NB} = 0$, $\text{CW} = 2$, $\text{BE} = 3$ (macMinBE), and the maximum backoff is 4.
On its **first attempt**, the random backoff generator selects **5 backoff periods**. After waiting, the node performs two CCAs.
- The first CCA shows the channel **busy** (CW decrements to 1).
- The second CCA also shows the channel **busy** (CW decrements to 0, but busy).
Determine the action the node takes next and the new values of NB, BE, and CW.

### Step 1 — Identify Initial State
- NB = 0, BE = 3, CW = 2.

### Step 2 — Random Backoff
The node backs off for 5 backoff periods (1 backoff period = 20 symbols = 320 µs).

$$
T_{\text{backoff}} = 5 \times 320\,\mu s = 1{,}600\,\mu s = 1.6\;\text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 — First CCA
The node samples the channel. Channel is **busy**. Per the algorithm, on a busy CCA, CW decrements but the algorithm does **not** exit early — it only exits after CW = 0 *and* a busy result.

$$
\text{CW}_{\text{new}} = 2 - 1 = 1
$$

### Step 4 — Second CCA
Channel is again **busy**. Now CW has reached 0 with a busy result.

$$
\text{CW}_{\text{new}} = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \text{(Channel busy at CW = 0)}
$$

### Step 5 — Increment NB
Since both CCAs were busy, the node has lost this attempt.

$$
\text{NB}_{\text{new}} = 0 + 1 = 1
$$

### Step 6 — Update BE (Backoff Exponent)
BE is incremented, but capped at macMaxBE = 5:

$$
\text{BE}_{\text{new}} = \min(\text{BE} + 1, 5) = \min(3 + 1, 5) = 4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does BE increase?** A larger BE means a *wider* random backoff window, so the probability of choosing a long wait time increases — this reduces the chance that multiple nodes retry simultaneously after a busy channel.

### Step 7 — Reset CW
After a failed attempt, CW is reset to its initial value:

$$
\text{CW}_{\text{new}} = 2
$$

### Step 8 — Check Termination Condition
Check if NB > macMaxCSMABackoffs (which is 4):
- $\text{NB} = 1 \le 4$, so the algorithm **continues** by going back to Step 2 with the new random backoff range $[0, 2^{\text{BE}} - 1] = [0, 2^{4} - 1] = [0, 15]$.

### Final State Summary Table

| Parameter | Old Value | New Value |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| NB | 0 | 1 |
| BE | 3 | 4 |
| CW | 2 | 2 |
| Backoff Range | [0, 7] | [0, 15] |
| Decision | Channel busy | Retry on next slot boundary |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — GTS Allocation Analysis

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A PAN coordinator has allocated **3 GTS slots** in the CFP, each occupying **1 time slot** of the active superframe. The superframe has $\text{SO} = 3$ and the active period contains 16 slots. Compute:
(a) Total number of symbols in the CFP.
(b) The number of symbols remaining for CAP.
(c) The maximum number of remaining GTS that can still be allocated.

### Step 1 — SD in symbols
$$
\text{SD} = 960 \times 2^{3} = 960 \times 8 = 7{,}680\;\text{symbols}
$$

### Step 2 — Slot duration in symbols
$$
\text{Slot} = \frac{7{,}680}{16} = 480\;\text{symbols}
$$

### Step 3 — CFP in symbols (3 slots allocated)
$$
\text{CFP} = 3 \times 480 = 1{,}440\;\text{symbols}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CFP} = 1{,}440\;\text{symbols}}
$$

### Step 4 — CAP = SD − CFP − Beacon slot
The beacon occupies slot 0, which is 480 symbols. However, the CAP is logically the *beacon + remaining contention slots*:

$$
\text{CAP} = 16 \times 480 - 3 \times 480 = 7{,}680 - 1{,}440 = 6{,}240\;\text{symbols}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CAP} = 6{,}240\;\text{symbols}}
$$

### Step 5 — Remaining GTS slots
Maximum allowable GTS = 7. Already allocated = 3.

$$
\text{GTS}_{\text{remaining}} = 7 - 3 = 4\;\text{slots}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Remaining GTS} = 4\;\text{slots}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The CAP must always be ≥ 3 backoff periods to allow for retransmissions. A student who mechanically fills CFP with 7 GTS slots and leaves only 9 slots for CAP might inadvertently violate this constraint in extreme cases.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe Structure — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram renders the precise time-domain layout of the beacon-enabled superframe, including all key regions and the embedded GTS slots.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[BEACON INTERVAL BI starts] --> B[BEACON FRAME Slot 0]
    B --> C[CONTENTION ACCESS PERIOD CAP]
    C --> D{Slotted CSMA/CA Region}
    D --> E[Node 1 transmits in CAP]
    D --> F[Node 2 transmits in CAP]
    D --> G[Collisions handled by random backoff]
    C --> H[CONTENTION FREE PERIOD CFP]
    H --> I[GTS Slot 1 dedicated to Node A]
    H --> J[GTS Slot 2 dedicated to Node B]
    H --> K[Unused or reserved GTS slots]
    H --> L{Up to 7 GTS allowed}
    K --> M[INACTIVE PERIOD Network Sleeps]
    L --> M
    M --> N[BI ends Next BEACON]
    N --> O[Cycle repeats every 2 BO x aBaseSuperframeDuration symbols]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Move from top to bottom following the time arrow. The horizontal branch on the right (`Node 1`, `Node 2`, `Collisions`) represents the contention-based access within the CAP. The vertical branch on the right (`GTS Slot 1`, `GTS Slot 2`) shows the contention-free TDMA-like allocation in the CFP.

---

## 4.2 Slotted CSMA/CA Algorithm — State Machine Flowchart

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init: Initialize NB equals 0, BE equals macMinBE, CW equals 2
    Init --> WaitSlot: Wait for next slot boundary
    WaitSlot: Delay random backoff 0 to 2 BE minus 1 periods
    WaitSlot --> CCA1
    CCA1: First Clear Channel Assessment
    CCA1 --> CCA2: Channel is IDLE
    CCA1 --> BusyPath: Channel is BUSY
    CCA2: Second Clear Channel Assessment
    CCA2 --> TxFrame: Channel is IDLE
    CCA2 --> BusyPath: Channel is BUSY
    TxFrame: Transmit MAC data frame
    TxFrame --> WaitACK
    WaitACK: Wait for ACK timeout
    WaitACK --> Success: ACK received
    WaitACK --> Retry: No ACK
    Success --> [*]
    Retry --> IncRetry: Increment frame retry counter
    IncRetry --> Drop: If retries greater than 3 then DROP
    IncRetry --> WaitSlot: If retries less than or equal to 3 then RETRY
    Drop --> [*]
    BusyPath: CW decrements then NB increments BE increments capped at 5
    BusyPath --> CheckNB: If NB greater than 4 then FAIL
    BusyPath --> WaitSlot: If NB less than or equal to 4 then RETRY
    CheckNB --> [*]: Channel access failure
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answers:** The state machine has three possible exit outcomes:
> 1. **Success path** — Frame transmitted and acknowledged.
> 2. **Channel access failure** — Too many busy CCAs (NB > 4).
> 3. **Frame drop** — Too many ACK failures (> 3 retries).
> Always state all three when asked to "explain the CSMA/CA algorithm."

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — IEEE 802.15.4 MAC Sublayer Components

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PHYInterface[PHY Layer Interface]
        PD[PHY Driver]
        CCA[CCA Engine]
    end
    subgraph MACCore[MAC Sublayer Core]
        BECN[Beacon Generator]
        SYNC[Synchronization Manager]
        CSMA[CSMA/CA Engine]
        GTSM[GTS Manager]
        ADDR[Addressing Module]
        FRM[Frame Builder Parser]
        SEC[Security Engine AES]
        ACK[ACK Handler]
        BATT[Battery and Sleep Controller]
    end
    subgraph UpperInterface[Upper Layer Interface]
        MCPS[MCPS Service Access Point]
        MLME[MLME Service Access Point]
    end
    PD --> CCA
    CCA --> CSMA
    BECN --> SYNC
    SYNC --> CSMA
    CSMA --> GTSM
    GTSM --> FRM
    ADDR --> FRM
    SEC --> FRM
    FRM --> ACK
    FRM --> PD
    CSMA --> BATT
    ACK --> BATT
    MCPS --> FRM
    MLME --> SYNC
    MLME --> GTSM
    MLME --> BECN
    MLME --> BATT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **MCPS** (MAC Common Part Sublayer) and **MLME** (MAC Sublayer Management Entity) are the two SAPs defined by the IEEE 802.15.4 standard.
> - The **Battery and Sleep Controller** is the energy-management bridge — it receives directives from MLME and CSMA/CA to put the radio to sleep during the inactive period or to wake up at the next beacon.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Mode1[Beacon Enabled Mode]
