## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between IEEE 802.15.4e and IEEE 802.15.4g amendments in terms of the layer modified, key innovation, and target application.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | IEEE 802.15.4e | IEEE 802.15.4g |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer Modified** | MAC Layer only | PHY Layer only |
| **Key Innovation** | Time Synchronized Channel Hopping (TSCH) with deterministic access, DSME (Deterministic & Synchronous Multi-channel Extension), and LLDN (Low Latency Deterministic Network) modes | Smart Utility Networks (SUN) PHY supporting MR-FSK, MR-OFDM, and MR-O-QPSK modulations with extended channel pages and optional FEC |
| **Target Application** | Industrial automation, process control, factory floor wireless where deterministic latency is critical | Outdoor large-scale networks such as smart metering, smart grid, and AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) requiring long range |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model]**
**List the three device classes in LoRaWAN and explain the fundamental operational difference between them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Class A**: 
- Lowest power consumption class.
- End device initiates uplink; **two receive windows (RX1 and RX2)** open only **after** an uplink transmission.
- Downlink communication is possible only at these scheduled windows.
- Bidirectional but downlink is server-initiated response to uplink.

**Class B**: 
- Extends Class A by adding **scheduled downlink slots synchronized via gateway beacons**.
- End device opens extra receive windows (ping slots) at scheduled times.
- Lower latency downlinks than Class A but higher power consumption.

**Class C**: 
- Continuously listens on RX2 window (except when transmitting).
- Lowest downlink latency, highest power consumption.
- Typically used for **mains-powered** devices like actuators or streetlight controllers.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Time Synchronized Channel Hopping (TSCH)** mechanism of IEEE 802.15.4e in detail. Discuss the concept of **timeslots, slotframe, Absolute Slot Number (ASN), and channel hopping sequence** with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ZigBee protocol stack architecture** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **Coordinator, Router, and End Device**, and explain the function of the **APS sub-layer and ZDO**. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**TSCH Mechanism:**

TSCH merges two well-known techniques—**TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** and **FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)** with **channel hopping**—to achieve high reliability.

**Key Concepts (Step-by-Step Explanation):**

1. **Timeslot**: 
   - The smallest unit of time in TSCH, typically **10 ms, 15 ms, or 25 ms**.
   - During a timeslot, a node can transmit a frame and receive an acknowledgment.
   - All timeslots have the same duration; the value is configured per PAN.

2. **Slotframe**: 
   - A group of timeslots that repeats indefinitely.
   - Example: A slotframe of 101 timeslots at 10 ms gives a cycle of **1.01 seconds**.
   - Within one slotframe, all scheduled transmissions and receptions occur.

3. **Absolute Slot Number (ASN)**: 
   - A global counter that increments with each timeslot across the network's lifetime.
   - Initialized to 0 at network formation.
   - Used by all nodes to compute the channel for each scheduled transmission.

4. **Channel Hopping**: 
   - At each scheduled cell, the channel is computed as:
   
   $$channel = ((ASN + channelOffset) \bmod numChannels)$$
   
   - This ensures that **interference and multipath fading affect different frequencies** in successive slotframes, providing frequency diversity.

5. **Synchronization**: 
   - Nodes remain synchronized via **Enhanced Beacons (EBs)** sent by the PAN coordinator and other nodes.
   - Beacons contain the ASN and other IE (Information Element) data.

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
| Slot 0  | Slot 1  | Slot 2  | Slot 3  | ... | Slot 100 | Slot 0  | Slot 1  | ...
| Chan 5  | Chan 12 | Chan 3  | Chan 18 | ... | Chan 27  | Chan 8  | Chan 15 | ...
| TX NodeA| RX NodeB| TX NodeB| RX NodeA| ... | TX NodeC | TX NodeA| RX NodeB| ...
```

**Advantages:**
- 99.999 % reliability achievable.
- Bounded latency for real-time control.
- Energy efficiency (no idle listening).

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**ZigBee Protocol Stack:**

1. **Coordinator (FFD)**: 
   - Exactly **one per ZigBee network**.
   - Forms the network by selecting channel, PAN ID.
   - Acts as **Trust Center** for security key management.
   - Stores all routing and binding tables.
   - Cannot be put to sleep.

2. **Router (FFD)**: 
   - Relays packets in mesh networks.
   - Can accept child End Devices or Routers.
   - Participates in routing using **AODV (Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector)** or **Tree routing**.
   - Always powered (mains).

3. **End Device (RFD)**: 
   - Battery-powered, low duty cycle.
   - Communicates only with its parent.
   - Cannot route packets; reduces memory/power requirements.

**APS Sub-Layer Functions:**
- **Binding**: Maps source endpoint to destination endpoint (decouples applications from addresses).
- **Fragmentation/Reassembly**: Splits long messages.
- **Reliable Transport**: Optional ACK-based delivery.
- **Address Mapping**: 64-bit IEEE → 16-bit network.
- **Group Addressing**: Multicast within a group.

**ZDO Functions:**
- **Device Discovery**: Finds other devices on the PAN.
- **Service Discovery**: Finds application services offered.
- **Network Management**: Joining, leaving, resetting.
- **Security Management**: Cooperates with Trust Center.
- **Binding Management**: Creates and clears binding tables.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain **LoRaWAN architecture** in detail with a block diagram. Discuss the roles of **End Device, Gateway, Network Server, and Application Server**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Modbus**? Explain the **Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP** frame structures with neat diagrams. List the four primary register types in Modbus. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**LoRaWAN Architecture (Star-of-Stars):**

**1. End Device (Node)**:
- Sensor/actuator with LoRa radio.
- Communicates **wirelessly** with one or more gateways.
- Identified by a **DevAddr (32-bit)** after activation.
- Sends uplinks opportunistically; receives downlinks in scheduled windows.
- Supports OTAA (Over-The-Air Activation) using DevEUI, AppEUI, AppKey.

**2. Gateway (Concentrator)**:
- Acts as a **transparent bridge** between LoRa radio and IP network.
- Multi-channel concentrator (typically **8 channels**, can receive on all simultaneously).
- Uses **Semtech SX1301/SX1302** baseband processor.
- Forwards raw LoRa frames to Network Server via **Packet Forwarder protocol (UDP)**.
- No MAC-layer intelligence—purely a relay.

**3. Network Server (NS)**:
- The "brain" of the LoRaWAN network.
- Responsibilities:
  - **MAC Command Processing**.
  - **Adaptive Data Rate (ADR)** algorithm.
  - **Deduplication** (same frame received by multiple gateways).
  - **Frame filtering, acknowledgments, retransmission scheduling**.
  - **Security**: NwkSKey, AppSKey validation.
  - **Downlink scheduling** based on device class and duty cycle.

**4. Application Server (AS)**:
- Receives decrypted application payload.
- Provides business logic, data storage, dashboards.
- Communicates with NS via **APIs (typically HTTP/HTTPS or MQTT)**.

**5. Join Server (JS)**:
- Stores root keys (AppKey for OTAA).
- Derives NwkSKey and AppSKey during join procedure.
- Cooperates with NS via **Join Procedure API**.

**Diagram (Block Representation):**
```
[End Devices] <--LoRa--> [Gateways] <--IP--> [Network Server] <--API--> [Application Server]
                                      ^
                                      |
                                  [Join Server]
```

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Modbus Definition**:
Modbus is an open, **client-server (master-slave)** application-layer messaging protocol developed by Modicon (1979) for industrial automation over serial lines and TCP/IP.

**Modbus RTU Frame Structure** (Binary mode over RS-485):
```
| Slave ID | Function Code | Data (N bytes) | CRC-16 (2 bytes) |
|   1 byte |    1 byte     |  0-252 bytes   |     2 bytes      |
```
- **Slave ID**: 1–247 (0 = broadcast).
- **Function Code**: 0x01 (Read Coils), 0x03 (Read Holding Registers), etc.
- **CRC-16**: Cyclic Redundancy Check, polynomial 0xA001.

**Modbus TCP Frame Structure** (MBAP Header + PDU):
```
| Tx ID | Proto ID | Length | Unit ID | Function Code | Data |
| 2 B   | 2 B (0)  | 2 B    | 1 B     | 1 B           | N B  |
```
- **Transaction ID**: Matching request/response.
- **Protocol ID**: Always 0x0000 for Modbus.
- **Length**: Number of bytes following (Unit ID + FC + Data).
- **Unit ID**: Maps to Slave ID (for serial gateway).

**Four Primary Register Types**:

| Register Type | Access | Size | Function Code |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coils** | Read/Write | 1 bit | 0x01, 0x05, 0x0F |
| **Discrete Inputs** | Read-Only | 1 bit | 0x02 |
| **Holding Registers** | Read/Write | 16 bits | 0x03, 0x06, 0x10 |
| **Input Registers** | Read-Only | 16 bits | 0x04 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Don't confuse Modbus RTU with Modbus ASCII**—RTU is binary, ASCII is human-readable hex.
> 2. **Always include the Unit ID** in Modbus TCP—it is the bridge for serial slaves.
> 3. **In ZigBee questions, students often forget the Trust Center's role**—it is the Coordinator by default, holds the network key, and authenticates joining devices.
> 4. **For LoRaWAN**, students often write that gateways decrypt the data—**gateways do NOT decrypt**; the Network Server does. This is a 1-mark loss in long answers.
> 5. **802.15.4e vs 4g confusion**—remember: **e = enhancement (MAC)**, **g = utility (PHY for grids)**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

#### **IEEE 802.15.4 Family**
- [ ] Base 802.15.4 = PHY + MAC for LR-WPAN.
- [ ] **802.15.4e** = MAC only → **TSCH**, DSME, LLDN for industrial.
- [ ] **802.15.4g** = PHY only → **SUN PHY**, MR-FSK/OFDM/O-QPSK, up to **1 Mbps**, long range.
- [ ] Channel hopping formula: $ch = (ASN + chOff) \bmod numCh$.
- [ ] Enhanced Beacons carry ASN and IEs.

#### **Modbus Essentials**
- [ ] Master-slave, originally RS-485, now **Modbus TCP on port 502**.
- [ ] RTU framing: **silence ≥ 3.5 char**, inter-char gap ≤ 1.5 char.
- [ ] MBAP header = Transaction ID + Protocol ID (0) + Length + Unit ID.
- [ ] **4 register types**: Coils, Discrete Inputs, Holding Registers, Input Registers.
- [ ] Public function codes: 01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 15, 16, 23.
- [ ] Exception codes start with **0x80 | FC**.

#### **ZigBee Architecture**
- [ ] Layered stack: PHY + MAC (802.15.4) → NWK → APS + ZDO → Application.
- [ ] **3 device types**: Coordinator (1), Router (FFD), End Device (RFD).
- [ ] Max nodes = **65,530**; max depth = 30 hops; max children = 254.
- [ ] Cskip algorithm for distributed address allocation.
- [ ] APS handles binding, fragmentation, group addressing.
- [ ] ZDO handles discovery, security, network management.
- [ ] Coordinator = Trust Center by default.
- [ ] Profile examples: HA, SE, BA, ZLL, HC, RC.

#### **LoRaWAN**
- [ ] LoRa = proprietary CSS PHY by Semtech; LoRaWAN = open MAC by LoRa Alliance.
- [ ] **3 classes**: A (lowest power, RX after TX), B (beacon-scheduled), C (continuous RX).
- [ ] Star-of-stars topology; gateways are **transparent**.
- [ ] **Network Server** is the brain: ADR, deduplication, security.
- [ ] Spreading Factor **SF 7–12**; BW 125/250/500 kHz; CR 4/5–4/8.
- [ ] $T_{sym} = 2^{SF}/BW$.
- [ ] $R_b = SF \cdot BW \cdot CR / 2^{SF}$.
- [ ] Higher SF → +3 dB sensitivity, +range, +time-on-air.
- [ ] Activation: **OTAA** (recommended) or **ABP**.
- [ ] **EU868 duty cycle**: 1 % (or 0.1 % depending on sub-band).
- [ ] Security: AES-128 (NwkSKey, AppSKey).
- [ ] **LoRaWAN versions**: 1.0, 1.0.2, 1.0.3, 1.0.4, 1.1 (current).

#### **Standardization & Alliances**
- [ ] **IEEE 802.15 WG** → 802.15.4 family.
- [ ] **CSA (Connectivity Standards Alliance)** → ZigBee, Matter, Thread.
- [ ] **LoRa Alliance** → LoRaWAN specification.
- [ ] **IETF** → 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP (Internet protocols for IoT).
- [ ] **ISA** → WirelessHART, ISA100.11a.
- [ ] **Modbus Organization** → Modbus RTU/TCP/Security.
- [ ] **OneM2M** → Horizontal service-layer IoT standard.
- [ ] **ETSI** → Spectrum and LPWAN regulations.
- [ ] **OCF** → Open Connectivity Foundation (now merged with CSA).

#### **Key Numerical Values to Memorize**
- [ ] ZigBee max nodes: **65,530**.
- [ ] LoRa max payload at SF7: **222 bytes**; at SF12: **51 bytes**.
- [ ] Modbus TCP port: **502**.
- [ ] ZigBee 2.4 GHz data rate: **250 kbps**.
- [ ] LoRaWAN SF sensitivity at SF12/BW125: **-137 dBm** (approx).
- [ ] TSCH typical timeslot: **10–25 ms**.
- [ ] Modbus RTU max slaves: **247**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Physical Layer (PHY)** is the **lowest layer (Layer 1)** of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP protocol stack**, responsible for the **transmission and reception of raw bit streams** over a physical communication medium. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, the Physical Layer governs the **electromagnetic, optical, or acoustic signaling mechanisms** that allow resource-constrained edge devices (sensors, actuators, RFID tags) to exchange data with gateways, edge nodes, and the cloud.

According to the **IEEE 802 Standard** family and **ITU-T recommendations**, the Physical Layer encapsulates the following sub-functions:

1. **Bit Synchronization** – Maintaining a shared clock reference between transmitter and receiver.
2. **Bit Rate Control** – Defining the number of bits transmitted per second (bps).
3. **Physical Topology** – Specifying how devices are physically arranged (bus, star, mesh, ring).
4. **Transmission Mode** – Determining directionality (simplex, half-duplex, full-duplex).
5. **Signal Modulation & Encoding** – Translating digital bits into physical signals (analog waveforms, light pulses, RF waves).
6. **Multiplexing / Multiple Access** – Allowing multiple devices to share a common medium.
7. **Physical Medium Attachment** – Mechanical and electrical interface to cables, antennas, fibers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Physical Layer in IoT is **not limited to wired Ethernet**. It explicitly encompasses **short-range wireless (BLE, Zigbee, LoRa), cellular (NB-IoT, LTE-M, 5G), and low-power WAN protocols**, all of which define their own PHY specifications (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4 PHY, LoRaWAN regional PHY).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you and your friend want to exchange a written message across a noisy construction site:

- The **paper and pen** = the *physical medium* (air, copper wire, fiber optic strand).
- The **language you write in** = the *modulation/line coding scheme* (how 0s and 1s are represented as electrical pulses, light flashes, or radio wave variations).
- The **shouting/whispering** = *transmission power and signal strength*.
- The **earplugs everyone else is wearing** = *noise and interference* from other simultaneous conversations.
- A **translator** who ensures the recipient reads the same message = the *modem* (modulator-demodulator) sitting at the Physical Layer.

> **Plain English Summary:** The Physical Layer is the *plumbing and electrical/electrical-engineering reality* of IoT communication. It does not understand packets, IP addresses, or application data — it only knows how to push electrical, optical, or RF energy from Point A to Point B in a way that the receiver can *reliably* reconstruct the original bits.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Layer Metrics (KTU Must-Knows)

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU question in this module is anchored on the following **bold** physical-layer metrics. Memorize their units and definitions.

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Rate** | $R_b$ | **bits per second (bps)** | Number of bits transmitted per second |
| **Baud Rate** | $R_s$ | **symbols/second (Bd)** | Number of signal changes (symbols) per second |
| **Bandwidth** | $B$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | Range of frequencies the channel can carry |
| **Signal Power** | $S$ | **Watts (W)** or **dBm** | Average power of the transmitted signal |
| **Noise Power** | $N$ | **Watts (W)** | Power of additive noise in the channel |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | SNR | **dimensionless (dB)** | Ratio $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ |
| **Bit Error Rate** | BER | **dimensionless** | Probability of a bit being received in error |
| **Propagation Delay** | $t_p$ | **seconds (s)** | Time for a signal to traverse the medium |
| **Attenuation** | $\alpha$ | **dB/m or dB/km** | Loss of signal power per unit length |
| **Wavelength** | $\lambda$ | **meters (m)** | Distance of one signal cycle: $\lambda = c/f$ |

> **Universal Constant Used in PHY:**
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - **Speed of light in fiber:** $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (refractive index $\approx 1.5$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Responsibilities of the Physical Layer

The Physical Layer performs **four fundamental operations** that gate all higher-layer functionality:

### A. Signal Generation & Modulation
Modulation is the process of **varying one or more properties of a periodic carrier waveform** (amplitude, frequency, phase) with a modulating signal (the data). This is essential because:
- **Baseband signals** (raw 0/1 pulses) often contain **low-frequency components** that cannot propagate efficiently over wireless or long copper channels.
- Modulation **shifts the signal spectrum** into a band suited for the medium.
- Modulation allows **multiple signals to coexist** via Frequency Division Multiplexing.

The general carrier signal is:
$$s(t) = A(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi(t))$$

- $A(t)$ → Amplitude → manipulated in **ASK, QAM**
- $f_c$ → Carrier frequency → manipulated in **FSK**
- $\phi(t)$ → Phase → manipulated in **PSK, BPSK, QPSK**

### B. Line Coding (Baseband Transmission)
Used when the channel is DC-coupled (e.g., wired Ethernet, UART). Maps bits to voltage levels.

| Code | Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **NRZ-L** | 0 = High, 1 = Low (or vice versa) | Simple but no clock recovery |
| **NRZ-I** | 1 = transition, 0 = no transition | Better DC balance |
| **Manchester** | 0 = High-to-Low, 1 = Low-to-High (per bit) | **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet**, self-clocking |
| **Differential Manchester** | Always transition mid-bit; 0 = transition at start | Token Ring |
| **AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion)** | 1 alternates +V / -V, 0 = 0V | T1 telecom |

### C. Channel Access / Multiple Access (IoT-Critical)
Multiple IoT nodes must share the same radio medium. The three principal schemes:

1. **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access):** Each node gets a dedicated time slot. Used in **cellular systems (LTE, 5G)**.
2. **FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access):** Each node gets a dedicated frequency band. Used in **analog cellular, satellite**.
3. **CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access):** Each node uses a unique orthogonal spreading code. Used in **3G, GPS**.
4. **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance):** Nodes listen before talking. Used in **Wi-Fi (802.11) and IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee/Thread)**.

### D. Signal Propagation & Channel Impairments
- **Attenuation:** $P_{rx} = P_{tx} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ (Friis free-space equation)
- **Path Loss:** $L = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (dB, $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz)
- **Multipath Fading:** Reflections cause constructive/destructive interference.
- **Noise:** Thermal noise floor = $N = kTB$, where $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every numerical in the KTU ESE pulls from here.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_b = R_s \times \log_2(M)$ | Bit rate = symbol rate × bits per symbol | bps; $M$ = signal constellation size |
| 2 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** — max error-free capacity | bps, $B$ in Hz |
| 3 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | W; $k = 1.38\times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |
| 5 | $L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss | dB; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 6 | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | s; $v$ = signal velocity |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength | m |
| 8 | $A = \alpha \cdot d$ | Total attenuation in dB | dB; $\alpha$ in dB/m |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{rx}}{P_{tx}}$ (linear) or $\eta_{dB}$ | Channel efficiency | dimensionless or dB |
| 10 | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{S/R_b}{N/B} = \dfrac{S}{N} \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | Energy per bit / noise spectral density | dimensionless (dB) |

> **Boundary Conditions / Domain Restrictions:**
> - $B > 0$ (bandwidth is strictly positive).
> - $M \ge 2$ (constellation size at least 2 for binary).
> - Shannon capacity $C$ is an **upper bound** — it is *achievable* only with optimal coding (e.g., turbo codes, LDPC).
> - Path loss equation valid for $d > $ Fresnel radius (far-field region).

---

## 2.3 Why These Concepts Matter in IoT Engineering

- **Energy Efficiency:** Modulation choice (e.g., LoRa's Chirp Spread Spectrum vs. FSK in Zigbee) directly determines battery life of sensor nodes. A 14-mark KTU question often asks you to *justify* modulation choice for an IoT scenario.
- **Range vs. Data Rate Trade-off:** Shannon's theorem implies a hard ceiling — to double range, you must accept lower data rate (or use more bandwidth).
- **Spectrum Regulation:** IoT PHY operates in unlicensed ISM bands (**433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz**) which have **duty cycle regulations** enforced by national regulators (in India: **National Frequency Allocation Plan**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bit Rate to Bandwidth (Nyquist & Shannon)

### Step 1 — Nyquist Criterion (Noiseless Channel)
For a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz using $M$ discrete signal levels (M-ary signaling), the **maximum data rate** is:

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2(M) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- A band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ can be represented by **2B independent samples per second** (Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem).
- Each sample can be encoded into one of $M$ symbols, carrying $\log_2(M)$ bits.
- Multiplying yields the formula above.

> **Worked Example (KTU-style):** A noiseless channel has $B = 3$ kHz and uses 8 signal levels ($M = 8$).
> Find the maximum bit rate.
>
> $$C = 2 \times 3000 \times \log_2(8) = 6000 \times 3 = 18{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

### Step 2 — Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel)
In a real channel with additive white Gaussian noise, the **maximum error-free capacity** is:

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

**Derivation Logic:**
- Starts from entropy of a Gaussian-distributed noise channel.
- The signal power $S$ carries information; the noise power $N$ corrupts it.
- Information-theoretic limit: maximum distinguishable signal levels $M \approx \sqrt{1 + S/N}$ per dimension.
- Two-dimensional signal space (IQ) gives the $\log_2$ capacity with $1 + S/N$.

> **Worked Example (KTU-style):** A Wi-Fi channel has $B = 20$ MHz and $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB.
> Compute (a) S/N ratio, (b) Shannon capacity, (c) spectral efficiency.
>
> **Step (a):** Convert SNR from dB to linear:
> $$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$
>
> **Step (b):** Apply Shannon:
> $$C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1001) = 20 \times 10^6 \times 9.967 \approx 199.3 \text{ Mbps}$$
>
> **Step (c):** Spectral efficiency:
> $$\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2(1001) \approx 9.97 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

### Step 3 — Relationship Between Bit Energy and SNR
For digital systems, the energy per bit $E_b$ is related to signal power and bit rate by:
$$E_b = \frac{S}{R_b} \quad \text{(Joules/bit)}$$

Noise spectral density $N_0$ is:
$$N_0 = \frac{N}{B} \quad \text{(W/Hz)}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b}$$

This metric is **fundamental in KTU questions about BER performance** of BPSK, QPSK, etc. The well-known BPSK BER formula is:
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$
where $Q(x)$ is the Gaussian Q-function.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Modulation vs. IoT Application

| Modulation | Spectral Efficiency (bps/Hz) | Robustness to Noise | Power Efficiency | KTU-Mentioned IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ASK / OOK** | Low (0.5–1) | Poor | Low | Simple RFID, IR remotes |
| **BFSK** | Low (≤1) | Good | Medium | RKE (Remote Keyless Entry), legacy RFID |
| **BPSK** | 1 | High | High | **LoRa, satellite IoT, NB-IoT downlink** |
| **QPSK** | 2 | High | High | Zigbee, BLE, LTE IoT |
| **16-QAM** | 4 | Medium | Medium | Wi-Fi, high-throughput IoT gateways |
| **64-QAM** | 6 | Low | Low | 5G eMBB (rare for LPWAN) |
| **OFDM** | Up to 6+ | Excellent (with FEC) | Medium | Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, NB-IoT |
| **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** | < 0.5 | Very high | Very high | **LoRa, long-range IoT** |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical: Path Loss & Received Power

**Problem (KTU 2024 style, 7-mark subpart):**
A LoRa gateway transmits at $P_{tx} = 14$ dBm at frequency $f = 868$ MHz. The sensor node is at distance $d = 5$ km. Assume free-space propagation, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi.
Calculate (a) free-space path loss in dB, (b) received power in dBm.

**Step (a) — Path Loss:**
Using the free-space path loss formula with $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz:
$$L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(868) + 32.44$$

Compute each term:
$$20 \log_{10}(5) = 20 \times 0.6990 = 13.98 \text{ dB}$$
$$20 \log_{10}(868) = 20 \times 2.9385 = 58.77 \text{ dB}$$
$$L_{dB} = 13.98 + 58.77 + 32.44 = 105.19 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (b) — Received Power:**
$$P_{rx,\,dBm} = P_{tx,\,dBm} + G_t + G_r - L_{dB}$$
$$P_{rx,\,dBm} = 14 + 2 + 2 - 105.19 = -87.19 \text{ dBm}$$

> **Reality check:** LoRa receivers operate at sensitivities of −120 dBm to −137 dBm (depending on spreading factor). So the link has ~30 dB of margin — a healthy LoRa link.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the IoT Physical Layer

The Mermaid diagram below shows the **end-to-end functional flow** of bits traversing the Physical Layer in a typical IoT sensor-to-cloud path, including the modulation, channel, and demodulation stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["Application Layer Payload e.g. temperature reading 25.5 C"]
    dataLink["Data Link Layer Framing and MAC Addressing"]
    phyTx["Physical Layer Transmitter Side"]
    bitStream["Serial Bit Stream 0 1 0 1 1 0 0 1"]
    lineCodec["Line Coding Stage NRZ Manchester 8B10B"]
    modulator["Modulation Stage ASK FSK PSK QAM OFDM CSS for LoRa"]
    rfFrontend["RF Front End DAC Power Amplifier Antenna"]
    channel["Wireless Channel Attenuation Noise Multipath Fading"]
    rfRx["RF Front End LNA Bandpass Filter ADC"]
    demod["Demodulation Stage Carrier Recovery Symbol Detection"]
    clockRec["Clock and Bit Synchronization PLL"]
    decoded["Recovered Bit Stream Passed to Data Link Layer"]
    cloud["Gateway and Cloud Server"]

    appLayer --> dataLink
    dataLink --> phyTx
    phyTx --> bitStream
    bitStream --> lineCodec
    lineCodec --> modulator
    modulator --> rfFrontend
    rfFrontend --> channel
    channel --> rfRx
    rfRx --> demod
    demod --> clockRec
    clockRec --> decoded
    decoded --> dataLink
    dataLink --> cloud
```

## 4.2 Modulation Taxonomy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mod["Modulation Techniques for IoT Physical Layer"]
    passband["Passband Modulation"]
    baseband["Baseband Line Coding"]
    analogMod["Analog Passband AM FM PM"]
    digitalMod["Digital Passband"]
    ask["ASK OOK On Off Keying"]
    fsk["FSK BFSK GFSK used in BLE and Zigbee"]
    psk["PSK BPSK QPSK used in LoRa and LTE"]
    qam["QAM 16 64 256 used in WiFi and 5G"]
    spreadSpec["Spread Spectrum DSSS FHSS CSS"]
    lora["CSS Chirp Spread Spectrum LoRa LPWAN"]
    nrz["NRZ L and NRZ I"]
    man["Manchester IEEE 802.3"]
    diffM["Differential Manchester"]
    ami["AMI Alternate Mark Inversion"]

    mod --> passband
    mod --> baseband
    passband --> analogMod
    passband --> digitalMod
    digitalMod --> ask
    digitalMod --> fsk
    digitalMod --> psk
    digitalMod --> qam
    digitalMod --> spreadSpec
    spreadSpec --> lora
    baseband --> nrz
    baseband --> man
    baseband --> diffM
    baseband --> ami
```

## 4.3 IoT Wireless Spectrum Allocation (KTU-Important Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spectrum["Global IoT Spectrum Map"]
    subGHz["Sub GHz Bands Low Power Long Range"]
    mhz24["2.4 GHz Band Universal ISM"]
    fiveG["Cellular Licensed Bands 4G 5G NB IoT LTE M"]
    lora1["433 MHz EU and Asia LoRa"]
    lora2["868 MHz Europe LoRa Sigfox"]
    lora3["915 MHz USA and Australia LoRa"]
    zigB["Zigbee Thread 2.4 GHz"]
    ble1["BLE 2.4 GHz"]
    wifi1["WiFi 802.11 b g n 2.4 and 5 GHz"]
    nb1["NB IoT LTE M Sub 1 GHz licensed cellular"]
    fiveG1["5G NR FR1 Sub 6 GHz and FR2 mmWave"]

    spectrum --> subGHz
    spectrum --> mhz24
    spectrum --> fiveG
    subGHz --> lora1
    subGHz --> lora2
    subGHz --> lora3
    mhz24 --> zigB
    mhz24 --> ble1
    mhz24 --> wifi1
    fiveG --> nb1
    fiveG --> fiveG1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:** Note that the **Physical Layer in IoT is almost always a radio-frequency (RF) layer**, not wired Ethernet. This is what distinguishes IoT PHY from classical networking PHY taught in CS courses.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]`
**Distinguish between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex transmission modes with one real-world IoT example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Direction | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One-way only; receiver cannot talk back | **AM radio broadcast tower** to car radios (no return path). In IoT: a **one-way RFID tag** transmitting its ID to a reader. |
| **Half-Duplex** | Two-way, but only one direction at a time | **Walkie-talkie**. In IoT: a **LoRa sensor node** that transmits a packet, then waits for an acknowledgement window. |
| **Full-Duplex** | Simultaneous two-way | **Telephone call**. In IoT: a **5G smartphone** simultaneously downloading a firmware update while uploading telemetry. |

> Award 1 mark per correct pairing of mode + example, 1 mark for the distinction table.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Shannon-Hartley theorem. What is its significance in designing an IoT link budget?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Shannon-Hartley theorem states that the **maximum achievable error-free data rate (capacity $C$)** over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal power $S$ and noise power $N$ is:

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bps}$$

**Significance in IoT (2 marks):**
1. Sets the **theoretical upper bound** on data rate — no modulation/coding scheme can exceed it. Engineers use it to evaluate how close a real system (e.g., LoRa, NB-IoT) is operating to the Shannon limit.
2. Quantifies the **bandwidth-power trade-off**: for a fixed capacity, doubling $B$ halves the required $S/N$ (and vice versa). This is critical for **battery-powered IoT** nodes, where minimizing transmit power is paramount.
3. Guides the choice of **modulation order $M$** and **error-correcting codes** to approach capacity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (either-or). Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solutions must show full working.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU 2024 Model Paper, Module 3, Set 1]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the Physical Layer in an IoT transmitter-receiver chain**. List the sub-functions performed by the PHY. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Physical Layer in an IoT system is responsible for converting a **digital bitstream** into a **physical signal** (electromagnetic wave, light pulse, electrical pulse) suitable for the channel, and back again at the receiver.

**Block Diagram (Description — 3 marks):**

```
[App Payload] → [Data Link] → [Bit Stream] → [Line Coding] → [Modulation] 
              → [RF Front End: DAC + PA + Antenna] → [Channel] 
              → [RF Front End: Antenna + LNA + ADC] → [Demodulation] 
              → [Clock Recovery] → [Recovered Bits] → [Data Link]
```

**Sub-functions (4 marks — 1 mark each for any 4):**

1. **Bit Synchronization** — provides clock edges so that receiver samples bits at the right instant (PLL-based recovery).
2. **Physical Medium Selection** — wired (Ethernet, RS-485), wireless (RF), or optical (Li-Fi, IR).
3. **Line Coding / Signal Generation** — NRZ, Manchester, AMI for baseband.
4. **Modulation** — ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM for passband; CSS for LoRa.
5. **Multiplexing / Multiple Access** — TDMA, FDMA, CDMA, CSMA/CA.
6. **Transmit Power Control & Link Budget Management** — sets output power, antenna gain, BER target.
7. **Channel Sensing & Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)** — required in CSMA systems (802.15.4, 802.11).

---

**Q. (b)** An IoT gateway transmits at **20 dBm** over a channel with **bandwidth 250 kHz**. The measured SNR at the receiver is **15 dB**.
Compute:
(i) Linear SNR ratio
(ii) Shannon capacity
(iii) Spectral efficiency
**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Linear SNR (1 mark):**
$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} = 31.62$$

**(ii) Shannon Capacity (3 marks):**
$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$
$$C = 250{,}000 \times \log_2(32.62)$$
$$C = 250{,}000 \times 5.027$$
$$C \approx 1.257 \text{ Mbps}$$

**(iii) Spectral Efficiency (3 marks):**
$$\eta = \frac{C}{B} = \log_2(32.62) \approx 5.027 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

> **Conclusion (bonus 1 mark):** A spectral efficiency of ~5 bps/Hz indicates a moderately efficient link. In practice, IoT systems like LoRa operate at 0.25–5 bps/Hz depending on spreading factor, which is intentionally low to maximize receiver sensitivity and range.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU 2024 Model Paper, Set 2]**

**Q. (a)** Compare the following **digital modulation schemes** suitable for IoT: **BFSK, BPSK, QPSK, and 16-QAM**. Tabulate the comparison on the basis of **bits per symbol, spectral efficiency, noise robustness, and typical IoT application**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Scheme | Bits/Symbol | Spectral Efficiency (bps/Hz) | Noise Robustness | IoT Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BFSK** | 1 | ≤ 1 | High (large Euclidean distance) | BLE, legacy 433 MHz sensors |
| **BPSK** | 1 | 1 | Very high | LoRa, NB-IoT downlink, satellite IoT |
| **QPSK** | 2 | 2 | High | Zigbee (802.15.4), LTE IoT, BLE |
| **16-QAM** | 4 | 4 | Medium (smaller Euclidean distance) | Wi-Fi IoT gateways, video surveillance |

**Trade-off (1 mark):** Higher-order modulation (16-QAM) packs more bits per symbol, increasing data rate, **but reduces minimum distance between constellation points**, making it more susceptible to noise. IoT LPWANs prefer **BPSK/QPSK** because of their superior link margin and battery life.

---

**Q. (b)** A wireless IoT sensor operates at **2.4 GHz** and the receiver is located **100 meters** away in free space. The transmit power is **10 mW** and the antenna gains are unity.
Calculate:
(i) Transmit power in dBm
(ii) Free-space path loss
(iii) Received power
**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) P_tx in dBm (1 mark):**
$$P_{tx,\,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10 \text{ mW}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right) = 10 \text{ dBm}$$

**(ii) Path Loss (3 marks):**
Convert to SI units: $d = 100$ m = $0.1$ km, $f = 2400$ MHz.
$$L_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.1) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) + 32.44$$
$$= 20 \times (-1) + 20 \times 3.3802 + 32.44$$
$$= -20 + 67.60 + 32.44 = 80.04 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Received Power (3 marks):**
$$P_{rx,\,dBm} = P_{tx,\,dBm} + G_t + G_r - L_{dB}$$
$$= 10 + 0 + 0 - 80.04 = -70.04 \text{ dBm}$$

> **Reality check:** BLE receivers at 2.4 GHz have a typical sensitivity of about **−90 dBm to −97 dBm**. Hence the link has a healthy **~20 dB margin** — sufficient for reliable indoor operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose NO Marks Here:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting to convert dBm ↔ mW (use $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{mW})$). A single mistake here loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 32.44** in path loss formula — it is for km and MHz units. If using meters and Hz, the constant is **−147.55**. Examiners watch for this.
> 3. **Shannon vs. Nyquist confusion** — Shannon is for *noisy* channels (uses SNR); Nyquist is for *noiseless* channels (uses $M$). Mixing them = 0 marks.
> 4. **Mistaking baud for bps** — they are equal *only* for binary (BPSK, BFSK). For QPSK, baud = bps / 2.
> 5. **Spectral efficiency unit** — must be **bps/Hz**, not bps. Examiners deduct 1 mark for wrong units.
> 6. **Not drawing block diagram** in 7-mark subparts where asked — even a hand-drawn rough sketch earns you 1–2 marks under KTU's "neat sketch" clause.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **Physical Layer = Layer 1 (OSI)**; provides **mechanical, electrical, functional, and procedural** means to transmit bits.
- **Three transmission modes:** Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex — know IoT examples for each.
- **Two major design choices:** *Baseband* (line coding: NRZ, Manchester, AMI) vs. *Passband* (modulation: ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM).
- **Key modulation formulas:**
  - **BPSK** = 1 bit/symbol, robust, used in LoRa
  - **QPSK** = 2 bits/symbol, used in Zigbee/BLE
  - **16-QAM** = 4 bits/symbol, used in Wi-Fi
  - **CSS (LoRa)** = sub-Hz spectral efficiency but extreme range
- **Shannon-Hartley Theorem:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — **memorize verbatim**; no KTU exam is complete without it.
- **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$.
- **Thermal noise:** $N = kTB$, with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- **Free-space path loss:** $L_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$.
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$, with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- **IoT spectrum bands to know:** 433/868/915 MHz (Sub-GHz ISM) and 2.4 GHz (Zigbee, BLE, Wi-Fi).
- **Spectral efficiency** $\eta = C/B$ (in bps/Hz) is a frequently asked 3-mark derivation.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)** for BPSK: $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
- **Multiple access schemes for IoT:** TDMA (cellular), FDMA (satellite), CDMA (3G), CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi, Zigbee).
- **IoT PHY layer is mostly wireless + low-power** — design priorities are *range, battery life, and cost*, **not** raw throughput.
- **Differentiator: LoRa (CSS) vs. Zigbee (OQPSK)** — LoRa = long range + low data rate; Zigbee = mesh + moderate rate.
- **Mandatory constants in any solution:** $c$, $k$ (Boltzmann), and the 32.44 path-loss constant.
- **For 14-mark questions:** Always (i) draw the block diagram (2 marks), (ii) explain sub-functions (3 marks), (iii) work the numerical with full unit conversion (7 marks), (iv) interpret the result (2 marks).
- **Common examiner traps:** Confusing dBm with dB; using $20\log$ where $10\log$ is required (and vice versa); missing the factor of 2 in $R_b = R_s \log_2(M)$.

> **Final Pro Tip:** For any IoT PHY numerical in KTU, **always state the assumptions** (free-space, isotropic antennas, AWGN) before applying formulas. Examiners reward clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MAC Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** MAC Layer # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **MAC Layer (Medium Access Control Layer)** in the context of IoT is a sublayer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) in the OSI model that governs how multiple IoT devices (nodes) share and access the common wireless communication medium without destructive collision, while optimizing energy consumption — a critical concern in resource-constrained IoT networks. The MAC layer is responsible for **channel access control**, **frame validation**, **acknowledgement**, **node association/disassociation**, **beacon management**, and **Guaranteed Time Slot (GTS)** allocation. In IoT, the dominant MAC standard is **IEEE 802.15.4**, which underpins protocols like **Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, and WirelessHART**. ### Formal Role in the IoT Protocol Stack $$ \text{IoT Protocol Stack (Lower Layers)} = \begin{cases} \text{Physical Layer (PHY)} \\ \text{MAC Sublayer} \leftarrow \textbf{our focus} \\ \text{Logical Link Control (LLC)} \\ \text{Network Layer (6LoWPAN / RPL)} \end{cases} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Analogy: A Round-Table Discussion in a Library** Imagine a small library reading room with **20 students** (IoT devices) wanting to ask the librarian (the wireless channel) questions. If everyone speaks at once, nothing is heard — this is **collision**. - A **loud "ding" bell** at fixed intervals acts as a **beacon** — telling everyone "you may now request to speak." This is the role of the **beacon-enabled superframe** in IEEE 802.15.4. - A **speaker's podium** that allows only one person at a time is the **CSMA/CA** mechanism (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance). - A **reservation slip system** where students can book a fixed 5-minute slot for important questions is the **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot)** mechanism for time-critical data. - The **rule that everyone must whisper** to save library energy is the **duty-cycling** principle, fundamental to IoT MAC protocols. ## 1.3 Key Design Goals of MAC in IoT | Goal | Description | Metric | |---|---|---| | **Energy Efficiency** | Minimize idle listening and overhearing | Current draw in **mA** | | **Scalability** | Support hundreds/thousands of nodes | Nodes per **PAN** | | **Latency** | Bounded delay for time-critical data | Time in **ms** | | **Throughput** | Effective data rate utilization | Bits per second (**bps**) | | **Fairness** | Equal channel access opportunity | Jain's Fairness Index ∈ [0,1] | | **Reliability** | Robust against interference & collision | Packet Delivery Ratio (**PDR**) | > [!NOTE] > The standard operating frequency bands are **868 MHz (Europe)**, **915 MHz (USA)**, and **2.4 GHz (Global)**, with the global **2.4 GHz ISM band** offering **16 channels** of 2 MHz bandwidth each. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 IEEE 802.15.4 MAC — Operational Pillars The IEEE 802.15.4 MAC is the foundational MAC protocol for Low-Rate Wireless Personal Area Networks (**LR-WPANs**), which form the bedrock of most IoT deployments. ### Pillar 1: Device Classes - **Full Function Device (FFD)** — Can act as a **PAN Coordinator**, coordinator, or end device. Talks to any node. - **Reduced Function Device (RFD)** — Limited to end-device role. Talks only to a single FFD. Cheaper, lower memory. ### Pillar 2: Two Operational Modes 1. **Beacon-enabled mode** — Uses **beacons** for synchronization, superframe structure, and GTS support. Suited for **star and tree topologies**. 2. **Non-beacon-enabled mode** — No beacons. Pure CSMA/CA channel access. Suited for **peer-to-peer topologies**. ### Pillar 3: Superframe Structure (Beacon-Enabled Mode) The superframe is bounded by **beacons** and is controlled by the **PAN Coordinator**. It consists of two parts: $$ \text{Superframe Duration (SD)} = \text{Active Period} + \text{Inactive Period} $$ $$ \text{SD} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{SO}} \quad \text{(in symbols)} $$ $$ \text{BI} = \text{aBaseSuperframeDuration} \times 2^{\text{BO}} \quad \text{(Beacon Interval in symbols)} $$ where: - **BO** = Beacon Order ∈ [0, 14] - **SO** = Superframe Order ∈ [0, 14], and **SO ≤ BO** - **aBaseSuperframeDuration** = **960 symbols** (constant, defined by the standard) ### Pillar 4: Active Period Composition The **active period** is divided into **16 equally-sized time slots**: | Component | Description | Slot Count | |---|---|---| | **Beacon** | Synchronization & structure announcement | Slot 0 | | **CAP (Contention Access Period)** | Slotted CSMA/CA channel access | Slots 1 to (15 − GTS) | | **CFP (Contention Free Period)** | GTS slots (up to 7 allocated) | Up to 7 slots at the end | ### Pillar 5: CSMA/CA Algorithm (Slotted) The slotted CSMA/CA used in 802.15.4 involves the following logical steps: 1. Locate the **next backoff slot boundary**. 2. Wait for a random number of backoff periods in **[0, 2^BE − 1]**, where **BE = macMinBE (default 3)**. 3. Perform **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)**. 4. If channel is **idle**, transmit. If **busy**, increment **NB** (Number of Backoffs) and **BE** (capped at **macMaxBE = 5**), and retry — up to **macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4** retries. ### Pillar 6: GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots) - Allocated only by the **PAN Coordinator**. - Up to **7 GTS slots** can be allocated. - Used for **time-critical, low-latency** traffic (e.g., industrial control). - GTS occupies contiguous slots at the **end of the active period**. ## 2.2 Why These Design Choices? — The Engineering 'Why' - **Slotted CSMA/CA** in beacon mode synchronizes all nodes, eliminating wasted idle listening — saving precious battery life in IoT nodes. - **Duty cycling** through the inactive period allows nodes to **sleep**, achieving duty cycles as low as **< 1%**, extending battery life to **months or years**. - **GTS** bypasses contention entirely for deterministic applications, providing bounded latency. - The restriction of **RFDs** reduces cost & memory — necessary for **ultra-low-cost** IoT sensors. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Variable Definitions | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Beacon Interval (BI) | $BI = 960 \times 2^{BO}$ | BO = Beacon Order | symbols | | Superframe Duration (SD) | $SD = 960 \times 2^{SO}$ | SO = Superframe Order | symbols | | Symbol duration (2.4 GHz) | $T_s = 16 \, \mu s$ | O-QPSK modulation | microseconds | | Active duration (seconds) | $T_{active} = SD \times 16 \mu s$ | SD in symbols | seconds | | Inactive duration | $T_{inactive} = (BI - SD) \times 16 \mu s$ | — | seconds | | Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{SD}{BI} = 2^{SO - BO}$ | — | dimensionless (%) | | Backoff window (initial) | $W_0 = 2^{macMinBE} - 1$ | macMinBE = 3 → $W_0$ = 7 | slots | | Maximum backoff window | $W_{max} = 2^{macMaxBE} - 1$ | macMaxBE = 5 → $W_{max}$ = 31 | slots | | Max CAP slots | $15 - GTS_{slots}$ | 16 total, 1 beacon | slots | | Max GTS slots | 7 | Fixed by standard | slots | | Data rate (2.4 GHz) | **250 kbps** | O-QPSK, 16 channels | bits/s | | Data rate (868 MHz) | **20 kbps** | BPSK | bits/s | | Data rate (915 MHz) | **40 kbps** | BPSK | bits/s | | Throughput efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{total} \times N_{tx}}$ | L = length, N = transmissions | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Conversion Tip for KTU Problems:** > 1 symbol = **16 μs** at 2.4 GHz PHY. So $SD$ (in seconds) = $SD$ (in symbols) × $16 \times 10^{-6}$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart homes**: Zigbee MAC for lighting, thermostats, door locks. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: WirelessHART MAC with TDMA + frequency hopping for factory floors. - **Healthcare**: IEEE 802.15.4-based body area networks (BANs) for patient monitoring. - **Smart agriculture**: 6LoWPAN + RPL over 802.15.4 for soil/weather sensors. - **Smart cities**: Wireless street lighting with GTS-based deterministic MAC. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Superframe Timing & Duty Cycle Calculation ### Problem Setup Given **BO = 3** and **SO = 1**, calculate the **BI, SD, active duration, inactive duration, and duty cycle** in both **symbols** and **milliseconds**. ### Step 1 — Calculate Beacon Interval (BI) in Symbols $$ BI = 960 \times 2^{BO} = 960 \times 2^{3} = 960 \times 8 = 7680 \, \text{symbols} $$ ### Step 2 — Calculate Superframe Duration (SD) in Symbols $$ SD = 960 \times 2^{SO} = 960 \times 2^{1} = 960 \times 2 = 1920 \, \text{symbols} $$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Real Time (using $T_s = 16 \, \mu s$) $$ BI_{time} = 7680 \times 16 \, \mu s = 122880 \, \mu s = 122.88 \, ms $$ $$ SD_{time} = 1920 \times 16 \, \mu s = 30720 \, \mu s = 30.72 \, ms $$ ### Step 4 — Inactive Duration $$ T_{inactive} = BI - SD = 122.88 - 30.72 = 92.16 \, ms $$ ### Step 5 — Duty Cycle $$ D = \frac{SD}{BI} = \frac{1920}{7680} = 0.25 = 25\% $$ Verification: $D = 2^{SO - BO} = 2^{1 - 3} = 2^{-2} = 0.25$ ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: The node sleeps for **75%** of every beacon interval — a massive energy saving for battery-powered IoT devices. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: GTS Allocation Analysis ### Problem Setup A PAN Coordinator wants to allocate **3 GTS slots** for time-critical traffic. The current superframe has **BO = 4, SO = 4** (full active period, no inactive). Determine how many slots remain for **CAP**. ### Step 1 — Total Slots in Active Period $$ \text{Total active slots} = 16 $$ ### Step 2 — Subtract Beacon Slot $$ \text{After beacon} = 16 - 1 = 15 \text{ slots} $$ ### Step 3 — Subtract Allocated GTS $$ \text{CAP slots} = 15 - 3 = 12 \text{ slots} $$ ### Step 4 — Verify Compliance with Standard The remaining **12 slots for CAP** must each be **≥ aMinCAPLength = 7** slots (per the 802.15.4 standard). $$ 12 \ge 7 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Valid allocation)} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: CSMA/CA Backoff Probability & Expected Delay ### Problem Setup In **slotted CSMA/CA** with macMinBE = 3, calculate: - (a) The initial backoff window. - (b) The maximum backoff window. - (c) Average backoff slots in the first attempt. ### Step (a) — Initial Window $$ W_0 = 2^{macMinBE} - 1 = 2^{3} - 1 = 8 - 1 = 7 \, \text{slots} $$ The node picks a uniform random integer from {0, 1, 2, ..., 7}. ### Step (b) — Maximum Window $$ W_{max} = 2^{macMaxBE} - 1 = 2^{5} - 1 = 32 - 1 = 31 \, \text{slots} $$ ### Step (c) — Average Backoff $$ E[B_0] = \frac{0 + 1 + 2 + \ldots + 7}{8} = \frac{7 \times 8 / 2}{8} = \frac{28}{8} = 3.5 \, \text{slots} $$ ### Step (d) — Backoff Doubling Rule After each failure, **BE increments by 1** (capped at 5). So successive backoff windows are: $$ \text{Window after 1st failure} = 2^{4} - 1 = 15 $$ $$ \text{Window after 2nd failure} = 2^{5} - 1 = 31 $$ $$ \text{Window after 3rd+ failure} = 31 \text{ (capped)} $$ Maximum retries: **macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4**. After this, the packet is dropped and a **channel access failure** is reported to the upper layer. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: MAC Frame Throughput Calculation ### Problem Setup A node transmits a MAC frame with: - MAC Header (MHR) = **31 bytes** - MAC Payload (MPDU body) = **100 bytes** (application data) - MAC Footer (MFR) = **2 bytes** (FCS) - **PHY preamble + SFD** = **6 bytes** overhead Each slot in the CAP = **20 bytes (160 bits)** of effective transmission capacity (for analysis). The node requires **2 transmissions** for reliability (one retransmission on failure). ### Step 1 — Total Frame Size at MAC Layer $$ L_{MAC} = MHR + Payload + MFR = 31 + 100 + 2 = 133 \, \text{bytes} = 1064 \, \text{bits} $$ ### Step 2 — Number of Slots Required (first attempt) $$ N_{slots} = \left\lceil \frac{1064}{160} \right\rceil = \lceil 6.65 \rceil = 7 \, \text{slots} $$ ### Step 3 — Total Slots with 1 Retransmission $$ N_{total} = 7 + 7 = 14 \, \text{slots} $$ ### Step 4 — Effective Throughput (per node, per superframe) $$ S_{eff} = \frac{100 \times 8}{14 \times 20 \times 8} = \frac{800}{2240} = 0.357 = 35.7\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Throughput Interpretation**: Only about **35.7%** of the channel time carries useful application data — the rest is overhead from MAC headers, retransmissions, and idle slots. This is why IoT applications favor **small payloads** and **batch aggregation**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MAC Protocols in IoT | Feature | IEEE 802.15.4 (Beacon) | IEEE 802.15.4 (Non-Beacon) | WirelessHART | LoRaWAN (MAC) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Topology** | Star / Tree | Peer-to-peer | Mesh | Star (of stars) | | **Access Method** | Slotted CSMA/CA + GTS | Unslotted CSMA/CA | TDMA + FHSS | ALOHA (pure) | | **Determinism** | High (GTS) | Low | Very High | Very Low | | **Energy Efficiency** | Excellent | Moderate | Good | Excellent | | **Scalability** | Moderate | Moderate | High | Very High | | **Max Nodes per Coord.** | ~65,000 (16-bit addr) | ~65,000 | Thousands | Millions | | **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | 250 kbps | 250 kbps | 0.3 – 50 kbps | | **Range** | 10–100 m | 10–100 m | 10–100 m | 2–15 km | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: IEEE 802.15.4 Superframe Structure ```mermaid graph TB subgraph BI["Beacon Interval (BI) = 960 x 2^BO symbols"] subgraph SD["Superframe Duration (SD) = 960 x 2^SO symbols"] B["BEACON
Slot 0

Contention Access Period
Slotted CSMA/CA
Slots 1 to N"] GTS1["GTS 1
Dedicated Slot"] GTS2["GTS 2
Dedicated Slot"] GTS3["GTS 3
Dedicated Slot"] end INACT["INACTIVE PERIOD
Nodes Sleep
Energy Saving"] end B --> CAP CAP --> GTS1 GTS1 --> GTS2 GTS2 --> GTS3 SD --> INACT INACT -.->|Next Beacon| B ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Slotted CSMA/CA Flowchart ```mermaid graph TB A["New Packet Arrives
at MAC Layer"] B["Init: NB=0, BE=macMinBE
Locate Backoff Slot Boundary"] C["Delay Random
Backoff in 0 to 2^BE-1"] D["Perform CCA
Clear Channel Assessment"] E{"Channel
Idle?"} F["Transmit Frame"] G["Increment NB
BE = min BE+1, macMaxBE"] H{"NB greater than
macMaxCSMABackoffs?"} I["Channel Access
Failure
Drop Packet"] J["Wait for
Acknowledgement"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E -- Yes --> F E -- No --> G G --> H H -- No --> C H -- Yes --> I F --> J ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: MAC Frame Structure (IEEE 802.15.4) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PHY["PHY Layer"] P1["Preamble
4 bytes"] P2["SFD
