IEEE 802.2"] end subgraph MAC["MAC Sublayer - IEEE 802.15.4"] M1["Channel Access
CSMA/CA or GTS"] M2["Frame Construction
and Validation"] M3["Beacon and
Superframe Mgmt"] M4["PAN Management
Association"] end subgraph PHY["PHY Layer - IEEE 802.15.4"] P1["Modulation
O-QPSK / BPSK"] P2["Channel Selection
868/915/2400 MHz"] P3["Transmit and
Receive RF"] end APP1 --> N1 N1 --> L1 L1 --> M1 M1 --> M2 M2 --> M3 M3 --> M4 M4 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 style MAC fill:#fff9c4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define the MAC layer. List any two key responsibilities of the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC sublayer in IoT.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Medium Access Control (MAC) layer** is a sublayer of the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) that manages how multiple IoT devices access a shared wireless medium reliably and efficiently. Two key responsibilities of IEEE 802.15.4 MAC: 1. **Channel access control** through **CSMA/CA** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) and **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slot)** allocation. 2. **Frame construction, validation, and acknowledgement** for reliable data delivery. 3. *(any 2 valid — full credit for 2)* *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for 2 responsibilities = 3 marks)* --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between CAP and CFP in the IEEE 802.15.4 superframe.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | CAP (Contention Access Period) | CFP (Contention Free Period) | |---|---|---| | Access Method | Slotted CSMA/CA (contention-based) | GTS slots (collision-free) | | Position | After beacon, before CFP | At the end of active period | | Use Case | Best-effort, normal traffic | Time-critical, real-time traffic | | Number of Slots | Variable (depends on GTS) | Up to 7 dedicated slots | *(2 marks for tabular comparison + 1 mark for any distinguishing point)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Both options below represent independent alternatives. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the IEEE 802.15.4 superframe structure with a neat diagram. Compute the Beacon Interval, Superframe Duration, and Duty Cycle when BO = 4 and SO = 2. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):** The IEEE 802.15.4 superframe is bounded by two consecutive beacons and is managed by the **PAN Coordinator**. It consists of: - An **active period** containing a **beacon**, a **CAP (Contention Access Period)** where devices use **slotted CSMA/CA**, and a **CFP (Contention Free Period)** where **GTS** slots may be allocated. - An **inactive period** during which all nodes **sleep** to conserve energy. **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Given: **BO = 4**, **SO = 2**, $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960$ symbols, $T_s = 16 \, \mu s$ Step 1 — Beacon Interval: $$ BI = 960 \times 2^{BO} = 960 \times 2^{4} = 960 \times 16 = 15360 \, \text{symbols} $$ $$ BI_{time} = 15360 \times 16 \, \mu s = 245760 \, \mu s = 245.76 \, ms $$ Step 2 — Superframe Duration: $$ SD = 960 \times 2^{SO} = 960 \times 2^{2} = 960 \times 4 = 3840 \, \text{symbols} $$ $$ SD_{time} = 3840 \times 16 \, \mu s = 61440 \, \mu s = 61.44 \, ms $$ Step 3 — Duty Cycle: $$ D = \frac{SD}{BI} = \frac{3840}{15360} = 0.25 = 25\% $$ Step 4 — Inactive Period: $$ T_{inactive} = 245.76 - 61.44 = 184.32 \, ms $$ **Conclusion:** The node is **active for only 25%** of the time, saving substantial battery life. --- **(b) Describe the Slotted CSMA/CA algorithm used in IEEE 802.15.4 MAC. List the key parameters involved. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Algorithm Steps (4 marks):** 1. The MAC layer initializes three variables: **NB (Number of Backoffs) = 0**, **BE (Backoff Exponent) = macMinBE (3)**, and locates the **next backoff slot boundary**. 2. The node delays for a **random number** of complete backoff periods in the range **[0, 2^BE − 1]**. 3. After this delay, it performs **CCA (Clear Channel Assessment)**. 4. **If channel is idle** → transmit the frame. 5. **If channel is busy** → increment NB and BE (capped at **macMaxBE = 5**), then retry from step 2. 6. If **NB > macMaxCSMABackoffs (4)** → declare **channel access failure** and drop the packet. **Key Parameters Table (3 marks):** | Parameter | Default Value | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `macMinBE` | **3** | Initial backoff exponent | | `macMaxBE` | **5** | Maximum backoff exponent (cap) | | `macMaxCSMABackoffs` | **4** | Max retry attempts | | `aUnitBackoffPeriod` | **20 symbols** | One backoff slot duration | | `aMaxFrameOverhead` | **25 bytes** | Max MAC + PHY header | | `aMaxPHYPacketSize` | **127 bytes** | Max MAC payload size | --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the IEEE 802.15.4 MAC frame structure. List the functions of each field. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Frame Structure Diagram (3 marks):** The MAC frame has four logical parts: 1. **MHR (MAC Header)** — Contains Frame Control, Sequence Number, Addressing fields, and Auxiliary Security. 2. **MSDU (MAC Service Data Unit)** — Carries the actual upper-layer payload. 3. **MFR (MAC Footer)** — Contains the 2-byte Frame Check Sequence (FCS) for error detection. Prepended to this is the **PHY Header** (Preamble + SFD). **Field Functions (4 marks):** | Field | Size | Function | |---|---|---| | Frame Control | 2 bytes | Frame type, addressing mode, security flags | | Sequence Number | 1 byte | Unique ID for matching ACK with data frame | | Addressing (PAN ID + Addresses) | 0–20 bytes | Source & destination addressing | | Auxiliary Security | 0–14 bytes | Security (encryption, MIC) | | Payload (MSDU) | 0–127 bytes | Upper-layer data | | FCS | 2 bytes | CRC-16 for error detection | | PHY Preamble | 4 bytes | Synchronization at receiver | | SFD | 1 byte | Start of Frame Delimiter | **Maximum MAC frame size** = 127 bytes (payload cap imposed by IEEE 802.15.4). --- **(b) Explain GTS mechanism in IEEE 802.15.4. A PAN coordinator allocates 2 GTS slots. If BO = SO = 3, calculate the number of CAP slots and verify its validity. (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **GTS Concept (4 marks):** **GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots)** are portions of the active superframe allocated by the **PAN Coordinator** to specific devices requiring **deterministic, low-latency** communication. - Maximum **7 GTS slots** can be allocated. - GTS is part of the **CFP**, located at the **end of the active period**, after the CAP. - Allocated via the **GTS request/grant** command frames. - A device is guaranteed **exclusive channel access** in its GTS — no contention. **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** Given: **BO = SO = 3**, GTS slots = **2** Step 1 — Total active slots = **16** Step 2 — Beacon occupies slot 0 → remaining = **15 slots** Step 3 — Subtract GTS: CAP slots = 15 − 2 = **13 slots** Step 4 — Verify validity: Standard requires CAP ≥ **aMinCAPLength = 7 slots** $$ 13 \ge 7 \quad \checkmark \text{ (Valid)} $$ Step 5 — Optional verification with timing: $$ SD = 960 \times 2^{3} = 7680 \, \text{symbols} = 122.88 \, ms $$ $$ \text{CAP duration} = 13 \times \left(\frac{122.88}{16}\right) = 13 \times 7.68 = 99.84 \, ms $$ $$ \text{GTS duration} = 2 \times 7.68 = 15.36 \, ms $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Unit Confusion**: Forgetting that $aBaseSuperframeDuration = 960$ **symbols**, and not multiplying by 16 μs to get time. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for direct comparison without time conversion. > > 2. **Forgetting the Beacon Slot**: Students often compute CAP = 16 − GTS, forgetting the beacon consumes slot 0. **Correct: CAP = 15 − GTS.** > > 3. **BE Doubling Misconception**: Students think BE doubles every retry. **Reality**: BE increments by **1** per retry (BE = BE + 1), not doubled. > > 4. **SO ≤ BO Violation**: Some students propose SO > BO. Standard explicitly **forbids this** — it would make SD > BI, which is geometrically impossible. > > 5. **Mixing Up FFD/RFD**: Writing that RFD can be a PAN Coordinator. **RFD cannot** act as PAN Coordinator. > > 6. **Confusing 802.11 vs 802.15.4**: 802.15.4 uses **slotted CSMA/CA** in beacon mode, not the DCF of 802.11. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **MAC layer** is a sublayer of **Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)** in the OSI model. - [x] **IEEE 802.15.4** is the foundational MAC for IoT (Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, Thread, WirelessHART). - [x] **Two device types**: **FFD** (full function) and **RFD** (reduced function). Only FFD can be PAN Coordinator. - [x] **Two operational modes**: **Beacon-enabled** (superframe + GTS) and **Non-beacon** (pure CSMA/CA). - [x] **Superframe = Active + Inactive period**. - [x] **BI = 960 × 2^BO** symbols; **SD = 960 × 2^SO** symbols; **SO ≤ BO**. - [x] **1 symbol = 16 μs** at 2.4 GHz PHY (O-QPSK modulation). - [x] **Duty Cycle = 2^(SO−BO)** — a critical energy-saving metric. - [x] **Active period = 16 slots**: 1 Beacon + CAP + GTS (up to 7). - [x] **CAP** uses **slotted CSMA/CA**; **CFP** contains GTS slots. - [x] **CSMA/CA parameters**: `macMinBE = 3`, `macMaxBE = 5`, `macMaxCSMABackoffs = 4`. - [x] **GTS**: Up to 7 slots, allocated by PAN Coordinator, for **deterministic low-latency** traffic. - [x] **MAC Frame**: MHR + MSDU + MFR, with max payload = **127 bytes**; total frame bounded by `aMaxPHYPacketSize`. - [x] **Data rate (2.4 GHz) = 250 kbps**, with **16 channels** of 2 MHz each. - [x] **CAP minimum length** = **7 slots** (aMinCAPLength) — GTS allocation must respect this. - [x] **Channel access failure** declared after 4 failed CSMA/CA attempts. - [x] **Beacon** in slot 0 carries **synchronization, PAN ID, superframe spec, GTS list**. - [x] **Inactive period** is when nodes **sleep** — the primary mechanism for energy efficiency in IoT MAC. - [x] **FFD ↔ FFD**, **FFD ↔ RFD**, but **RFD ↔ RFD** communication is **not allowed**.

topics.title: Topology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Topology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Network Topology** is the schematic arrangement or geometric organization of interconnected IoT nodes (sensors, actuators, gateways, and edge devices) along with the logical interconnections defining the data routing paths, communication hierarchy, and physical placement strategy that collectively govern how heterogeneous IoT devices exchange information across the network infrastructure.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for the course **PEMRT525 - IoT and Applications**, Module 3 frames **Topology** as the structural backbone of any IoT deployment. It defines two parallel dimensions:

- **Physical Topology** — The actual physical layout of nodes, cabling (or wireless links), gateways, and routers on a substrate (PCB, building, factory floor, agricultural field).
- **Logical Topology** — The data flow paths, irrespective of how the devices are physically arranged. Logical topology dictates how packets traverse the network (broadcast, point-to-point, token-passing, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMRT525 - Module 3):** Students must be able to differentiate between **Star, Mesh, Tree (Hierarchical), Bus, Ring, and Hybrid** topologies and justify topology selection for given IoT use-cases such as **Smart Home, Industrial IoT (IIoT), Smart Agriculture, and Healthcare IoT (IoMT)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **city's road network**. Now picture the city planner deciding how to lay down roads:
- A **Star** is like houses connected only to a central post office (the hub). Every letter must go through the post office.
- A **Mesh** is like a neighborhood where every house has a private road to every other house — extremely robust, but expensive.
- A **Bus** is like a single main road with houses lined along it; traffic jams occur if too many houses try to send letters at once.
- A **Ring** is houses arranged in a circle, where each house passes the letter to the next in sequence.
- A **Tree** is a post-office hierarchy: Local branch → District HQ → State HQ → National HQ. Organized, scalable, but a failure at the top halts everything below.
- A **Hybrid** is a city using a combination — star clusters connected by a ring backbone, for instance.

> [!TIP]
> **For your 3-mark answer opener:** "Topology in IoT refers to the architectural blueprint defining how smart devices, sensors, gateways, and the cloud are interconnected to enable seamless data exchange."

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The following standard metrics govern any IoT topology evaluation. They appear repeatedly in KTU ESE problems and must be memorized:

| Metric | Standard Definition | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **End-to-End Delay (Latency)** | Time for a packet to travel from source to destination | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| **Throughput** | Successful data delivery per unit time | **kbps / Mbps** |
| **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | Ratio of packets received to packets sent | **percentage (%)** |
| **Network Lifetime** | Time until first node energy exhaustion | **hours / years** |
| **Hop Count** | Number of intermediate nodes a packet traverses | **integer (hops)** |
| **Node Degree** | Number of direct neighbours of a node | **integer** |
| **Network Diameter** | Longest shortest path between any two nodes | **hops** |
| **Connectivity** | Minimum number of nodes whose removal disconnects the graph | **integer (κ)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Whenever a topology question is asked, the examiner expects a **comparison table** of these metrics across the different topologies. Always include a comparative table in your 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The "Why" and "How" Behind Topologies

A topology in IoT is fundamentally a **graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of IoT nodes (sensors, actuators, gateways, edge devices)
- $E$ = set of communication links (wired or wireless)

Every topology decision in IoT is a trade-off between **four fundamental constraints**:

1. **Energy Efficiency** (Critical for battery-powered sensor nodes)
2. **Scalability** (IoT deployments range from 10 to 10,000+ nodes)
3. **Reliability / Fault Tolerance** (Industrial and medical applications demand $\geq 99.999\%$ uptime)
4. **Latency** (Real-time control loops require sub-10ms response)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — The "Topology-Constraint Triangle":**
>
> No single IoT topology simultaneously optimizes Energy, Scalability, and Reliability. The art of IoT system design is **selecting the topology that best matches the dominant constraint of the use-case** (e.g., Mesh for reliability, Star for energy, Tree for scalability).

## 2.2 Exhaustive Topology Analysis

### 2.2.1 Star Topology

- **Architecture:** All nodes connect to a central hub (gateway or coordinator).
- **Graph Property:** Hub is a node with **degree = $N-1$**; all other nodes have **degree = $1$**.
- **Pros:** Simple, easy to install, low energy at leaf nodes, hub failure isolation.
- **Cons:** **Single point of failure (hub)**. Not scalable beyond a few hundred nodes due to hub bottleneck.
- **IoT Use-Case:** **ZigBee home automation, BLE star networks, Philips Hue lighting.**

### 2.2.2 Mesh Topology (Full and Partial)

- **Architecture:** Every node can connect to multiple other nodes.
- **Graph Property:** In a **full mesh** of $N$ nodes, number of edges $E = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$. In a **partial mesh**, $E$ is a subset of this.
- **Pros:** **Self-healing**, multi-path routing, highest reliability.
- **Cons:** Expensive, complex routing protocols (RPL, AODV), higher energy at router nodes.
- **IoT Use-Case:** **Thread, ZigBee PRO mesh, WirelessHART, Z-Wave, Industrial IoT sensor networks.**

### 2.2.3 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology

- **Architecture:** Root node at the top, branches to child nodes, leaves at the bottom.
- **Graph Property:** It is a **connected acyclic graph** with a designated root. Also called a **star-of-stars**.
- **Pros:** Naturally scales to thousands of nodes, easy to manage in tiers (edge → fog → cloud).
- **Cons:** Root failure partitions the entire network. Higher latency at deep levels.
- **IoT Use-Case:** **Large-scale agricultural monitoring, smart city deployments, multi-floor building automation.**

### 2.2.4 Bus Topology

- **Architecture:** All nodes share a single common communication medium (the "bus" or "backbone").
- **Graph Property:** All nodes are connected to a single edge. Node degree = $1$ for all nodes (excluding the bus itself).
- **Pros:** Cheap, simple for very small deployments.
- **Cons:** **Collision-prone** in high-traffic scenarios, single cable failure collapses the network. Largely **obsolete in modern IoT** but historically important (e.g., CAN bus in automotive, LIN bus).
- **IoT Use-Case:** **In-vehicle networks (CAN/LIN), legacy industrial control.**

### 2.2.5 Ring Topology

- **Architecture:** Each node connects to exactly two neighbours, forming a closed loop.
- **Graph Property:** Node degree = **2** for all nodes. Network diameter = $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor$.
- **Pros:** Predictable performance, easy to identify faults (token-based).
- **Cons:** A single node/link failure breaks the ring (unless dual-ring). Adding/removing nodes disrupts the network.
- **IoT Use-Case:** **Fiber-based industrial backbones, legacy Token Ring LANs, certain SCADA systems.**

### 2.2.6 Hybrid Topology

- **Architecture:** Combination of two or more of the above topologies. Most real-world IoT deployments are hybrid.
- **Graph Property:** Mixed; modelled as a multi-partite graph in formal analysis.
- **Pros:** Tailored to specific use-case; combines strengths of constituent topologies.
- **Cons:** Complex design, requires sophisticated network management.
- **IoT Use-Case:** **Smart factories (mesh + tree), smart homes (star + mesh), smart cities (ring backbone + star clusters).**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced with `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity. Use `\vert` for absolute value in all tables below.

### Table 2.3.A — Graph-Theoretic Formulas for IoT Topologies

| Formula | Description | Applicable Topology |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_{\text{star}} = N - 1$ | Number of links in a Star with $N$ nodes | Star |
| $E_{\text{full-mesh}} = \dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | Number of links in a Full Mesh with $N$ nodes | Full Mesh |
| $E_{\text{tree}} = N - 1$ | Number of links in a Tree with $N$ nodes (acyclic, connected) | Tree |
| $E_{\text{ring}} = N$ | Number of links in a Ring with $N$ nodes | Ring |
| $D_{\text{star}} = 2$ | Network diameter (max hops between any two nodes) | Star |
| $D_{\text{ring}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{N}{2} \right\rfloor$ | Network diameter in a Ring | Ring |
| $D_{\text{full-mesh}} = 1$ | Network diameter in a Full Mesh | Full Mesh |
| $D_{\text{tree}} = h_{\text{max}}$ | Network diameter equals the height of the tree | Tree |
| $\text{Conn}_{\text{star}} = 1$ | Vertex connectivity (remove hub = disconnect) | Star |
| $\text{Conn}_{\text{full-mesh}} = N - 1$ | Vertex connectivity (remove up to $N-2$ nodes) | Full Mesh |
| $\text{Conn}_{\text{ring}} = 2$ | Vertex connectivity | Ring |
| $E_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{2 \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert}$ | Average node degree from edges | All |

### Table 2.3.B — Performance & Energy Formulas

| Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| $T_{\text{end-to-end}} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} \left( t_{\text{prop},i} + t_{\text{trans},i} + t_{\text{queue},i} + t_{\text{proc},i} \right)$ | Total end-to-end delay over $h$ hops |
| $t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay; $d$ = link length, $v$ = signal speed ($3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in vacuum) |
| $t_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission delay; $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| $E_{\text{tx}}(k,d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \epsilon_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{n}$ | Transmit energy (free-space $n=2$, multipath $n=4$) |
| $E_{\text{rx}}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k$ | Receive energy |
| $\text{PDR} = \dfrac{P_{\text{received}}}{P_{\text{sent}}} \times 100\%$ | Packet Delivery Ratio |
| $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Channel utilization efficiency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Home (Star/Mesh):** Energy-efficient sensor clusters with ZigBee mesh for self-healing lighting.
- **Industrial IoT (Mesh):** WirelessHART in oil refineries — reliability is non-negotiable.
- **Smart Agriculture (Tree/Star):** Long-range LoRaWAN star-of-stars over hectares of farmland.
- **Healthcare (Star/Mesh):** Body Area Networks (BAN) using BLE star with critical-event mesh fallback.
- **Automotive (Bus/Ring):** CAN bus in ECUs, FlexRay (hybrid) for advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS).
- **Smart City (Hybrid):** LoRaWAN/Star access + Fiber/Ring backhaul + Cloud aggregation (Tree).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Links in a Full Mesh Network

**Problem Statement:** A smart factory deploys a full-mesh wireless sensor network with $N = 12$ nodes. Compute the number of communication links and the average node degree.

### Step 1 — Recall the Full-Mesh Edge Formula

In a full-mesh graph, every node is directly connected to every other node. The number of unordered pairs of $N$ distinct nodes is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{N}{2}$.

$$E_{\text{full-mesh}} = \binom{N}{2} = \frac{N!}{2! \cdot (N-2)!} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $N = 12$

$$E_{\text{full-mesh}} = \frac{12 \times (12 - 1)}{2} = \frac{12 \times 11}{2} = \frac{132}{2} = 66 \text{ links}$$

**Textual explanation:** Twelve distinct nodes can be paired in 66 unique combinations, each pair requiring a dedicated link.

### Step 3 — Compute the Average Node Degree

The average node degree of any graph is twice the number of edges divided by the number of nodes (Handshaking Lemma).

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert E \vert}{\vert V \vert} = \frac{2 \times 66}{12} = \frac{132}{12} = 11$$

**Textual explanation:** Each of the 12 nodes is connected to the remaining 11 nodes, confirming the average degree equals $N - 1 = 11$.

### Step 4 — Real-World Interpretation

> [!TIP]
> For $N = 12$ nodes, a full mesh requires 66 links, while a star topology would require only 11 links. This 6:1 overhead is the cost paid for the **maximum reliability** of a mesh — a classic engineering trade-off.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: End-to-End Latency Across a Multi-Hop IoT Link

**Problem Statement:** A 6LoWPAN IoT node sends a packet of size $L = 256$ bytes over a 3-hop path. Each link has a data rate of $R = 250$ kbps and a propagation distance of $d = 50$ m. Compute the **end-to-end delay**, assuming negligible queuing and processing delays. Signal speed $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (typical in copper/fiber).

### Step 1 — Identify the Components of Delay

The total end-to-end delay over $h$ hops is the sum of transmission, propagation, queueing, and processing delays at each hop.

$$T_{\text{e2e}} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} \left( t_{\text{trans},i} + t_{\text{prop},i} + t_{\text{queue},i} + t_{\text{proc},i} \right)$$

Since $t_{\text{queue}} = 0$ and $t_{\text{proc}} = 0$ (given), this simplifies to:

$$T_{\text{e2e}} = h \cdot \left( t_{\text{trans}} + t_{\text{prop}} \right)$$

### Step 2 — Convert Units Carefully

Convert packet size to bits: $L = 256 \text{ bytes} = 256 \times 8 = 2048 \text{ bits}$.

Convert rate to bps: $R = 250 \text{ kbps} = 250 \times 10^{3} \text{ bps}$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Transmission Delay per Hop

$$t_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2048}{250 \times 10^{3}} = 8.192 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8.192 \text{ ms}$$

**Textual explanation:** It takes 8.192 milliseconds for the sender to push all 2048 bits onto the 250 kbps link.

### Step 4 — Compute the Propagation Delay per Hop

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{50}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Textual explanation:** The signal traverses 50 m of medium in 0.25 microseconds, which is six orders of magnitude smaller than the transmission delay.

### Step 5 — Compute the Total End-to-End Delay for 3 Hops

$$T_{\text{e2e}} = 3 \times (8.192 \times 10^{-3} + 2.5 \times 10^{-7})$$

$$\phantom{T_{\text{e2e}}} = 3 \times (8.192000 + 0.000250) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\phantom{T_{\text{e2e}}} = 3 \times 8.192250 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\phantom{T_{\text{e2e}}} = 24.576750 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 24.577 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The total end-to-end delay is approximately **24.58 ms**, well within the latency budget for most IoT applications (typically $\leq 100$ ms for monitoring, $\leq 10$ ms for closed-loop control).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Star Topology Energy Consumption Comparison

**Problem Statement:** A star network has $N = 20$ sensor nodes, each located $d = 10$ m from the central hub. Each node transmits a $k = 128$-bit packet using the free-space model with $E_{\text{elec}} = 50$ nJ/bit and $\epsilon_{\text{amp}} = 10$ pJ/bit/m². Compute total network energy per round.

### Step 1 — Recall the First-Order Radio Energy Model

For a transmitter to send a $k$-bit packet over distance $d$, the energy consumed is:

$$E_{\text{tx}}(k, d) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k + \epsilon_{\text{amp}} \cdot k \cdot d^{2}$$

The energy consumed by the receiver is:

$$E_{\text{rx}}(k) = E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$E_{\text{tx}} = (50 \times 10^{-9}) \times 128 + (10 \times 10^{-12}) \times 128 \times (10)^{2}$$

$$\phantom{E_{\text{tx}}} = 6400 \times 10^{-9} + 1280 \times 10^{-12} \times 100$$

$$\phantom{E_{\text{tx}}} = 6400 \times 10^{-9} + 128000 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\phantom{E_{\text{tx}}} = 6400 \times 10^{-9} + 128 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\phantom{E_{\text{tx}}} = 6528 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J} = 6.528 \text{ \mu J}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Energy per Round for the Whole Star

Each node transmits once, the hub receives $N$ packets.

$$E_{\text{round}} = N \cdot E_{\text{tx}} + E_{\text{rx}} = N \cdot (E_{\text{tx}} + E_{\text{elec}} \cdot k)$$

$$E_{\text{round}} = 20 \cdot (6.528 \times 10^{-6} + 6.4 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\phantom{E_{\text{round}}} = 20 \cdot 12.928 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\phantom{E_{\text{round}}} = 258.56 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J} = 258.56 \text{ \mu J per round}$$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Star vs. Mesh vs. Tree vs. Ring)

| Parameter | Star | Mesh (Full) | Tree | Ring |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of Edges ($\vert E \vert$) | $N - 1$ | $\dfrac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $N - 1$ | $N$ |
| Number of Hops (avg.) | 2 | 1 | $O(\log N)$ | $\dfrac{N}{4}$ |
| Network Diameter | 2 | 1 | $2 \cdot \log_2 N$ (balanced) | $\left\lfloor \dfrac{N}{2} \right\rfloor$ |
| Vertex Connectivity ($\kappa$) | 1 | $N - 1$ | 1 | 2 |
| Edge Connectivity ($\lambda$) | 1 | $N - 1$ | 1 | 2 |
| Cable/Link Cost | Low | Very High | Low | Moderate |
| Energy at Leaf Nodes | Low | High | Low | Moderate |
| Failure Impact | Hub fails = total collapse | Negligible | Root fails = subtree collapses | Single break = ring fails |
| Scalability | Poor ($< 100$ nodes) | Poor ($< 30$ nodes) | Excellent ($> 10{,}000$) | Moderate |
| Routing Complexity | Trivial | High (multi-path) | Moderate (hierarchical) | Simple (token) |
| Typical IoT Use-Case | Smart Home BLE, ZigBee coordinator | Industrial WirelessHART | Smart Agriculture, Smart City | Fiber industrial backbone |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram — IoT Topology Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[IoT Network Topology] --> B0[Physical]
    A0 --> C0[Logical]
    B0 --> D0[Star]
    B0 --> E0[Mesh]
    B0 --> F0[Tree]
    B0 --> G0[Bus]
    B0 --> H0[Ring]
    B0 --> I0[Hybrid]
    C0 --> J0[Broadcast]
    C0 --> K0[Point to Point]
    C0 --> L0[Token Passing]
    E0 --> M0[Full Mesh]
    E0 --> N0[Partial Mesh]
    I0 --> O0[Star of Stars]
    I0 --> P0[Mesh of Rings]
    D0 --> Q0[BLE Smart Home]
    E0 --> R0[WirelessHART IIoT]
    F0 --> S0[Smart Agriculture]
    H0 --> T0[Fiber Backbone]
    I0 --> U0[Smart City Deployments]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The root node "IoT Network Topology" branches into Physical and Logical dimensions. Physical further branches into the six canonical IoT topology types. Hybrid sub-branches illustrate the most common real-world combinations. Each leaf at the bottom is a real-world IoT deployment example.

## 4.2 Star Topology — Hub-and-Spoke IoT Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Node1]:::leaf --> HUB[Gateway Hub]:::hub
    S2[Node2]:::leaf --> HUB
    S3[Node3]:::leaf --> HUB
    S4[Node4]:::leaf --> HUB
    S5[Node5]:::leaf --> HUB
    S6[Node6]:::leaf --> HUB
    S7[Node7]:::leaf --> HUB
    HUB --> CLOUD[Cloud Server]:::cloud
    classDef leaf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef hub fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef cloud fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note for Mermaid safety:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (S1, S2, HUB, CLOUD). No reserved keywords are used. Labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside the quotes.

## 4.3 Full Mesh Topology — Self-Healing Industrial IoT

```mermaid
graph TD
    M1[Node1] --> M2[Node2]
    M1 --> M3[Node3]
    M1 --> M4[Node4]
    M2 --> M3
    M2 --> M4
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5[Node5]
    M2 --> M5
    M3 --> M5
    M1 --> M5
```

## 4.4 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology — Edge-Fog-Cloud IoT Stack

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Cloud Root] --> FOG1[Fog Gateway North]
    ROOT --> FOG2[Fog Gateway South]
    FOG1 --> EDGE1[Edge Node A1]
    FOG1 --> EDGE2[Edge Node A2]
    FOG2 --> EDGE3[Edge Node B1]
    FOG2 --> EDGE4[Edge Node B2]
    EDGE1 --> S1[Sensor1]
    EDGE1 --> S2[Sensor2]
    EDGE2 --> S3[Sensor3]
    EDGE3 --> S4[Sensor4]
    EDGE4 --> S5[Sensor5]
    EDGE4 --> S6[Sensor6]
```

## 4.5 Bus Topology — Legacy IoT (CAN Bus in Automotives)

graph LR
    BUS((Communication Bus)):::bus
    BUS --- N1[ECU1]
    BUS --- N2[ECU2]
    BUS --- N3[ECU3]
    BUS --- N4[ECU4]
    BUS --- N5[ECU5]
    classDef bus fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

## 4.6 Ring Topology — Token-Passing Industrial Backbone

graph TD
    R1[Node1] --> R2[Node2]
    R2 --> R3[Node3]
    R3 --> R4[Node4]
    R4 --> R5[Node5]
    R5 --> R6[Node6]
    R6 --> R1
```

## 4.7 Hybrid Topology — Star-of-Stars (Real Smart Home + Mesh Fallback)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CC[Central Coordinator]:::core
    CC --> STARA[Cluster A Hub]
    CC --> STARB[Cluster B Hub]
    CC --> STARC[Cluster C Hub]
    STARA --> A1[Sensor1]
    STARA --> A2[Sensor2]
    STARB --> B1[Sensor3]
    STARB --> B2[Sensor4]

```

## 4.8 Functional Architecture Flow — Topology Selection in IoT

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start Topology Selection] --> Q1{Use Case Type?}

    Q1 -->|Industrial| Q3{Reliability Critical?}
    Q1 -->|Agriculture / City| Q4{Long Range and Scale?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| STAR[Star with BLE]
    Q2 -->|No| MESH1[ZigBee Mesh]
    Q3 -->|Yes| MESH2[WirelessHART Mesh]
    Q3 -->|No| TREE1[Tree Hierarchical]
    Q4 -->|Yes| TREE2[LoRaWAN Tree Star]
    Q4 -->|No| HYB[Hybrid Mesh Tree]
    STAR --> END[Deploy and Monitor]
    MESH1 --> END
    MESH2 --> END
    TREE1 --> END
    TREE2 --> END
    HYB --> END
```
