$$\text{Base} = \text{roundUp}[\min(5.8731 + 3.8829,\ 10)] = \text{roundUp}(9.756) = 9.8$$

**Result: Base Score = 9.8 → CRITICAL severity**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the OWASP IoT Top 10 vulnerabilities. Explain any five in detail with their real-world impact.**

**Model Solution:**

The **OWASP IoT Top 10 (2018)** is the industry-standard catalog of the most critical IoT security risks, published by the Open Web Application Security Project. The complete list (with 5 detailed explanations) is presented below:

**1. Weak, Hard-coded, or Guessable Passwords**

- **Description:** Devices ship with default credentials that users rarely change.
- **Example:** Cameras with `admin:12345`, routers with `admin:admin`.
- **Impact:** Enables large-scale botnet recruitment. **Mirai (2016)** and **Reaper (2017)** both leveraged this weakness.
- **Mitigation:** Enforce password change on first login; use unique per-device passwords; support MFA.

**2. Insecure Network Services**

- **Description:** Unnecessary services running on open ports (e.g., Telnet on port 23, FTP, debug ports).
- **Impact:** Provides direct attack surface to remote adversaries.
- **Mitigation:** Disable unused services; firewall all non-essential ports; use TLS everywhere.

**3. Insecure Ecosystem Interfaces**

- **Description:** APIs, web dashboards, mobile apps, and cloud backends with weak authentication, missing authorization, or input validation.
- **Impact:** Account takeover, mass device hijacking, and data exfiltration.
- **Example:** In 2019, a vulnerability in **Ring doorbells** allowed attackers to view camera feeds by exploiting an authentication flaw.
- **Mitigation:** OAuth 2.0, RBAC, input sanitization, rate limiting.

**4. Lack of Device Update Mechanism**

- **Description:** No secure method to deliver firmware/security patches after deployment.
- **Impact:** Once a vulnerability is disclosed, the device remains permanently vulnerable.
- **Example:** **TP-Link router vulnerability CVE-2023-1389** remained unpatched on many models for over 6 months.
- **Mitigation:** Signed OTA updates, automatic patch rollout, A/B partitioning for safe rollbacks.

**5. Insecure Data Transfer and Storage**

- **Description:** Sensitive data transmitted in plaintext or stored unencrypted.
- **Impact:** Eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle attacks, and post-breach data exposure.
- **Example:** In 2017, the **Spirited Away IoT doll** leaked 2 million parent-child voice recordings due to an unauthenticated MongoDB instance.
- **Mitigation:** TLS 1.3 for transport, AES-256 for storage, hardware secure elements for keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Format:** When asked about OWASP, always present answers using the **Description → Example → Impact → Mitigation** structure. Examiners award marks for completeness across all four dimensions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Patterns:**
> 1. Confusing *vulnerability* with *attack*. A vulnerability is a weakness; an attack is the exploitation. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this conflation.
> 2. Using only bullet points without a diagram. For 14-mark questions, **a block diagram is mandatory** in Part (a). The KTU scheme explicitly requires visual organization.
> 3. Omitting severity bands in CVSS questions. Always state the band (Low/Medium/High/Critical) in addition to the numeric score.
> 4. Failing to cite at least one **real-world example** (Mirai, Ring, Spirited Away) when describing vulnerabilities. This shows applied understanding.
> 5. Spelling Mirai as "Mirai/Miray/Mirae" — examiners note the exact spelling.
> 6. Forgetting the `roundUp` in CVSS computations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Vulnerabilities**

- ✅ **Definition:** Vulnerability = weakness; Threat = adversary; Risk = probability × impact
- ✅ **CIA Triad:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — affected by all vulnerabilities
- ✅ **4 IoT Layers:** Perception, Network, Middleware, Application
- ✅ **OWASP IoT Top 10 (2018):** 10 canonical vulnerabilities — know at least 5 in detail
- ✅ **Mirai Botnet (2016):** Used 61 default credentials; 1.2 Tbps peak DDoS; affected Twitter/Netflix/GitHub
- ✅ **CVSS 3.1:** Score 0.0–10.0; severity bands: Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0)
- ✅ **CVSS Base Formula:** $\text{Base} = \text{roundUp}[\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10)]$
- ✅ **ALE Formula:** $ALE = SLE \times ARO = (AV \times EF) \times ARO$
- ✅ **Risk Formula:** $R = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact)
- ✅ **Attack Types:** MitM, Sybil, Sinkhole, Replay, Eavesdropping, Jamming, Tampering
- ✅ **Real-World Cases to Remember:** Mirai (2016), Ring (2019), Spirited Away (2017), TP-Link CVE-2023-1389
- ✅ **Regulations Mentioned:** California SB-327 (2018), UK PSTI Act (2024)
- ✅ **Key Mnemonic for Layers:** **P**erception → **N**etwork → **M**iddleware → **A**pplication = "**PNMA**"
- ✅ **Always include a block diagram** in 14-mark vulnerability questions
- ✅ **Always cite a real-world example** in 7-mark descriptive questions
- ✅ **Always state the severity band** when computing CVSS

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Security Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Security Requirements** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refer to the systematic set of functional and non-functional specifications, policies, and cryptographic primitives that must be satisfied to protect IoT systems, networks, data, and devices from unauthorized access, tampering, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, IoT security requirements are formally defined as the **confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, authorization, non-repudiation, data freshness, and privacy** guarantees that collectively ensure trustworthy operation across the Perception, Network, and Application layers of the IoT architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Security Requirements in IoT are the foundational pillars — **CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** augmented with **Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Privacy, and Trust** — that must be engineered into every node, gateway, and cloud service to defend against the unique threat landscape of resource-constrained, massively-scaled IoT deployments."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **smart-home ecosystem** as a high-security apartment building:

- The **door lock with a fingerprint scanner** = **Authentication** (proving *who* you are).
- The **keycard that only opens your floor** = **Authorization** (proving *what* you can do).
- The **letter sealed inside an opaque envelope** = **Confidentiality** (no one reads your private mail).
- The **signed receipt from the courier** = **Non-Repudiation** (the courier cannot deny delivering it).
- The **front desk always open 24/7** = **Availability** (services are accessible when needed).
- The **letter that has not been swapped, torn, or replaced** = **Integrity** (data is unaltered in transit).
- The **mail delivered today, not last week's** = **Data Freshness** (messages are current, not replayed).

In IoT, the "building" contains billions of tiny devices (sensors, actuators, RFID tags) — each acting as a *room* that must be individually secured without overloading the *power supply* (battery) or the *hallway traffic* (network bandwidth). This is precisely why IoT security requirements demand **lightweight, energy-efficient, and protocol-aware** mechanisms.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Bold

- **Cryptographic Key Length (Symmetric):** 128 bits (AES-128 standard for IoT)
- **Cryptographic Key Length (Asymmetric):** 256 bits (ECC equivalent of RSA-3072)
- **Hash Output Length:** 256 bits (SHA-256, IoT-friendly)
- **NIST Recommended Entropy:** $\geq$ **128 bits** for cryptographic seeds
- **Trust Anchor Lifespan:** Typically **5–10 years** for industrial IoT deployments
- **PKI Certificate Validity (DTLS):** **1–3 years** for constrained devices

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** The CIA Triad is **not optional** — every IoT layer (Perception/Network/Application) must explicitly map its security controls to one or more pillars of CIA. Examiner questions in ESE frequently test this layer-to-pillar mapping.

---

## 1.4 Why IoT Security Requirements Are Unique

Unlike traditional IT systems, IoT introduces **three new dimensions** to security requirements:

1. **Scale** — millions to billions of heterogeneous devices.
2. **Constraint** — limited CPU, RAM (often < 64 KB), battery, and bandwidth.
3. **Heterogeneity** — diverse communication protocols (Zigbee, LoRaWAN, BLE, MQTT, CoAP).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Impact:** The **Mirai Botnet (2016)** exploited default-credential IoT devices (CCTV cameras, DVRs) to launch a 1.1 Tbps DDoS attack. This single incident redefined IoT security requirements globally, mandating **mandatory unique device credentials, secure boot, and firmware integrity verification** as baseline requirements.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Pillars of IoT Security Requirements

IoT security requirements are not monolithic — they decompose into **eight discrete but inter-dependent pillars**. Each pillar addresses a specific class of threat and maps to a specific architectural layer.

### Pillar 1: Confidentiality
- **Definition:** Ensures that IoT data (sensor readings, actuator commands, user PII) is accessible *only* to authorized parties.
- **Mechanism:** Symmetric encryption (AES-128 in CCM mode), asymmetric encryption (ECC, RSA).
- **IoT-Specific Challenge:** Energy cost of encryption on battery-powered Class 0 devices (< 10 KB RAM).
- **Layer Application:** Primarily **Network Layer** (TLS/DTLS tunnels) and **Perception Layer** (lightweight ciphers like PRESENT, SIMON, Speck).

### Pillar 2: Integrity
- **Definition:** Guarantees that data has *not been altered* in transit or at rest.
- **Mechanism:** Message Authentication Codes (MAC), HMAC-SHA-256, digital signatures (ECDSA).
- **IoT-Specific Challenge:** Collision resistance must hold even on 8-bit microcontrollers.

### Pillar 3: Availability
- **Definition:** Ensures IoT services and data are accessible *when needed* by authorized users.
- **Mechanism:** Redundancy, DDoS mitigation, rate-limiting, fog computing offload.
- **Threat Vector:** Sleep deprivation attacks, jamming, flooding attacks on constrained networks (e.g., 6LoWPAN).

### Pillar 4: Authentication
- **Definition:** Verifies the *identity* of a device, user, or service.
- **Mechanism:** Pre-shared keys (PSK), X.509 certificates, biometric tokens, challenge-response protocols.
- **IoT-Specific Challenge:** Public-key authentication is computationally expensive — DTLS with ECC is preferred over RSA.

### Pillar 5: Authorization
- **Definition:** Determines *what actions* an authenticated entity is permitted to perform.
- **Mechanism:** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC), OAuth 2.0, Capability tokens.
- **KTU Insight:** RBAC is the *de facto* standard in KTU-evaluated smart-city and industrial IoT case studies.

### Pillar 6: Non-Repudiation
- **Definition:** Ensures that a party *cannot deny* having performed an action.
- **Mechanism:** Digital signatures (ECDSA, EdDSA), audit logs with cryptographic chaining.
- **Use Case:** Billing in smart-grid IoT — the smart meter must not be able to deny consumption records.

### Pillar 7: Data Freshness
- **Definition:** Guarantees that messages are *current* and not replayed from a previous session.
- **Mechanism:** Sequence numbers, timestamps (with NTP synchronization), nonces.
- **Why Critical in IoT:** Replay attacks on actuator commands (e.g., "OPEN VALVE") can be catastrophic.

### Pillar 8: Privacy
- **Definition:** Protection of personally identifiable information (PII) generated by IoT devices.
- **Mechanism:** Data minimization, anonymization, differential privacy, homomorphic encryption.
- **Regulatory Anchor:** GDPR Article 25 (Privacy by Design), India's DPDP Act 2023.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Unit / Constraint | Layer of Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C$ | Confidentiality strength | $C = \log_2(K)$ where $K$ = key space size | All layers |
| $H(m)$ | Cryptographic hash function | bits (typically 256) | Perception, Network |
| $MAC$ | Message Authentication Code | $MAC = H(K \oplus m)$ | Network (DTLS) |
| $T_{auth}$ | Authentication time budget | $\leq 500$ ms for real-time IoT | Network |
| $E_{enc}$ | Energy cost of encryption | Joules per bit | Perception (battery) |
| $R_{avail}$ | Availability ratio | $R_{avail} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Application, Network |
| $N_{replay}$ | Nonce uniqueness window | $N_{replay} \geq 2^{64}$ per session | Network |
| $P_{priv}$ | Privacy leakage probability | $P_{priv} \leq 10^{-6}$ (GDPR-grade) | Application |
| $\lambda_{sec}$ | Security failure rate (FIT) | Failures-In-Time per $10^9$ hours | All layers |
| $K_{length}$ | Symmetric key length | $\geq 128$ bits (NIST SP 800-131A) | All layers |
| $K_{pub}$ | Asymmetric key length (ECC) | $\geq 256$ bits (≈ RSA-3072 strength) | Network, Application |
| $S_{boot}$ | Secure boot integrity check | $S_{boot} = H(firmware) \stackrel{?}{=} H_{stored}$ | Perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "CIA AANP":**
> **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability, **A**uthentication, **A**uthorization, **N**on-repudiation, **P**rivacy (plus Data Freshness and Trust as the "hidden" two).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Healthcare:** Patient telemetry requires Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability (life-critical).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Actuator commands demand Authorization + Non-Repudiation + Freshness (safety-critical).
- **Smart Grid:** Billing data needs Non-Repudiation + Integrity (regulatory-critical).
- **Connected Vehicles:** V2X messages demand Authentication + Freshness + Integrity (millisecond-critical).
- **Smart Home:** Voice/Video streams require Confidentiality + Privacy (consumer-trust-critical).

## 2.4 IoT-Specific Security Constraints

| Constraint | Typical Value | Impact on Security Design |
|---|---|---|
| RAM | **10–256 KB** | Disables full TLS handshake with certificates |
| Flash | **100 KB – 1 MB** | Limits cryptographic library footprint |
| CPU | **8–32 bit, ≤ 100 MHz** | Favors ECC over RSA |
| Battery | **1–5 years (CR2032)** | Dictates duty-cycled crypto |
| Bandwidth | **0.3 – 250 kbps (LPWAN)** | Favors symmetric over asymmetric |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Why not use RSA-2048 in IoT?"* the correct answer references **energy cost** (RSA-2048 signature = ~10 mJ vs ECC-256 = ~0.5 mJ) and **code size** (RSA stack ≈ 30 KB vs ECC stack ≈ 6 KB).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Authentication Strength from Key Space

The **theoretical authentication strength** $S$ in bits is defined as the binary logarithm of the key space size $K$:

$$
S = \log_2(K)
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define the key space.**
For a symmetric cipher with key length $L$ bits, the key space is:

$$
K = 2^{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to logarithmic scale.**
The number of bits of security is the binary logarithm:

$$
S = \log_2(2^{L}) = L \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply NIST IoT recommendation.**
NIST SP 800-131A mandates $L \geq 128$ for new IoT deployments, so:

$$
S = 128 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Translate to attack effort.**
A 128-bit key requires on average $2^{127}$ trials for brute force, which is computationally infeasible (approx. $1.7 \times 10^{38}$ operations).

> For **ECC-256** equivalent security: $L_{RSA} \approx 16 \times L_{ECC}$, so $L_{RSA} \approx 16 \times 256 / 16 = 3072$ bits for matching security.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Cost of Cryptographic Operation

The energy cost of a single AES-128 block encryption on a typical IoT mote (CC2538) is:

$$
E_{enc} = V \cdot I \cdot t
$$

Where:
- $V$ = supply voltage (typically **3.0 V**)
- $I$ = current draw during encryption (typically **10 mA**)
- $t$ = time per block (typically **10 µs** for hardware AES)

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation:**

$$
E_{enc} = 3.0 \text{ V} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}
$$

$$
E_{enc} = 3.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} = 0.30 \text{ µJ per block}
$$

**Comparison with RSA-2048 software implementation:**

$$
E_{RSA} \approx 9.5 \text{ mJ} = 9500 \text{ µJ}
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_{RSA}}{E_{AES}} = \frac{9500}{0.30} \approx 31667
$$

> **Conclusion:** RSA-2048 consumes **~31,667×** more energy than AES-128 per cryptographic operation on a constrained IoT node. This is the *quantitative* justification for preferring symmetric + hybrid (ECDH) cryptography in IoT.

## 3.3 Derivation: Availability Ratio (Reliability Metric)

For a redundant IoT gateway cluster, availability is:

$$
R_{avail} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Assume Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).**
For a hardened industrial IoT gateway: $MTBF = 50000$ hours.

**Step 2 — Assume Mean Time To Repair (MTTR).**
With on-site technician: $MTTR = 4$ hours.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
R_{avail} = \frac{50000}{50000 + 4} = \frac{50000}{50004}
$$

$$
R_{avail} \approx 0.99992 = 99.992\%
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to "nines" notation:**

$$
R_{avail} = 1 - \frac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR} \approx 1 - 8 \times 10^{-5}
$$

> **Industry Benchmark:** Tier-III data centers require $R_{avail} \geq 99.982\%$. The derived IoT gateway exceeds this benchmark.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Threat → Requirement → Mechanism

| Threat | Security Requirement Pillar | Counter-Mechanism | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eavesdropping | Confidentiality | AES-128-CCM encryption | BLE pairing encryption |
| Data Tampering | Integrity | HMAC-SHA-256 | MQTT payload MAC |
| Node Impersonation | Authentication | ECC-based DTLS handshake | CoAP over DTLS |
| Privilege Escalation | Authorization | RBAC + OAuth 2.0 scopes | AWS IoT policies |
| Replay Attack | Data Freshness | Nonces + sequence numbers | 6LoWPAN timestamp |
| Service Disruption | Availability | Rate-limiting + redundancy | MQTT broker clustering |
| Firmware Tampering | Integrity (boot) | Secure boot + TPM | ESP32 secure boot V2 |
| Identity Forgery | Non-Repudiation | ECDSA digital signature | Smart grid billing |
| Location Tracking | Privacy | Mix-zones + differential privacy | Smart city mobility |
| Physical Capture | Confidentiality (keys) | PUFs (Physical Unclonable Functions) | RFID anti-counterfeit |

## 3.5 Layer-Wise Security Requirements Mapping

| IoT Layer | Primary Security Requirements | Threats Mitigated | Standard/Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authenticity, Privacy | Node tampering, side-channel, cloning | IEEE 802.15.4 security, BLE LE Secure |
| **Network Layer** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Freshness | Eavesdropping, MITM, replay | DTLS, TLS 1.3, IPsec |
| **Application Layer** | Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Availability, Privacy | Unauthorized access, DDoS, data leakage | OAuth 2.0, XACML, RBAC |
| **Cross-Layer** | Trust, Key Management, Identity | Sybil attacks, key compromise | PKI, 6LoWPAN, RPL security |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** The **Cross-Layer Trust** requirement is the *most frequently missed* in student answers. Always mention that trust cannot be enforced at a single layer — it requires coordinated key management, secure boot, and runtime attestation across perception-network-application.

```mermaid
    rom["Immutable ROM\nRoot of Trust"]
    bootloader["Stage 1 Bootloader\nCryptographically Signed"]

    runtime["Runtime Attestation\nContinuous Verification"]
    rom -- Verify Stage 1 Signature --> bootloader
    bootloader -- Load Signed Kernel --> oskernel
    oskernel -- Verify App Hash --> appfw
    appfw -- Periodic Attestation --> runtime
    runtime -- Alert on Tamper --> reject["Reject and Halt Device"]
```

```mermaid
    subCA -- Issues --> gwCert
    subCA -- Issues --> devCert
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain any three security requirements specific to the Perception Layer of an IoT architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Perception Layer of IoT handles physical sensors, RFID tags, and actuators, and requires the following security guarantees:

1. **Device Authentication (1 Mark):** Every sensor/RFID tag must cryptographically prove its identity before joining the network. Implemented via unique device certificates (X.509 with ECC-256) or pre-shared keys for Class 0 devices.

2. **Data Confidentiality at Edge (1 Mark):** Sensor readings (e.g., patient vitals, industrial parameters) must be encrypted *before* transmission using lightweight ciphers like AES-128-CCM, preventing eavesdropping on wireless channels (IEEE 802.15.4, BLE).

3. **Tamper Resistance and Secure Boot (1 Mark):** The device firmware must be cryptographically verified at boot time using a hardware root of trust (ROM-stored public key). Any modification halts the boot process, mitigating malicious firmware injection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Perception Layer" security with "Network Layer" security. *Perception* is about the *physical sensor node* itself — secure boot, hardware identity, local encryption. *Network* is about routing and transport.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography) preferred over RSA in resource-constrained IoT devices? Support with a quantitative comparison.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Key Length Efficiency (1 Mark):** ECC-256 provides equivalent security to RSA-3072. A 256-bit ECC key fits in constrained devices (32 bytes) while a 3072-bit RSA key requires 384 bytes — 12× more memory.

2. **Computational Cost (1 Mark):** An ECDSA signature verification on a typical IoT mote consumes approximately **0.5 mJ**, while an RSA-2048 verification consumes approximately **9.5 mJ** — an **~19× energy penalty** for RSA.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency (1 Mark):** Smaller signatures (ECC-256 ≈ 64 bytes vs RSA-2048 ≈ 256 bytes) reduce LoRaWAN/Zigbee frame overhead, critical for low-bandwidth LPWAN IoT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do *not* answer with "RSA is slow" — that is incomplete. Always quantify with **key size, energy, and bandwidth**.

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CIA Triad** in the context of IoT. For each pillar, give one IoT-specific attack scenario and the corresponding counter-mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the **eight extended security requirements** of an IoT system (CIA AANP + Freshness + Trust). Construct a layer-wise mapping table showing which requirement applies to which architectural layer.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — The CIA Triad in IoT [7 Marks]**

**1. Confidentiality [2 Marks]**
- **Definition:** Confidentiality ensures that IoT data (sensor streams, actuator commands, user PII) is accessible only to authorized parties.
- **IoT Attack Scenario:** An attacker places a software-defined radio (SDR) near a smart-home Zigbee network and passively sniffs unencrypted temperature sensor data, learning the homeowner's daily routine (when they leave for work, sleep, etc.).
- **Counter-Mechanism:** Enable AES-128-CCM encryption on all Zigbee application-layer payloads using a network key established via the Zigbee Trust Center during joining.

**2. Integrity [2 Marks]**
- **Definition:** Integrity guarantees that data has not been altered (intentionally or accidentally) during transmission or storage.
- **IoT Attack Scenario:** A man-in-the-middle attacker intercepts a Modbus TCP command to an industrial PLC and changes "VALVE_OPEN" to "VALVE_CLOSE", causing a chemical spill.
- **Counter-Mechanism:** Append an HMAC-SHA-256 tag to every Modbus command; the PLC rejects any command whose HMAC does not match the locally recomputed value.

**3. Availability [2 Marks]**
- **Definition:** Availability ensures that IoT services and data are accessible when needed by legitimate users.
- **IoT Attack Scenario:** A flooding attack on a 6LoWPAN mesh exhausts the limited buffers of constrained nodes, causing the wireless sensor network to crash (a "sleep deprivation" attack).
- **Counter-Mechanism:** Implement rate-limiting at the RPL border router, redundant routing paths, and duty-cycle enforcement to ensure critical health-monitoring packets always reach the sink.

**4. Summary Diagram (1 Mark — Synthesis):**
The three pillars are inter-dependent: compromising any one degrades the system. KTU examiners award marks for showing this interconnection.

#### **Part (b) — Extended Security Requirements + Layer Mapping [7 Marks]**

**The CIA AANP Framework (5 Marks — 0.5 Mark per requirement + 0.5 Mark IoT example):**

| # | Requirement | Definition (0.25 M) | IoT Example (0.25 M) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Confidentiality | Data accessible only to authorized parties | Encrypted BLE pairing in wearable health devices |
| 2 | Integrity | Data unaltered in transit/at rest | HMAC-signed MQTT messages in factory automation |
| 3 | Availability | Services accessible when needed | Redundant LoRaWAN gateways for smart agriculture |
| 4 | Authentication | Identity verification of devices/users | ECC-based DTLS handshake in CoAP over 6LoWPAN |
| 5 | Authorization | Permission to perform specific actions | RBAC in smart-building access control |
| 6 | Non-Repudiation | Actor cannot deny an action | ECDSA-signed smart-meter billing records |
| 7 | Data Freshness | Messages are current, not replayed | Nonce + sequence number in actuator commands |
| 8 | Privacy | PII protection | Differential privacy in smart-city mobility analytics |

**Layer-Wise Mapping Table (2 Marks):**

| IoT Layer | Active Security Requirements |
|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Privacy |
| **Network Layer** | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Data Freshness |
| **Application Layer** | Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Availability, Privacy |
| **Cross-Layer (Cross-cutting)** | Trust, Key Management, Identity Lifecycle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students list "all 8 requirements for all 3 layers" — this is *wrong*. Confidentiality is *not enforced* at the application layer (it was already enforced in transit). The examiner deducts **1 mark** for incorrect mapping.
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting **Data Freshness** in the Network Layer. Actuator replay attacks are a frequent KTU question topic.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Secure Boot chain** in an IoT device. How does it enforce the **Integrity** security requirement?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **symmetric (AES-128)**, **asymmetric (ECC-256)**, and **hybrid (ECDH+AES)** cryptographic approaches for IoT. Recommend the best approach for: (i) a Class 0 RFID tag, (ii) a Class 2 smart-home gateway, (iii) a Class 3 cloud-edge aggregator.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Secure Boot Chain in IoT [7 Marks]**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 Marks):**
Secure boot is a hardware-rooted, cryptographically-verified boot process that ensures only *authentic, untampered* firmware is allowed to execute on an IoT device. It enforces the **Integrity** security requirement by chaining trust from immutable hardware (ROM) all the way up to the application.

**Step-by-Step Boot Chain (3 Marks):**
1. **ROM Stage:** An immutable, mask-programmed ROM contains a vendor public key (or hash). This is the *root of trust* — physically unmodifiable.
2. **Stage-1 Bootloader Verification:** The ROM verifies the digital signature (e.g., ECDSA-P256) of the Stage-1 bootloader. If invalid, the device halts.
3. **Stage-2 OS Kernel:** The verified Stage-1 bootloader verifies the OS kernel image (typically using SHA-256 hash comparison stored in signed metadata).
4. **Application Firmware:** The OS kernel verifies the application firmware hash before loading. Any mismatch triggers a *fail-secure* halt.
5. **Runtime Attestation (Optional):** A Trusted Platform Module (TPM) or Secure Element continuously reports the device's current state to a remote verifier.

**Diagram (1 Mark):** See Section 4.4 — "Secure Boot and Firmware Integrity Chain" Mermaid block.

**Integrity Enforcement Argument:**
Each stage *cryptographically* checks the next. Since the ROM cannot be modified, the entire chain inherits trust from a single hardware root. Any firmware tampering (e.g., Mirai botnet injection) fails the ECDSA signature check and the device refuses to boot.

#### **Part (b) — Cryptographic Approach Comparison [7 Marks]**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | AES-128 (Symmetric) | ECC-256 (Asymmetric) | ECDH + AES-128 (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Key Size | 128 bits (16 bytes) | 256 bits (32 bytes) | 256 + 128 bits |
| Operations/sec on IoT mote | ~10,000 | ~50 (sign) / ~200 (verify) | 1 ECDH + many AES |
| Energy per op | 0.30 µJ | 0.5 mJ (sign) | ~0.5 mJ (once) + 0.3 µJ (per data) |
| Code Footprint | ~3 KB | ~12 KB | ~15 KB total |
| Use Case | Bulk data encryption | Identity / signatures | Key exchange + bulk encryption |
| Suitability for Class 0 | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Too heavy | ❌ Too heavy (one-time only) |

**Recommendations (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

**(i) Class 0 RFID Tag (< 10 KB RAM, battery-less or coin-cell):**
**Recommendation: AES-128 with pre-shared key (PSK).**
- *Reason:* RFID tags cannot afford the 12 KB ECC stack. A pre-shared symmetric key burned into the tag at manufacture time allows lightweight AES-128 encryption with negligible code footprint. ECDH key exchange is impossible on a passive RFID tag.

**(ii) Class 2 Smart-Home Gateway (~256 KB RAM, mains-powered):**
**Recommendation: Hybrid ECDH + AES-128 (DTLS 1.3).**
- *Reason:* Gateways have enough resources to run ECC for initial handshake (mains power eliminates the energy concern) and AES-128 for high-throughput data encryption. ECDH provides *forward secrecy* and *mutual authentication*, which a pure symmetric key cannot.

**(iii) Class 3 Cloud-Edge Aggregator (server-class):**
**Recommendation: Full PKI with ECC-256 certificates + RSA-4096 for root CA + AES-256-GCM for data at rest.**
- *Reason:* Aggregators handle millions of devices and must support mutual TLS (mTLS) with certificate rotation. ECC-256 client certificates balance security and bandwidth. RSA-4096 (or post-quantum) is reserved for the offline root CA.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"Use RSA everywhere"* — this loses **2 marks** instantly. RSA-2048/4096 should *never* be deployed on constrained IoT devices. The recommended KTU-2024 answer always references **ECC for constrained nodes** and **hybrid schemes** for gateways.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Print This Section Before ESE**

- ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability. The **foundational three** pillars — every IoT security answer must address them.
- ✅ **Extended AANP** = Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Privacy. Add **Data Freshness** and **Trust** for full marks.
- ✅ **Mnemonic:** **"CIA AANP FT"** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Privacy, Freshness, Trust.
- ✅ **Layer Mapping (must memorize):**
  - *Perception* → Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Privacy.
  - *Network* → Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, **Freshness**.
  - *Application* → Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Availability, Privacy.
- ✅ **ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072** in security strength. ECC is **~19× more energy-efficient** and **~12× smaller in key size**.
- ✅ **AES-128-CCM** = standard symmetric cipher for IoT bulk data (provides *both* confidentiality and integrity).
- ✅ **HMAC-SHA-256** = standard message authentication for IoT.
- ✅ **DTLS 1.3** over CoAP = standard secure transport for constrained IoT.
- ✅ **Secure Boot Chain:** ROM → Stage-1 → Kernel → App → Runtime Attestation. Each stage cryptographically verifies the next.
- ✅ **Mirai Botnet (2016)** = canonical example of *default-credential IoT vulnerability* — always cite in authentication answers.
- ✅ **Energy Cost (must memorize):** AES-128 ≈ 0.3 µJ/block, ECC-256 sign ≈ 0.5 mJ, RSA-2048 sign ≈ 9.5 mJ.
- ✅ **PUFs (Physical Unclonable Functions)** = hardware-based device identity for anti-counterfeiting and key storage.
- ✅ **Replay Attack Defense** = Nonces + Sequence Numbers + Timestamps (mandatory for actuator commands).
- ✅ **RBAC vs ABAC:** RBAC = role-based (simpler, KTU-preferred); ABAC = attribute-based (finer-grained, policy-driven).
- ✅ **GDPR Article 25 (Privacy by Design)** = regulatory anchor for IoT privacy requirements.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-131A** mandates **≥ 128-bit symmetric keys** for new IoT deployments.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $R_{avail} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. Tier-III standard = 99.982%.
- ✅ **Cross-Layer Trust** = the *most-missed* KTU point — always mention that trust cannot be enforced at a single layer alone.

> **Final Examiner Advice:** When answering any KTU 2024 IoT security question, **always (1) identify the layer, (2) name the CIA pillar(s) at risk, (3) propose a lightweight counter-mechanism, and (4) cite a real-world IoT example (Mirai, Stuxnet, smart-grid, healthcare).** This four-step structure is what distinguishes a 14/14 answer from a 7/14 answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threat Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Threat Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Threat Analysis** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) is a systematic, engineering-grade methodology used to identify, classify, prioritize, and evaluate the potential security threats that can compromise the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**) of an IoT ecosystem. It is a critical sub-process of the broader **Risk Management Lifecycle** and forms the foundation of secure IoT system design, as mandated by NIST SP 800-183, OWASP IoT Top 10, and ETSI EN 303 645 standards referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Threat Analysis is the structured process of enumerating potential adversaries, identifying attack surfaces, modeling attack vectors, assessing the likelihood and impact of compromise, and deriving mitigation requirements for IoT systems operating across the perception, network, and application layers.*

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Threat Analysis

| Pillar | Formal Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | Asset & Adversary Enumeration | Catalog devices, data, and threat actors |
| **Assessment** | Likelihood × Impact Modeling | Quantify or qualify the severity |
| **Mitigation** | Countermeasure Mapping | Assign controls and residual risk |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Home as a Fortified Castle

Imagine a **smart home** filled with IoT devices — smart locks, CCTV cameras, voice assistants, and connected thermostats. Now imagine you are the homeowner who wants to protect this home.

- **Assets** → Your jewelry, documents, family members (the *data* and *physical entities* in IoT).
- **Threats** → Burglars, hackers, curious neighbors (the *adversaries*).
- **Vulnerabilities** → Unlocked back window, default passwords on cameras, weak Wi-Fi password.
- **Impact** → Loss of privacy, financial damage, physical harm.
- **Likelihood** → How probable is it that a burglar targets your home *given* the vulnerability exists?

**Threat Analysis** is the act of walking around your digital "castle," listing every door, window, and vent (the *attack surface*), and deciding which ones need a stronger lock, an alarm, or a guard dog (the *countermeasures*). Just as a homeowner wouldn't leave the front door wide open, an IoT engineer cannot deploy a device without understanding the threats it faces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Beginners:** Threat analysis answers three simple questions:
> 1. *What can go wrong?*
> 2. *How bad is it if it goes wrong?*
> 3. *What do we do to prevent it?*

### 1.4 The CIA Triad — The Universal Yardstick

Every threat in IoT is ultimately evaluated against its potential damage to:

$$\text{CIA Triad} = \{ \text{Confidentiality}, \text{Integrity}, \text{Availability} \}$$

- **Confidentiality (C)** → Can an unauthorized party read sensor data? (e.g., a hacker listening to a baby monitor).
- **Integrity (I)** → Can an adversary alter commands or readings? (e.g., spoofing a smart meter to show lower consumption).
- **Availability (A)** → Can the system be knocked offline? (e.g., a DDoS attack on a smart city's traffic lights).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking students to map a given attack to the CIA element it violates. Always mention the *specific* asset and the *specific* CIA property in your answers to score full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Threat Analysis Pipeline (5-Stage Logic Decomposition)

A rigorous threat analysis is not guesswork — it follows a deterministic pipeline:

1. **Asset Identification** → Enumerate every IoT device, data flow, protocol, and interface. *Why?* You cannot protect what you have not inventoried.
2. **Threat Agent Profiling** → Define the adversary: script kiddie, insider, nation-state, malware botnet. *Why?* Different actors have different motivations, capabilities, and resources.
3. **Vulnerability Discovery** → Map attack surfaces: firmware bugs, weak authentication, open ports, insecure APIs. *Why?* Vulnerabilities are the *entry points*.
4. **Likelihood & Impact Assessment** → Score each threat using quantitative or qualitative scales. *Why?* To prioritize limited engineering and budget resources.
5. **Countermeasure Specification & Residual Risk Acceptance** → Propose controls (technical, administrative, physical) and document the accepted residual risk. *Why?* No system is 100% secure; risk must be formally accepted.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **mathematical backbone** of quantitative threat analysis and are frequently tested in KTU Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Where $T$ = Threat Likelihood (0–1), $V$ = Vulnerability (0–1), $I$ = Impact (0–10). *Dimensionless scalar.* |
| 2 | **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Where $SLE$ = Single Loss Expectancy (₹), $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence (events/yr). *Units: ₹/year.* |
| 3 | **Single Loss Expectancy** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Where $AV$ = Asset Value (₹), $EF$ = Exposure Factor (0–1). *Units: ₹.* |
| 4 | **DREAD Score** | $D = \frac{D+R+E+A+10}{5}$ | Average of five 1–10 ratings; final DREAD = 1–10. *Dimensionless.* |
| 5 | **Risk Priority Number (FMEA)** | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection. *Range: 1–1000.* |
| 6 | **Shannon Threat Information** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Entropy of attack vector distribution; higher entropy = more diverse threat landscape. *Units: bits.* |
| 7 | **Return on Security Investment** | $ROSI = \frac{ALE_{\text{before}} - ALE_{\text{after}} - \text{Cost of Control}}{Cost of Control}$ | Justifies security spending to management. *Dimensionless ratio.* |
| 8 | **Mean Time to Compromise** | $MTTC = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{attack}}}$ | Where $\lambda$ is attack rate. *Units: hours.* |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $R = T + V + I$ (additive). This is **incorrect** in most threat models. Risk is the *product* of independent factors because a zero in any factor must yield zero risk (e.g., no vulnerability ⇒ no risk, even with high threat).

### 2.3 The STRIDE Threat Model (Microsoft Methodology)

STRIDE is the **de facto standard** taught in KTU 2024 scheme IoT modules. Each letter is a threat category:

| Letter | Threat | CIA Property Violated | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S** | **Spoofing** | Authentication | Cloning an RFID tag to bypass an access control |
| **T** | **Tampering** | Integrity | Modifying firmware of a smart meter over-the-air |
| **R** | **Repudiation** | Non-repudiation | A user denies issuing a remote door unlock command (logs were not signed) |
| **I** | **Information Disclosure** | Confidentiality | Eavesdropping on ZigBee traffic between a sensor and hub |
| **D** | **Denial of Service** | Availability | Jamming LoRaWAN frequencies to disable a smart farm |
| **E** | **Elevation of Privilege** | Authorization | Exploiting a buffer overflow in a camera to gain root shell |

### 2.4 The DREAD Qualitative Scoring System

Each threat is rated 1–10 on five dimensions:

- **D** — *Damage potential*: How much harm if the threat succeeds?
- **R** — *Reproducibility*: How easy is it to reproduce the attack?
- **E** — *Exploitability*: How much skill/effort is needed?
- **A** — *Affected users*: What percentage of users are impacted?
- **D** — *Discoverability*: How easy is it to find the vulnerability?

A common KTU trap: the *two D's* — **D**amage and **D**iscoverability — both start with D, so students often forget one.

### 2.5 Why Threat Analysis Matters in Real Engineering

In industry, threat analysis is a **compliance gate** for:
- ISO/IEC 27001 (Information Security Management)
- IEC 62443 (Industrial IoT/OT Security)
- ETSI EN 303 645 (Consumer IoT Cyber Security)
- India's **CERT-In directives (2022)** mandating IoT security incident reporting

A flawed threat analysis can lead to **catastrophic failures**: the Mirai botnet (2016) exploited default-credential IoT cameras to launch a 1.2 Tbps DDoS attack that crippled DNS provider Dyn, taking down Twitter, Netflix, and Reddit across the US East Coast. A proper threat analysis would have mandated unique, non-default credentials at the firmware level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Computing the DREAD Score for a Smart Camera Threat

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A smart home camera transmits video over an unencrypted Wi-Fi link. An attacker within 50 m can intercept the feed. The firmware has no authentication for the RTSP stream. Compute the DREAD score and classify the threat.

**Step 1 — Define each rating (1–10 scale):**

We assign ratings based on the engineering reality of the scenario:
- Damage potential: High (privacy breach, recordings leaked) → **$D_1 = 8$**
- Reproducibility: Very easy (kismet + VLC works) → **$R = 9$**
- Exploitability: Low skill needed (script available) → **$E = 9$**
- Affected users: All users with this camera model → **$A = 9$**
- Discoverability: Publicly documented → **$D_2 = 8$**

**Step 2 — Apply the DREAD summation formula:**

The Microsoft DREAD formula (a weighted sum) is:

$$\text{DREAD} = \frac{D_1 + R + E + A + D_2}{5}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$\text{DREAD} = \frac{8 + 9 + 9 + 9 + 8}{5}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the numerator:**

$$\text{DREAD} = \frac{43}{5}$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$\text{DREAD} = 8.6$$

**Step 6 — Threat classification:**

| Range | Severity |
|---|---|
| 1 – 3 | Low |
| 4 – 6 | Medium |
| 7 – 8 | **High ← 8.6 falls here** |
| 9 – 10 | Critical |

> **Conclusion:** The threat is classified as **HIGH** severity. The recommended mitigation is to enforce **TLS 1.3 encryption**, **digest authentication** for the RTSP stream, and **802.11w (PMF)** on the Wi-Fi link.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) for a Smart Meter

> **Problem Statement:**
> A smart electricity meter has an Asset Value of **₹50,000**. A successful cyberattack on the meter has an Exposure Factor of **0.6** (60% of the asset is lost). The historical ARO from the industry database is **0.25 events per year** (i.e., once every four years). Compute the SLE, ALE, and recommend a control budget.

**Step 1 — Recall the SLE formula:**

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Asset Value and Exposure Factor:**

$$SLE = 50{,}000 \times 0.6$$

**Step 3 — Compute SLE:**

$$SLE = 30{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 4 — Recall the ALE formula:**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$ALE = 30{,}000 \times 0.25$$

**Step 6 — Compute ALE:**

$$ALE = 7{,}500 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 7 — Justify the security control budget using ROSI:**

A proposed TPM-based secure boot costs **₹4,000 per meter** and reduces ARO from 0.25 to 0.05.

The new ALE is:

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = 30{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1{,}500 \text{ ₹/year}$$

The reduction in expected loss is:

$$\Delta ALE = 7{,}500 - 1{,}500 = 6{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

The Return on Security Investment is:

$$ROSI = \frac{\Delta ALE - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and solve:**

$$ROSI = \frac{6{,}000 - 4{,}000}{4{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{2{,}000}{4{,}000} = 0.50$$

> **Conclusion:** Every ₹1 spent on the TPM module returns ₹1.50 in avoided risk over the first year. The control is **economically justified**, and the engineering team should deploy it. ROSI > 0 indicates a positive return.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping STRIDE to IoT Protocol Stack

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **examination gold** for KTU Part B 14-mark questions on threat analysis. Memorize the mapping.

| IoT Layer | Protocol Examples | Spoofing | Tampering | Repudiation | Info Disclosure | DoS | Elevation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perception** | ZigBee, BLE, LoRa, RFID | Tag cloning | Sensor data forgery | Missing audit logs | Eavesdropping | Jamming | Firmware rootkit |
| **Network** | 6LoWPAN, RPL, MQTT | Rogue router | Route manipulation | Sinkhole attack | Traffic sniffing | RPL rank attack | Compromised gateway |
| **Application** | HTTP/CoAP APIs, Cloud | API key theft | DB injection | Log tampering | Data exfiltration | API flooding | Container escape |
| **Edge/Fog** | Docker, K8s | Image poisoning | Sidecar tamper | Audit gap | Side-channel | Resource exhaustion | Kernel exploit |

### 3.4 Risk Matrix Construction — Step-by-Step

A **5×5 Risk Matrix** is the most common visualization in KTU question papers. Construction steps:

**Step 1 — Define Likelihood scale (L):** Rare (1), Unlikely (2), Possible (3), Likely (4), Almost Certain (5).

**Step 2 — Define Impact scale (I):** Insignificant (1), Minor (2), Moderate (3), Major (4), Catastrophic (5).

**Step 3 — Compute Risk Score for each threat:**

$$R = L \times I$$

**Step 4 — Categorize:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &\in [1, 4] \rightarrow \text{Low} \\
R &\in [5, 9] \rightarrow \text{Medium} \\
R &\in [10, 15] \rightarrow \text{High} \\
R &\in [16, 25] \rightarrow \text{Critical}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Example threat assignment:**

| Threat | L | I | R = L×I | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Default password on camera | 5 | 4 | **20** | Critical |
| Physical tampering of sensor | 2 | 3 | **6** | Medium |
| Cloud API misconfiguration | 3 | 5 | **15** | High |
| Firmware OTA downgrade | 2 | 4 | **8** | Medium |

> **Engineering Insight:** The "default password" threat scores 20 (Critical) — this is precisely why ETSI EN 303 645 mandates provision of a unique password or forces a password change on first boot.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Threat Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the end-to-end threat analysis workflow, from asset discovery to residual risk acceptance. It maps cleanly to the NIST SP 800-30 risk assessment process and is exactly the kind of figure KTU examiners reward with full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: IoT System Definition] --> A1[Step 1: Asset Enumeration]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Define Trust Boundaries]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Identify Threat Agents]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Map Attack Surfaces]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Apply STRIDE per Asset]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Score via DREAD or CVSS]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Build Attack Trees]
    A7 --> A8{DREAD >= 7?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Step 8a: Assign Critical Controls]
    A8 -- No --> A10[Step 8b: Add to Watch List]
    A9 --> A11[Step 9: Compute Residual Risk]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12{Residual Acceptable?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Step 10: Document and Sign Off]
    A12 -- No --> A14[Step 10b: Loop - Add Layered Controls]
    A14 --> A6
    A13 --> A15[End: Secure Deployment]
```

### 4.2 The STRIDE-Threat Matrix (Block-Level View)

This block diagram visualizes how each STRIDE category breaks down into IoT-specific sub-threats, which is useful for exam answers requiring concrete examples.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STRIDE[STRIDE Threat Model] --> S[S - Spoofing]
    STRIDE --> T[T - Tampering]
    STRIDE --> R[R - Repudiation]
    STRIDE --> I[I - Info Disclosure]
    STRIDE --> D[D - Denial of Service]
    STRIDE --> E[E - Elevation of Privilege]

    S --> S1[RFID Cloning]
    S --> S2[MAC Address Spoofing]
    S --> S3[MQTT Credential Theft]

    T --> T1[Firmware Modification]
    T --> T2[Sensor Data Falsification]
    T --> T3[Replay Attack]

    R --> R1[Unsigned Logs]
    R --> R2[Clock Skew Attack]
    R --> R3[Missing Audit Trail]

    I --> I1[Eavesdropping on BLE]
    I --> I2[Cloud Bucket Misconfig]
    I --> I3[Side Channel Leakage]

    D --> D1[LoRa Jamming]
    D --> D2[MQTT Broker Flood]
    D --> D3[Battery Drain Attack]

    E --> E1[Buffer Overflow in Camera]
    E --> E2[Container Escape in Edge]
    E --> E3[Hardcoded Backdoor]
```

### 4.3 Generic IoT Attack Tree — "Compromise the Smart Home Gateway"

An **attack tree** is a hierarchical decomposition of how an attacker could achieve a goal. The root is the attacker's objective; the leaves are the individual attack steps. This block diagram abstracts the structure for the KTU 2024 Module 4 context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GOAL[OR - Compromise Smart Home Gateway]

    GOAL --> P1[AND - Physical Access]
    GOAL --> P2[AND - Network Access]
    GOAL --> P3[OR - Remote Exploit]

    P1 --> P11[Open UART via Case Opening]
    P1 --> P12[Steal Device from Mailbox]

    P2 --> P21[Join Open Wi-Fi]
    P2 --> P22[ARP Spoofing on LAN]
    P2 --> P23[Bluetooth Pairing Sniff]

    P3 --> P31[Exploit CVE in Web UI]
    P3 --> P32[Brute Force Default Password]
    P3 --> P33[Malicious OTA Firmware]
    P3 --> P34[DNS Rebinding Attack]

    P11 --> ROOT[Gain Root Shell on Gateway]
    P12 --> ROOT
    P21 --> ROOT
    P22 --> ROOT
    P23 --> ROOT
    P31 --> ROOT
    P32 --> ROOT
    P33 --> ROOT
    P34 --> ROOT
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the exam:** Notice the use of **AND** and **OR** gates — this is the formal notation of attack trees. *AND* means all children must succeed; *OR* means any one child suffices. KTU examiners often award an extra mark if students use this notation correctly.

### 4.4 Risk Treatment Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RISK[Identified Risk] --> Q1{Risk Score >= 16?}
    Q1 -- Yes Critical --> A1[Mitigate Immediately]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Risk Score 10 to 15?}
    Q2 -- Yes High --> A2[Mitigate within Sprint]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Risk Score 5 to 9?}
    Q3 -- Yes Medium --> A3[Schedule for Next Release]
    Q3 -- No --> A4[Accept and Document]
    A1 --> CTRL[Apply Layered Controls: Prevent, Detect, Respond]
    A2 --> CTRL
    A3 --> CTRL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Define threat analysis. List any four threat categories in the STRIDE model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Threat analysis is the systematic process of identifying, classifying, and evaluating potential security threats to an IoT system in terms of likelihood and impact. The STRIDE model, developed by Microsoft, categorizes threats into six classes:
1. **S** — Spoofing (identity forgery)
2. **T** — Tampering (data modification)
3. **R** — Repudiation (denial of action)
4. **I** — Information Disclosure (data leakage)
5. **D** — Denial of Service (availability loss)
6. **E** — Elevation of Privilege (unauthorized access escalation)

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for four correct STRIDE categories with brief explanation.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *What is the CIA Triad? How is it relevant to IoT threat analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CIA Triad is the foundational model of information security consisting of:
- **Confidentiality** — Ensuring that data is accessed only by authorized parties.
- **Integrity** — Ensuring that data is not altered in an unauthorized manner.
- **Availability** — Ensuring that systems and data are accessible when needed.

In IoT threat analysis, every identified threat is evaluated based on which CIA property it compromises. For example, an **eavesdropping attack on a ZigBee smart lock** violates *Confidentiality*, while a **jamming attack on a LoRaWAN smart farm** violates *Availability*. The CIA Triad provides a consistent, vendor-neutral framework to prioritize mitigation efforts.

*(Award 1 mark each for C, I, A definition; 1 bonus mark for IoT-specific example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice I]**

**(a)** Explain the DREAD scoring methodology in detail. Compute the DREAD score for the following IoT threat and classify its severity.
*Threat:* A medical IoT infusion pump accepts unauthenticated firmware updates over Bluetooth. An attacker within 10 m can flash arbitrary firmware and alter drug dosage.
*Ratings:* Damage = 10, Reproducibility = 7, Exploitability = 6, Affected Users = 8, Discoverability = 9. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the IoT attack tree methodology. Construct a partial attack tree for the goal *"Compromise a Smart Home Voice Assistant"*. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — DREAD Definition (2 marks):**
DREAD is a qualitative risk-scoring model proposed by Microsoft. It evaluates each threat on five dimensions rated 1–10:
- **D**amage potential
- **R**eproducibility
- **E**xploitability
- **A**ffected users
- **D**iscoverability

**Step 2 — Formula recall (1 mark):**

$$D = \frac{D_1 + R + E + A + D_2}{5}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution (2 marks):**

$$D = \frac{10 + 7 + 6 + 8 + 9}{5}$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation (1 mark):**

$$D = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0$$

**Step 5 — Severity classification (1 mark):**
A DREAD score of **8.0** falls in the **HIGH** category (7–8). The threat is critical from a safety perspective (medical device) and must be mitigated via **signed firmware**, **secure boot**, and **Bluetooth pairing with out-of-band authentication (NFC tap)**.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define attack trees (2 marks):**
An attack tree is a hierarchical, graphical model that decomposes an attacker's top-level goal into sub-goals and atomic attack steps, connected by **AND** and **OR** logical gates. The root node is the ultimate objective; leaf nodes are concrete attack vectors.

**Step 2 — Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GOAL[OR Goal: Compromise Smart Home Voice Assistant]

    GOAL --> P1[AND Physical Proximity]
    GOAL --> P2[OR Remote Exploit]
    GOAL --> P3[OR Social Engineering]

    P1 --> P11[Open Microphone Always-Listening]
    P1 --> P12[Infer Commands from Audio]

    P2 --> P21[Exploit Web Dashboard CVE]
    P2 --> P22[Default Cloud Account Password]
    P2 --> P23[Spoof Wake Word using ML Model]

    P3 --> P31[Phishing for Voice Pin]
    P3 --> P32[Malicious Third-Party Skill]
```

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation (2 marks):**
The tree reveals that the voice assistant has **three independent attack paths**, meaning defense-in-depth is required. Mitigations include: (i) mute button with hardware disconnect, (ii) signed firmware with mandatory MFA on the cloud account, and (iii) curated third-party skill marketplace with static analysis.

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice II]**

**(a)** Define Risk in the context of IoT security. Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) and Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) for a smart factory PLC whose compromise causes a production line shutdown. Given: Asset Value = ₹10,00,000; Exposure Factor = 0.8; ARO = 0.5. Justify a security control costing ₹2,00,000 that reduces ARO to 0.1 by computing ROSI. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the STRIDE threat model. For each of the six STRIDE categories, give **one specific IoT example** and state the **CIA property** violated. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Define Risk (1 mark):**
Risk is the product of the likelihood of a threat exploiting a vulnerability and the resulting impact on the asset:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Step 2 — Compute SLE (2 marks):**

$$SLE = AV \times EF = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE before control (1 mark):**

$$ALE_{\text{before}} = SLE \times ARO = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ALE after control (1 mark):**

$$ALE_{\text{after}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}$$

**Step 5 — Compute ROSI (2 marks):**

$$ROSI = \frac{(4{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000) - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = \frac{3{,}20{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 0.60$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** ROSI = 0.60 (i.e., ₹1.60 returned per ₹1 invested). The control is economically justified.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — STRIDE definition (1 mark):**
STRIDE is Microsoft's mnemonic-based threat classification model. Each letter denotes a threat class and the CIA property it primarily attacks.

**Step 2 — Six categories (6 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Letter | Threat | CIA Property | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| S | Spoofing | Authentication | Cloning a ZigBee smart key |
| T | Tampering | Integrity | Modifying sensor reading on a weather station |
| R | Repudiation | Non-repudiation | Driver denies issuing a remote unlock via mobile app |
| I | Information Disclosure | Confidentiality | Eavesdropping on BLE traffic of a fitness band |
| D | Denial of Service | Availability | Jamming a LoRaWAN smart agriculture deployment |
| E | Elevation of Privilege | Authorization | Exploiting a buffer overflow in a CCTV NVR to gain root |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Threat Analysis Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the two D's in DREAD** — Damage and Discoverability. Examiners *will* deduct a mark if a student uses "Damage" twice.
> 2. **Using additive risk** — Writing $R = T + V + I$ instead of the multiplicative $R = T \times V \times I$. Full mark loss on risk definition questions.
> 3. **Forgetting units in ALE** — Always state **₹/year**. Many students write "₹" alone and lose 0.5 marks.
> 4. **No AND/OR notation in attack trees** — Vague trees without logical gates lose at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Mapping STRIDE wrongly** — A common error is putting *Spoofing* under "Integrity." Spoofing is fundamentally an *Authentication* failure.
> 6. **Skipping residual risk** — When asked for mitigation, students list controls but forget to compute/document the *residual* risk after controls. KTU examiners expect this.
> 7. **No IoT-specific examples** — Generic answers (e.g., "hacker breaks into server") score lower than IoT-specific scenarios (e.g., "attacker spoofs a BLE heart-rate monitor to inject false ECG data into a clinical dashboard").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Threat Analysis = Identify → Assess → Mitigate** of security threats in IoT systems.
- ✅ **CIA Triad** is the universal evaluation yardstick: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ✅ **STRIDE** = Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, DoS, Elevation of Privilege.
- ✅ **DREAD** = Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, Discoverability (note the two D's).
- ✅ **Risk Formula** = $R = T \times V \times I$ (multiplicative, not additive).
- ✅ **ALE = SLE × ARO**; **SLE = AV × EF**; both have ₹/year and ₹ units respectively.
- ✅ **ROSI > 0 ⇒ control is justified** economically.
- ✅ **Attack Trees** use **AND/OR gates**; root = attacker's goal; leaves = atomic attack steps.
- ✅ **IoT Layers** for threat mapping: Perception, Network, Application (and Edge/Fog in modern stacks).
- ✅ **Real-world relevance**: Mirai botnet (2016), Verkada camera breach (2021), OWASP IoT Top 10.
- ✅ **Compliance frameworks**: NIST SP 800-183, ETSI EN 303 645, IEC 62443, India's CERT-In 2022 directives.
- ✅ **Standard score ranges** to memorize: DREAD 1–3 Low, 4–6 Medium, 7–8 High, 9–10 Critical.
- ✅ **Always use IoT-specific examples** in KTU answers — generic "computer security" answers lose marks.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** principle: apply Prevent → Detect → Respond controls in layers.
- ✅ **Residual risk** must be documented and formally accepted by stakeholders.

> **End of Module 4 — Threat Analysis Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEMRT525 — IoT and Applications*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Tomography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Security Tomography ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Security Tomography** is a multi-layered, measurement-driven methodology that constructs a comprehensive, holistic view of the security state of a distributed system (such as an IoT network) by **collecting, fusing, and correlating security observations from multiple spatially and logically distributed monitoring points** across different architectural layers (device, network, application, and data). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Security tomography is the process of inferring the internal security posture of a network or IoT system by analyzing externally observable measurements obtained from distributed sensors, without requiring direct access to each individual node.* It is the **security analog of medical computed tomography (CT) scanning** and **network tomography**. The term is derived from the Greek word **"tomos"** meaning "slice" or "section", indicating that the system is observed in multiple cross-sectional slices, which are then mathematically reconstructed into a unified security image. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a doctor trying to diagnose a patient with a possible internal injury. * The doctor cannot look inside the body directly. * Instead, the **CT (Computed Tomography) scanner** takes **X-ray slices from many angles** around the patient. * A computer then **combines all these slices** to construct a complete **3D internal image**. **Security Tomography works exactly the same way for a network/IoT system:** * The "patient" is the **IoT network**. * The "X-ray slices" are **security measurements** (packet counts, anomaly scores, log events, delay profiles) collected by distributed sensors/monitors. * The "3D image" is the **reconstructed security state** showing where attacks, vulnerabilities, or compromised nodes exist. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this needed in IoT?** > IoT networks can contain **thousands of heterogeneous, resource-constrained devices** (sensors, actuators, gateways). It is **practically impossible to instrument every single node** directly. Security tomography allows us to **infer** the internal security state using only **edge measurements**, just as a doctor infers internal health from a CT scan. ### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Monitor Node** | A trusted node that observes and records security-related metrics on the links/nodes it can see. | | **Origin-Destination (OD) Pair** | A source-sink pair whose traffic behavior is analyzed to infer internal network state. | | **Probe Packet** | A specially crafted packet sent to elicit measurable responses (delay, loss, success/failure) for security inference. | | **Loss Tomography** | Inferring link-level packet drops (which may indicate DoS attacks) from end-to-end measurements. | | **Delay Tomography** | Inferring queuing delays on internal links (which may indicate congestion-based attacks) from end-to-end timing. | | **Boolean Tomography** | Binary classification of links as "attack-corrupted" (1) or "clean" (0). | | **Data Fusion** | The process of combining observations from multiple sensors/monitors into a single coherent security picture. | | **Inference Engine** | The mathematical/statistical core that reconstructs internal state from observed measurements. | | **Attack Surface** | The complete set of points where an unauthorized entry could occur; tomography maps this surface. | | **Hypothesis Testing** | Statistical method used to decide whether a link is compromised based on observed metrics. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metric:** The reconstructed security image is typically expressed as a **probability map $P(\ell_k \text{ is compromised})$** for every internal link $\ell_k$, with values in the closed interval $[0, 1]$. A value of **1.0** means **certain compromise**; a value of **0.0** means **certainly clean**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture of Security Tomography The process of security tomography follows a structured pipeline: 1. **Sensor/Monitor Deployment** * Place trusted monitoring agents at strategic network boundaries (e.g., gateways, edge routers, cloud ingress points). * In IoT, these are typically the **IoT gateways** and **cloud-side aggregators**, since end-nodes are too constrained. 2. **Measurement Collection (The "Slices")** * Collect metrics across **multiple layers** simultaneously: * **Device Layer:** Battery drain, firmware integrity, sensor reading anomalies. * **Network Layer:** Packet loss, delay, jitter, hop counts, route changes. * **Application Layer:** API call failures, authentication failures, unusual request patterns. * **Data Layer:** Outliers in sensed data, integrity hash mismatches. 3. **Statistical Modeling** * Model the relationship between internal link state and observable measurements using **probabilistic graphical models**, most commonly **Markov Random Fields (MRF)** or **Bayesian Networks**. 4. **Inference (The "Reconstruction")** * Use **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** or **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** estimation to deduce the most likely internal security state from the observed external measurements. 5. **Visualization & Response** * Render the inferred state as a **heat-map / tomographic image** of the network. * Trigger automated responses (isolation, re-authentication, patching). ### 2.2 Classification of Security Tomography | Type | What it Reconstructs | Typical Technique | |---|---|---| | **Loss Tomography** | Which internal links are dropping packets (DoS indicator) | Boolean / probabilistic inference on loss rates | | **Delay Tomography** | Which internal links have abnormal latency | Variance-based statistical inference | | **Topology Tomography** | Logical topology + security-critical paths | End-to-end probe-based tree discovery | | **Cross-Layer Tomography** | Combined view across device + network + app layers | Multi-modal sensor fusion, MRF | | **Boolean Tomography** | Binary "compromised / clean" label per link | Maximum likelihood on binary variables | | **Adversarial-Aware Tomography** | State under active attacker manipulation | Robust statistics, game-theoretic inference | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme derivations and numerical questions. | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P(L \mid M) = \dfrac{P(M \mid L)\, P(L)}{P(M)}$ | **Bayes' Theorem** — posterior probability of link state $L$ given measurement $M$ | Core of all Bayesian tomographic inference | | 2 | $\hat{L}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{L}\, P(M \mid L)$ | **Maximum Likelihood Estimate** of link state | Used when no prior is assumed | | 3 | $\hat{L}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{L}\, P(M \mid L)\, P(L)$ | **Maximum A Posteriori** estimate | Used when prior $P(L)$ is known | | 4 | $P(\ell \text{ compromised}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - p_i)$ | **Independent link compromise** probability from $n$ independent evidence sources | Sensor fusion formula | | 5 | $\text{Detection Confidence} = 1 - \beta$ | Where $\beta$ is the probability of **Type II error (miss)** | KTU standard detection metric | | 6 | $\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate of compromise detection | ROC analysis | | 7 | $\text{Specificity} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate (clean detection) | ROC analysis | | 8 | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | **Shannon Entropy** — uncertainty in link state | Used to quantify residual uncertainty after tomography | | 9 | $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q) = \sum_i P(i) \log \dfrac{P(i)}{Q(i)}$ | **KL Divergence** — distance between true and inferred distribution | Measures tomographic accuracy | | 10 | $X = A \cdot L + \epsilon$ | **Linear tomographic model**: measurement $X$ = sensing matrix $A$ × link state $L$ + noise $\epsilon$ | Foundational linear algebra view | | 11 | $CRB(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{I(\theta)}$ | **Cramér-Rao Bound** — lower bound on estimator variance | Theoretical accuracy limit | | 12 | $N_{monitors} \ge k \cdot \log_2 N_{links}$ | Minimum monitor count for identifiability (order sense) | Deployment planning rule | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** > In real-world IoT deployments (smart cities, industrial IoT, healthcare IoT), security tomography enables a **single Security Operations Center (SOC)** to monitor **millions of devices** by placing only a **few hundred monitor nodes** at strategic points, reducing monitoring cost and overhead by **2–3 orders of magnitude**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Bayesian Tomographic Inference for a Single Link **Scenario.** Consider a single internal link $\ell$ in an IoT mesh network. Let $L \in \{0, 1\}$ denote the link state, where $L = 1$ means "compromised by an attacker" and $L = 0$ means "clean". A monitor at the gateway observes a measurement $M$ (e.g., a packet-loss indicator) that depends stochastically on $L$. **Step 1 — Define the likelihood function.** We are given the conditional probability of the measurement given the state: $$P(M = 1 \mid L = 1) = p_d \quad \text{(probability of detection when actually compromised)}$$ $$P(M = 1 \mid L = 0) = p_{fa} \quad \text{(probability of false alarm when clean)}$$ These are the **sensitivity** and **false-alarm rate** of the monitor. **Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem.** We want the **posterior probability** that the link is compromised given the observed measurement: $$P(L = 1 \mid M = 1) = \frac{P(M = 1 \mid L = 1)\, P(L = 1)}{P(M = 1)}$$ **Step 3 — Expand the denominator using the law of total probability.** $$P(M = 1) = P(M = 1 \mid L = 1)\, P(L = 1) + P(M = 1 \mid L = 0)\, P(L = 0)$$ $$P(M = 1) = p_d \cdot \pi + p_{fa} \cdot (1 - \pi)$$ where we have used the prior $\pi = P(L = 1)$ (the *a priori* probability that any given link is compromised, based on threat intelligence). **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.** $$P(L = 1 \mid M = 1) = \frac{p_d \cdot \pi}{p_d \cdot \pi + p_{fa} \cdot (1 - \pi)}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** This is the **posterior compromise probability** for the link, after observing a single alarm $M = 1$ from the monitor. **Step 6 — Multi-monitor extension.** If we have $n$ independent monitors, each producing an alarm $M_i$, the joint posterior under independence is: $$P(L = 1 \mid M_1, \dots, M_n) = \frac{\pi \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L = 1)}{\pi \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L = 1) + (1 - \pi) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L = 0)}$$ This is the **sensor fusion** formula that allows multiple distributed IoT gateways to collaboratively reconstruct the security state. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem.** An IoT gateway monitors a critical link. Historical threat intelligence gives a prior compromise probability $\pi = 0.05$. The monitor's detection probability is $p_d = 0.95$ and false-alarm rate is $p_{fa} = 0.10$. The monitor raises an alarm. Find the **posterior probability** that the link is actually compromised. **Solution.** Step 1: Write down the known values. $$P(L = 1) = \pi = 0.05, \quad P(L = 0) = 0.95$$ $$P(M = 1 \mid L = 1) = p_d = 0.95, \quad P(M = 1 \mid L = 0) = p_{fa} = 0.10$$ Step 2: Compute the numerator of Bayes' formula. $$\text{Numerator} = p_d \cdot \pi = 0.95 \times 0.05 = 0.0475$$ Step 3: Compute the denominator. $$\text{Denominator} = (0.95)(0.05) + (0.10)(0.95) = 0.0475 + 0.0950 = 0.1425$$ Step 4: Divide. $$P(L = 1 \mid M = 1) = \frac{0.0475}{0.1425} \approx 0.3333$$ **Interpretation:** Even with a high-quality monitor ($p_d = 0.95$), a single alarm only raises the compromise probability from **5% to ~33%** because the **low base rate** dominates. This is the classic **base-rate fallacy** — and is precisely why **multiple independent monitors** are required for reliable tomography. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** If 3 independent monitors each raise an alarm, the posterior jumps to **> 99%**, justifying the multi-monitor deployment strategy in IoT. ### 3.3 Derivation: Linear Algebraic View of Tomography **Step 1 — Model setup.** Let there be $N$ internal links with state vector $L = [L_1, L_2, \dots, L_N]^T$ (each $L_i \in \{0, 1\}$). Let $M$ monitors produce a measurement vector $X = [X_1, \dots, X_M]^T$. **Step 2 — Sensing matrix.** Define a binary sensing matrix $A \in \{0, 1\}^{M \times N}$ where $A_{ij} = 1$ if monitor $i$ observes traffic on link $j$. **Step 3 — Linear model.** $$X = A \cdot L + \epsilon$$ where $\epsilon$ is additive Gaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$. **Step 4 — MLE solution (continuous relaxation).** $$\hat{L} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T X$$ This is the **least-squares pseudo-inverse** estimate. **Step 5 — Identifiability condition.** The state is uniquely recoverable only if $A$ has **full column rank**, i.e., $\text{rank}(A) = N$. This gives the rule: $$M \ge N \quad \text{(need at least as many monitors as links)}$$ In practice, by exploiting **sparsity** of attacks, the bound is much weaker: $$M \ge \mathcal{O}(k \log N)$$ where $k$ is the number of compromised links (sparse recovery via compressed sensing). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End Architecture of Security Tomography in IoT ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SOURCES["DATA SOURCES - THE TOMOGRAPHIC SLICES"] direction LR DEV[DEVICE LAYER METRICS
Battery, Firmware, Sensor Outliers] NET[NETWORK LAYER METRICS
Packet Loss, Delay, Jitter] APP[APPLICATION LAYER METRICS
Auth Failures, API Anomalies] DAT[DATA LAYER METRICS
Integrity Hash, Outliers] end subgraph MONITORS["DISTRIBUTED MONITOR NODES"] direction LR MON1[IoT Gateway 1
Edge Monitor] MON2[IoT Gateway 2
Edge Monitor] MON3[Cloud Aggregator
Central Monitor] end subgraph FUSION["DATA FUSION AND INFERENCE LAYER"] direction TB NORM[Normalization Engine] BAYES[Bayesian Inference Module
Posterior Computation] MRF[Markov Random Field Solver
Spatial Correlation] DEC[Decision Module
MAP / MLE Output] end subgraph OUTPUT["RECONSTRUCTED SECURITY IMAGE"] direction LR HEAT[2D Heat-Map of IoT Network
Red = Compromised Links] ALERT[Automated Alerts to SOC] RESP[Automated Response
Isolate, Re-auth, Patch] end DEV --> MON1 NET --> MON1 NET --> MON2 APP --> MON3 DAT --> MON3 APP --> MON2 DAT --> MON1 MON1 --> NORM MON2 --> NORM MON3 --> NORM NORM --> BAYES BAYES --> MRF MRF --> DEC DEC --> HEAT DEC --> ALERT ALERT --> RESP ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied * All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `MON1`, `BAYES`, `HEAT`). * No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone node names. * Node labels are **raw uppercase alphanumeric text** only, with `
` for line breaks — no bold, italics, or HTML tables. ### 4.3 Information Flow Description (for KTU descriptive answers) The diagram above depicts the **complete tomographic pipeline** in an IoT environment. The system functions in five sequential stages: 1. **Multi-layer Data Acquisition:** The four sources (device, network, application, data) provide the "raw slices". 2. **Distributed Monitoring:** Edge gateways and cloud aggregators act as trusted observers, just like X-ray detectors positioned around the patient. 3. **Normalization & Fusion:** Raw metrics are normalized to a common scale and passed into the inference core. 4. **Bayesian + MRF Inference:** The mathematical reconstruction engine computes the posterior probability of compromise for every internal link. 5. **Visualization & Response:** A 2D heat-map is generated for human analysts, while automated alerts trigger defensive actions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Security Tomography. Why is it particularly important in IoT networks?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Security Tomography is a measurement-driven methodology that reconstructs the internal security state of a distributed system by combining observations from multiple, spatially distributed monitoring points across different layers. (2 marks)* It is particularly important in IoT because IoT systems consist of thousands of resource-constrained, heterogeneous devices that cannot be individually instrumented with heavy security agents.
