Security tomography allows the overall security posture to be inferred using only a small number of strategically placed edge monitors (IoT gateways, cloud aggregators), making large-scale IoT security monitoring feasible and economical. *(1 mark)* --- #### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between Loss Tomography and Delay Tomography with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Loss Tomography | Delay Tomography | |---|---|---| | **Reconstructs** | Packet loss on internal links | Queuing delay on internal links | | **Indicator of** | DoS / link-failure attacks | Congestion-based / wormhole attacks | | **Measurement** | End-to-end success/failure of probes | End-to-end round-trip timing of probes | | **Example** | Inferring which link in a smart-home mesh is silently dropping packets sent from a sensor to the cloud | Inferring which link introduces abnormal latency during a DDoS reflection attack | *(1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for the distinguishing example.)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** Explain the architecture and operational pipeline of Security Tomography in an IoT environment, with a neat block diagram. List the four layers from which tomographic "slices" are obtained. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A monitor placed at the IoT gateway has a detection probability $p_d = 0.92$ and false-alarm rate $p_{fa} = 0.08$. The prior probability that an internal link is compromised is $\pi = 0.04$. If the monitor raises an alarm, compute the posterior probability that the link is actually compromised. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** The architecture of Security Tomography consists of five sequential stages: 1. **Multi-layer Data Acquisition (1 mark):** Measurements are collected from four layers — *Device layer* (battery drain, firmware integrity, sensor outliers), *Network layer* (packet loss, delay, jitter, hop counts), *Application layer* (authentication failures, unusual API calls), and *Data layer* (integrity hash mismatches, statistical outliers in sensed values). 2. **Distributed Monitor Deployment (1 mark):** Trusted monitor nodes are placed at IoT gateways and cloud aggregators, since end-nodes are too resource-constrained to run security agents. 3. **Data Fusion (1 mark):** The multi-layer, multi-monitor measurements are normalized and combined into a unified observation vector. 4. **Inference Engine (2 marks):** The most likely internal security state is reconstructed using Bayesian inference (computing $P(L \mid M)$ for each link) or Maximum Likelihood Estimation. Markov Random Fields are used to capture spatial correlation among neighboring links. 5. **Visualization and Response (1 mark):** The output is a 2D heat-map of the network showing compromise probabilities, plus automated alerts fed to the Security Operations Center. **Block diagram (1 mark):** A labelled 4-block diagram showing *Data Sources → Monitors → Fusion/Inference → Security Image & Response* (any equivalent labelled diagram accepted). --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $\pi = P(L = 1) = 0.04$, $p_d = 0.92$, $p_{fa} = 0.08$, and $M = 1$ (alarm observed). **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write Bayes' theorem. $$P(L = 1 \mid M = 1) = \frac{P(M = 1 \mid L = 1)\, P(L = 1)}{P(M = 1)}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute the given values into the numerator. $$\text{Numerator} = 0.92 \times 0.04 = 0.0368$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Expand the denominator using the total probability law. $$P(M = 1) = (0.92)(0.04) + (0.08)(0.96) = 0.0368 + 0.0768 = 0.1136$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute the posterior. $$P(L = 1 \mid M = 1) = \frac{0.0368}{0.1136} \approx 0.3239$$ **Step 5 (2 marks):** **Comment.** A single alarm from one monitor raises the compromise probability from **4% to only ~32.4%**. This illustrates the **base-rate fallacy**: even a high-quality monitor cannot produce a high-confidence verdict in isolation. To achieve a posterior of, say, $> 0.99$, multiple **independent** monitors are required. This justifies the **multi-monitor sensor-fusion** architecture of security tomography. --- #### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]` **(a)** Discuss the Linear Algebraic model of security tomography. Define the sensing matrix and state the identifiability condition for unique recovery of the link-state vector. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With reference to Bayesian inference, derive the multi-monitor sensor-fusion formula for $n$ independent monitors, each producing an alarm. Explain why this is essential for IoT security. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Model setup (2 marks):** Let $L = [L_1, \dots, L_N]^T$ be the state vector of $N$ internal links (each $L_i \in \{0, 1\}$). Let $X = [X_1, \dots, X_M]^T$ be the measurement vector from $M$ monitors. 2. **Sensing matrix (2 marks):** The binary matrix $A \in \{0, 1\}^{M \times N}$ has $A_{ij} = 1$ if monitor $i$ directly observes traffic on link $j$, and $0$ otherwise. $A$ encodes *which monitors can see which links*. 3. **Linear model (1 mark):** $$X = A \cdot L + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$$ 4. **MLE / pseudo-inverse solution (1 mark):** $\hat{L} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T X$. 5. **Identifiability condition (1 mark):** Unique recovery requires $\text{rank}(A) = N$, i.e., $M \ge N$. Under sparsity, the bound relaxes to $M \ge \mathcal{O}(k \log N)$. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Under independence, the joint likelihood factorizes: $$P(M_1, \dots, M_n \mid L) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L)$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Applying Bayes' theorem: $$P(L = 1 \mid M_1, \dots, M_n) = \frac{P(M_1, \dots, M_n \mid L = 1)\, \pi}{P(M_1, \dots, M_n \mid L = 1)\, \pi + P(M_1, \dots, M_n \mid L = 0)\,(1 - \pi)}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Substituting the factored likelihoods: $$P(L = 1 \mid M_1, \dots, M_n) = \frac{\pi \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L = 1)}{\pi \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L = 1) + (1 - \pi) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(M_i \mid L = 0)}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** **Why essential for IoT:** * IoT gateways and cloud monitors are physically and logically independent → their alarms are approximately independent. * Independence allows the *product* of likelihoods to **exponentially amplify** the signal of a true attack while **suppressing** uncorrelated false alarms. * For example, 3 monitors each with $p_d = 0.92$, $p_{fa} = 0.08$ and $\pi = 0.04$ jointly raise the posterior to **> 99%**, achieving a verdict that no single monitor could. * This makes multi-monitor sensor fusion the **operational backbone of IoT-scale security tomography.** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:** > > 1. **Confusing "Security Tomography" with "Network Tomography"** — Network tomography infers *topology and performance*; security tomography infers the *security state* of the same system. Always state the distinction explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the prior $\pi$ in Bayes' formula** — Many students write only the numerator. The denominator (total probability) is mandatory. > 3. **Ignoring the base-rate fallacy** — In numerical answers, *always* add a comment explaining why a single monitor is insufficient. Examiners explicitly test this conceptual understanding. > 4. **Mermaid/diagram mistakes** — Do not put `**bold**` inside node labels; the diagram will break. Use plain uppercase text with `
` for line breaks. > 5. **Vague "Security Tomography improves security" answers** — Always quantify: state the *posterior probability*, the *base rate*, the *number of monitors*, and the *achieved confidence*. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember * **One-line definition:** Security Tomography reconstructs the internal security state of an IoT system from distributed multi-layer measurements, exactly like a medical CT scan reconstructs a 3D body image. * **Four data-source layers:** Device, Network, Application, Data — all must be sampled for reliable tomography. * **Core equation (must memorize):** $P(L = 1 \mid M) = \dfrac{p_d\, \pi}{p_d\, \pi + p_{fa}\,(1 - \pi)}$. * **MLE vs MAP:** MLE ignores the prior; MAP uses it. MAP is preferred in IoT because threat-intelligence priors are available. * **Base-rate fallacy:** A single high-quality monitor cannot produce high-confidence verdicts — *multi-monitor fusion is essential*. * **Sensing matrix rank condition:** $\text{rank}(A) = N$ guarantees identifiability; under sparsity, $M \ge \mathcal{O}(k \log N)$ suffices. * **MRF (Markov Random Field)** captures spatial correlation between neighboring links, improving inference accuracy. * **KL Divergence** $D_{KL}(P \Vert Q)$ is the standard metric for tomographic accuracy. * **Compressed sensing** reduces the number of required monitors when attacks are sparse. * **Real-world deployment:** IoT gateways + cloud aggregators act as monitors; SOC receives the reconstructed heat-map. * **Engineering benefit:** Reduces monitoring overhead by **2–3 orders of magnitude** in large-scale IoT deployments. * **Examiner hot-button:** Always quantify your answers with numbers, equations, and explicit layer references. * **Mnemonic:** "**S**lice, **F**use, **I**nfer, **V**isualize, **R**espond" — the five stages of the pipeline.

topics.title: Layered Attacker Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layered Attacker Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Layered Attacker Model** is a security threat-modeling framework used in the Internet of Things (IoT) paradigm that systematically categorizes potential adversaries based on **the architectural layer of the IoT stack they target and compromise**. It adopts the standard reference IoT architecture (typically a 4-layer or 5-layer model) and enumerates, layer by layer, the attack surface, the attacker's capability, the attacker's location relative to the system, and the corresponding impact on **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA triad)**.

The model is formally defined as a mapping:

$$
\mathcal{A} : (L, C, M, P) \rightarrow \mathcal{T}
$$

Where:
- $L$ = Target Layer (Perception, Network, Transport, Processing/Support, Application)
- $C$ = Capability of the attacker (skill level, resources, knowledge)
- $M$ = Motive (eavesdropping, disruption, modification, fabrication)
- $P$ = Position (internal vs external, local vs remote)
- $\mathcal{T}$ = Threat outcome / attack taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – IoT Security):** The Layered Attacker Model is the foundational mental model students must master before studying individual attacks (DoS, replay, Sybil, sinkhole, etc.). Every attack discussed in subsequent modules MUST be mapped back to a layer in this model during ESE answers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Building Security Model"

Imagine a **5-floor smart office building**:

| Floor (Layer) | What's inside | Who can attack? | How? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ground (Perception) | Sensors, cameras, doors | Janitor, visitor, outsider | Tamper with sensor physically, swap hardware |
| 1st (Network) | Routers, switches, Wi-Fi | Hacker with Wi-Fi adapter | Sniff traffic, inject packets |
| 2nd (Transport) | TCP/UDP, gateways | Network engineer | Flood connections, hijack sessions |
| 3rd (Processing) | Cloud server, database | Cloud admin / insider | Steal DB, modify records |
| 4th (Application) | Mobile/Web app | End-user / app exploit | XSS, SQLi, credential theft |

The **Layered Attacker Model** simply says: *"At every floor of the building (layer), there exists a different kind of intruder (attacker), using a different weapon (attack vector), seeking a different prize (asset)."* A complete security design must defend **every floor**, not just the lobby.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A *single* real-world attack (e.g., the **Mirai Botnet DDoS of 2016**) often crosses multiple layers — cameras compromised at the Perception layer were weaponized to flood the Application/Transport layer. Layered Attacker Models force designers to think holistically rather than tunnel-vision on one layer.

## 1.3 The Five Reference Layers of the IoT Stack (KTU Standard)

The most widely used layering is the **5-layer IoT Architecture** proposed by the IERC (Internet of Things European Research Cluster) and adopted in KTU reference material:

1. **Perception / Sensing Layer** – physical sensors, actuators, RFID, edge devices.
2. **Network / Object Abstraction Layer** – routing, addressing, MAC protocols (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, LoRa).
3. **Transport / Communication Layer** – TCP, UDP, CoAP, MQTT, end-to-end delivery.
4. **Processing / Service Management / Middleware Layer** – cloud platforms, databases, analytics, decision engines.
5. **Application Layer** – smart-home, healthcare, industrial dashboards, user interfaces.

Some authors compress layers 3 & 4 into a single "Network/Service" tier (4-layer model). Both are acceptable in KTU answers; the **5-layer model scores higher** in marking schemes.

## 1.4 Classification of Attackers Across Layers

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Attacker:** Any entity (human, malware, rogue device, insider) that intentionally attempts to violate one or more pillars of the CIA triad.

Attackers are classified along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Categories | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Capability** | Script kiddie, Skilled hacker, Organized crime, Nation-state APT | Resource and expertise level |
| **Position** | External (outside network), Internal (inside the IoT deployment) | Attack origin |
| **Activity** | Passive (eavesdropping), Active (modification/injection) | Engagement style |

A *Layered Attacker Model* combines these axes with the 5 architectural layers to produce a **15–20 cell threat matrix** that engineers use to plan defenses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How of the Layered Attacker Model

The model's operational logic proceeds in **five structured steps**:

### Step 1 — Decompose the IoT System into Layers
Why: Defense is impossible against an undefined surface.
How: Map every component (sensor, gateway, broker, server, UI) to exactly one of the 5 layers. Cross-layer components (e.g., an edge gateway) are assigned to their **dominant function layer**.

### Step 2 — Identify Assets at Each Layer
Each layer owns distinct assets with different value/risk:

- **Perception:** Sensor data, physical device identity, location.
- **Network:** Routing tables, topology, IP/MAC addresses.
- **Transport:** Session state, encryption keys, flow identifiers.
- **Processing:** Stored data, ML models, credentials, backups.
- **Application:** User accounts, business logic, output integrity.

### Step 3 — Enumerate Attackers by Capability
Capability determines **what the attacker can do**, not just what they intend:

$$
\text{Capability Score} = w_1 S + w_2 R + w_3 K
$$

Where $S$ = skill, $R$ = resources, $K$ = knowledge of the system. (Conceptual metric — not a strict KTU formula, but useful for examiner's perspective.)

### Step 4 — Map Attack Vectors to (Layer, Asset) Pairs
A *vector* is the technical pathway. Example mapping:

$$
(\text{Layer} = \text{Network},\ \text{Asset} = \text{Routing Table}) \rightarrow \text{Vector: Sinkhole Attack}
$$

### Step 5 — Quantify Impact in Terms of the CIA Triad
Each attack degrades one or more pillars. KTU examiners expect the student to **explicitly name which CIA pillar is violated**:

| Attack | Layer | CIA violated |
|---|---|---|
| Node tampering | Perception | Integrity, Availability |
| Eavesdropping | Network | Confidentiality |
| Sinkhole | Network | Integrity, Availability |
| DoS / Flooding | Transport | Availability |
| Cloud DB theft | Processing | Confidentiality |
| SQL Injection | Application | Integrity, Confidentiality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Expectation:** Whenever you name an attack in the ESE, **always** state: (a) the layer, (b) the CIA pillar affected, (c) a one-line mitigation. Answers missing the layer mapping lose 2–3 marks automatically.

## 2.2 Layer-by-Layer Attacker Profile (High-Yield Analysis)

### Layer 1 — Perception / Physical Layer Attackers
- **Profile:** Insider with physical access, hardware hacker, "war-driving" outsider with SDR.
- **Typical attacks:** Node capture, node tampering, RF jamming, side-channel, hardware Trojan insertion, cloning of RFID tags.
- **CIA focus:** Integrity and Availability are the primary targets.
- **Why easy:** Sensors are physically exposed; lightweight devices lack tamper-proofing.

### Layer 2 — Network Layer Attackers
- **Profile:** Wireless eavesdropper, man-in-the-middle, compromised router.
- **Typical attacks:** Sniffing, sinkhole, wormhole, Sybil, blackhole, selective forwarding, routing spoofing.
- **CIA focus:** Confidentiality and Integrity.
- **Why easy:** Wireless media is shared; many IoT protocols (802.15.4, ZigBee) ship with weak or no authentication.

### Layer 3 — Transport Layer Attackers
- **Profile:** Network-based adversary, on-path attacker, botnet operator.
- **Typical attacks:** TCP/UDP flooding (SYN flood), session hijacking, replay attacks, desynchronization, fragmentation attacks.
- **CIA focus:** Availability (and Integrity for replay/hijack).
- **Why easy:** Constrained devices cannot run full TLS stacks; CoAP runs over UDP with no built-in security unless DTLS is added.

### Layer 4 — Processing / Middleware Layer Attackers
- **Profile:** Cloud insider, supply-chain attacker, malicious firmware updater.
- **Typical attacks:** Data exfiltration, privilege escalation, unauthorized API calls, malicious firmware injection, ransomware on cloud DB.
- **CIA focus:** All three — most damaging layer when breached.
- **Why easy:** Centralized aggregator creates a "honeypot" effect; weak API authentication is the #1 cloud-side IoT vulnerability.

### Layer 5 — Application Layer Attackers
- **Profile:** Remote attacker, end-user, malicious third-party app developer.
- **Typical attacks:** Cross-site scripting (XSS), SQL injection, broken authentication, broken access control, insecure direct object references.
- **CIA focus:** Confidentiality (data theft) and Integrity (UI spoofing).
- **Why easy:** Apps iterate fast; security review is often skipped in IoT startups.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Unit / Type | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attacker Model mapping | $\mathcal{A}:(L,C,M,P)\rightarrow\mathcal{T}$ | Set-theoretic | **High** |
| 2 | CIA Triad pillars | $\{C,\ I,\ A\}$ | Conceptual | **High** |
| 3 | Number of standard IoT layers | $N_L = 5$ | Count | **High** |
| 4 | Capability Score (conceptual) | $\text{Cap} = w_1 S + w_2 R + w_3 K$ | Weighted score | Medium |
| 5 | Risk severity | $R = P \times I$ | Numeric | **High** |
| 6 | Where $P$ = probability, $I$ = impact | — | — | — |
| 7 | Attacker position | $\in \{\text{External},\ \text{Internal}\}$ | Categorical | **High** |
| 8 | Attacker activity | $\in \{\text{Passive},\ \text{Active}\}$ | Categorical | **High** |
| 9 | Layer-to-asset ratio | 1 layer : $\ge 3$ critical assets | — | Medium |
| 10 | Cross-layer attack count | Often $\ge 2$ layers affected | — | Medium |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Discipline:** In the table above, vertical bars are written as `\vert` (e.g., `{C,\ I,\ A}`) and the OR symbol in `{External, Internal}` uses commas. This is to prevent markdown table parser breakage — the same discipline is required in your ESE answer sheets if you draw tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Layered Attacker Model

In professional IoT engineering, this model drives:

1. **Threat Modeling Workshops** (STRIDE, PASTA, ATT&CK all start by enumerating the layered attacker).
2. **Security-by-Design** decisions — where to place encryption, where to add intrusion detection.
3. **Compliance and Certification** — ETSI EN 303 645, NIST IR 8259, and India's NCIIPC IoT guidelines all require layer-wise threat analysis.
4. **Penetration Test Scoping** — penetration testers charge per layer; this model becomes a bill of work.
5. **Incident Response** — once a breach occurs, responders use the layer model to identify the *patient zero* device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry tie-in:** Bosch, Siemens, and Philips use a layered attacker model internally before any consumer IoT product reaches the market. KTU examiners reward answers that cite one such industry context.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Raw Adversary Description to Layer-Indexed Threat

**Problem Context:** A smart agriculture IoT deployment has 4 components: soil-moisture sensor (wireless, battery), LoRa gateway, MQTT broker in the cloud, and a farmer's Android app. Identify the *layered attacker model* for this system, listing one attacker type, one attack, the layer, and the CIA pillar affected for **each of the 5 layers**.

### Step 1 — Decompose the System

We assign each component to a layer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Soil sensor} &\rightarrow L_1\ \text{(Perception)} \\
\text{LoRa gateway} &\rightarrow L_2\ \text{(Network)} \\
\text{MQTT broker} &\rightarrow L_3\ \text{(Transport)} \\
\text{Cloud DB} &\rightarrow L_4\ \text{(Processing)} \\
\text{Android app} &\rightarrow L_5\ \text{(Application)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:** The sensor's job is sensing → Perception. The gateway's job is forwarding frames over LoRa → Network. MQTT broker's job is end-to-end pub/sub over TCP → Transport. The cloud DB stores analytics → Processing. The app is the end-user interface → Application.

### Step 2 — Enumerate an Attacker at Each Layer

We choose a representative adversary for each layer (one per cell, as examiners expect):

| Layer | Attacker Profile | Concrete Attack | Asset Targeted | CIA Pillar Violated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_1$ Perception | Insider farm laborer | Sensor node tampering | Soil-moisture reading | **Integrity** (false readings), **Availability** (device disabled) |
| $L_2$ Network | Wireless eavesdropper with SDR | Sniffing LoRa frames | Frame payload (data + key) | **Confidentiality** |
| $L_3$ Transport | Remote botnet | TCP SYN flood on MQTT broker port 1883 | Broker availability | **Availability** |
| $L_4$ Processing | Compromised cloud admin credential | Unauthorized data exfiltration via API | Stored historical sensor data | **Confidentiality** |
| $L_5$ Application | Remote attacker with phishing link | Credential theft → account takeover | User account | **Confidentiality**, **Integrity** |

### Step 3 — Compute Aggregate Risk Score (Using $R = P \times I$)

Assume probability $P \in [0,1]$ and impact $I \in [0,10]$ are assigned by the security team:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{L_1} &= 0.7 \times 8 = 5.6 \\
R_{L_2} &= 0.6 \times 6 = 3.6 \\
R_{L_3} &= 0.8 \times 9 = 7.2 \\
R_{L_4} &= 0.4 \times 10 = 4.0 \\
R_{L_5} &= 0.5 \times 7 = 3.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Higher risk demands prioritized mitigation. Here, $L_3$ (Transport DoS) and $L_1$ (sensor tampering) are top priorities.

### Step 4 — Map Mitigation per Layer (for completeness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &: \text{Tamper-proof enclosures, hardware crypto chips} \\
L_2 &: \text{AES-128 link-layer encryption on LoRa frames} \\
L_3 &: \text{Rate limiting, DTLS for MQTT, CDN / anycast} \\
L_4 &: \text{MFA for cloud admins, key rotation, audit logs} \\
L_5 &: \text{OAuth 2.0, input validation, WAF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification of the Model

A "valid" Layered Attacker Model must satisfy:

1. **Coverage:** Every architectural layer $L_i,\ i=1..5$ must have $\ge 1$ attacker entry.
2. **Non-redundancy:** Two entries from the same layer must target *different* assets or use *different* CIA pillars.
3. **Defensibility:** Each entry must have at least one feasible mitigation (otherwise it represents a fatal design flaw).

The matrix above satisfies all three.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When an ESE question says *"explain the layered attacker model with an example"*, this exact 5-column format (Layer, Attacker, Attack, Asset, CIA) typically scores full marks (7/7 in a 7-mark sub-part).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Layer Attack Scenarios

A more advanced class of attacks is **cross-layer** — the attacker breaches one layer to weaponize another. KTU frequently tests this:

| Cross-Layer Attack | Initial Layer | Pivot Layer | Mechanism | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mirai Botnet (2016) | Perception (cameras with default password) | Application / Transport (DDoS target) | Mirai malware turned 600k+ IoT cameras into botnet flooding Dyn DNS | Dyn outage — Twitter, Netflix, GitHub down |
| Stuxnet | Perception (PLCs) | Application (SCADA HMI) | Reprogrammed centrifuges via HMI lies | Iran Natanz uranium enrichment |
| VPNFilter | Network (routers) | Processing (cloud creds) | Routers exfiltrate traffic credentials | 500k+ home routers affected (2018) |
| Smart Thermostat DoS | Transport (CoAP) | Application (HVAC service) | Flood overwhelms thermostat backend | University thermostatic lockout attack |

**Engineering Takeaway:** Defense in depth is *not optional*. Securing only the Application layer is useless if the Perception layer devices ship with `admin:admin` credentials.

## 3.3 Mathematical Formulation of Layered Attacker Risk (Optional but Bonus-Marking)

Total system risk:

$$
R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \sum_{j=1}^{N_{A,i}} P_{ij} \cdot I_{ij}
$$

Where $N_L$ is the number of layers and $N_{A,i}$ is the number of attacker profiles for layer $i$. This summation lets the security team rank mitigations by marginal risk reduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula has appeared in KTU's optional question on IoT security risk modeling. Memorize it for the 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 5-Layer IoT Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the canonical 5-layer IoT reference architecture. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy the engine's node-identifier rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1 PERCEPTION SENSORS AND RFID"] --> L2["LAYER 2 NETWORK GATEWAYS AND ROUTING"]
    L2 --> L3["LAYER 3 TRANSPORT MQTT COAP TCP UDP"]
    L3 --> L4["LAYER 4 PROCESSING CLOUD DATABASE ANALYTICS"]
    L4 --> L5["LAYER 5 APPLICATION MOBILE WEB DASHBOARD"]
    USER["END USER"] --> L5
    L5 --> USER
    ADMIN["CLOUD ADMIN"] --> L4
    L4 --> ADMIN
```

## 4.2 Layered Attacker Model — Adversary Deployment Map

This diagram shows *where* attackers are deployed across the 5-layer stack. External attackers can target any layer; internal attackers typically have privileged access to one specific layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT["EXTERNAL ATTACKER SCRIPT KIDDIE HACKER NATION STATE"] --> L1A["L1 PERCEPTION ATTACK"]
    EXT --> L2A["L2 NETWORK ATTACK"]
    EXT --> L3A["L3 TRANSPORT ATTACK"]
    EXT --> L4A["L4 PROCESSING ATTACK"]
    EXT --> L5A["L5 APPLICATION ATTACK"]
    INS["INTERNAL ATTACKER INSIDER MALWARE ROGUE DEVICE"] --> L1A
    INS --> L4A
    INS --> L5A
    L1A --> CIA1["CIA VIOLATED INTEGRITY AND AVAILABILITY"]
    L2A --> CIA2["CIA VIOLATED CONFIDENTIALITY AND INTEGRITY"]
    L3A --> CIA3["CIA VIOLATED AVAILABILITY"]
    L4A --> CIA4["CIA VIOLATED CONFIDENTIALITY INTEGRITY AVAILABILITY"]
    L5A --> CIA5["CIA VIOLATED CONFIDENTIALITY AND INTEGRITY"]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Layer Attack Flow — Mirai Botnet Example

This block diagram models the *Mirai* attack chain as a cross-layer attacker scenario. Reading the flow: Perception-layer camera → compromised → Network-layer scan → Transport-layer DDoS → Application-layer target down.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["STEP 1 SCAN INTERNET FOR IOT CAMERAS AT L2 NETWORK"] --> A2["STEP 2 TRY DEFAULT CREDENTIALS L1 PERCEPTION"]
    A2 --> A3["STEP 3 INFECT WITH MIRAI MALWARE L4 PROCESSING"]
    A3 --> A4["STEP 4 JOIN BOTNET ARMY L2 NETWORK"]
    A4 --> A5["STEP 5 COORDINATED SYN FLOOD AT L3 TRANSPORT"]
    A5 --> A6["STEP 6 TARGET SERVER GOES DOWN L5 APPLICATION"]
    A2 -.CROSSES.-> L1N["L1 NODE COMPROMISED"]
    A3 -.CROSSES.-> L4N["L4 CLOUD AGENT FOR C2"]
    A5 -.CROSSES.-> L3N["L3 TRANSPORT FLOOD"]
    A6 -.CROSSES.-> L5N["L5 APP LAYER OFFLINE"]
```

## 4.4 Layer-to-Asset-to-Mitigation Mapping (Functional Architecture)

A compact functional view used by IoT security architects. Each layer has an asset, a primary attacker class, and a representative mitigation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[LAYER 1 PERCEPTION]
        A1["ASSET SENSOR DATA AND DEVICE IDENTITY"] --> AT1["ATTACKER INSIDER AND HW HACKER"]
        AT1 --> M1["MITIGATION TAMPER PROOF ENCLOSURES AND HW CRYPTO"]
    end
    subgraph L2[LAYER 2 NETWORK]
        A2["ASSET ROUTING TABLES AND FRAME PAYLOAD"] --> AT2["ATTACKER WIRELESS EAVESDROPPER"]
        AT2 --> M2["MITIGATION LINK LAYER ENCRYPTION AND AUTHENTICATED ROUTING"]
    end
    subgraph L3[LAYER 3 TRANSPORT]
        A3["ASSET SESSION KEYS AND FLOW IDS"] --> AT3["ATTACKER REMOTE BOTNET OPERATOR"]
        AT3 --> M3["MITIGATION DTLS RATE LIMITING AND INTRUSION DETECTION"]
    end
    subgraph L4[LAYER 4 PROCESSING]
        A4["ASSET CLOUD DB AND ML MODELS"] --> AT4["ATTACKER CLOUD INSIDER OR APT"]
        AT4 --> M4["MITIGATION MFA KEY ROTATION AND AUDIT LOGS"]
    end
    subgraph L5[LAYER 5 APPLICATION]
        A5["ASSET USER ACCOUNTS AND UI"] --> AT5["ATTACKER REMOTE PHISHER"]
        AT5 --> M5["MITIGATION OAUTH 2 INPUT VALIDATION AND WAF"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Over Physical Drawings:** IoT attacker models are abstract and multi-architectural. The KTU evaluator rewards clear *information flow* and *layer mapping* far more than visual aesthetics. A clean Mermaid block diagram with labelled layers, attackers, and CIA tags always scores higher than a hand-drawn approximation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**Define the Layered Attacker Model in the context of IoT security. List the five reference IoT layers.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

The **Layered Attacker Model** is a security threat-modeling framework that systematically classifies potential adversaries in an IoT system according to **(a) the architectural layer they target, (b) their capability, (c) their position, and (d) their activity type**. It maps the attack surface layer-by-layer to the assets and the CIA pillars at risk.

The **five reference IoT layers** are:

1. **Perception / Sensing Layer** – sensors, actuators, RFID.
2. **Network / Object Abstraction Layer** – gateways, routing, MAC protocols.
3. **Transport / Communication Layer** – TCP/UDP, MQTT, CoAP.
4. **Processing / Service Management Layer** – cloud, database, analytics.
5. **Application Layer** – end-user apps, dashboards, business logic.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the 5 layers.)*

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between passive and active attackers in a Layered Attacker Model. Give one IoT-layer example for each.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Aspect | Passive Attacker | Active Attacker |
|---|---|---|
| **Engagement** | Silently observes traffic | Modifies, injects, or disrupts |
| **Detection risk** | Very low (hard to detect) | Higher (often logged) |
| **CIA focus** | **Confidentiality** (eavesdropping) | **Integrity / Availability** (modification / DoS) |
| **Example — Perception layer** | Sniffing RF emissions from sensors without connecting | Jamming the 2.4 GHz band to disable ZigBee sensors |
| **Example — Transport layer** | Capturing MQTT packets to read unencrypted topics | Flooding the broker with forged PUBLISH messages |

*(3 marks — 2 for the table, 1 for the IoT example.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]`

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer IoT architecture**. For each layer, identify the **primary asset**, **one attacker type**, and **one representative attack**.

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss the **Layered Attacker Model** in detail. Using the **CIA triad**, classify how the attackers at each layer compromise security, and suggest **one mitigation per layer**.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 5-Layer IoT Architecture

The 5-layer IoT architecture (IERC reference) decomposes the IoT stack as follows:

| Layer | Function | Primary Asset | Attacker Type | Representative Attack |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Perception | Sense the physical world | Sensor data | Insider with physical access | **Node tampering** |
| 2. Network | Frame / packet routing | Routing tables, addresses | Wireless eavesdropper | **Sinkhole attack** |
| 3. Transport | End-to-end delivery | Session state, keys | Remote botnet operator | **SYN flood / DoS** |
| 4. Processing | Storage, analytics, decisions | Cloud DB, ML models | Compromised cloud admin | **Data exfiltration** |
| 5. Application | User interface / business logic | User accounts, output | Remote phisher | **Credential theft / XSS** |

**Block Diagram (already given in SECTION 4.1)** — reproduce it on the answer sheet.

#### Part (b) — Layered Attacker Model + CIA Classification + Mitigation

**Definition:** The Layered Attacker Model is a structured threat-modeling framework that *indexes* all plausible IoT adversaries by (layer, capability, position, activity) and *maps* them to violated CIA pillars and mitigations.

**Layer-wise analysis:**

1. **Perception layer:** Attacker tampers with a sensor → false data injected.
   - CIA violated: **Integrity** (false readings), **Availability** (device disabled).
   - Mitigation: tamper-proof enclosures, hardware cryptographic modules, secure boot.

2. **Network layer:** Attacker runs a sinkhole or selective forwarding attack.
   - CIA violated: **Integrity** (dropped/redirected packets), **Availability** (loss of reachability).
   - Mitigation: authenticated routing (e.g., TESLA, geographic routing checks), link-layer encryption.

3. **Transport layer:** Attacker floods MQTT broker with TCP SYNs (SYN flood).
   - CIA violated: **Availability**.
   - Mitigation: rate limiting, DTLS for MQTT, deploy anycast / CDN, SYN cookies.

4. **Processing layer:** Attacker with stolen cloud admin credential runs bulk exfiltration.
   - CIA violated: **Confidentiality** (data stolen), **Integrity** (modified records), **Availability** (ransomware).
   - Mitigation: MFA for admins, key rotation, immutable audit logs, role-based access.

5. **Application layer:** Attacker phishes farmer credentials, takes over account.
   - CIA violated: **Confidentiality** (account data), **Integrity** (false commands issued).
   - Mitigation: OAuth 2.0, MFA for end users, input validation, WAF.

**Conclusion:** The Layered Attacker Model ensures that *no single layer is left undefended*. A multi-layer (defense-in-depth) security strategy is the only robust response to layered attackers.

*(14 marks — 7 for the layer table + diagram, 7 for the CIA classification + mitigations.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Classify IoT attackers along three axes: **Capability, Position, and Activity**. Explain each axis with a one-line example.

**(b) (7 marks)** Explain the concept of **Cross-Layer Attacks** with **two real-world examples** (e.g., Mirai, Stuxnet). Justify why a *Layered Attacker Model* is essential for defending against them.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Three Axes of Attacker Classification

| Axis | Categories | Example (with IoT layer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Capability** | Script kiddie / Skilled hacker / Organized crime / Nation-state APT | A script kiddie uses a downloaded tool to run a **TCP SYN flood** at the Transport layer. A nation-state APT custom-designs **firmware implants** at the Perception layer. |
| **Position** | External / Internal | External: a remote attacker on the public Internet probing the **Application layer**. Internal: a malicious employee with valid badge accessing the **Processing layer's** cloud console. |
| **Activity** | Passive / Active | Passive: an attacker in a coffee shop **sniffs MQTT packets** at the Network layer. Active: a Mirai-infected camera **floods a DNS provider** at the Transport layer. |

#### Part (b) — Cross-Layer Attacks

A **cross-layer attack** is one that breaches a *low-value* layer (e.g., Perception) to weaponize or pivot to a *high-value* layer (e.g., Application). The Layered Attacker Model is essential because the *root compromise* and the *damage point* lie in different layers — designing security in only one layer leaves the system exposed.

**Example 1 — Mirai Botnet (2016):**
- *Root compromise at L1 Perception:* thousands of IoT cameras (cheap Chinese DVRs) with default `admin:admin` credentials.
- *Pivot to L2 Network:* infected devices scan the Internet and recruit more devices.
- *Damage at L3 Transport + L5 Application:* coordinated **DDoS flood** against Dyn DNS, taking Twitter, Netflix, GitHub offline.
- *CIA violated:* **Availability** at Application layer; **Integrity** at Perception layer (firmware replaced).
- *Mitigation implied:* enforce unique credentials at L1, rate-limit at L3, anycast at L5.

**Example 2 — Stuxnet (2010):**
- *Root compromise at L2 Network:* USB drive into Iranian Natanz PLC network.
- *Pivot to L1 Perception:* reprogrammed Siemens S7-315 PLCs controlling centrifuges.
- *Damage at L5 Application:* SCADA HMI displayed normal readings while centrifuges spun to destruction.
- *CIA violated:* **Integrity** (false readings), **Availability** (physical destruction of equipment).
- *Mitigation implied:* network segmentation (L2), signed firmware (L1), HMI integrity checks (L5).

**Conclusion:** Cross-layer attacks demonstrate that *securing a single layer is insufficient*. The Layered Attacker Model, by enumerating attackers and assets at every layer, forces engineers to plan **defense in depth**.

*(14 marks — 7 for the three-axis table, 7 for the two real-world examples with layer mapping.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in Layered Attacker Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing only 4 layers** (omitting Processing/Support layer) — KTU model answer expects 5; you lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Naming an attack without naming its layer** — always tag attacks as "Perception-layer node tampering" or "Transport-layer SYN flood".
> 3. **Forgetting to map attacks to the CIA triad** — examiners explicitly allocate 2–3 marks for this. Skipping it is fatal.
> 4. **Confusing passive and active attackers** — passive ≠ harmless; passive = confidentiality attack (still severe).
> 5. **Ignoring cross-layer attacks** — Module 4 has at least one 14-mark question that *requires* Mirai/Stuxnet. Memorize one.
>
> **Bonus marking:** adding a real-world example (Mirai, Stuxnet, Dyn, VPNFilter) typically gains +1 to +2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Layered Attacker Model = a framework that categorizes IoT adversaries by **target layer + capability + position + activity**, mapped to the **CIA triad**.
- **5 Layers (in canonical order):** Perception → Network → Transport → Processing → Application.
- **CIA Triad:** **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability — every attack must be classified against at least one pillar.
- **Three attacker axes:** Capability (skill/resources), Position (internal/external), Activity (passive/active).
- **Layer-vs-typical attack quick recall:**
  - Perception → node tampering, jamming, side-channel
  - Network → sinkhole, wormhole, Sybil, sniffing
  - Transport → SYN flood, replay, desync, session hijack
  - Processing → DB exfiltration, privilege escalation, malicious firmware
  - Application → XSS, SQLi, credential theft
- **Risk formula:** $R = P \times I$; aggregate risk: $R_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i,j} P_{ij} I_{ij}$.
- **Cross-layer attack = root layer ≠ damage layer**; examples: **Mirai, Stuxnet, VPNFilter, Smart-Thermostat DoS**.
- **Defense principle:** **Defense in Depth** — secure every layer; never trust a single layer.
- **Industry standards cited:** ETSI EN 303 645, NIST IR 8259, IERC 5-layer architecture.
- **Exam mantra:** "**Layer + Attacker + Attack + Asset + CIA + Mitigation**" — the 6-tuple that secures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identity Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Identity Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Identity Management (IdM)** in the context of IoT is the comprehensive administrative framework, set of policies, technologies, and processes used to establish, maintain, protect, and eventually revoke the digital identities of *users*, *devices (things)*, *services*, and *applications* operating within an IoT ecosystem. It encompasses the full lifecycle of an identity — from creation (provisioning) to authentication, authorization, auditing, and final de-provisioning.

According to the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, Identity Management in IoT specifically addresses the unique challenge of uniquely identifying billions of heterogeneous, resource-constrained "things" (sensors, actuators, RFID tags, smart devices) and managing their access rights across distributed networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (Board-Examiner Preferred Wording):**
> "Identity Management is the combination of technical systems, policies, and processes that create, store, and manage identity information for entities (people, devices, and services) and provide authentication, authorization, and accountability services to ensure secure access to resources."

### 1.2 Conceptual Decomposition — The Four A's

For any KTU answer, identity management must be broken down into its four foundational pillars:

| Pillar | Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | Claiming *who* you are | Showing your **Aadhaar/SSN card** at a counter |
| **Authentication** | Proving *that* you are who you claim | Entering your **OTP/PIN** linked to that card |
| **Authorization** | Deciding *what* you are allowed to do | The card only lets you open a **bank locker**, not drive a car |
| **Accountability/Auditing** | Logging *what* you actually did | The bank's **CCTV footage** of the locker room |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The University Campus Pass System"**
>
> Imagine a large university campus with **1 lakh students, 5,000 faculty, 10,000 IoT devices** (projectors, ACs, biometric scanners, smart lights), and dozens of external vendors.
>
> - The **Identity Card** is the *identifier* (a unique number).
> - The **biometric or PIN check** at the library gate is *authentication*.
> - The rule *"Only MTech students can access the GPU lab"* is *authorization*.
> - The **biometric logs** maintained at every lab door are *accountability*.
> - The **central database** maintained by the Registrar's office is the *Identity Provider (IdP)*.
> - The **libraries, labs, and canteens** that accept the card are *Service Providers (SPs)*.
>
> Identity Management is the **entire automated machinery** that issues cards, verifies fingerprints, enforces "MTech-only" rules, and stores logs. In IoT, this same machinery must work for *inanimate objects* — a sensor must also "carry an ID", "prove its identity", "have permissions", and "be auditable".

### 1.4 Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

- **Entity / Subject:** The actor (user, device, service) requesting access.
- **Identity:** A set of attributes (name, role, key) that uniquely represents an entity.
- **Identifier (ID):** A unique label (e.g., UUID, MAC address, URI) bound to an identity.
- **Identity Provider (IdP):** The trusted authority that creates, stores, and verifies identities.
- **Service Provider (SP) / Relying Party (RP):** The resource server that consumes identity assertions.
- **Principal:** The entity whose identity is being asserted.
- **Assertion / Token:** A cryptographically signed statement about a principal (e.g., a JWT or SAML token).
- **Provisioning:** The act of creating a new identity and binding it to credentials.
- **Deprovisioning:** Securely revoking and destroying an identity when no longer needed.

### 1.5 Why Identity Management is *Critical* in IoT (vs Traditional IT)

| Challenge | Traditional IT | IoT |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | Thousands of users | **Billions** of devices |
| **Lifetime** | 2–5 years for a laptop | **10–20 years** for a sensor |
| **Heterogeneity** | Mostly homogeneous | Fragmented hardware, OS, protocols |
| **Mobility** | Mostly static | Highly **mobile** (vehicles, wearables) |
| **Constraints** | Powerful CPUs, ample RAM | Limited **8/16-bit MCUs, 4–32 KB RAM** |
| **Reachability** | Always online | Often **intermittent / sleeping** |
| **Trust** | Inside corporate firewall | Often deployed **in the wild / hostile environments** |
| **Confidentiality of payload** | Focus on data-at-rest | Devices carry **physical world measurements** (health, location) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention these contrasts in 14-mark answers. Examiners award marks for the *IoT-specific* challenges, not generic IT-security talk.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Identity Management Life-Cycle (Provisioning → Deprovisioning)

A robust IdM system must handle **seven distinct phases**. Examiners love the *flow-diagram* style answer; remember the acronym **P-AA-LAR-D**.

1. **Provisioning (P):** Creation of the identity record and binding it to credentials. In IoT, this often happens at the *factory* (burning a X.509 cert into flash) or via *zero-touch onboarding* (e.g., FIDO Device Onboard).
2. **Authentication (A):** Verification of the identity claim at every access.
3. **Authorization (A):** Enforcement of access-control policies (RBAC, ABAC, ACL).
4. **Logging (L):** Recording every access for **accountability** and **forensics**.
5. **Auditing (A):** Periodic review of logs to detect anomalies.
6. **Renewal / Rotation (R):** Periodic rotation of keys, certificates, and passwords.
7. **Deprovisioning (D):** Revocation (CRL, OCSP) and physical/secure erasure of identity on end-of-life.

### 2.2 Classification of Identity Management Models

The **four** canonical models — KTU's most frequently tested sub-topic:

| Model | Structure | Strength | Weakness | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Isolated / Silo Model** | Each service maintains its own DB | Simple, no inter-service trust | User has N passwords; no SSO | Early web apps |
| **Centralized Model** | A single IdP serves all SPs | Single point of administration, easier audit | Single point of failure, **SPOF**, privacy risk | LDAP/Kerberos in a campus |
| **Federated Model** | Multiple trusted IdPs share identities across organizational boundaries | True **SSO** across companies; user-centric | Complex trust agreements, latency | SAML 2.0, Shibboleth, eduGAIN |
| **User-Centric / Decentralized Model** | User holds their own identity (wallet) and presents it | Privacy-preserving, no central authority | Hard key management for IoT devices | Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI), **Verifiable Credentials**, DID |

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT-Specific Hybrid Model:** In practice, large-scale IoT deployments use a *Centralized + Federated* hybrid. The manufacturer acts as a *federated IdP* issuing device certificates, and a cloud broker (e.g., AWS IoT Core) acts as the *centralized IdP* for downstream apps.

### 2.3 Identification Schemes Used in IoT

| Identifier Type | Format | Strength | IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAC Address (EUI-48/EUI-64)** | 48-bit / 64-bit hex | Globally unique, hardware-baked | LAN device discovery |
| **IPv6 address** | 128-bit | End-to-end addressable | 6LoWPAN networks |
| **UUID / GUID** | 128-bit random | No central authority required | Application-layer things |
| **EPC (Electronic Product Code)** | 64/96/198-bit | Standardized for supply-chain | RFID tags |
| **URI / URN** | Text string | Human-readable, semantic | CoAP resources, Web of Things |
| **DID (Decentralized Identifier)** | W3C standard | Cryptographically verifiable, no registry | SSI / Blockchain IoT |

### 2.4 Authentication Factors (Authentication Pillars)

The strength of any authentication mechanism is rated by the **number of independent factors** it uses. The standard taxonomy:

- **Factor 1 — Knowledge (Something you *know*):** Passwords, PINs, security questions.
- **Factor 2 — Possession (Something you *have*):** Smart cards, OTP tokens, mobile phones (TOTP), X.509 certificates in TPM/SE.
- **Factor 3 — Biometric (Something you *are*):** Fingerprint, iris, face, voice, gait.
- **Factor 4 — Contextual / Location (Somewhere you *are*):** GPS, IP geo-location.
- **Factor 5 — Behavioral (Something you *do*):** Typing cadence, gait pattern (for wearables).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MFA / 2FA Rule:** **Multifactor Authentication (MFA)** requires at least **two** factors from **different categories**. *Password + OTP* = MFA. *Password + security question* = **NOT MFA** (both are "knowledge").

### 2.5 Access-Control Models (Authorization Layer)

| Model | Full Form | Decision Based On | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ACL** | Access Control List | Subject → Resource mapping | Simple, low-resource IoT nodes |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | User's role (Admin, Operator, Viewer) | Enterprise IoT platforms |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Attributes of subject, object, environment, action | Complex smart-city policies |
| **CapBAC** | Capability-Based Access Control | Possession of an unforgeable token | Lightweight IoT, publish/subscribe brokers (MQTT) |
| **ORCON** | Originator Controlled | Originator's persistent control | Data-sharing in smart homes |

### 2.6 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical relations are the **only** ones an examiner expects you to know for "Identity Management" — there is no heavy physics here, only lightweight crypto and information theory.

**Table 2.6 — High-Yield Identity-Management Formulas**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cryptographic hash of identity token | $H_{\text{id}} = \mathcal{H}(M) \;$ where $M$ = identity payload | Output size: 256 bits for SHA-256 |
| 2 | Digital signature on identity assertion | $S = \text{Sign}_{K_{\text{priv}}^{\text{IdP}}}(\,H_{\text{id}}\,)$ | Verified with $\text{Verify}_{K_{\text{pub}}^{\text{IdP}}}(S)$ |
| 3 | HMAC for device authentication | $\text{HMAC} = \mathcal{H}\bigl((K \oplus opad) \,\|\, \mathcal{H}((K \oplus ipad) \,\|\, M)\bigr)$ | Used in **CoAP + DTLS-PSK** |
| 4 | Password entropy (Shannon) | $E = L \cdot \log_2(N) \;$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = character set size; unit: **bits** |
| 5 | Password strength classification | $E < 28$ : weak; $28 \le E < 60$ : reasonable; $E \ge 60$ : strong | NIST SP 800-63B |
| 6 | Brute-force time | $T_{\text{crack}} = \dfrac{N^{L}}{R \cdot 2}$ | $R$ = guesses/sec |
| 7 | JWT token structure | $\text{JWT} = \underbrace{B64H}_{\text{Header}} \,.\, \underbrace{B64P}_{\text{Payload}} \,.\, \underbrace{B64S}_{\text{Signature}}$ | Used in OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect |
| 8 | UUID v4 randomness | $2^{122}$ unique values | 6 bits fixed for version + variant |
| 9 | X.509 certificate binding | $\text{Cert} = \text{Sign}_{K_{\text{priv}}^{\text{CA}}}\bigl(\,\text{ID} \,\|\, K_{\text{pub}}^{\text{subject}} \,\|\, \text{Validity}\,\bigr)$ | PKI trust chain |
| 10 | PKI trust chain (3 levels) | $\text{Root CA} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate CA} \rightarrow \text{End-entity (device)}$ | Path length $\le 3$ in lightweight IoT PKI |
| 11 | OCSP request size | $\approx 100$–$400$ bytes | Real-time cert revocation check |
| 12 | CRL size growth | $\text{size} \approx (\text{revoked entries} \times 80\,\text{B}) + 100\,\text{B}$ | Grows linearly with revocations |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Each machine, robot, and PLC on the shop-floor carries a **device certificate** issued by the factory's CA. IdM allows the MES (Manufacturing Execution System) to authenticate the machine and push firmware updates only to authorized devices.
- **Smart Healthcare:** Wearables (ECG, glucose monitors) authenticate with hospital gateways via **OAuth 2.0 client-credentials flow**, ensuring only legitimate devices stream PHI (Protected Health Information).
- **Connected Vehicles (V2X):** Vehicles carry **IEEE 1609.2 certificates** issued by a **PCA (Pseudonym Certificate Authority)** that rotate every 5 minutes for privacy — a textbook case of *identity management with privacy*.
- **Smart Grid:** Smart meters authenticate with utility head-end systems using **DLMS/COSEM** with PKI; the IdM system must support **millions of meters** with 15+ year lifetimes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Identity Management is *concept-heavy*, so the derivations here are lightweight-crypto and information-theoretic — exactly the kind of 7-mark analytical sub-questions KTU 14-mark papers demand. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

### 3.1 Derivation: Password Entropy and Crack-Time (Sub-Question Type)

**Problem (Typical KTU style):** A smart-home gateway uses a **6-character PIN** drawn from digits $0$–$9$ (so $N = 10$). An attacker mounts an online brute-force attack, rate $R = 50$ guesses/sec. Compute (a) entropy, (b) worst-case crack time.

**Step 1 — Identify variables**
Length $L = 6$, character set $N = 10$, attack rate $R = 50$ guesses/sec.

**Step 2 — Apply the entropy formula**

$$
E \;=\; L \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**

$$
E \;=\; 6 \cdot \log_2(10)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate $\log_2(10)$**

$$
\log_2(10) \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(10)}{\ln(2)} \;=\; \dfrac{2.302585}{0.693147} \;\approx\; 3.321928
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by length**

$$
E \;\approx\; 6 \times 3.321928 \;\approx\; 19.9316\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Classify using NIST SP 800-63B**

$$
E \;\approx\; 19.93\ \text{bits} \;<\; 28\ \text{bits} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \textbf{Weak}
$$

**Step 7 — Worst-case search space**

$$
S \;=\; N^{L} \;=\; 10^{6} \;=\; 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 8 — Worst-case crack time**

$$
T_{\text{crack}} \;=\; \dfrac{N^{L}}{R \cdot 2} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{50 \times 2} \;=\; \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{100} \;=\; 10{,}000\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert to hours**

$$
T_{\text{crack}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{10{,}000}{3600} \;\approx\; 2.78\ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 10 — Engineering conclusion**

$$
\boxed{\;E \approx 19.93\ \text{bits (Weak)};\quad T_{\text{crack}} \approx 2.78\ \text{hours (highly insecure)}\;}
$$

> **Examiner interpretation:** A 6-digit numeric PIN is *insecure* for a smart-home gateway exposed to the internet. Recommended fix: switch to an **8-character alphanumeric password** and enforce **MFA via TOTP**, which raises $E$ to $8 \times \log_2(95) \approx 52.4$ bits (Reasonable).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: HMAC Computation for DTLS-PSK Authentication

**Problem:** Two IoT devices (sensor $A$, gateway $B$) share a pre-shared key $K$. Sensor $A$ sends a CoAP message $M$ = `GET /temp` over DTLS-PSK. Compute the HMAC-SHA-256 used for authentication, given the SHA-256 compression function outputs $h_1$ and $h_2$ for the two padding rounds. Use the standard IPAD/OPAD values: $\text{ipad} = 0x36$, $\text{opad} = 0x5C$ (both 64-byte blocks for SHA-256).

**Step 1 — State the HMAC equation**

$$
\text{HMAC}(K, M) \;=\; \mathcal{H}\!\bigl(\,(K \oplus opad) \,\|\, \mathcal{H}\bigl((K \oplus ipad) \,\|\, M\bigr)\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Key length handling (for SHA-256, block size $B = 64$ bytes)**

If $|K| < B$, pad $K$ with zeros to $K_0$ of length $B$.

**Step 3 — Compute inner-pad key block**

$$
K_{\text{inner}} \;=\; K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}_{64}
$$

Each byte of $K_0$ is XORed with $0x36$.

**Step 4 — Compute outer-pad key block**

$$
K_{\text{outer}} \;=\; K_0 \oplus \text{opad}_{64}
$$

Each byte of $K_0$ is XORed with $0x5C$.

**Step 5 — Concatenate inner key with message**

$$
X_1 \;=\; K_{\text{inner}} \,\|\, M
$$

**Step 6 — Apply SHA-256 to inner block (first hash call)**

$$
h_1 \;=\; \text{SHA-256}(X_1) \quad\text{(32-byte output)}
$$

**Step 7 — Concatenate outer key with $h_1$**

$$
X_2 \;=\; K_{\text{outer}} \,\|\, h_1
$$

**Step 8 — Apply SHA-256 again (second hash call)**

$$
h_2 \;=\; \text{SHA-256}(X_2) \quad\text{(32-byte output = HMAC tag)}
$$

**Step 9 — Result**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HMAC}(K, M) \;=\; h_2 \;\in\; \{0,1\}^{256}\;}
$$

**Step 10 — Engineering note**

> [!NOTE]
> The 32-byte (256-bit) HMAC tag is appended to the DTLS record. The gateway $B$ recomputes the HMAC with the *same* $K$ and $M$. A mismatch indicates either tampering or a desynchronised key — triggering re-handshake or session tear-down. This is the **de-facto** authentication method for resource-constrained CoAP nodes.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of IoT IdM Standards

> **KTU Use-Case:** A 14-mark question often asks: *"Compare the identity management approaches suitable for IoT."* The following table is the **gold-standard** board answer.

| Dimension | **X.509 PKI** | **OAuth 2.0** | **OpenID Connect** | **SAML 2.0** | **DID / SSI** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Token format** | X.509 certificate | OAuth access token (opaque/JWT) | **ID Token (JWT)** | SAML assertion (XML) | **Verifiable Credential (VC)** |
| **Transport** | TLS / DTLS | HTTPS / CoAP | HTTPS | HTTPS / SOAP | Decentralized (DIDComm) |
| **Best for** | Device-to-cloud authentication | Delegated authorization | Federated user login | Enterprise SSO | Privacy-preserving IoT |
| **Crypto cost** | **High** (RSA/ECDSA) | Low–Medium | Low | **High** (XML signing) | Medium |
| **Storage on device** | 1–2 KB cert | ~200 B token | ~1–2 KB JWT | ~5–10 KB XML | ~1 KB VC |
| **Trust model** | Hierarchical CA | Centralized IdP | Federated IdP | Federated IdP | **Decentralized** |
| **Offline verification** | Yes (if CRL/OCSP cached) | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| **Suitability for Class 0/1 IoT** | Poor (heavy) | Good | Fair | Poor | Fair |
| **Suitability for Class 2/3 IoT** | Good | **Excellent** | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Standards body** | IETF (RFC 5280) | IETF (RFC 6749) | OpenID Foundation | OASIS | W3C |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Trade-off Between Identity Storage and Lookup Cost

In an IoT broker maintaining $N$ devices, a hash-map index keyed by device-ID gives average $O(1)$ lookup but stores $N$ identity records. Memory cost:

$$
M_{\text{store}} \;=\; N \cdot (S_{\text{id}} + S_{\text{key}} + S_{\text{policy}})
$$

For a 6LoWPAN gateway with $64\ \text{KB}$ flash, $S_{\text{id}} = 16\ \text{B}$ (UUID), $S_{\text{key}} = 32\ \text{B}$ (ECC pubkey), $S_{\text{policy}} = 64\ \text{B}$ (CapBAC token):

$$
M_{\text{store}} \;=\; N \times 112\ \text{B} \;\le\; 64\ \text{KB} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; N_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{65{,}536}{112} \;\approx\; 585\ \text{devices}
$$

This explains *why* large-scale IoT IdM is **off-loaded to an edge / cloud IdP** rather than stored on the constrained device itself.

### 4.2 State Diagram — The Identity Lifecycle (P-AA-LAR-D)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Provisioning: New device / user onboarded
    Provisioning --> Authentication: Identity record created + credentials bound
    Authentication --> Authorization: Token issued
    Authorization --> Logging: Access decision logged
    Logging --> Auditing: Periodic review
    Auditing --> Logging: Continue operations
    Logging --> Authorization: Subsequent access requests
    Authorization --> Renewal: Key / cert nearing expiry
    Renewal --> Authentication: Re-credentialed
    Authorization --> Deprovisioning: Revoke (compromise / EOL)
    Deprovisioning --> [*]: Identity securely destroyed
```

### 4.4 IoT Device Authentication Flow (OAuth 2.0 Client-Credentials)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as IoT Device
    participant GW as Edge Gateway
    participant IdP as Authorization Server
    participant API as Resource Server

    Dev->>GW: TLS ClientHello (Device Cert)
    GW->>IdP: POST /oauth2/token\n(client_id, client_secret, scope=read.temp)
    IdP-->>GW: 200 OK {access_token: JWT, expires_in: 3600}
    GW->>API: GET /sensors/temp\nAuthorization: Bearer <JWT>
    API->>API: Verify JWT signature with IdP public key
    API-->>GW: 200 OK {temp: 26.4 C}
    GW-->>Dev: Forward sensor data
    Note over IdP,API: Audit log entry created for accountability
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
*Define Identity Management. List its four primary components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Identity Management (IdM)** is the framework of policies, processes, and technologies used to create, maintain, and revoke digital identities of users, devices, and services, while providing authentication, authorization, and accountability services.
>
> The four primary components are:
> 1. **Identification** — assigning a unique identifier to an entity.
> 2. **Authentication** — verifying the claimed identity.
> 3. **Authorization** — granting or denying access to specific resources.
> 4. **Accountability (Auditing)** — logging and reviewing access events.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark naming components + 1 mark brief description)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Differentiate between identification and authentication with a suitable example from an IoT context.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Identification | Authentication |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Claims *who* the entity is | *Proves* that claim |
| **Output** | An identity claim (ID string) | A boolean **true/false** verdict |
| **IoT Example** | A temperature sensor sending its **UUID `b3f4…`** in a CoAP request | The sensor presenting an **X.509 certificate** that the gateway validates against the CA's public key |
| **Failure mode** | Anonymous / unknown | Access denied / re-handshake |

*(1.5 marks for crisp difference + 1.5 marks for IoT-specific example — examiners love this)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question has *two sub-parts* of **7 marks each**, with internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (full alternative).

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**
*(a)* Explain the **four canonical identity management models** with neat diagrams and compare their suitability for IoT. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Describe the **identity management life-cycle** in IoT, highlighting the security challenges at each phase. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**1. Isolated / Silo Model (1 mark)**
Each service maintains its own user database. No inter-service sharing. Example: a temperature sensor and a motion sensor each having separate local credential stores. *Drawback:* password fatigue, no SSO.

**2. Centralized Model (2 marks)**
A single **Identity Provider (IdP)** authenticates users for all services.
- **Strength:** Single point of administration, easier auditing, supports SSO.
- **Weakness:** **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)**, privacy concern (one DB knows everything).
- **IoT fit:** Suitable for *single-organization* smart-building deployments.
- *Example:* A corporate LDAP server authenticating all IoT gateways in a campus.

**3. Federated Model (2 marks)**
Multiple trusted **IdPs** across organizations share identity assertions via standards like **SAML 2.0** or **OpenID Connect**.
- **Strength:** Cross-organizational SSO; no single IdP owns all data.
- **Weakness:** Complex trust agreements, latency in cross-domain assertions.
- **IoT fit:** Multi-vendor smart-city platforms where utility, transport, and emergency services share identity.

**4. User-Centric / Decentralized Model (2 marks)**
The user (or device) holds their own identity in a **digital wallet**, presenting **Verifiable Credentials (VCs)** to verifiers.
- **Strength:** Privacy-preserving (no central IdP), supports selective disclosure.
- **Weakness:** Key management burden; not all IoT devices can host a wallet.
- **IoT fit:** High-value IoT (medical implants, V2X) where privacy is paramount.

*(Marks distribution: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 7)*

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

| Phase | Activity | Security Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Provisioning** | Create identity, issue cert/credential | Cloning attacks at factory; secure key injection needed |
| **2. Authentication** | Verify identity at every access | Replay attacks, MITM, key compromise |
| **3. Authorization** | Enforce access-control policy | Confused-deputy, privilege escalation |
| **4. Logging** | Record every access event | Log injection, log tampering, storage exhaustion |
| **5. Auditing** | Periodically review logs | False positives, alert fatigue |
| **6. Renewal / Rotation** | Periodic key/cert rotation | Window of vulnerability, certificate expiry storms |
| **7. Deprovisioning** | Revoke and erase identity | Orphan identities, residual credentials, GDPR compliance |

**IoT-specific overlay (additional 1 mark for full marks):**
- **Scale:** Billions of devices makes centralized revocation (CRL) bandwidth-heavy → prefer **OCSP stapling** or **short-lived certificates**.
- **Constraints:** Lightweight devices cannot run full TLS → use **DTLS + PSK** or **ECQV** implicit certificates.
- **Mobility:** Devices roaming across networks need **federated trust** with roaming IdPs.
- **Lifecycle asymmetry:** A sensor deployed today may be running for 20 years while crypto standards evolve → use **crypto-agility** and **post-quantum ready** PKI.

*(Marks distribution: 6 phases × 0.5 mark = 3 marks; 1 mark for table summary; 1 mark for IoT overlay + conclusion = 7)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*(a)* Compare the identity management models — **SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect, and OAuth 2.0** — in terms of token format, use-case, and suitability for IoT. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the **OAuth 2.0 Client-Credentials flow** for authenticating an IoT device against a cloud resource server. Mention two security risks. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

| Parameter | **SAML 2.0** | **OpenID Connect** | **OAuth 2.0** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Token format | XML assertion | **JWT (ID Token)** | Opaque / JWT access token |
| Primary purpose | Authentication + SSO | **Authentication** (federated login) | **Authorization** (delegated access) |
| Transport | SOAP / HTTP Redirect / POST | HTTPS REST | HTTPS REST |
| Token size | ~5–10 KB (XML + signature) | ~1–2 KB (JWT) | ~200 B – 1 KB |
| Crypto cost | High (XML-DSig, XML-Enc) | Low (JWS / JWE) | Low |
| Typical IoT use | Rare (heavy) | Smart-home user logins | **Device-to-cloud token issuance** |
| Suitability for constrained IoT | Poor | Fair | **Good** |
| Standards body | OASIS | OpenID Foundation | IETF RFC 6749 |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For *constrained* IoT devices (CoAP, Class 0/1), **OAuth 2.0 with DTLS-PSK** is preferred. For *application-layer* user logins from browsers/apps, **OpenID Connect** is the modern choice. **SAML 2.0** is largely legacy, retained for enterprise SSO interop.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Flow diagram (4 marks):**

1. IoT device `D` boots up with a pre-provisioned **client_id** and **client_secret** (or JWT assertion).
2. `D` sends `POST /oauth2/token` to the **Authorization Server (AS)** with `grant_type=client_credentials` and `scope=read.sensors`.
3. AS validates the client credentials against the IdP database.
4. AS returns `{ "access_token": "<JWT>", "token_type": "Bearer", "expires_in": 3600 }`.
5. `D` calls `GET /api/sensors/temp` with header `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>`.
6. The **Resource Server (RS)** validates the JWT signature using the AS's public key (cached or fetched via JWKS endpoint).
7. RS enforces the scope (`read.sensors`) and returns the data.
8. Both AS and RS write **audit log entries** (accountability).

**Two security risks (2 marks):**

1. **Client-secret leakage:** If the client secret is embedded in firmware and the firmware is extracted (e.g., via JTAG/SWD), the attacker can forge token requests. *Mitigation:* store credentials in a **TPM / Secure Element** and rotate them periodically.
2. **Token replay / theft:** Bearer tokens captured in transit (or via XSS / memory scraping) can be reused. *Mitigation:* use **short-lived tokens** (≤ 1 hour) plus **refresh tokens**; bind tokens to device fingerprints via **DPoP (RFC 9449)**.

**Engineering conclusion (1 mark):**
> The OAuth 2.0 client-credentials flow is the **industry standard** for *machine-to-machine* identity in IoT (e.g., AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT). It scales to billions of devices and decouples authentication from authorization — two responsibilities that should never be conflated.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on "Identity Management" questions — Examiner's Checklist:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Authentication with Authorization** — These are *different* layers. Authentication answers *"Are you really Alice?"*; Authorization answers *"What is Alice allowed to do?"* Mixing them up costs **at least 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the IoT context** — A generic IT-security answer (e.g., "LDAP, Active Directory, Kerberos") without IoT-specific examples (DTLS, CoAP, MQTT, X.509 on TPM) loses up to **3 marks**. Always tie back to *constrained devices*, *MQTT brokers*, *CoAP*, *6LoWPAN*.
> 3. **Missing the four A's** — Identification, Authentication, Authorization, Accountability. Memorise this acronym; it's the "fingerprint" of IdM answers.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram** — A 14-mark answer without a flow diagram or model-comparison table almost always gets a **1–2 mark penalty**. Always include one.
> 5. **Ignoring provisioning & deprovisioning** — Many students only discuss *authentication*. Examiners specifically test the **full lifecycle**. Mention **P-AA-LAR-D**.
> 6. **Writing about OAuth without mentioning the four grant types** — Know them: *Authorization Code, Implicit, Resource Owner Password Credentials, Client Credentials.* For IoT, **Client Credentials** is the relevant one.
> 7. **Forgetting GDPR/Privacy** — A 14-mark question on IdM in 2024 will likely include a privacy angle. Mention **deprovisioning**, **right-to-be-forgotten**, and **DID/SSI** for privacy.
> 8. **Token format confusion** — Distinguish **JWT, SAML assertion, OAuth token, X.509 certificate**. Examiners drop marks for sloppy use of these terms.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Night Read)**

- **Definition:** Identity Management = policies + processes + tech to create, store, manage, and revoke digital identities.
- **The Four A's:** **I**dentification → **A**uthentication → **A**uthorization → **A**ccountability.
- **Lifecycle acronym:** **P-AA-LAR-D** — Provisioning, Authentication, Authorization, Logging, Auditing, Renewal, Deprovisioning.
- **Four models:** Silo, Centralized, Federated, Decentralized (SSI/DID).
- **IoT hybrid reality:** *Centralized + Federated* is the most common production pattern.
- **Identifiers:** MAC (EUI-48/64), IPv6, UUID, EPC, URI, DID.
- **Auth factors:** Knowledge, Possession, Biometric, Location, Behaviour. **MFA = ≥ 2 different categories**.
- **Authz models:** ACL, RBAC, ABAC, CapBAC, ORCON.
- **Standards:** X.509 PKI, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, SAML 2.0, DID/VC.
- **For constrained IoT:** DTLS-PSK with HMAC-SHA-256 or ECQV implicit certs.
- **For M2M auth in cloud IoT:** OAuth 2.0 *Client-Credentials* flow.
- **Key formula:** $E = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ bits of password entropy.
- **Crack time:** $T_{\text{crack}} = \dfrac{N^{L}}{2R}$.
- **JWT structure:** `B64(Header).B64(Payload).B64(Signature)`.
- **X.509 chain:** Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity (device).
- **Privacy angle:** GDPR right-to-be-forgotten mandates secure deprovisioning.
- **Emerging trend:** **Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) with W3C DIDs and Verifiable Credentials** — high-yield for future KTU papers.
- **Examiner mantra:** Always draw a **diagram**, always give an **IoT-specific example**, always end with a **conclusion line**.
- **One-line board answer:** *"Identity management is the lifecycle management of digital identities of users, devices, and services, encompassing identification, authentication, authorization, and accountability."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note:** Identity Management in IoT is the *gateway topic* to advanced IoT security modules. Master the four A's, the four models, the five standards, and the P-AA-LAR-D lifecycle. A clear diagram + an IoT-specific example is the difference between a **6-mark** answer and a **12-mark** answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Access Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Access Control** is a security mechanism that regulates who or what can view, use, or access resources in a computing environment. In the context of **IoT (Internet of Things)**, it is a foundational pillar of the **AAA Framework** — **Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting** — that ensures only legitimate, verified, and authorized entities (devices, users, services) are permitted to interact with IoT resources, data streams, and actuation commands.

The **three pillars of the AAA security model** are defined as follows:

1. **Authentication** — The process of verifying the identity of a user, device, or service. In IoT, this typically involves validating unique device certificates, pre-shared keys (PSK), or biometric credentials.
2. **Authorization** — The process of granting or denying specific permissions to an authenticated entity. It defines *what* an authenticated party is allowed to do.
3. **Accounting** — The process of tracking and logging the consumption of resources, recording actions for audit, billing, and forensic analysis.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — A Secured Office Building:**
> Imagine a corporate office building as an IoT network. Every employee (a device) is given a **smart ID card** (their authentication token). When they arrive at the main door, a **security guard** checks the card — this is **Authentication**. Once inside, the card only opens the doors the employee is allowed to enter (e.g., the server room card does not open the CFO's office) — this is **Authorization**. Finally, the building's **CCTV and entry logs** record exactly when each person entered and exited, and which rooms they accessed — this is **Accounting**.
> 
> In the same way, IoT Access Control acts as the security infrastructure that decides **WHO** can connect, **WHAT** they can do, and **RECORDS** everything they did.

### Key IoT Metrics and Standards

- **Authentication Latency:** typically **< 100 ms** for resource-constrained IoT devices.
- **Key Length Standards:** **128-bit** minimum for symmetric encryption (AES), **2048-bit** for asymmetric (RSA).
- **Standardized IoT Security Protocols:** **DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security)**, **TLS 1.3**, **X.509 certificates**, **OAuth 2.0**, **CoAPs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Access Control is positioned as a *cross-cutting security requirement* that overlaps with **Identity Management, Cryptography, and Network Security** layers in the IoT Reference Architecture (typically aligned with the **three-layer** or **five-layer IoT models**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Access Control in IoT

The Access Control workflow in any IoT system operates through a sequence of logical decision points:

- **Step 1 — Identity Claim:** A device or user presents credentials (e.g., certificate, token, biometric hash).
- **Step 2 — Identity Verification (Authentication):** The system validates the presented credentials against a trusted **Identity Provider (IdP)** or local database.
- **Step 3 — Permission Lookup (Authorization):** The system consults a **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** to check whether the verified identity has the required permissions.
- **Step 4 — Policy Enforcement (PE):** A **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** either grants or denies the requested action.
- **Step 5 — Audit Log Update (Accounting):** Every decision is recorded with a timestamp, identity, resource accessed, and outcome.

### 2.2 Access Control Models (High-Yield for KTU)

| Model | Full Name | Decision Basis | Typical IoT Use Case | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ACL** | Access Control List | Resource-centric (user-list per resource) | Simple sensor networks | Easy to implement | Hard to scale |
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner's discretion | Smart home personal devices | Flexible | Vulnerable to insider threats |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | Centralized security labels | Military / industrial IoT | Extremely secure | Rigid, complex administration |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Predefined roles | Enterprise IoT, smart factories | Scalable, intuitive | Role explosion problem |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Multi-attribute policies | Cloud-federated IoT | Highly granular | Computationally expensive |
| **CapBAC** | Capability-Based Access Control | Cryptographic tokens | Distributed IoT, edge computing | Decentralized, lightweight | Token revocation complexity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in IoT:** Resource-constrained IoT nodes (e.g., Class-0 devices, less than **10 KB RAM**) cannot run heavyweight RBAC servers. Therefore, **CapBAC (Capability-Based Access Control)** and **ABAC** with simplified policy engines are the *de facto* choices in modern lightweight IoT protocols like **CoAP** and **MQTT-SN**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Access Decision Function** | $D(u, r, e) = P(u) \cap A(r, e)$ | Decision: user *u*, resource *r*, environment *e*; P = permissions, A = ACL | Boolean (Allow / Deny) |
