| **RBAC Permission Mapping** | $Perm(role) = \bigcup_{i} P_i(role_i)$ | Union of all permissions across assigned roles | Set of permissions |
| **Authentication Confidence Score** | $C = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - c_i)$ | Combined confidence of *n* multi-factor authentications | $0 \leq C \leq 1$ |
| **Bit Strength** | $E = 2^k$ | Effective keyspace for a *k*-bit key | For **AES-128**: $E = 2^{128}$ |
| **False Acceptance Rate** | $FAR = \frac{FA}{FA + TR}$ | Probability of wrongly accepting an impostor | Dimensionless |
| **False Rejection Rate** | $FRR = \frac{FR}{FR + TA}$ | Probability of wrongly rejecting a genuine user | Dimensionless |
| **Equal Error Rate (EER)** | $EER : FAR = FRR$ | Crossover point for biometric systems | Lower is better |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL PIPING RULE:** All vertical bars used as absolute value or conditional probability delimiters inside the table have been converted to `\vert` or word-rewrite (e.g., "FA divided by FA plus TR") to preserve valid Markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Access control is the **cornerstone of zero-trust security architectures** in:
- **Smart Cities** (streetlight control, traffic sensors).
- **Healthcare IoT** (HIPAA-compliant patient wearables).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** (SCADA, PLC programming access).
- **Smart Home / Consumer IoT** (voice assistants, smart locks).
- **Connected Vehicles** (V2X communication, OTA updates).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Combined Multi-Factor Authentication Confidence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme frequently tests the **"Combining Independent Authentication Factors"** concept. The derivation below is a frequently asked exam derivation.

**Problem Setup:** A smart-locking IoT system employs **three independent authentication factors**: a fingerprint ($c_1 = 0.85$), a PIN ($c_2 = 0.90$), and a proximity device ($c_3 = 0.75$). Compute the combined authentication confidence.

**Step 1 — Define the independence model.**

The factors are statistically independent. The probability that **all** factors *fail* to authenticate (false negative) is the product of individual failure probabilities.

$$P(\text{all fail}) = (1 - c_1) \cdot (1 - c_2) \cdot (1 - c_3)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{all fail}) &= (1 - 0.85) \times (1 - 0.90) \times (1 - 0.75) \\
&= 0.15 \times 0.10 \times 0.25 \\
&= 0.00375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the combined confidence.**

The system is authenticated if **at least one** factor succeeds. This is the complement of all-fail.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 1 - P(\text{all fail}) \\
&= 1 - 0.00375 \\
&= 0.99625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage.**

$$C = 99.625\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

> [!NOTE]
> Individually, the highest factor offers only **90%** confidence. Layered multi-factor authentication boosts the combined confidence to **99.625%**, demonstrating the *defense in depth* principle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RBAC Permission Union for a Composite Role

**Problem Setup:** A *Smart Factory Operator* role inherits permissions from three sub-roles: *SensorMonitor*, *ActuatorController*, and *AlarmAcknowledge*. Given:

- $P(\text{SensorMonitor}) = \{ \text{read\_temp}, \text{read\_vib} \}$
- $P(\text{ActuatorController}) = \{ \text{start\_pump}, \text{stop\_pump} \}$
- $P(\text{AlarmAcknowledge}) = \{ \text{ack\_alarm} \}$

Compute $P(\text{Operator})$.

**Step 1 — Apply the RBAC union rule.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Operator}) &= P(\text{SensorMonitor}) \cup P(\text{ActuatorController}) \cup P(\text{AlarmAcknowledge})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the set union (eliminate duplicates if any).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{Operator}) &= \{ \text{read\_temp}, \text{read\_vib} \} \cup \{ \text{start\_pump}, \text{stop\_pump} \} \cup \{ \text{ack\_alarm} \} \\
&= \{ \text{read\_temp},\ \text{read\_vib},\ \text{start\_pump},\ \text{stop\_pump},\ \text{ack\_alarm} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cardinality check (total permissions).**

$$|P(\text{Operator})| = 5 \text{ permissions}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Access Control Model Selection Guide

| IoT Scenario | Recommended Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Personal smart home with 5 devices | **DAC / ACL** | Low scale, owner-centric trust |
| Hospital IoT with 500+ wearables | **RBAC** | Role-based (Doctor, Nurse, Admin) |
| Defense-grade battlefield sensors | **MAC** | Strict security labels, no exceptions |
| Cross-organization supply chain IoT | **ABAC** | Attribute-based federation across firms |
| Edge-fog distributed sensor mesh | **CapBAC** | Lightweight tokens, no central server |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AAA Access Control Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Device / User] --> B[Request Access]
    B --> C[Authentication Phase]
    C --> C1{Credentials Valid?}
    C1 -- No --> D1[Access Denied - Log Attempt]
    C1 -- Yes --> E[Authorization Phase]
    E --> E1{Policy Permits?}
    E1 -- No --> D2[Access Denied - Log Reason]
    E1 -- Yes --> F[Resource Granted]
    F --> G[Accounting Phase]
    G --> H[Audit Log Storage]
    H --> I[Session Active]
    I --> J[Session Termination]
    J --> K[Final Audit Entry]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style H fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

### 4.2 RBAC Role Hierarchy for Smart Healthcare IoT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SuperAdmin] --> B[HospitalAdmin]
    A --> C[NetworkAdmin]
    B --> D[Doctor]
    B --> E[Nurse]
    B --> F[Receptionist]
    C --> G[DeviceManager]
    C --> H[SecurityAuditor]
    D --> D1[Read Patient Vitals]
    D --> D2[Prescribe Treatment]
    E --> E1[Read Patient Vitals]
    E --> E2[Update Care Notes]
    F --> F1[Schedule Appointment]
    G --> G1[Provision New Sensor]
    G --> G2[Firmware Update]
    H --> H1[Read Audit Logs]
    H --> H2[Flag Anomalies]

    style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style B fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style H fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
```

### 4.3 Capability-Based Access Control (CapBAC) Token Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IoT Sensor
    participant Edge as Edge Gateway
    participant Cloud as Cloud Authorization Server
    participant Sink as Data Sink

    Sensor->>Edge: 1. Connection Request
    Edge->>Cloud: 2. Verify Device Identity
    Cloud->>Cloud: 3. Generate Capability Token
    Cloud->>Edge: 4. Issue Token (with expiry and scope)
    Edge->>Sensor: 5. Forward Token
    Sensor->>Sink: 6. Request Data with Token
    Sink->>Sink: 7. Validate Token Signature
    Sink->>Sensor: 8. Grant Access
    Sink->>Cloud: 9. Send Usage Log
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of IoT Access Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHY [Physical Layer]
        PHY1[Smart Sensors]
        PHY2[RFID Tags]
        PHY3[Actuators]
    end

    subgraph NET [Network Layer]
        NET1[Gateway / Edge]
        NET2[Firewall / IDS]
    end

    subgraph ACC [Access Control Layer]
        ACC1[Authentication Module]
        ACC2[Authorization Engine]
        ACC3[Accounting Logs]
    end

    subgraph APP [Application Layer]
        APP1[Cloud Dashboard]
        APP2[Mobile App]
        APP3[Third-Party Services]
    end

    PHY --> NET1 --> NET2 --> ACC1 --> ACC2 --> ACC3 --> APP
    APP2 --> ACC1
    APP3 --> ACC1

    style ACC fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style ACC1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style ACC2 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style ACC3 fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define Access Control in the context of IoT. List any two access control models used in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Access Control is a security mechanism that regulates which entities (users, devices, services) are allowed to interact with specific resources in an IoT system. It is a key component of the AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) framework.

Two access control models are:
1. **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):** Grants permissions based on the role assigned to a user/device.
2. **DAC (Discretionary Access Control):** The resource owner decides who can access it.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each model = 3 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization in IoT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Verifies *identity* | Verifies *permissions* |
| **Question Answered** | "Who are you?" | "What can you do?" |
| **Mechanism** | Passwords, biometrics, certificates | ACLs, RBAC roles, ABAC policies |
| **Order** | Always precedes authorization | Always follows authentication |

*(1 mark for each correct distinction, 1 mark for table layout = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **AAA security framework** in detail, with specific reference to IoT. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

The **AAA framework** is a foundational security model that governs access to IoT resources through three sequential processes:

**1. Authentication (2 marks):**
- Verifies the identity of the requesting entity.
- IoT-specific methods include **X.509 device certificates**, **pre-shared keys (PSK)**, and **PUF (Physically Unclonable Function)** based hardware fingerprints.
- Lightweight protocols: **DTLS handshake** for CoAP-based devices.

**2. Authorization (3 marks):**
- Determines what the authenticated entity is permitted to do.
- Implemented using **RBAC** (role-based), **ABAC** (attribute-based), or **CapBAC** (capability-based) models.
- Example: A *temperature sensor* may be authorized to *publish* data but not to *subscribe* to actuator commands.

**3. Accounting (2 marks):**
- Logs every authentication attempt, access decision, and resource consumption.
- Essential for **forensic analysis**, **regulatory compliance** (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA), and **anomaly detection**.
- Typically stored in **SIEM (Security Information and Event Management)** systems.

---

**(b)** Compare the following access control models with suitable IoT examples: **DAC, MAC, RBAC, ABAC**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Model | Description | Strength | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Owner controls access | Flexible | Smart home owner's personal devices |
| **MAC** | Central policy based on labels | Very secure | Military battlefield sensors |
| **RBAC** | Permissions tied to roles | Scalable | Hospital staff accessing medical devices |
| **ABAC** | Policy based on attributes | Highly granular | Cross-firm supply chain access |

*Each model = 1.5 marks; comparison table layout = 1 mark = 7 marks total.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **RBAC model**. How is it implemented in a smart factory IoT deployment? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**1. RBAC Definition (2 marks):**
RBAC assigns permissions to *roles* rather than to individual users. Users acquire permissions by being assigned to one or more roles.

**2. Core Components (2 marks):**
- **Users** (operators, engineers, auditors)
- **Roles** (Monitor, Controller, Supervisor)
- **Permissions** (read, write, execute)
- **Sessions** (binding of user to active role)

**3. Smart Factory IoT Implementation (3 marks):**
- **Operator Role** → can *read* sensor values, *acknowledge* alarms.
- **Maintenance Role** → can *start/stop* actuators, *update firmware*.
- **Supervisor Role** → inherits Operator + Maintenance + *override* capabilities.
- Permissions are enforced at the **edge gateway**, with central role management in the **cloud IAM (Identity and Access Management)** system.

---

**(b)** Discuss **Capability-Based Access Control (CapBAC)** and its advantages for resource-constrained IoT nodes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
CapBAC issues a *cryptographically signed capability token* to an entity, which contains the entity's identity, the resource identifier, and the permitted actions. The token itself is the proof of authorization.

**2. Operational Steps (2 marks):**
- **Issuance:** Authorization server generates and signs a token after authenticating the device.
- **Presentation:** The device presents the token with each request.
- **Verification:** The resource server validates the signature and the token's expiry.

**3. Advantages for IoT (3 marks):**
- **Decentralized:** No continuous online lookup at a central server.
- **Lightweight:** Token validation requires only a signature check, ideal for Class-0 devices.
- **Scalable:** Tokens are self-contained, supporting millions of devices.
- **Privacy-preserving:** No persistent identity disclosure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Authentication with Authorization** — students often interchange the two. Remember: Authentication = *Who you are*; Authorization = *What you can do*.
> 2. **Skipping diagrams in RBAC questions** — examiners frequently award 1–2 marks *only* for a clear hierarchy diagram. Always draw a role-inheritance tree.
> 3. **Not mentioning IoT-specific examples** — generic textbook definitions fetch only partial marks. Tie every model to a *smart factory / smart home / healthcare* scenario.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Accounting" leg of AAA** — many students only discuss *A* and *A*, skipping *Accounting*. This costs up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Hand-waving cryptography** — when discussing CapBAC, *explicitly* mention the cryptographic signature (e.g., ECDSA, EdDSA) and key length.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**
> 
> - **AAA Framework** is the golden trio: **Authentication → Authorization → Accounting**.
> - **DAC** = owner discretion; **MAC** = mandatory labels; **RBAC** = roles; **ABAC** = attributes; **CapBAC** = signed tokens.
> - **RBAC** is the most widely deployed enterprise IoT model; expect a 7-mark diagram question.
> - **CapBAC** is the modern answer for resource-constrained Class-0/Class-1 IoT devices.
> - **Multi-Factor Authentication** boosts confidence via the formula $C = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - c_i)$.
> - **X.509 certificates + DTLS** are the standard authentication mechanism for CoAP-based IoT.
> - **OAuth 2.0** is the *de facto* delegated authorization framework for cloud-federated IoT.
> - **Audit logging (Accounting)** is non-negotiable for **GDPR**, **HIPAA**, and **ISO 27001** compliance.
> - Always tie theoretical models to **smart factory / smart home / healthcare IoT** examples in your exam answers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure Message Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Secure Message Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Secure Message Communication** in the context of the Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the set of cryptographic protocols, algorithms, and infrastructure mechanisms that ensure confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation of data exchanged between IoT devices, gateways, cloud platforms, and end-users.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PEMRT525, secure message communication encompasses the application of cryptographic primitives (symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, hashing, and digital signatures) over lightweight IoT transport protocols such as **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**, **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)**, **AMQP**, and **HTTP/HTTPS**.

The four pillars of secure message communication are:

| Pillar | Cryptographic Tool | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | Symmetric/Asymmetric Encryption (AES, RSA) | Prevents eavesdropping |
| **Integrity** | Hash Functions (SHA-256) | Detects tampering |
| **Authentication** | Digital Certificates, MAC | Verifies sender identity |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Digital Signatures | Sender cannot deny origin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Secure Message Communication is a 14-mark weightage topic in Module 4. Examiners frequently test the *differences between symmetric and asymmetric cryptography*, the *TLS handshake flow*, and *application-level security in MQTT/CoAP*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sealed Notary Letter

Imagine you want to send a confidential letter to a friend living in another city, but you are worried about three things:

1. **A thief reading it in transit** → You lock the letter inside a **tamper-proof steel box** (this is **Encryption**).
2. **The thief opening the box and modifying the letter** → You seal the box with a unique **wax seal** that breaks if tampered (this is a **Hash / Message Digest**).
3. **Your friend wanting to verify it was really you who sent it** → You sign the letter with your **unique handwritten signature** that only you can produce, verified by a **government-issued ID card** (this is a **Digital Signature + PKI Certificate**).

In the IoT world, a temperature sensor in a smart factory is your "friend" — it must securely send data to a cloud server, and all three protections must be applied in a lightweight, low-power manner suitable for constrained devices.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Cryptographic Parameters (Bold values are KTU-recommended):**
> - **AES Key Size:** **128 bits** (standard), 192 bits, 256 bits
> - **RSA Key Size:** **2048 bits** (minimum secure), 4096 bits (high security)
> - **SHA-256 Output Size:** **256 bits (32 bytes)**
> - **ECC Key Size:** **256 bits** (equivalent to 3072-bit RSA)
> - **TLS Version:** **TLS 1.3** (current standard, IETF RFC 8446)
> - **DTLS Version:** **DTLS 1.2** (for CoAP, RFC 6347)

## 1.4 Threat Model in IoT Communication

IoT messages are vulnerable to attacks at multiple layers. Secure message communication addresses:

- **Eavesdropping (Passive Attack):** Unauthorized reading of sensor data
- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM):** Attacker intercepts and modifies messages
- **Replay Attack:** Attacker re-sends a previously captured valid message
- **Impersonation:** Fake device pretends to be a legitimate sensor
- **Data Tampering:** Modification of payload during transit

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cryptographic Techniques

Secure message communication uses three primary classes of cryptographic primitives:

### 2.1.1 Symmetric Key Cryptography (Secret Key)

- **Single shared key** is used for both encryption and decryption.
- **Speed:** Very fast, suitable for bulk data encryption in IoT.
- **Key Distribution Problem:** Key must be shared securely out-of-band.
- **Algorithms:** **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)**, 3DES, ChaCha20.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Sender and receiver agree on a shared secret key $K$.
2. Sender computes Ciphertext $C = E_K(M)$ where $M$ is plaintext.
3. Receiver computes Plaintext $M = D_K(C)$.
4. Symmetry property: $D_K(E_K(M)) = M$ for all valid messages.

### 2.1.2 Asymmetric Key Cryptography (Public Key)

- Uses a mathematically linked **key pair**: Public Key $K_{pub}$ and Private Key $K_{pr}$.
- **Public key** is freely distributed; **Private key** is kept secret.
- **Speed:** ~1000x slower than symmetric; used for key exchange and digital signatures.
- **Algorithms:** **RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman)**, **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)**, Diffie-Hellman.

### 2.1.3 Hash Functions (One-Way Functions)

- Takes arbitrary-length input and produces **fixed-length output** (digest).
- **Properties:** Deterministic, fast, pre-image resistant, collision resistant.
- **Algorithms:** **SHA-256** (Secure Hash Algorithm), SHA-3, MD5 (deprecated).

## 2.2 Hybrid Encryption — The Practical Standard

Pure asymmetric encryption is too slow for IoT bulk data. Pure symmetric encryption suffers from the key distribution problem. **Hybrid encryption** solves both:

**Operational Logic:**
1. Generate a **random symmetric session key** $K_s$.
2. Encrypt the message $M$ with $K_s$ using AES: $C_1 = AES_{K_s}(M)$.
3. Encrypt $K_s$ with the receiver's RSA public key: $C_2 = RSA_{K_{pub}}(K_s)$.
4. Transmit $(C_1 \parallel C_2)$ over the network.
5. Receiver uses private key to recover $K_s$, then decrypts $C_1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** TLS 1.3 uses a hybrid scheme combining **X25519 (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman)** for key exchange and **AES-256-GCM** for bulk encryption. This is the de-facto standard for secure MQTT communication.

## 2.3 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) and Digital Certificates

**PKI** is the framework that binds public keys to real-world identities through **X.509 Digital Certificates** issued by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.

**Certificate Hierarchy:**
- **Root CA** (self-signed, stored in device trust store)
- **Intermediate CA** (signed by Root CA)
- **End-Entity Certificate** (signed by Intermediate CA, belongs to the IoT device/server)

A typical X.509 certificate contains: Subject DN, Issuer DN, Public Key, Validity Period, Serial Number, and the **CA's Digital Signature**.

## 2.4 Digital Signatures — Ensuring Non-Repudiation

A digital signature is the asymmetric inverse of encryption:

**Signing Process:**
1. Compute hash: $h = SHA\text{-}256(M)$.
2. Sign hash with private key: $\sigma = RSA_{K_{pr}}(h)$.
3. Send $(M \parallel \sigma)$.

**Verification Process:**
1. Recover hash: $h' = RSA_{K_{pub}}(\sigma)$.
2. Compute received hash: $h = SHA\text{-}256(M)$.
3. Accept if and only if $h = h'$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is mandatory for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic.**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Symmetric Encryption | $C = E_K(M)$ | $E_K$ = encryption function | Ciphertext $C$ |
| Symmetric Decryption | $M = D_K(C)$ | $D_K$ = decryption function | Plaintext $M$ |
| RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$ | $e$ = public exponent | $n = p \cdot q$ |
| RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$ | $d$ = private exponent | Euler totient $\phi(n)$ |
| RSA Key Relation | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Multiplicative inverse | Modular arithmetic |
| Euler Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | For prime $p, q$ | Number theory |
| Hash Output Size | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ | SHA-256 | **256 bits** |
| HMAC | $HMAC_K(M) = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | Keyed hash | Authentication |
| Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $S = B^a \pmod{p} = A^b \pmod{p}$ | $a,b$ private, $A,B$ public | Primes $p,g$ |
| ECC Point Addition | $P + Q = R$ (on curve $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$) | Point group | Discrete logarithm |
| TLS Cipher Suite | $TLS\_AES\_256\_GCM\_SHA384$ | Protocol negotiation | TLS 1.3 |
| Modulus Size (RSA) | $\geq 2048$ bits | NIST recommendation | **128-bit security** |
| ECC Equivalent | $256$-bit ECC $\approx 3072$-bit RSA | NIST level 5 | **128-bit security** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Secure message communication is deployed in:

- **Smart Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant transmission of patient vitals from wearables to hospital cloud.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** OPC-UA over TLS protects factory-floor control messages.
- **Smart Grid:** IEC 62351 standard mandates TLS/DTLS for smart meter communication.
- **Connected Vehicles:** V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) uses **IEEE 1609.2** with ECDSA signatures.
- **Smart Home:** Zigbee 3.0 uses **AES-128-CCM** for message confidentiality and integrity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 RSA Key Generation — Exhaustive Derivation

RSA security relies on the computational difficulty of factoring large composite numbers. The following derivation walks through the complete RSA setup, which is a **frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU exams**.

### Step 1: Choose Two Large Primes

Select two distinct large primes $p$ and $q$. For KTU exam purposes, we use small primes for illustration:

$$p = 61, \quad q = 53$$

### Step 2: Compute the Modulus $n$

The modulus $n$ is the product of the two primes and forms part of both the public and private keys.

$$n = p \times q = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$

**Logic:** The modulus $n$ is publicly known, but factoring it back into $p$ and $q$ is computationally infeasible when $p$ and $q$ are each 1024+ bits long.

### Step 3: Compute Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$

For $n = p \cdot q$ where both $p$ and $q$ are prime, Euler's totient is:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1) \times (q - 1)$$

Substituting the chosen values:

$$\phi(n) = (61 - 1) \times (53 - 1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$$

**Logic:** $\phi(n)$ counts the integers from 1 to $n$ that are coprime to $n$. This value is critical because it determines the size of the valid key space.

### Step 4: Choose the Public Exponent $e$

Select $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. A common choice is $e = 65537$ (a Fermat prime), but for illustration we use a smaller value:

$$e = 17$$

Verify coprimality: $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ (since 17 is prime and does not divide 3120). ✓

**Logic:** $e$ must be coprime to $\phi(n)$ to ensure a unique modular inverse $d$ exists.

### Step 5: Compute the Private Exponent $d$

Compute $d$ as the modular multiplicative inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:

$$d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

$$d \cdot 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9$$
$$17 = 1 \times 9 + 8$$
$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1$$
$$8 = 8 \times 1 + 0$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 9 - 1 \times 8$$
$$1 = 9 - 1 \times (17 - 1 \times 9) = 2 \times 9 - 1 \times 17$$
$$1 = 2 \times (3120 - 183 \times 17) - 1 \times 17 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17$$

Therefore $d \equiv -367 \pmod{3120}$:

$$d = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

**Logic:** The private key $d$ is the unique value that, when multiplied by $e$ and reduced mod $\phi(n)$, gives 1. This inverse relationship is what makes RSA decryption work.

### Step 6: Publish the Public Key and Secure the Private Key

$$\boxed{Public\ Key = (e, n) = (17, 3233)}$$
$$\boxed{Private\ Key = (d, n) = (2753, 3233)}$$

### Step 7: Encrypt a Plaintext Message

Suppose the sender wants to send the plaintext message $M = 65$ (representing ASCII 'A').

The sender uses the receiver's public key $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$:

$$C \equiv M^e \pmod{n}$$
$$C \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233}$$

Computing modular exponentiation step-by-step:

$$65^2 = 4225 = 1 \times 3233 + 992, \quad \text{so } 65^2 \equiv 992 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^4 \equiv 992^2 = 984064 \pmod{3233}$$
$$984064 \div 3233 \approx 304.36, \quad 304 \times 3233 = 982832$$
$$984064 - 982832 = 1232, \quad \text{so } 65^4 \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^8 \equiv 1232^2 = 1517824 \pmod{3233}$$
$$1517824 \div 3233 \approx 469.48, \quad 469 \times 3233 = 1516277$$
$$1517824 - 1516277 = 1547, \quad \text{so } 65^8 \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{16} \equiv 1547^2 = 2393209 \pmod{3233}$$
$$2393209 \div 3233 \approx 740.21, \quad 740 \times 3233 = 2392420$$
$$2393209 - 2392420 = 789, \quad \text{so } 65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$$

Now, $17 = 16 + 1$, so:

$$65^{17} = 65^{16} \times 65 \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233}$$
$$789 \times 65 = 51285$$
$$51285 \div 3233 \approx 15.86, \quad 15 \times 3233 = 48495$$
$$51285 - 48495 = 2790$$

$$C = 2790$$

### Step 8: Decrypt the Ciphertext

The receiver uses their private key $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$:

$$M \equiv C^d \pmod{n}$$
$$M \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233}$$

By Euler's theorem, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$M^{ed} \equiv M^1 \pmod{n}$$

The recovered plaintext is:

$$M = 65$$

Which corresponds to the original ASCII 'A'. **Decryption successful.** ✓

## 3.2 Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange — Worked Example

Two IoT devices $A$ and $B$ want to establish a shared symmetric key over an insecure channel.

### Step 1: Public Parameters

Agree on a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$:

$$p = 23, \quad g = 5$$

### Step 2: Each Device Chooses a Private Key

$$a = 6 \quad (Device\ A's\ private\ key)$$
$$b = 15 \quad (Device\ B's\ private\ key)$$

### Step 3: Each Device Computes Its Public Key

$$A = g^a \pmod{p} = 5^6 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^6 \equiv 4 \times 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$$

$$A = 8$$

$$B = g^b \pmod{p} = 5^{15} \pmod{23}$$
$$5^8 \equiv 5^6 \times 5^2 \equiv 8 \times 2 = 16 \pmod{23}$$
$$5^{15} = 5^8 \times 5^4 \times 5^2 \times 5^1 \equiv 16 \times 4 \times 2 \times 5 \pmod{23}$$
$$16 \times 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \times 23 = 18 \pmod{23}$$
$$18 \times 2 = 36 \equiv 36 - 23 = 13 \pmod{23}$$
$$13 \times 5 = 65 \equiv 65 - 2 \times 23 = 19 \pmod{23}$$

$$B = 19$$

### Step 4: Exchange Public Keys and Compute Shared Secret

Device A computes:

$$S_A = B^a \pmod{p} = 19^6 \pmod{23}$$

Device B computes:

$$S_B = A^b \pmod{p} = 8^{15} \pmod{23}$$

For Device A:
$$19^2 = 361 = 15 \times 23 + 16, \quad 19^2 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$$
$$19^4 \equiv 16^2 = 256 = 11 \times 23 + 3, \quad 19^4 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}$$
$$19^6 \equiv 3 \times 16 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$$

$$S_A = 2$$

By the mathematical property of Diffie-Hellman, $S_A = S_B = 2$. The shared symmetric key is **2** (in practice, this is a 256-bit number).

**Logic:** The security relies on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** — given $A$, $g$, and $p$, computing $a$ is computationally infeasible for large primes.

## 3.3 Hash Function Verification (SHA-256 Conceptual Trace)

For a message $M$, SHA-256 processes it in 512-bit blocks through 64 rounds of bitwise operations:

$$H_0 = \text{6a09e667}, \quad H_1 = \text{bb67ae85}, \quad \ldots \quad H_7 = \text{5be0cd19}$$

Each round computes:

$$T_1 = H_7 + \Sigma_1(H_4) + \text{Ch}(H_4, H_5, H_6) + K_t + W_t$$
$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(H_0) + \text{Maj}(H_0, H_1, H_2)$$

Where:
- $\text{Ch}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$ (choice function)
- $\text{Maj}(x,y,z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$ (majority function)
- $K_t$ are 64 round constants derived from cube roots of primes
- $W_t$ is the message schedule expansion

The final 256-bit hash is the concatenation of the updated $H_0$ through $H_7$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Plaintext Message M] --> B{Choose Method}
    B -->|Symmetric AES| C[Shared Secret Key K]
    C --> D[Encryption: C = AES_K M]
    D --> E[Transmit Ciphertext C]
    E --> F[Decryption: M = AES_K_inverse C]
    F --> G[Plaintext M Recovered]
    
    B -->|Asymmetric RSA| H[Receiver Public Key e,n]
    H --> I[Encryption: C = M to power e mod n]
    I --> J[Transmit Ciphertext C]
    J --> K[Receiver uses Private Key d,n]
    K --> L[Decryption: M = C to power d mod n]
    L --> M[Plaintext M Recovered]
    
    style C fill:#ffcc99
    style H fill:#99ccff
    style G fill:#90ee90
    style M fill:#90ee90
```

## 4.2 TLS 1.3 Handshake Protocol Flow (IoT MQTT Context)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant IoT_Device as IoT Device Client
    participant Broker as MQTT Broker Server
    participant CA as Certificate Authority

    Note over IoT_Device,Broker: Phase 1: Key Exchange
    IoT_Device->>Broker: ClientHello (supported cipher suites, key_share)
    Broker->>CA: Verify Server Certificate
    CA-->>Broker: Certificate Valid + Chain
    Broker->>IoT_Device: ServerHello (selected cipher, server key_share, Certificate, CertificateVerify, Finished)
    
    Note over IoT_Device,Broker: Phase 2: Authentication
    IoT_Device->>CA: Verify Server Certificate
    CA-->>IoT_Device: Certificate Valid
    IoT_Device->>Broker: (Optional Client Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished)
    
    Note over IoT_Device,Broker: Phase 3: Encrypted Application Data
    IoT_Device->>Broker: Encrypted MQTT CONNECT (using derived session keys)
    Broker->>IoT_Device: Encrypted MQTT CONNACK
    IoT_Device->>Broker: Encrypted MQTT PUBLISH (sensor data)
    Broker->>IoT_Device: Encrypted MQTT PUBACK
```

## 4.3 Digital Signature Generation and Verification

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original Message M] --> B[SHA-256 Hash Function]
    B --> C[Hash Digest h 256 bits]
    C --> D[Sign with Private Key K_pr]
    D --> E[Digital Signature sigma]
    E --> F[Transmit M and sigma]
    F --> G[Receiver Side]
    A --> G
    G --> H[Recompute SHA-256 M]
    H --> I[Hash h_prime 256 bits]
    F --> J[Decrypt sigma with Public Key K_pub]
    J --> K[Recovered Hash h_recovered]
    I --> L{Compare h_prime = h_recovered}
    K --> L
    L -->|Match| M_MSG[Message Authentic and Unmodified]
    L -->|Mismatch| N_MSG[Rejected Tampered or Forged]
    
    style M_MSG fill:#90ee90
    style N_MSG fill:#ff6666
```

## 4.4 IoT Protocol Security Stack (MQTT vs CoAP vs HTTP)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph APP[Application Layer]
        A1[MQTT Message]
        A2[CoAP Message]
        A3[HTTP Request]
    end
    
    subgraph SEC[Security Layer]
        S1[TLS 1.3 over TCP Port 8883]
        S2[DTLS 1.2 over UDP Port 5684]
        S3[TLS 1.3 over TCP Port 443]
    end
    
    subgraph TRANS[Transport Layer]
        T1[TCP]
        T2[UDP]
        T3[TCP]
    end
    
    subgraph NET[Network Layer]
        N1[IPsec Optional]
        N2[IPsec Optional]
        N3[IPsec Optional]
    end
    
    A1 --> S1 --> T1 --> N1
    A2 --> S2 --> T2 --> N2
    A3 --> S3 --> T3 --> N3
    
    style S1 fill:#ffcc99
    style S2 fill:#ffcc99
    style S3 fill:#ffcc99
```

## 4.5 Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) Certificate Chain

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootCA[Root CA Self Signed Certificate] --> intCA1[Intermediate CA 1]
    rootCA --> intCA2[Intermediate CA 2]
    intCA1 --> deviceCert1[IoT Device Certificate Sensor A]
    intCA1 --> deviceCert2[IoT Device Certificate Sensor B]
    intCA2 --> serverCert[MQTT Broker Server Certificate]
    intCA2 --> gatewayCert[Edge Gateway Certificate]
    
    style rootCA fill:#ff9999
    style intCA1 fill:#ffcc99
    style intCA2 fill:#ffcc99
    style deviceCert1 fill:#99ccff
    style deviceCert2 fill:#99ccff
    style serverCert fill:#90ee90
    style gatewayCert fill:#90ee90
```

## 4.6 Hybrid Encryption Workflow (Real-World IoT Use)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sensor Data Plaintext M] --> B[Generate Random AES Session Key K_s]
    B --> C[Encrypt M: C1 = AES_K_s M]
    C --> D[Lookup Receiver RSA Public Key e,n]
    D --> E[Encrypt K_s: C2 = RSA_K_s_public K_s]
    E --> F[Concatenate C1 and C2]
    F --> G[Transmit over MQTT/TLS]
    G --> H[Receiver extracts C1 and C2]
    H --> I[Decrypt C2 with private key: K_s = RSA_private C2]
    I --> J[Decrypt C1 with K_s: M = AES_inverse C1]
    J --> K[Plaintext M Recovered]
    
    style B fill:#ffcc99
    style K fill:#90ee90
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Keys Used** | Single shared secret key | Key pair (public + private) |
| **Speed** | Fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slow (~1000x slower) |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult (must be shared securely) | Easy (public key is open) |
| **Scalability** | Poor ($n(n-1)/2$ keys for $n$ users) | Excellent (only $n$ key pairs) |
| **Example Algorithm** | **AES-128** | **RSA-2048** |
| **Primary Use** | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |

*(3 marks — KTU expected 1 mark per correct differentiation row)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**What is a hash function? List any two properties of SHA-256.**

**Model Answer:**

A hash function is a one-way mathematical function that converts an input of arbitrary length into a fixed-size output (digest). In IoT secure messaging, SHA-256 produces a **256-bit (32-byte) digest** that uniquely represents the input message.

**Two key properties of SHA-256:**
1. **Pre-image Resistance:** Given a hash $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find the original message $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.
2. **Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks definition + 1.5 marks properties)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **omit the "key distribution problem"** in symmetric crypto answers, losing 2 marks.
> - For RSA, students often **confuse public and private exponents** ($e$ vs $d$) during encryption/decryption. Always use $e$ (public) for encryption and $d$ (private) for decryption.
> - For TLS handshake diagrams, **missing the CertificateVerify step** results in a 3-mark deduction because authentication of the server cannot be proven without it.
> - In CoAP vs MQTT security comparisons, students write about **"TLS only"** — remember CoAP uses **DTLS** (Datagram TLS) because CoAP runs over UDP.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Explain the RSA algorithm with a suitable example. Show key generation, encryption, and decryption steps. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Key Generation (3 marks)**

1. Select two distinct large primes: $p = 61$, $q = 53$.
2. Compute modulus: $n = p \times q = 3233$.
3. Compute Euler totient: $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \times 52 = 3120$.
4. Choose public exponent $e = 17$ such that $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$.
5. Compute private exponent $d$ such that $d \times e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Using extended Euclidean algorithm: $d = 2753$.
6. **Public key** = $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$. **Private key** = $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$.

**Step 2: Encryption (2 marks)**

For plaintext $M = 65$ (ASCII 'A'), the sender uses the receiver's public key:

$$C \equiv M^e \pmod{n} \equiv 65^{17} \pmod{3233} = 2790$$

**Step 3: Decryption (2 marks)**

The receiver uses their private key:

$$M \equiv C^d \pmod{n} \equiv 2790^{2753} \pmod{3233} = 65$$

Original plaintext 'A' recovered successfully.

**Security basis:** Factoring $n$ into $p \cdot q$ is computationally infeasible for 2048-bit primes, making it impossible for an attacker to recover $d$ from $e$ and $n$.

---

**(b) Describe the TLS 1.3 handshake process used in secure MQTT communication. Explain how it provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TLS 1.3 Handshake Phases (4 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Key Exchange (1-RTT):**
- **Client → Server:** Sends `ClientHello` containing supported cipher suites, key share (e.g., X25519 public key), and PSK extension.
- **Server → Client:** Sends `ServerHello` with selected cipher suite, server's key share, **Server Certificate** (X.509), **CertificateVerify** (ECDSA signature proving server owns private key), and `Finished` (MAC over handshake transcript).

**Phase 2 — Client Authentication (Optional, 0-RTT):**
- Client verifies server certificate chain up to trusted Root CA.
- If client authentication is required, client sends its own `Certificate`, `CertificateVerify`, and `Finished`.

**Phase 3 — Encrypted Application Data:**
- Both sides derive session keys using HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) over the shared Diffie-Hellman secret.
- MQTT `CONNECT`, `PUBLISH`, and `SUBSCRIBE` messages are encrypted with **AES-256-GCM**.

**Security Properties Achieved (3 marks):**

| Property | Mechanism | Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | AES-256-GCM symmetric encryption with keys derived from ECDHE shared secret | Sensor payload unreadable to eavesdropper |
| **Integrity** | GCM authentication tag (AEAD) on every TLS record | Any tampering is detected and rejected |
| **Authentication** | X.509 certificates + ECDSA CertificateVerify signature | Server identity verified against trusted CA |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Ephemeral X25519 keys generated per session | Compromise of long-term key does not reveal past sessions |

**0-RTT Mode Trade-off:** TLS 1.3 supports zero round-trip resumption but is vulnerable to replay attacks; IoT deployments typically disable it.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Diffie-Hellman key exchange algorithm. What is the man-in-the-middle attack vulnerability, and how is it prevented? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Public Parameters:** Sender and receiver agree on a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$. These values are public.
2. **Private Key Selection:** Device A picks random private key $a$; Device B picks random private key $b$.
3. **Public Key Computation:**
   - Device A computes $A = g^a \pmod p$.
   - Device B computes $B = g^b \pmod p$.
4. **Exchange and Shared Secret:**
   - A sends $A$ to B; B sends $B$ to A (over insecure channel).
   - A computes $S = B^a \pmod p$.
   - B computes $S = A^b \pmod p$.
   - Both arrive at the same shared secret $S$.

**Mathematical Justification:**

$$B^a \pmod p = (g^b)^a \pmod p = g^{ab} \pmod p$$
$$A^b \pmod p = (g^a)^b \pmod p = g^{ab} \pmod p$$
$$S_A = S_B = g^{ab} \pmod p$$

**Worked Example (1 mark):** With $p = 23$, $g = 5$, $a = 6$, $b = 15$:
- $A = 5^6 \pmod{23} = 8$
- $B = 5^{15} \pmod{23} = 19$
- $S = 19^6 \pmod{23} = 2$ (computed by both)

**Man-in-the-Middle Attack and Prevention (2 marks):**

In a basic Diffie-Hellman exchange, an attacker Mallory can intercept $A$ and $B$, substitute her own values $A'$ and $B'$, and establish two separate shared secrets — one with A and one with B — thereby reading and modifying all messages.

**Prevention using Authenticated Diffie-Hellman:**
- **Station-to-Station (STS) Protocol:** Each party signs their public key with their RSA/ECDSA private key. The signature is verified using long-term certificates.
- **TLS 1.3 approach:** Combines ephemeral X25519 key exchange with X.509 certificate authentication, ensuring that the public keys truly belong to the claimed identities.
- **Mutual Authentication:** Both client and server present certificates, eliminating the MITM gap.

---

**(b) Compare MQTT and CoAP protocols with respect to their security mechanisms. Explain how DTLS is applied to secure CoAP messages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MQTT vs CoAP Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport Protocol** | TCP (reliable, ordered) | UDP (lightweight, connectionless) |
| **Security Protocol** | **TLS 1.3** over TCP port 8883 | **DTLS 1.2** over UDP port 5684 |
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe via central broker | Request/Response (client-server) |
| **Message Overhead** | Higher (TCP handshake + TLS) | Lower (single UDP datagram) |
| **Suitability** | Reliable telemetry, home automation | Constrained sensors, lossy networks |
| **Authentication** | Username/Password, Client Certificates, OAuth 2.0 | Pre-shared keys, Raw Public Keys, X.509 certificates |
| **Quality of Service** | QoS 0, 1, 2 (at-least, at-most, exactly once) | Confirmable (CON) and Non-confirmable (NON) |
| **Keep-Alive** | Mandatory PINGREQ every 1.5 × keepalive | Optional heartbeat via CON/NACK |

**DTLS Application to CoAP (3.5 marks):**

DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security) is the UDP analogue of TLS. Since CoAP runs over UDP, TLS cannot be used directly because TLS assumes reliable, ordered delivery.

**DTLS Adaptations over TLS:**
1. **Explicit Sequence Numbers:** Each DTLS record contains a sequence number to detect replay attacks (since UDP packets may be reordered).
2. **Cookie Exchange:** DTLS server sends a HelloVerifyRequest containing a cookie to the client, which must echo it back. This prevents UDP-based Denial-of-Service amplification attacks.
3. **Retransmission Handshake Messages:** Unlike TLS, DTLS retransmits lost handshake messages because UDP does not guarantee delivery.
4. **AEAD Cipher Suites:** DTLS 1.2 uses AES-128-GCM or AES-256-GCM for combined encryption and authentication.

**CoAP Security Modes (RFC 7252):**
- **NoSec:** No security (acceptable only in trusted networks).
- **PreSharedKey:** Symmetric keys pre-configured on devices.
- **RawPublicKey:** Devices have asymmetric key pairs but no certificates (used in constrained environments).
- **Certificate:** Full X.509 certificate-based authentication (highest security).

**Practical IoT Example:** A constrained temperature sensor using CoAP can establish a DTLS session with a gateway using ECC-based ECDHE_ECDSA cipher suite, achieving 128-bit security with a 256-bit key — far smaller than the 3072-bit RSA equivalent, ideal for low-power IoT devices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Secure Message Communication (Module 4):**

- **Four Security Goals:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation — map each to a cryptographic tool.
- **Symmetric vs Asymmetric:** Symmetric = fast, single key, key distribution problem. Asymmetric = slow, key pair, solves key distribution.
- **AES:** Symmetric block cipher, key sizes 128/192/256 bits, used for bulk IoT data encryption.
- **RSA:** Asymmetric, based on prime factorization hardness, minimum 2048-bit key for security.
- **ECC:** Asymmetric, based on elliptic curve discrete logarithm, 256-bit ECC = 3072-bit RSA security. **Preferred for IoT.**
- **SHA-256:** Cryptographic hash producing 256-bit digest; properties = pre-image resistance, collision resistance, avalanche effect.
- **HMAC:** Keyed hash for message authentication; uses secret key + hash function.
- **Digital Signature:** Hash of message encrypted with sender's private key; verified using sender's public key. Provides non-repudiation.
- **PKI / X.509:** Hierarchical certificate chain (Root CA → Intermediate CA → End Entity). Binds public keys to identities.
- **TLS 1.3:** Used to secure MQTT over TCP port 8883. 1-RTT handshake, mandatory forward secrecy via ephemeral Diffie-Hellman.
- **DTLS 1.2:** Used to secure CoAP over UDP port 5684. Includes cookie exchange for DoS protection and explicit sequence numbers for replay defense.
- **Diffie-Hellman:** Key exchange protocol vulnerable to MITM; prevented by adding certificate-based authentication (STS or TLS).
- **Hybrid Encryption:** Combines RSA (for key exchange) + AES (for bulk data) — the practical standard in IoT.
- **Replay Attack Defense:** Use nonces, timestamps, or sequence numbers in every authenticated message.
- **IoT Lightweight Crypto:** Prefer ECC, ChaCha20-Poly1305, and AES-128 over RSA-2048 for constrained devices.
- **Standards to Remember:** RFC 5246 (TLS 1.2), RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3), RFC 6347 (DTLS 1.2), RFC 7252 (CoAP), OASIS MQTT 5.0 spec, IEEE 802.1AR (device identity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Step-by-step Process → Security Analysis → Real-world Application**. Examiners award partial marks generously when the structure is clear, even if the final numerical answer is slightly off.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart City IoT Architecture - Street Layer, City Layer, Data Center Layer, Services Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT525 - IOT AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Smart City IoT Architecture - Street Layer, City Layer, Data Center Layer, Services Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart City IoT Architecture** is a hierarchical, layered reference model that systematically organizes the heterogeneous physical devices, communication networks, data processing infrastructure, and end-user service components required to transform a conventional urban environment into an intelligent, data-driven, and responsive ecosystem. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this architecture is canonically divided into **four functional layers**:

1. **Street Layer** (also called the Perception / Sensing / Device Layer)
2. **City Layer** (also called the Network / Communication / Transmission Layer)
3. **Data Center Layer** (also called the Processing / Management / Platform Layer)
4. **Services Layer** (also called the Application / Service Layer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT525 - Module 4):** Students must be able to draw and explain the four-layer architecture of a Smart City IoT system, identify the components within each layer, and describe the data flow from the physical sensor (Street) all the way to the end-user application (Service). Examiner's frequently ask for **labelled block diagrams** worth 7 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

To make this abstract architecture instantly intuitive, imagine a Smart City as a **living human body**:

| Smart City Layer | Human Body Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Street Layer** | Sensory Nerves & Skin | Detects light, temperature, pressure, motion |
| **City Layer** | Spinal Cord & Nervous System | Transmits raw signals toward the brain |
| **Data Center Layer** | Brain & Memory | Processes signals, stores memories, makes decisions |
| **Services Layer** | Muscles & Actions | Executes decisions (moves hand, speaks, walks) |

When you touch a hot surface:
- **Skin (Street Layer)** feels the heat via nerve endings.
- **Nerves (City Layer)** carry that pain signal up the spinal cord.
- **Brain (Data Center Layer)** interprets it as "dangerous" and decides to withdraw.
- **Muscles (Services Layer)** actually move your hand away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why four layers?** Each layer abstracts the complexity of the layer below it. A city planner using the **Services Layer** does NOT need to know the firmware version of a streetlight sensor — that detail is hidden within the **Street Layer**. This is the fundamental principle of **abstraction in IoT architecture design**.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

When designing a Smart City, the following standard metrics govern each layer:

- **Latency budget:** **< 10 ms** for safety-critical (e.g., traffic signal control), **< 1 s** for monitoring, **< 1 hour** for analytics.
- **Device density:** **10,000 to 1,000,000 devices per km²** in dense urban deployments.
- **Data volume:** Smart cities generate roughly **1.5 GB per citizen per day** in sensor and behavioral data.
- **Uptime requirement:** **99.99 % (Four Nines)** for critical city services.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer 1 — The Street Layer (Perception Layer)

The **Street Layer** is the physical foundation of any Smart City. It consists of all the **edge devices** that interact directly with the urban environment.

### 2.1.1 Core Components

- **Sensors:** Temperature, humidity, air quality (PM2.5/PM10), noise, vibration, light, gas, motion, water level, parking occupancy.
- **Actuators:** Smart streetlights (LED dimming), traffic signal controllers, automatic water valves, public announcement speakers, electric vehicle charging stations.
- **RFID / NFC Tags:** Asset tracking, smart parking, waste bin identification.
- **Smart Meters:** Electricity, gas, and water consumption monitoring.
- **Cameras & Vision Sensors:** License plate recognition, crowd density, surveillance (with edge-AI inference).
- **Wearable & Mobile Devices:** Citizen smartphones, health bands.

### 2.1.2 Communication Technologies Used (Short-Range)

- **RFID (Radio-Frequency Identification):** Passive/active tags, range **< 10 m**, used for inventory and parking.
- **NFC (Near Field Communication):** Range **< 10 cm**, used for contactless payments and ticketing.
- **Bluetooth / BLE:** Range **~ 10–100 m**, used for indoor navigation and personal area networks.
- **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4):** Range **10–100 m**, mesh networking, low-power, used in streetlight clusters.
- **Z-Wave:** Range **~ 30 m**, primarily for home automation but extends to small city zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic — Why so many protocols?** Each protocol optimizes for one parameter: *range, bandwidth, or power consumption*. A streetlight cluster needs a low-power mesh (ZigBee), while a surveillance camera needs high bandwidth (Wi-Fi/5G). The Street Layer is therefore **heterogeneous** by design.

---

## 2.2 Layer 2 — The City Layer (Network/Transmission Layer)

The **City Layer** is the **nervous system** of the Smart City. Its job is to **transport** the raw, unstructured sensor data from millions of street-level devices to the Data Center, and to relay **control commands** back down to actuators.

### 2.2.1 Core Components

- **Gateways & Aggregators:** Aggregate data from thousands of street devices using short-range protocols and forward via long-range links.
- **Routers & Switches:** Backbone wired infrastructure (Fiber-to-the-Home / Fiber-to-the-Curb).
- **Base Stations:** 4G LTE / 5G NR cell towers providing wide-area cellular coverage.
- **LPWAN Base Stations:** LoRaWAN gateways, NB-IoT, Sigfox towers.
- **Middleware:** Message brokers (MQTT broker, Kafka, CoAP server) that decouple producers (sensors) from consumers (applications).
- **Edge / Fog Nodes:** Local mini-data-centers (e.g., on traffic signal poles) that perform preliminary filtering.

### 2.2.2 Communication Technologies Used (Long-Range)

- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11ax / Wi-Fi 6/6E):** Up to **9.6 Gbps**, range **< 100 m**, used for hotspots and video.
- **Cellular 4G LTE:** Up to **1 Gbps**, ubiquitous coverage.
- **Cellular 5G:** Up to **10 Gbps**, latency **< 1 ms** (URLLC slice), supports **1 million devices/km²** (mMTC slice).
- **LoRaWAN:** Range **2–15 km**, data rate **0.3–50 kbps**, ultra-low power.
- **NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT):** Range **< 35 km**, optimized for static sensors.
- **Sigfox:** Range **30–50 km**, ultra-narrowband, very low data rate.

### 2.2.3 The Data Flow Pipeline

$$\text{Street Sensor} \xrightarrow{\text{Short-range RF (ZigBee/BLE)}} \text{Gateway} \xrightarrow{\text{Long-range (5G/LoRa)}} \text{City Backbone} \xrightarrow{\text{Fiber}} \text{Data Center}$$

---

## 2.3 Layer 3 — The Data Center Layer (Processing/Platform Layer)

The **Data Center Layer** is the **brain** of the Smart City. It receives massive volumes of unstructured, noisy, real-time data and transforms it into **clean, structured, actionable intelligence**.

### 2.3.1 Core Components

- **Cloud Computing Infrastructure:** Virtualized servers (AWS EC2, Azure VMs, OpenStack).
- **Edge / Fog Computing Nodes:** Process latency-critical data close to the source.
- **Big Data Storage:** Hadoop HDFS, NoSQL databases (MongoDB, Cassandra), Data Lakes.
- **Stream Processing Engines:** Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Apache Spark Streaming.
- **Analytics & AI/ML Engines:** Time-series forecasting (LSTM), anomaly detection, computer vision inference.
- **Digital Twin Platform:** A real-time virtual replica of the city for simulation and what-if analysis.

### 2.3.2 Key Equations for Layer Sizing

The processing load $P_{\text{DC}}$ of a data center handling $N$ street devices each producing $D$ bits per second is given by:

$$P_{\text{DC}} = N \cdot D \cdot C_{\text{proc}}$$

where $C_{\text{proc}}$ is the average CPU cycles required per bit of data.

The **storage requirement** for a city of population $H$ over a retention period of $T$ days:

$$S_{\text{storage}} = H \cdot 1.5\,\text{GB/day} \cdot T\,\text{days}$$

### 2.3.3 Latency Calculation for Edge vs Cloud

For a device at distance $d$ from the cloud data center communicating at propagation speed roughly equal to $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber, the **propagation latency** is:

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{fiber}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Edge Computing?** If $d = 2000$ km, $t_{\text{prop}} = 10$ ms just for one-way fiber travel. For autonomous vehicle collision avoidance requiring **< 5 ms** total reaction time, cloud-only processing is **physically impossible**. Edge nodes placed within **< 50 km** drop this to **< 0.25 ms**.

---

## 2.4 Layer 4 — The Services Layer (Application Layer)

The **Services Layer** is the visible, citizen-facing top of the stack. It delivers **value** by translating raw intelligence into user experiences, dashboards, alerts, and automated actions.

### 2.4.1 Core Service Categories (KTU-Exam Favourite)

| Service Domain | Example Use Case | Key IoT Inputs |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Transportation** | Adaptive traffic signals, real-time bus tracking | Traffic cameras, GPS, loop sensors |
| **Smart Healthcare** | Remote patient monitoring, epidemic tracking | Wearables, air-quality sensors |
| **Smart Grid / Energy** | Dynamic load balancing, outage detection | Smart meters, solar inverters |
| **Smart Water Management** | Leak detection, flood prediction | Pressure sensors, flow meters |
| **Smart Waste Management** | Route optimization for collection trucks | Ultrasonic fill-level sensors in bins |
| **Public Safety** | Gunshot detection, crowd density alerts | Acoustic sensors, video analytics |
| **Smart Governance** | Citizen grievance portals, digital twins | Aggregated city KPIs |

### 2.4.2 Service Delivery Mechanisms

- **Web Dashboards** (for city operators)
- **Mobile Apps** (for citizens)
- **SMS / IVR Alerts** (for low-internet populations)
- **APIs** (REST/GraphQL for third-party developers)
- **Automated Actuator Commands** (e.g., opening a drainage valve)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data Center Processing Load | $P_{\text{DC}} = N \cdot D \cdot C_{\text{proc}}$ | CPU cycles/s |
| 2 | Storage Requirement | $S_{\text{storage}} = H \cdot 1.5\,\text{GB/day} \cdot T$ | GB |
| 3 | Propagation Latency | $t_{\text{prop}} = d \,/\, v_{\text{fiber}}$ | seconds |
| 4 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = B \cdot RTT$ | bits |
| 5 | Device Density (5G mMTC) | Up to $10^6$ devices $/ \text{km}^2$ | devices/km² |
| 6 | Battery Life (LoRaWAN node) | $L = \frac{C_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{avg}}} \cdot \eta$ | years |
| 7 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick for Exams — "S-C-D-S" = "Skin-Cord-Brain-Speech"** = **Street – City – Data Center – Services**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Layer Comparison for KTU Answers

| Parameter | Street Layer | City Layer | Data Center Layer | Services Layer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Equivalent** | Physical / Data Link | Network / Transport | Session / Presentation | Application |
| **Primary Function** | Sense & Actuate | Transport & Route | Process & Store | Serve & Act |
| **Key Devices** | Sensors, RFID tags, actuators | Gateways, routers, 5G towers | Servers, databases, AI engines | Apps, dashboards, APIs |
| **Data State** | Analog → Digital | Encapsulated Packets | Structured Records | Insights / Actions |
| **Protocol Examples** | BLE, ZigBee, Modbus | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, 5G NR | Kafka, Spark, gRPC | REST, GraphQL, WebSocket |
| **Power Profile** | Battery / Energy Harvesting | Mains Powered | Utility-Powered (MW) | End-User Device |
| **Latency Contribution** | **< 1 ms** | **1–50 ms** | **10–500 ms** | **User-Perceived (seconds)** |
| **Security Concern** | Physical tampering | Man-in-the-middle | Data breach | Privacy / API abuse |

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Latency Budget for an Adaptive Traffic Signal

A canonical KTU problem: *"An adaptive traffic signal receives vehicle density data from a roadside camera. The camera is 500 m from a fog node, and the fog node is 10 km from the central data center. Calculate the total one-way latency for a cloud-based decision versus an edge-based decision. Assume $v_{\text{fiber}} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s and processing time at the data center = 80 ms, at the fog node = 5 ms."*

### Step 1 — Define the latency components

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{proc}} + t_{\text{queue}}$$

For simplicity in KTU problems, we assume $t_{\text{queue}} \approx 0$.

### Step 2 — Cloud-Based Path (Camera → Base Station → Fiber → Data Center)

**Propagation across 10 km of fiber:**

$$t_{\text{prop,cloud}} = \frac{10{,}000\,\text{m}}{2 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s} = 0.05\,\text{ms}$$

**Processing at data center:**

$$t_{\text{proc,cloud}} = 80\,\text{ms}$$

**Total cloud latency:**

$$t_{\text{total,cloud}} = 0.05\,\text{ms} + 80\,\text{ms} = 80.05\,\text{ms}$$

### Step 3 — Edge-Based Path (Camera → 500 m wireless → Fog Node)

**Propagation over 500 m (assume wireless, $v \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s):**

$$t_{\text{prop,edge}} = \frac{500\,\text{m}}{3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}} = 1.67\,\mu\text{s} \approx 0.0017\,\text{ms}$$

**Processing at fog node:**

$$t_{\text{proc,edge}} = 5\,\text{ms}$$

**Total edge latency:**

$$t_{\text{total,edge}} = 0.0017\,\text{ms} + 5\,\text{ms} = 5.0017\,\text{ms}$$

### Step 4 — Comparative Decision

$$\Delta t = t_{\text{total,cloud}} - t_{\text{total,edge}} = 80.05 - 5.0017 = 75.05\,\text{ms}$$

**Conclusion:** Edge processing reduces latency by **~ 16×**, which is critical when a traffic signal must change state within the **green-signal safety window of 100–200 ms** to prevent rear-end collisions.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Data Volume Estimation for KTU Problems

**Problem:** *"A smart city deploys 50,000 streetlight nodes. Each node reports brightness, energy consumption, and fault status every 5 minutes. Each packet is 64 bytes. Calculate the daily data volume in GB."*

### Step 1 — Number of packets per node per day

$$N_{\text{pkts/node/day}} = \frac{24 \times 60}{5} = 288\,\text{packets/day}$$

### Step 2 — Total packets per day across the city

$$N_{\text{pkts,total}} = 50{,}000 \times 288 = 1.44 \times 10^7\,\text{packets/day}$$

### Step 3 — Total bytes per day

$$D_{\text{bytes}} = 1.44 \times 10^7 \times 64\,\text{B} = 9.216 \times 10^8\,\text{B}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to GB (using 1 GB = 10⁹ B for engineering estimate)

$$D_{\text{GB}} = \frac{9.216 \times 10^8}{10^9} = 0.9216\,\text{GB/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single streetlight network generates nearly **1 GB/day**. With video, air quality, and water sensors added, total smart-city data easily reaches **terabytes per day**, justifying the need for Data Center Layer investment.

## 4.2 Data Flow Sequence — Citizen Reporting a Pothole via App

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Citizen as Citizen Smartphone
    participant App as Services Layer Mobile App
    participant API as Services Layer REST API
    participant Cloud as Data Center Layer AI Engine

    Citizen->>App: Captures photo and GPS of pothole
    App->>API: POST /report with image and location
    API->>Cloud: Forwards to image classification AI
    Cloud->>DB: Stores report with severity score
    DB-->>Cloud: Confirms write
    Cloud-->>API: Returns ticket ID and ETA
    API-->>App: Sends confirmation to citizen
    Cloud->>Worker: Pushes work order to municipal tablet
    Worker->>Sensor: Cross-validates with vibration sensor data
    Sensor-->>Worker: Confirms pothole location
    Worker->>Cloud: Marks ticket as resolved
    Cloud->>App: Sends resolution notification
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
*List the four layers of a Smart City IoT architecture and state the primary function of each in one sentence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Street Layer:** Contains physical sensors, actuators, RFID tags, and smart meters that sense urban phenomena and act on the environment. **(1 mark)**
2. **City Layer:** Provides the communication infrastructure (gateways, 5G, LoRaWAN, fiber, MQTT middleware) that transports data between the Street and Data Center layers. **(1 mark)**
3. **Data Center Layer:** Performs storage, processing, analytics, and AI-based decision-making on the aggregated city data. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
*Differentiate between the Street Layer and the Services Layer in a Smart City IoT system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Street Layer | Services Layer |
|---|---|---|
| **Position** | Bottom of the stack | Top of the stack |
| **Nature** | Physical / hardware | Software / user-facing |
| **Examples** | Temperature sensor, RFID tag, smart streetlight | Mobile app, web dashboard, SMS alert |
| **User Interaction** | None (machine-to-machine) | Direct human interaction |
| **Data Direction** | Generates raw data upward | Sends commands downward (or displays insights) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the four-layer architecture of a Smart City IoT system. List **two example devices** from each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart city has **100,000 air quality sensors**. Each sensor transmits a **32-byte** packet **every 10 minutes** to the Data Center Layer via LoRaWAN gateways. Calculate:
- (i) The number of packets generated per day by the entire network.
- (ii) The total daily data volume in **MB**.
- (iii) Justify whether a cloud-only architecture can handle this load, or whether edge processing is required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Four-Layer Architecture Diagram (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a block diagram with four clearly labelled stacked blocks from bottom to top:

```
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  SERVICES LAYER  Mobile Apps, Web Dashboards,|
|                  Public APIs, Alert Systems   |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  DATA CENTER LAYER  Cloud Servers, Big Data,  |
|                      AI/ML, Digital Twin      |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  CITY LAYER  Gateways, 5G/LoRa, Fiber, MQTT  |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  STREET LAYER  Sensors, Actuators, RFID,      |
|                Smart Meters, Cameras          |
+-----------------------------------------------+
```

**Explanation (2 marks):** The Street Layer interacts with the physical city; the City Layer transports the data; the Data Center Layer processes and stores it; the Services Layer delivers intelligence to citizens and operators.

**Two devices per layer (2 marks):**

- **Street Layer:** PM2.5 air quality sensor, smart streetlight. **(0.5 mark)**
- **City Layer:** LoRaWAN gateway, 5G gNodeB base station. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Data Center Layer:** Hadoop cluster, TensorFlow AI server. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Services Layer:** Citizen mobile app, city operator dashboard. **(0.5 mark)**

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**(i) Packets per day per sensor:**

$$N_{\text{pkts/sensor/day}} = \frac{24 \times 60\,\text{min}}{10\,\text{min}} = 144\,\text{packets/day}$$

**Total packets per day for the network:**

$$N_{\text{pkts,total}} = 100{,}000 \times 144 = 1.44 \times 10^7\,\text{packets/day} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Total daily data volume:**

$$D_{\text{bytes}} = 1.44 \times 10^7 \times 32\,\text{B} = 4.608 \times 10^8\,\text{B}$$

Converting to MB (1 MB = 10⁶ B):

$$D_{\text{MB}} = \frac{4.608 \times 10^8}{10^6} = 460.8\,\text{MB/day} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Cloud-only vs Edge justification (3 marks):**

- The data volume of **~ 461 MB/day is small for cloud** — cloud can easily handle it in terms of storage.
- However, **latency-critical applications** (e.g., pollution alerts to asthma patients) require **< 1 second** end-to-end response. Cloud propagation alone from edge to data center can take **20–50 ms**, plus processing **100 ms**, plus return trip — totaling **> 200 ms**.
- **Conclusion:** Cloud-only is sufficient for **historical analytics and dashboards**, but **edge processing is required for real-time alerting and anomaly detection**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **City Layer** of a Smart City IoT architecture. Discuss the roles of **gateways, 5G, LoRaWAN, and middleware (MQTT)**, with their typical range, bandwidth, and use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A traffic camera is located **15 km** away from the central data center. The signal travels over fiber at $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The data center takes **120 ms** to process the image and return a decision.
- (i) Calculate the **one-way propagation delay**.
- (ii) Calculate the **total round-trip latency** (propagation + processing × 2).
- (iii) For an autonomous vehicle that must brake within **50 ms** of obstacle detection, determine whether the cloud-only solution is feasible. If not, propose an alternative. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — City Layer Detailed Explanation (7 marks)

The **City Layer** (also called the Network or Transmission Layer) is responsible for the **reliable, secure, and timely transport** of data between the millions of devices in the Street Layer and the processing infrastructure in the Data Center Layer. **(1 mark)**

**Key Components:**

- **Gateways (2 marks):**
  - Aggregate data from short-range protocols (ZigBee, BLE) and forward via long-range links.
  - Example: A ZigBee-to-5G gateway collects data from 500 streetlights in a mesh and uploads via cellular.
  - Range: 10–100 m on the device side, **several km** on the WAN side.

- **5G NR (New Radio) (1 mark):**
  - Range: **up to 1 km in dense urban**, **up to 10 km in suburban**.
  - Bandwidth: **up to 10 Gbps** peak; supports **1 million devices/km²** (mMTC) and **< 1 ms latency** (URLLC).
  - Use case: Autonomous vehicles, real-time HD video surveillance.

- **LoRaWAN (1 mark):**
  - Range: **2–15 km** in urban areas.
  - Bandwidth: **0.3–50 kbps** (very low).
  - Use case: Battery-powered air-quality sensors, smart water meters.

- **MQTT Middleware (1 mark):**
  - Lightweight publish-subscribe protocol running on a **broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ).
  - Decouples sensors (publishers) from applications (subscribers).
  - Sits on TCP port 1883; very low overhead (~ 2 bytes header).

**Summary statement (1 mark):** The City Layer is therefore a **heterogeneous, protocol-rich transport fabric** that must simultaneously support ultra-low-power meters and bandwidth-hungry cameras.

---

##### Part (b) — Latency Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) One-way propagation delay:**

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{fiber}}} = \frac{15{,}000\,\text{m}}{2 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s} = 0.075\,\text{ms} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Total round-trip latency:**

$$t_{\text{RTT}} = 2 \cdot t_{\text{prop}} + 2 \cdot t_{\text{proc}}$$

$$t_{\text{RTT}} = 2(0.075) + 2(120) = 0.15 + 240 = 240.15\,\text{ms} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Feasibility analysis (3 marks):**

- **Required reaction time:** **50 ms**.
- **Cloud-only latency:** **240.15 ms**.
- **Verdict:** **Cloud-only is NOT feasible.** It is **~ 4.8× slower** than the safety threshold. A vehicle traveling at 60 km/h covers **16.67 m per second** — in 240 ms it moves **4 m** after the obstacle is already detected, which is **catastrophically late**.
- **Proposed Alternative:** **Edge / Fog Computing.** Place a fog node at the traffic intersection (e.g., on the traffic signal pole) within **< 100 m** of the camera. With $d = 100$ m and $t_{\text{proc,edge}} = 5$ ms:

$$t_{\text{RTT,edge}} = 2 \times \frac{100}{2 \times 10^8} + 2 \times 5 \approx 0.001 + 10 = 10.001\,\text{ms}$$

- This is **safely under the 50 ms threshold**, making autonomous braking **physically possible**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the City Layer with the Street Layer** — students often write "5G base station" in the Street Layer. Remember: **Street = sensing hardware; City = communication infrastructure.**
> 2. **Forgetting the arrow direction in the diagram** — always show **data flowing upward** (sensors → apps) and **control commands flowing downward** (apps → actuators). A unidirectional diagram loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors in numericals** — mixing up KB (10³), KiB (2¹⁰), MB (10⁶), and MiB (2²⁰). KTU expects the **SI/declaration form**: 1 MB = 10⁶ B unless specified.
> 4. **Omitting middleware in the City Layer** — MQTT broker is **mandatory** in KTU's expected answer for 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Writing "IoT" generically** — use specific terms like *LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, ZigBee, 5G mMTC, MQTT* to score full marks.
> 6. **Skipping the Digital Twin** in the Data Center Layer — this is a **2024-scheme new addition** and carries high weightage.
> 7. **Not labelling the diagram axes/layers clearly** — a diagram without clear layer names and at least 2 components per layer loses **2 marks** out of 7.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smart City IoT Four-Layer Architecture**

- **Street Layer** = **Perception / Sensing** = physical sensors, actuators, RFID, smart meters, cameras. Uses short-range protocols (ZigBee, BLE, NFC).
- **City Layer** = **Network / Transmission** = gateways, 5G, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, fiber backbone, MQTT/CoAP middleware. Handles transport of data.
- **Data Center Layer** = **Processing / Platform** = cloud servers, Hadoop data lake, Kafka stream engine, AI/ML models, digital twin. Turns data into intelligence.
- **Services Layer** = **Application** = citizen mobile apps, web dashboards, alert systems, public APIs, automated actuator control. Delivers value to users.
- **Data flow** is **bidirectional**: raw data flows up, control commands flow down.
- **Latency budget:** safety-critical < 10 ms, monitoring < 1 s, analytics can tolerate minutes/hours.
- **Edge / Fog computing** is essential when cloud round-trip latency exceeds the application's safety threshold (e.g., autonomous vehicles).
- **5G mMTC** supports up to **1 million devices per km²** — the enabler of dense urban IoT.
- **MQTT** is the de-facto messaging protocol in the City Layer for lightweight, low-bandwidth, publish-subscribe IoT communication.
- **Digital Twin** is a real-time virtual replica of the city used for simulation, planning, and predictive maintenance.
- **Memory trick:** **"S-C-D-S" = Street → City → Data Center → Services**, analogous to **Skin → Nerves → Brain → Muscles** in the human body.
- **Key formulae to remember:**
  - $t_{\text{prop}} = d / v_{\text{fiber}}$
  - $P_{\text{DC}} = N \cdot D \cdot C_{\text{proc}}$
  - $S_{\text{storage}} = H \cdot 1.5\,\text{GB/day} \cdot T$
  - $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$
- **For a 14-mark question**, always include: (1) labelled diagram, (2) at least 2 components per layer, (3) example use case, (4) latency/bandwidth protocol details, (5) one numerical example.
- **For a 3-mark question**, use a comparison table for crisp presentation.
